two-sum.name: Brute Force
two-sum.description: Build a correctness baseline for Two Sum (Sorted & Unsorted) by directly enumerating the candidate states allowed by the problem. This version prioritizes transparent control flow and exhaustive coverage over asymptotic efficiency.

two-sum.name: Better
two-sum.description: Reduce repeated work in Two Sum (Sorted & Unsorted) with the intermediate structure or cached state used by the standard better approach. It preserves the brute-force reasoning while improving one major time or space bottleneck.

two-sum.name: Optimal (Two Pointers / Sorting approach)
two-sum.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Two Sum asks for two different elements whose values add up to the target. The important detail in this entry is that the array may be unsorted, but the selected approach becomes powerful only after we sort. If the problem asks for original indices, we must sort value-index pairs, not just raw values. If the problem only asks whether a pair exists or asks for values in a sorted array, raw sorting is enough.

### Core intuition
After sorting, the smallest remaining value sits on the left and the largest remaining value sits on the right. Their sum tells us which side is impossible. If the sum is too small, pairing the left value with anything smaller than the right would only be even smaller, so the left value cannot be part of a solution and left moves forward. If the sum is too large, pairing the right value with anything larger than the left would only be too large, so the right value is too big and right moves backward.

### Invariant
At every step, all pairs outside the current left-right window have already been ruled out by sorted order. We are not skipping a single pair by guesswork; we are discarding a whole group of impossible pairs.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not return sorted indices if the original problem expects original indices. Keep pairs like value plus originalIndex before sorting. Also remember that left must stay strictly less than right, because the same element cannot be used twice.

### Complexity
Sorting costs O(N log N), and the inward scan costs O(N). The scan uses constant extra state, while index-preserving sorting stores pairs if original indices are required.

kadanes-algorithm.name: Better

kadanes-algorithm.name: Optimal (Kadane's Dynamic Approach)
kadanes-algorithm.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Kadane's Algorithm solves maximum subarray sum: choose one contiguous block whose sum is as large as possible. The word contiguous is the heart of the problem. We cannot pick scattered positives; once we start a block, every element until we stop is included.

### Core intuition
At each index, there are only two honest choices: extend the subarray that ended at the previous index, or start a new subarray at the current number. If the previous running sum is negative, it actively hurts any future block. A future subarray is always better without carrying that negative baggage, so we reset.

### State meaning
currentSum means the best sum of a subarray that must end at the current position. maxSum means the best subarray sum seen anywhere so far. These are different jobs. currentSum is local and may reset; maxSum is the historical record and must never forget a previous peak.

### Invariant
After processing nums[i], currentSum is the best sum of a subarray ending at i after discarding harmful negative prefixes, and maxSum is the best sum among all subarrays seen from 0 through i.

### Edge cases
All-negative arrays are the trap. maxSum must update before a reset, or [-3, -1, -2] would incorrectly return 0 instead of -1.

### Complexity
One scan, O(N) time. Only currentSum and maxSum are stored, so O(1) extra space.

sort-an-array-of-0-s-1-s-and-2-s.name: Optimal (Dutch National Flag Algorithm)
sort-an-array-of-0-s-1-s-and-2-s.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Sort Colors is not a general sorting problem. The array contains only 0, 1, and 2, and the solution should sort in-place. That special three-value constraint lets us beat comparison sorting.

### Core intuition
Maintain three regions. Everything before low is already 0. Everything from low to mid - 1 is already 1. Everything after high is already 2. The mid pointer is the inspector standing at the first unknown item. If mid sees 0, swap it into the left region and move both low and mid. If mid sees 1, it already belongs in the middle and only mid moves. If mid sees 2, swap it into the right region and move high left.

### Critical detail
When mid sees 2, do not increment mid after the swap. The item swapped in from high came from the unknown region, so it still needs inspection. Skipping it is the classic bug.

### Invariant
Before every loop: [0, low) is all 0s, [low, mid) is all 1s, (high, end] is all 2s, and [mid, high] is unknown.

### Complexity
Each step shrinks the unknown region or grows a solved region. Time is O(N), and the sort uses only three pointers, so O(1) extra space.

subarray-sum-equals-k.name: Optimal (Prefix Sum + Hash Map)
subarray-sum-equals-k.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Subarray Sum Equals K counts how many contiguous subarrays sum to k. The array can contain negative numbers, so a sliding window is not reliable: adding a number can lower the sum, and removing a number can raise it.

### Core intuition
Use prefix sums. currentSum is the total from index 0 to the current index. If an earlier prefix was currentSum - k, then removing that earlier prefix leaves exactly k between the next index and the current index. The map stores how many times each prefix sum has appeared because the same prefix value can create multiple valid subarrays.

### Why the map starts with 0: 1
The prefix sum before the array starts is 0. Seeding 0 lets us count subarrays that begin at index 0, such as [1,2] when k is 3.

### Invariant
Before storing the current prefix, the map contains prefix sums from earlier positions. Each lookup counts valid subarrays ending exactly at the current index.

### Complexity
One pass over nums, O(N) time with average O(1) hash lookups. In the worst case every prefix is unique, so space is O(N).

merge-intervals.name: Optimal (Sort & Merge)
merge-intervals.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Merge Intervals receives ranges and asks for the smallest list of ranges that covers the same points. Overlapping intervals become one interval; separated intervals remain separate.

### Core intuition
Sort by start time. Once intervals are chronological, the only interval that can overlap the current one is the last interval already placed in merged. Older merged intervals end no later than that last interval, so if current does not overlap the last one, it cannot overlap any older one either.

### Merge rule
Let last be the final interval in merged. If current.start <= last.end, the two ranges overlap or touch, so stretch last.end to max(last.end, current.end). If current.start > last.end, there is a gap, so current starts a new block.

### Invariant
After each sorted interval is processed, merged contains non-overlapping intervals that exactly cover all intervals seen so far.

### Edge cases
A contained interval like [2,3] inside [1,10] must not shrink the end. Touching intervals such as [1,4] and [4,5] merge in the standard definition because the boundary is shared.

### Complexity
Sorting costs O(N log N), and the scan is O(N). The output can hold up to N intervals, so output space is O(N).

next-permutation.name: Brute Force
next-permutation.description: Build a correctness baseline for Next Permutation by directly enumerating the candidate states allowed by the problem. This version prioritizes transparent control flow and exhaustive coverage over asymptotic efficiency.

next-permutation.name: Optimal (Naravana Pandita Algorithm)
next-permutation.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Next Permutation asks for the next lexicographically greater arrangement using the same numbers, modified in place. If the current arrangement is already the greatest possible order, we wrap around to the smallest order.

### Core intuition
Look from the right because the right side is the lowest-place part of the permutation. A descending suffix, such as 4,3,2, is already the largest arrangement of those suffix values. To make the whole array just slightly bigger, find the first index before that suffix where nums[i] < nums[i + 1]. That index is the pivot.

### Why the successor and reverse steps are both needed
The pivot needs to increase by the smallest possible amount. In the descending suffix, scanning from the right finds the smallest value greater than the pivot. Swap them. After the swap, reverse the suffix so the tail becomes the smallest possible order. That gives the immediate next permutation, not merely some larger permutation.

### Invariant
Everything to the right of the pivot was the largest possible suffix before the swap. After raising the pivot minimally, reversing the suffix makes the tail as small as possible.

### Complexity
At most three linear walks: find pivot, find successor, reverse suffix. Time is O(N), extra space is O(1).

find-the-duplicate-number.name: Optimal (Floyd's Tortoise & Hare)
find-the-duplicate-number.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Find the Duplicate Number gives n + 1 values where every value is between 1 and n. There is at least one repeated value, and we must find it without modifying the array and without extra memory.

### Core intuition
Treat each index as a node and each value as a pointer to the next index. Because values are valid indices from 1 to n, nums[i] always points somewhere inside the array. The duplicate means two different positions point to the same next position. In a linked-list shape, that creates a cycle. The duplicate value is the entry of that cycle.

### Floyd's two phases
First, slow moves one step and fast moves two steps until they meet inside the cycle. This proves a cycle exists but not where it begins. Second, reset slow to nums[0]. Move slow and fast one step at a time. Their meeting point is the cycle entry, which equals the duplicate value.

### Invariant
Pointer movement follows values, not array order. slow = nums[slow] means the value at slow tells the next index. The algorithm never edits nums, so it satisfies the no-modification rule.

### Complexity
Both phases together move O(N) steps. Only slow and fast are stored, so space is O(1).

majority-element-n-2-times.name: Better
majority-element-n-2-times.description: Reduce repeated work in Majority Element (>N/2 times) with the intermediate structure or cached state used by the standard better approach. It preserves the brute-force reasoning while improving one major time or space bottleneck.

majority-element-n-2-times.name: Optimal (Boyer-Moore Voting Algorithm)
majority-element-n-2-times.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Majority Element asks for the value that appears more than floor(N / 2) times. That guarantee is strong: the majority appears more often than all other values combined.

### Core intuition
Boyer-Moore Voting pairs off different values and cancels them. If one majority value truly appears more than half the time, every non-majority value can cancel one occurrence of it, and the majority still has at least one survivor. The candidate variable stores the current survivor, and count stores its temporary vote balance.

### State meaning
When count is zero, the previous segment has fully canceled itself out, so the current number can safely become the new candidate. When the current number equals candidate, count increases. When it differs, count decreases because one candidate vote and one opposing vote cancel.

### Invariant
After processing a prefix, removing canceled pairs from that prefix leaves count copies of the current candidate as unresolved votes. If a global majority exists, it cannot be completely canceled by all other values.

### Edge cases
If the problem does not guarantee a majority, a second verification pass is required. In this standard version, the guarantee lets us return the final candidate.

### Complexity
One pass, O(N) time, and two variables, O(1) space.

best-time-to-buy-and-sell-stock.name: Brute Force
best-time-to-buy-and-sell-stock.description: Build a correctness baseline for Best Time to Buy and Sell Stock by directly enumerating the candidate states allowed by the problem. This version prioritizes transparent control flow and exhaustive coverage over asymptotic efficiency.
best-time-to-buy-and-sell-stock.spaceComplexity: O(1),We use only constant extra space (for variables like `maxProfit`, `profit`)

best-time-to-buy-and-sell-stock.name: Optimal (Greedy Min-Pointer)
best-time-to-buy-and-sell-stock.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Best Time to Buy and Sell Stock asks for one buy day and one later sell day that maximize profit. We cannot sell before buying, and we return zero profit if prices only fall.

### Core intuition
When standing on a possible sell day, the best possible buy day is the lowest price seen before or on that day. So we scan left to right, keeping minPrice as the cheapest buy opportunity so far. For each price, treat it as today's sell price and compute price - minPrice. If that beats maxProfit, update the answer.

### Why this is safe
A future sell day can only use buy days before it. Since minPrice is always the minimum of all earlier prices, it is the best buy candidate for every future day. We never need to remember all previous prices, only the cheapest one.

### Invariant
After processing day i, minPrice is the minimum price among days 0..i, and maxProfit is the best legal profit using a sell day from 0..i.

### Edge cases
Strictly decreasing prices should return 0, not a negative number. A single price also returns 0 because there is no later sell day.

### Complexity
One scan gives O(N) time. minPrice and maxProfit are the only state, so O(1) space.

rearrange-array-elements-by-sign.name: Optimal (Two Index Filling)
rearrange-array-elements-by-sign.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Rearrange Array Elements by Sign asks for a new arrangement where positive and negative values alternate, starting with a positive value. In the standard version of this problem, the number of positives and negatives is equal, and we should preserve the original relative order inside each sign group.

### Core intuition
The target positions are known before the scan even begins. Positives belong at even indices: 0, 2, 4, and so on. Negatives belong at odd indices: 1, 3, 5, and so on. Instead of first collecting positives and negatives separately, we can walk through the array once and place each number into the next open slot for its sign.

### State meaning
positiveIndex is the next even position waiting for a positive number. negativeIndex is the next odd position waiting for a negative number. After placing a value, that pointer jumps by 2 because the next valid slot for the same sign is two cells away.

### Invariant
After processing any prefix of nums, the answer array has the earliest seen positives placed in the earliest even slots, and the earliest seen negatives placed in the earliest odd slots. Nothing is reordered within the positive group or within the negative group.

### Edge cases
This approach assumes the balanced version of the problem. If positives and negatives are not equal in count, the problem needs an extra rule for leftover values. Also, zero is not part of the usual input contract here; if it appears, the sign policy must be defined.

### Complexity
The scan is O(N), and every number is written exactly once. The answer array uses O(N) extra space, which is expected because the problem asks for a rearranged array.

leaders-in-an-array.name: Optimal (Reverse Scan)
leaders-in-an-array.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Leaders in an Array asks for every element that is greater than every element on its right side. The rightmost element is always a leader because no later value can beat it.

### Core intuition
The decision for index i depends only on the suffix to its right. Scanning from the left leaves that suffix unknown, but scanning from the right makes it known. Keep maxRight as the largest value already seen on the right. If the current value is greater than maxRight, it is a leader.

### State meaning
maxRight is the strongest value to the right of the current index. leaders stores accepted values while scanning right to left, so it is reversed at the end to restore original order.

### Invariant
Before checking arr[i], maxRight is exactly the maximum value in arr[i + 1...end]. That turns a nested comparison against the whole suffix into one comparison.

### Edge cases
This catalog follows the strict greater-than wording. If a platform treats equal values as leaders too, change the test from current > maxRight to current >= maxRight.

### Complexity
Each element is inspected once, so time is O(N). Ignoring the output list, extra space is O(1).

longest-consecutive-sequence.name: Better
longest-consecutive-sequence.description: Reduce repeated work in Longest Consecutive Sequence with the intermediate structure or cached state used by the standard better approach. It preserves the brute-force reasoning while improving one major time or space bottleneck.

longest-consecutive-sequence.name: Optimal (Set Lookup)
longest-consecutive-sequence.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Longest Consecutive Sequence gives an unsorted array and asks for the length of the longest run of consecutive integer values. The values do not need to be next to each other in the array; only the numeric sequence matters.

### Core intuition
Put all values into a set for O(1) average lookup. Then count a sequence only from its true beginning. A value x is a beginning only when x - 1 is absent. If x - 1 exists, then x belongs to a sequence that started earlier, so counting from x would repeat work.

### State meaning
numSet stores every unique value. longest stores the best run length found so far. When a start is found, current walks forward through current + 1 while those values exist.

### Invariant
Each consecutive run is expanded exactly once, from its smallest value. Non-start values are skipped because their sequence has already been assigned to an earlier start.

### Edge cases
Duplicates disappear inside the set, which is fine because duplicates do not lengthen a consecutive run. Empty input returns 0. Negative values work naturally because the rule is still x + 1.

### Complexity
Building the set is O(N). Every value participates in at most one forward expansion, so total time is O(N) on average. Space is O(N).

set-matrix-zeroes.description: Build a correctness baseline for Set Matrix Zeroes by directly enumerating the candidate states allowed by the problem. This version prioritizes transparent control flow and exhaustive coverage over asymptotic efficiency.
set-matrix-zeroes.timeComplexity: O(m * n * (m + n)), We iterate through every cell (m * n)

set-matrix-zeroes.name: Better
set-matrix-zeroes.description: Reduce repeated work in Set Matrix Zeroes with the intermediate structure or cached state used by the standard better approach. It preserves the brute-force reasoning while improving one major time or space bottleneck.

set-matrix-zeroes.name: Optimal (In-place Hashing)
set-matrix-zeroes.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Set Matrix Zeroes asks us to modify the matrix in place. If a cell is originally zero, its whole row and whole column must become zero. Newly written zeroes must not create extra zeroing decisions.

### Core intuition
We need to remember which rows and columns contained original zeroes. Instead of using separate arrays, store those markers inside the matrix itself. The first cell of a row marks that row. The first cell of a column marks that column.

### Why column zero is special
matrix[0][0] belongs to both the first row and the first column, so it cannot store both facts safely. This implementation uses col0 to remember whether the first column needs to become zero.

### Invariant
After the marking pass, matrix[i][0] tells whether row i must be zeroed, matrix[0][j] tells whether column j must be zeroed, and col0 tells whether column 0 must be zeroed.

### Edge cases
Zeros in the first row or first column are the trap. The second pass runs bottom-up so marker cells are used before they are overwritten.

### Complexity
The matrix is scanned a constant number of times, so time is O(M * N). Extra space is O(1).

rotate-matrix-by-90-degrees.name: Optimal (Transpose + Reverse)
rotate-matrix-by-90-degrees.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Rotate Matrix by 90 Degrees asks us to rotate a square matrix clockwise in place. In place means we should not build a second matrix just to hold the rotated answer.

### Core intuition
A clockwise rotation can be split into two simpler transformations. First transpose the matrix across its main diagonal, turning rows into columns. Then reverse every row, which flips those columns into the correct clockwise order.

### State meaning
During transpose, swap matrix[i][j] with matrix[j][i] only when j is greater than i. This swaps each off-diagonal pair exactly once. During row reversal, each row is mirrored horizontally.

### Why it works
After transpose, the old first row becomes the first column. Clockwise rotation needs that old first row to become the last column. Reversing each row moves it there.

### Edge cases
This method assumes an N by N matrix. Rectangular matrices change shape when rotated, so this exact in-place trick does not apply.

### Complexity
Transpose and row reversal together touch O(N^2) cells. Only temporary swap state is used, so extra space is O(1).

print-the-matrix-in-spiral-manner.name: Optimal (Boundary Shrinking)
print-the-matrix-in-spiral-manner.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Spiral Matrix asks us to return all cells in the order they are visited by walking around the outside layer, then the next inner layer, until nothing remains.

### Core intuition
The unvisited part is always a rectangle. Track four boundaries: top, bottom, left, and right. Walk across the top edge, down the right edge, across the bottom edge backward, and up the left edge. After each edge, shrink that boundary inward.

### State meaning
top and bottom are active row limits. left and right are active column limits. The result list records cells in visit order.

### Guard checks
After shrinking top or right, the rectangle may collapse. Check top <= bottom before reading the bottom edge, and check left <= right before reading the left edge. These checks prevent duplicate visits in thin layers.

### Invariant
At the start of every loop, cells outside the boundaries have already been added, and cells inside the boundaries are still unvisited.

### Complexity
Every cell is appended exactly once, so time is O(M * N). Extra working space is O(1), ignoring the required output list.

pascal-s-triangle.name: Optimal (Dynamic Construction)
pascal-s-triangle.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Pascal's Triangle asks us to generate the first numRows rows of a triangle where every row starts and ends with 1, and every inner value is the sum of the two values directly above it.

### Core intuition
Build from top to bottom. When row i is being created, row i - 1 is already complete. That means every inner cell has two known parents: previous[j - 1] and previous[j].

### State meaning
triangle stores completed rows. row is the new row being built. It can start filled with 1s because both ends are always 1; only the middle values need calculation.

### Invariant
After finishing row i, triangle contains correct rows from 0 through i. The next row can rely on the last completed row as its source of truth.

### Edge cases
numRows = 0 returns an empty list. numRows = 1 returns [[1]]. The inner loop only runs for rows with middle positions.

### Complexity
The output contains 1 + 2 + ... + N values, so time and output space are O(N^2).

majority-element-n-3-times.name: Better
majority-element-n-3-times.description: Reduce repeated work in Majority Element (>N/3 times) with the intermediate structure or cached state used by the standard better approach. It preserves the brute-force reasoning while improving one major time or space bottleneck.

majority-element-n-3-times.name: Optimal (Boyer-Moore Voting Variant)
majority-element-n-3-times.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Majority Element greater than N/3 asks for every value that appears more than floor(N / 3) times. There can be two answers, one answer, or no answers, but never three.

### Core intuition
Three different values cannot all appear more than N/3 times because that would require more than N total positions. So we keep at most two candidates. When a number matches a candidate, its counter grows. When a candidate slot has count 0, the number can take that slot. When the number matches neither candidate and both counts are positive, all three disagree, so both counts are reduced.

### Why verification is required
The voting phase gives possible survivors, not guaranteed winners. Unlike the common N/2 version, this problem does not promise that an answer exists. A second pass must count the survivors and check the threshold exactly.

### Invariant
Removing groups of three different values cannot completely destroy a true greater-than-N/3 value. If such a value exists, it can survive as one of the two candidates.

### Edge cases
Avoid returning the same value twice. Empty input returns an empty list. For very small arrays, the threshold is low, so unique values may qualify.

### Complexity
One voting pass plus one verification pass gives O(N) time. Only two candidates and two counters are stored, so extra space is O(1).

3-sum.description: Build a correctness baseline for 3 Sum (Unique Triplets) by directly enumerating the candidate states allowed by the problem. This version prioritizes transparent control flow and exhaustive coverage over asymptotic efficiency.

3-sum.name: Better
3-sum.description: Reduce repeated work in 3 Sum (Unique Triplets) with the intermediate structure or cached state used by the standard better approach. It preserves the brute-force reasoning while improving one major time or space bottleneck.

3-sum.name: Optimal (Sorting + Two Pointers)
3-sum.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
3Sum asks for all unique triplets whose values add up to zero. Unique matters: the same value triplet should appear once even if the input contains duplicate numbers.

### Core intuition
Sort the array, fix one value, and solve a two-sum problem on the values to its right. Sorting makes pointer movement meaningful: if the sum is too small, move left rightward; if the sum is too large, move right leftward.

### State meaning
i is the fixed first value. left and right search the remaining range. The current sum decides which pointer can move without missing a valid pair.

### Duplicate handling
Skip repeated fixed values at i. After recording a triplet, move left past equal values and right past equal values. These skips prevent duplicate triplets while preserving all unique answers.

### Invariant
For each fixed i, the two-pointer search checks the remaining sorted range without revisiting impossible pairs or repeating equal-value triplets.

### Complexity
Sorting costs O(N log N), and the fixed loop with two pointers costs O(N^2). Extra space depends on the sorting implementation, ignoring the output.

4-sum.name: Brute Force
4-sum.description: Build a correctness baseline for 4 Sum by directly enumerating the candidate states allowed by the problem. This version prioritizes transparent control flow and exhaustive coverage over asymptotic efficiency.

4-sum.name: Better
4-sum.description: Reduce repeated work in 4 Sum with the intermediate structure or cached state used by the standard better approach. It preserves the brute-force reasoning while improving one major time or space bottleneck.

4-sum.name: Optimal (Sorting + Nested Loops + Two Pointers)
4-sum.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
4Sum asks for all unique quadruplets whose values add up to a target. It is the same family as 3Sum, but now we fix two values before searching for the remaining pair.

### Core intuition
Sort the array. Pick the first value with i, pick the second with j, then use left and right on the range after j. For each fixed pair, the remaining problem is two-sum for target - nums[i] - nums[j].

### State meaning
i and j are the fixed prefix of the quadruplet. left and right search the rest of the sorted array. If the total is too small, left moves right. If the total is too large, right moves left.

### Duplicate handling
Skip duplicate values for i and j. After recording a quadruplet, skip duplicate left and right values before continuing. Without those skips, the same value quadruplet can appear multiple times.

### Invariant
For each unique fixed pair, the two-pointer scan explores all unique pairs to its right that could complete the target.

### Complexity
Sorting costs O(N log N). Two fixed loops plus a linear two-pointer scan give O(N^3) time. Extra working space is O(1) besides sorting and output.

largest-subarray-with-0-sum.name: Optimal (Prefix Sum Map)
largest-subarray-with-0-sum.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Largest Subarray with 0 Sum asks for the maximum length of a contiguous block whose values add up to zero. Contiguous is essential; scattered numbers do not count.

### Core intuition
Use prefix sums. If the same prefix sum appears at two different indices, then the values between those indices sum to zero. The total before the block and the total after the block are equal, so the block itself added no net value.

### State meaning
sum is the running prefix sum. firstSeen maps each prefix sum to the earliest index where it appeared. maxLen stores the best zero-sum length found so far.

### Why earliest index matters
When a prefix sum repeats, the longest subarray ending at the current index comes from the first time that sum appeared. Updating the map to a later index would shorten future answers, so store each sum only once.

### Edge cases
If the running sum itself becomes zero, the subarray from index 0 to the current index is valid. This can be handled by seeding prefix sum 0 at index -1 or by a direct sum == 0 check, as the current implementation does.

### Complexity
The scan is O(N), and map operations are O(1) on average. Space is O(N) in the worst case when all prefix sums are different.

count-number-of-subarrays-with-given-xor.name: Brute Force
count-number-of-subarrays-with-given-xor.description: Build a correctness baseline for Subarrays with XOR K by directly enumerating the candidate states allowed by the problem. This version prioritizes transparent control flow and exhaustive coverage over asymptotic efficiency.

count-number-of-subarrays-with-given-xor.name: Optimal (Prefix XOR Hash Map)
count-number-of-subarrays-with-given-xor.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Subarrays with XOR K asks us to count contiguous subarrays whose XOR is exactly K. Contiguous means the chosen values must form one uninterrupted block.

### Core intuition
Use prefix XOR, just like prefix sum, but with XOR's cancellation property. If xr is the XOR from the start of the array to the current index, and an earlier prefix had value need, then the subarray between them has XOR xr ^ need. We want that to equal K, so need must be xr ^ K.

### State meaning
xr is the running prefix XOR. freq maps each prefix XOR value to how many times it has appeared. count stores how many valid subarrays have been found so far.

### Why the map starts with 0: 1
The empty prefix before index 0 has XOR 0. Seeding 0 lets us count subarrays that begin at the first element.

### Invariant
Before storing the current prefix, freq contains only earlier prefixes. A lookup of xr ^ K counts all valid subarrays ending at the current index.

### Complexity
The array is scanned once, and hash map operations are O(1) on average. Time is O(N), and space is O(N) in the worst case.

merge-two-sorted-arrays-without-extra-sp.name: Optimal (Gap Method / Shell Sort Intuition)
merge-two-sorted-arrays-without-extra-sp.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Merge Sorted without Extra Space gives two sorted arrays and asks us to make their combined values sorted while keeping the values inside the two original arrays. We cannot allocate a third merged array.

### Core intuition
Treat both arrays as one virtual combined array. The gap method compares pairs of values that are gap positions apart, swapping if they are out of order. Then the gap shrinks, like Shell sort, until gap becomes 1 and all neighboring virtual positions are ordered.

### State meaning
gap controls how far apart two compared virtual positions are. left and right slide across the virtual combined length. A helper comparison decides whether each virtual index belongs to arr1 or arr2.

### Why this works
Large gaps move badly misplaced values across long distances early. Smaller gaps clean up the remaining local disorder. When gap is 1 and no adjacent virtual pair is inverted, the two arrays together are sorted.

### Edge cases
The comparison can happen inside arr1, across arr1 and arr2, or inside arr2. Boundary math must translate virtual index j into arr2[j - n] when j crosses the first array.

### Complexity
The scan for each gap is O(N + M), and there are O(log(N + M)) gap levels. Space is O(1).

find-the-repeating-and-missing-number.name: Better
find-the-repeating-and-missing-number.description: Reduce repeated work in Repeating & Missing Number with the intermediate structure or cached state used by the standard better approach. It preserves the brute-force reasoning while improving one major time or space bottleneck.

find-the-repeating-and-missing-number.name: Optimal (Mathematical Equations)
find-the-repeating-and-missing-number.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Repeating & Missing Number gives an array that should contain every number from 1 to N exactly once, but one number appears twice and one number is absent. We need return the repeating number and the missing number.

### Core intuition
Let X be the repeating number and Y be the missing number. The actual sum is larger or smaller than the expected sum by X - Y. The actual sum of squares differs by X^2 - Y^2, which equals (X - Y)(X + Y). Once we know X - Y and X + Y, solving for X and Y is direct.

### State meaning
expectedSum and expectedSquareSum describe a perfect array from 1 to N. actualSum and actualSquareSum come from the given array. diff is X - Y. squareDiff / diff gives X + Y.

### Invariant
The only corruption is one duplicated value and one missing value, so every difference between actual and expected can be explained entirely by X replacing Y.

### Edge cases
Use a wide numeric type for large N because sums of squares can overflow 32-bit integers. If the input does not follow the exactly-one-repeat-and-one-missing contract, these equations are not reliable.

### Complexity
One pass computes actual sums, and the formulas are constant time. Time is O(N), extra space is O(1).

count-inversions.name: Brute Force
count-inversions.description: Build a correctness baseline for Count Inversions by directly enumerating the candidate states allowed by the problem. This version prioritizes transparent control flow and exhaustive coverage over asymptotic efficiency.

count-inversions.name: Optimal (Merge Sort Variant)
count-inversions.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Count Inversions asks how many pairs i, j exist such that i < j and arr[i] > arr[j]. Each inversion is a place where a bigger value appears before a smaller value.

### Core intuition
Brute force checks every pair. Merge sort lets us count many pairs during the merge step. When the left half and right half are already sorted, if left[i] > right[j], then right[j] is smaller than left[i] and every later value in the left half. That creates left.length - i inversions at once.

### State meaning
The recursive sort returns a sorted version of each segment plus the inversion count inside it. The merge step adds cross inversions between the left and right sorted halves.

### Invariant
Before merging two halves, each half is individually sorted and its internal inversions have already been counted. The merge only needs to count inversions that cross from the left half to the right half.

### Edge cases
Equal values are not inversions because the condition is strictly greater. Use <= when taking from the left side to avoid counting equal pairs.

### Complexity
Merge sort has O(log N) levels and each level merges O(N) values, so time is O(N log N). The temporary merge array uses O(N) space.

maximum-product-subarray.name: Brute Force
maximum-product-subarray.description: Build a correctness baseline for Max Product Subarray by directly enumerating the candidate states allowed by the problem. This version prioritizes transparent control flow and exhaustive coverage over asymptotic efficiency.

maximum-product-subarray.name: Optimal (Prefix & Suffix Scan)
maximum-product-subarray.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Max Product Subarray asks for the largest product obtainable from one contiguous block. Products are trickier than sums because negative numbers can flip bad into good, and zero can reset everything.

### Core intuition
Inside a zero-free segment, a negative count decides the answer. If there are an even number of negatives, the whole segment can be best. If there are an odd number, the best product removes either the prefix through the first negative or the suffix after the last negative. Scanning products from both ends catches both possibilities.

### State meaning
prefix is the running product from the left. suffix is the running product from the right. result stores the largest product seen in either direction. When a running product becomes zero, reset it to 1 before starting the next segment.

### Invariant
At each position, prefix represents a product ending at the current left-side index since the last zero, and suffix represents a product starting at the mirrored right-side index since the last zero.

### Edge cases
Zeros split the array into independent sections. Single negative arrays should still return that negative value, so initialize the result from nums[0], not from 0.

### Complexity
The algorithm scans the array once while updating prefix and suffix, so time is O(N). Extra space is O(1).

binary-search.name: Brute Force
binary-search.description: Search through the array from start to end, comparing each element with the target value.

binary-search.name: Optimal (Divide & Conquer)
binary-search.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Binary Search asks for the index of a target value in a sorted array, or -1 if the target is absent. The sorted order is the reason the search can be logarithmic.

### Core intuition
Look at the middle value of the current search range. If it equals the target, return it. If it is smaller than the target, every value to its left is also too small, so discard the left half. If it is larger, discard the right half.

### State meaning
left and right are the inclusive boundaries of the range where the target may still exist. mid is the current test index. Everything outside left..right has already been proven impossible.

### Invariant
At the start of each loop, if the target exists, it must be inside the current left..right range. Each comparison preserves that promise while cutting the range smaller.

### Edge cases
Use mid = left + floor((right - left) / 2) in languages where left + right can overflow. Empty arrays immediately return -1 because left starts greater than right.

### Complexity
Each step halves the search range, so time is O(log N). The algorithm stores only a few indices, so space is O(1).

search-in-rotated-sorted-array.name: Brute Force
search-in-rotated-sorted-array.description: Build a correctness baseline for Search in Rotated Sorted Array by directly enumerating the candidate states allowed by the problem. This version prioritizes transparent control flow and exhaustive coverage over asymptotic efficiency.

search-in-rotated-sorted-array.name: Modified Binary Search
search-in-rotated-sorted-array.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Search in Rotated Sorted Array asks us to find a target in an array that was sorted, then rotated at some pivot. We still need O(log N), so we cannot first find everything by scanning.

### Core intuition
A rotated sorted array may look broken overall, but when you cut it at mid, at least one half is still properly sorted. Identify the sorted half, then ask whether the target lies inside that half's numeric boundaries. If yes, search there. If not, search the other half.

### State meaning
left and right bound the possible target range. mid tests the current split. The comparison nums[left] <= nums[mid] tells whether the left half is sorted; otherwise the right half is sorted.

### Invariant
At the start of each loop, the target, if present, is within left..right. Each step discards a half only after proving the target cannot be in that half.

### Edge cases
This version assumes distinct values. With duplicates, nums[left], nums[mid], and nums[right] can be equal, making it impossible to identify the sorted half without shrinking the bounds.

### Complexity
Each iteration discards half the remaining search space, so time is O(log N). Extra space is O(1).

koko-eating-bananas.name: Binary Search on Answer Space
koko-eating-bananas.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Koko Eating Bananas asks for the minimum integer eating speed k such that all piles can be finished within h hours. We are not searching inside the piles array; we are searching the answer space of possible speeds.

### Core intuition
If Koko can finish at speed k, she can also finish at any faster speed. If she cannot finish at speed k, every slower speed also fails. This yes/no pattern is monotonic, which is exactly what binary search needs.

### State meaning
left is the slowest speed still possible. right is the fastest speed still worth checking, usually max(piles). mid is the trial speed. hoursUsed is computed by summing ceil(pile / mid) for every pile.

### Invariant
The true minimum speed is always inside the current search range or already saved as the best valid answer. Valid speeds move the search left; invalid speeds move it right.

### Edge cases
Use ceiling division carefully. In integer code, ceil(pile / speed) can be written as (pile + speed - 1) / speed. Also use a wide type for total hours if piles are large.

### Complexity
Each trial scans all piles, and binary search tries O(log maxPile) speeds. Time is O(N log maxPile), space is O(1).

implement-lower-bound.name: Optimal (Binary Search)
implement-lower-bound.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Lower Bound asks for the first index where arr[index] is greater than or equal to x. If every value is smaller than x, the answer is n, the insertion position after the last element.

### Core intuition
This is binary search for the first true position of the condition arr[i] >= x. When mid satisfies the condition, mid is a possible answer, but there may be an earlier valid index, so search left. When mid is too small, every index up to mid is also too small, so search right.

### State meaning
low and high are the current search boundaries. ans stores the best valid index found so far. It starts as n so the no-valid-index case is handled naturally.

### Invariant
All indices before low have been proven too small, and ans is the smallest valid candidate found among checked positions. The true lower bound is still either in low..high or already stored in ans.

### Edge cases
Duplicates are the reason lower bound matters: for [4,4,4], searching 4 should return the first 4, not any 4. If x is smaller than all values, return 0.

### Complexity
The range halves each step, so time is O(log N). Only three integer variables are stored, so space is O(1).

implement-upper-bound.name: Optimal (Binary Search)
implement-upper-bound.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Upper Bound asks for the first index where arr[index] is strictly greater than x. If no value is greater than x, the answer is n.

### Core intuition
This is the first true position of the condition arr[i] > x. When mid is greater than x, it is a valid candidate, but an earlier greater value may exist, so search left. When mid is less than or equal to x, it cannot be the answer, and neither can anything to its left that is no larger.

### State meaning
low and high bound the active search range. ans stores the smallest index seen so far whose value is greater than x. It starts at n to represent insertion after all existing values.

### Difference from lower bound
Lower bound uses arr[mid] >= x. Upper bound uses arr[mid] > x. That means duplicates equal to x are skipped, and the returned index points after the block of equal values.

### Invariant
All positions before low are known to be not greater than x, unless a better answer is already saved. ans always holds the best greater-than-x index discovered so far.

### Complexity
The search range halves on every iteration, so time is O(log N). Space is O(1).

search-insert-position.name: Optimal (Lower Bound)
search-insert-position.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Search Insert Position asks for the index of target in a sorted array. If target is not present, return the index where it should be inserted so the array remains sorted.

### Core intuition
This is lower bound with a friendlier name. We need the first index whose value is greater than or equal to target. If that value equals target, it is the search answer. If it is larger, that same position is where target should be inserted.

### State meaning
low and high bound the active search range. ans starts as nums.length and stores the best insertion position found so far. Whenever nums[mid] >= target, mid is valid, but we continue left to find an earlier valid spot.

### Invariant
Everything before low has been proven smaller than target. ans is the earliest known position where target could legally sit without breaking sorted order.

### Edge cases
If target is smaller than every value, the answer becomes 0. If target is larger than every value, ans stays n and the insertion position is at the end.

### Complexity
Binary search halves the range each step, so time is O(log N). Only a few indices are stored, so space is O(1).

floor-ceil-in-sorted-array.name: Optimal (Dual Binary Search)
floor-ceil-in-sorted-array.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Floor/Ceil in Sorted Array asks for two nearby values around x. The floor is the largest array value less than or equal to x. The ceil is the smallest array value greater than or equal to x.

### Core intuition
Because the array is sorted, both answers are boundary searches. For floor, whenever arr[mid] <= x, mid is a valid candidate, but a larger valid value may exist on the right. For ceil, whenever arr[mid] >= x, mid is a valid candidate, but a smaller valid value may exist on the left.

### State meaning
floor stores the best value seen so far that does not exceed x. ceil stores the best value seen so far that is not below x. Each can be found with its own binary search or with careful combined logic.

### Invariant
During the floor search, floor is the largest valid value discovered so far. During the ceil search, ceil is the smallest valid value discovered so far.

### Edge cases
If no floor exists because every value is greater than x, return -1 or the platform's sentinel. If no ceil exists because every value is smaller than x, return -1.

### Complexity
Each binary search takes O(log N), and two such searches are still O(log N). Extra space is O(1).

first-or-last-occurrence-of-a-number.name: Optimal (Lower/Upper Bound)
first-or-last-occurrence-of-a-number.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
First and Last Occurrence asks for the starting and ending indices of a target value in a sorted array. If the target does not appear, return [-1, -1].

### Core intuition
All equal target values form one continuous block because the array is sorted. The first index of that block is lower bound of target. The position just after the block is upper bound of target, so the last occurrence is upperBound - 1.

### State meaning
lowerBound gives the first index where value >= target. upperBound gives the first index where value > target. Together they describe the entire target block.

### Invariant
If target exists, every occurrence lies between lowerBound(target) and upperBound(target) - 1. Values before that range are smaller, and values after it are larger.

### Edge cases
After computing lower bound, verify that it is inside the array and nums[lowerBound] equals target. If not, the target is absent and the answer is [-1, -1].

### Complexity
Two binary searches are used, each O(log N). Extra space is O(1).

search-in-rotated-sorted-array-ii.name: Brute Force
search-in-rotated-sorted-array-ii.description: Build a correctness baseline for Search in Rotated Sorted Array II by directly enumerating the candidate states allowed by the problem. This version prioritizes transparent control flow and exhaustive coverage over asymptotic efficiency.

search-in-rotated-sorted-array-ii.name: Optimal (Bound Compression)
search-in-rotated-sorted-array-ii.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Search in Rotated Sorted Array II asks whether a target exists in a rotated sorted array that may contain duplicates. The answer is boolean, not necessarily an index.

### Core intuition
The distinct-value version identifies which half is sorted. Duplicates can hide that information. If nums[left], nums[mid], and nums[right] are equal, we cannot tell which side is sorted, so we safely shrink both ends by one and try again.

### State meaning
left and right bound where the target may still exist. mid tests the current range. When one half is clearly sorted, use range checks just like Rotated Search I. When both boundaries equal mid, compress the bounds.

### Invariant
If target exists, it remains inside the current left..right range. When equal boundaries are removed, we only discard values already checked against target at mid or duplicates that do not reveal direction.

### Edge cases
Worst-case time becomes O(N), such as an array full of duplicates where the algorithm can only shrink one step at a time. Average cases still behave like binary search.

### Complexity
Average time is O(log N), but worst-case time is O(N) because duplicates can destroy the half-discard guarantee. Space is O(1).

minimum-days-to-make-m-bouquets.name: Optimal (BS on Days)
minimum-days-to-make-m-bouquets.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Minimum Days for M Bouquets asks for the earliest day when we can make m bouquets, each using k adjacent bloomed flowers. If there are not enough flowers overall, return -1.

### Core intuition
The answer is a day, not an index. If a certain day works, every later day also works because more flowers have bloomed. If a day fails, every earlier day also fails. That monotonic pattern lets us binary search over days.

### State meaning
low and high bound possible days. mid is the day being tested. During the test, consecutive counts adjacent bloomed flowers, and bouquets counts how many k-sized adjacent groups have been formed.

### Invariant
The earliest valid day is always inside the current search range or saved by moving high left after a successful test. Failed days move low right.

### Edge cases
If m * k is greater than bloomDay.length, it is impossible. Adjacency matters: a flower that has not bloomed resets the consecutive count to zero.

### Complexity
Each feasibility test scans all flowers, and binary search tries O(log maxDay) days. Time is O(N log maxDay), space is O(1).

find-the-smallest-divisor-given-a-thresh.name: Optimal (BS on Divisor)
find-the-smallest-divisor-given-a-thresh.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Smallest Divisor asks for the smallest positive divisor such that the sum of ceil(nums[i] / divisor) over all values is less than or equal to threshold.

### Core intuition
As the divisor gets larger, every rounded-up division result stays the same or decreases. So the total sum also never increases. That creates a monotonic yes/no answer: small divisors may fail, large divisors eventually work.

### State meaning
left and right bound possible divisors. mid is the divisor being tested. total is the sum of rounded-up divisions. ans is the smallest working divisor seen so far, often represented by moving right left after success.

### Invariant
The minimum valid divisor remains inside the search range. If mid works, try smaller divisors. If mid fails, all smaller divisors fail too, so move rightward.

### Edge cases
Use integer ceiling division: ceil(num / d) = (num + d - 1) // d. The upper bound can be max(nums), because at that divisor each term is at most 1.

### Complexity
Each divisor test scans N numbers, and binary search tries O(log maxNum) divisors. Time is O(N log maxNum), space is O(1).

capacity-to-ship-packages-within-d-days.name: Optimal (BS on Capacity)
capacity-to-ship-packages-within-d-days.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Ship Packages in D Days asks for the smallest ship capacity that can ship all packages within a given number of days. Packages must be shipped in the original order.

### Core intuition
Capacity is the answer space. A very small capacity fails because too many days are needed. A larger capacity may work, and once a capacity works, every larger capacity also works. That monotonic property allows binary search.

### State meaning
low starts at max(weights), because the ship must carry the heaviest single package. high starts at sum(weights), because one huge ship could carry everything in one day. For a trial capacity, a greedy scan counts how many days are needed.

### Why the greedy check is correct
For a fixed capacity, load packages in order until the next package would exceed capacity, then start a new day. Delaying a package earlier than necessary never helps reduce days, so the greedy day count is minimal for that capacity.

### Edge cases
Do not set low below the heaviest package. The order cannot be rearranged, so sorting weights would change the problem.

### Complexity
Each capacity check scans all packages, and binary search tries O(log sumWeights) capacities. Time is O(N log sumWeights), space is O(1).

aggressive-cows.name: Brute Force
aggressive-cows.description: Build a correctness baseline for Aggressive Cows by directly enumerating the candidate states allowed by the problem. This version prioritizes transparent control flow and exhaustive coverage over asymptotic efficiency.

aggressive-cows.name: Optimal (Binary Search on Answer Range)
aggressive-cows.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Aggressive Cows asks us to place k cows in stalls so that the minimum distance between any two placed cows is as large as possible.

### Core intuition
Sort the stall positions. Then binary search the answer distance. If we can place all cows with at least distance d between neighbors, then smaller distances also work, so we try a larger d. If distance d fails, every larger distance fails too.

### State meaning
low and high bound possible minimum distances. mid is the distance being tested. The feasibility check greedily places the first cow in the first stall, then places each next cow in the earliest stall at least mid away from the last placed cow.

### Why greedy placement is correct
For a fixed minimum distance, placing each cow as early as possible leaves maximum room for the remaining cows. If this earliest-placement strategy cannot place all cows, no later-placement strategy can do better.

### Edge cases
Sorting is mandatory. If k is 2, the answer is simply the distance between the first and last stall after sorting, but the general binary search handles it too.

### Complexity
Sorting costs O(N log N). Each feasibility check is O(N), and binary search over distances adds O(log maxDistance), so total time is O(N log N + N log maxDistance). Space is O(1) beyond sorting.

book-allocation-problem.name: Optimal (Binary Search on Capacity)
book-allocation-problem.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Book Allocation asks us to assign books in order to m students so that the maximum pages assigned to any student is as small as possible. Each student receives a contiguous block of books.

### Core intuition
Binary search the maximum allowed pages per student. If a cap is high enough, the books can be assigned using m or fewer students. If a cap is too low, too many students are needed. That makes feasibility monotonic.

### State meaning
low is max(pages), because no student can receive less capacity than the largest single book. high is sum(pages), where one student could take everything. mid is the cap being tested. The helper scan counts how many students are needed under that cap.

### Why the helper scan is greedy
For a fixed cap, keep giving books to the current student until the next book would exceed the cap, then move to the next student. Splitting earlier cannot reduce the number of students needed.

### Edge cases
If there are more students than books in the standard version where each student needs at least one book, return -1. Book order must not be rearranged.

### Complexity
Each cap test scans N books, and binary search tries O(log sumPages) caps. Time is O(N log sumPages), space is O(1).

find-the-row-with-maximum-number-of-1s.name: Optimal (Binary Search per Row)
find-the-row-with-maximum-number-of-1s.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Row with Max 1s gives a binary matrix where each row is sorted, usually all 0s followed by all 1s. We need return the row index containing the most 1s.

### Core intuition
In a sorted binary row, the number of 1s is determined by the first 1. If the first 1 is at index j and the row has cols columns, then that row has cols - j ones. So run lower bound for 1 on each row.

### State meaning
rowIdx stores the best row found so far. maxCount stores how many 1s that row has. firstOne is the lower-bound index for value 1 in the current row.

### Invariant
After processing row r, rowIdx points to the row with the most 1s among rows 0 through r, with maxCount storing that count.

### Edge cases
A row with no 1s has firstOne = cols and count = 0. If all rows have zero 1s, return -1 as the current catalog implementation does. Ties usually keep the earliest row because we update only when count is greater.

### Complexity
There are R rows, and each lower-bound search costs O(log C). Total time is O(R log C), and extra space is O(1).

search-in-a-2d-matrix.name: Brute Force
search-in-a-2d-matrix.description: Build a correctness baseline for Search in 2D Matrix by directly enumerating the candidate states allowed by the problem. This version prioritizes transparent control flow and exhaustive coverage over asymptotic efficiency.

search-in-a-2d-matrix.name: Better
search-in-a-2d-matrix.description: Reduce repeated work in Search in 2D Matrix with the intermediate structure or cached state used by the standard better approach. It preserves the brute-force reasoning while improving one major time or space bottleneck.

search-in-a-2d-matrix.name: Optimal (Virtual 1D Array)
search-in-a-2d-matrix.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Search in 2D Matrix asks whether a target exists in a matrix where every row is sorted and each row starts after the previous row ends. That second condition means the whole matrix behaves like one sorted list.

### Core intuition
Instead of doing binary search twice, pretend the matrix is flattened into one sorted array of length rows * cols. A virtual index idx maps back to matrix row idx / cols and column idx % cols.

### State meaning
left and right are virtual indices, not physical row-column pairs. mid is converted into row and col only when reading the matrix value.

### Invariant
At every step, if the target exists, its virtual position must be between left and right. The normal binary search comparison is valid because the virtual flattened order is globally sorted.

### Edge cases
Handle empty matrices before reading matrix[0]. Be careful that this problem is different from Matrix II; here the last value of a row is smaller than the first value of the next row.

### Complexity
Binary search runs over rows * cols virtual positions, so time is O(log(rows * cols)). Extra space is O(1).

search-in-a-2d-matrix-ii.name: Optimal (Staircase Search)
search-in-a-2d-matrix-ii.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Search in 2D Matrix II asks whether a target exists in a matrix where each row is sorted left to right and each column is sorted top to bottom. Unlike Search in 2D Matrix I, the whole matrix is not necessarily one globally sorted list.

### Core intuition
Start at the top-right corner. From that cell, moving left makes values smaller, and moving down makes values larger. If the current value is too large, the whole current column below it is also too large, so move left. If it is too small, the whole current row to its left is also too small, so move down.

### State meaning
row and col describe the current staircase position. Each move removes one full row or one full column from consideration.

### Invariant
At every step, all cells above the current row or to the right of the current column have already been safely ruled out. The remaining search region is the rectangle below-left of the current position.

### Edge cases
Starting at bottom-left works symmetrically. Starting at top-left does not help because both right and down are larger, so it gives no clean discard direction.

### Complexity
Each move decreases col or increases row, so there are at most rows + cols moves. Time is O(rows + cols), space is O(1).

find-peak-element-2d-matrix.name: Optimal (BS on Columns)
find-peak-element-2d-matrix.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Peak Element in 2D Matrix asks for any cell that is greater than its left, right, top, and bottom neighbors. Returning any valid peak is enough.

### Core intuition
Binary search over columns. For the middle column, find the row containing the maximum value in that column. Because it is the maximum in its column, it is already greater than or equal to its vertical neighbors. Now only the left and right neighbors can beat it. If the right neighbor is larger, a peak must exist to the right. If the left neighbor is larger, a peak must exist to the left. Otherwise the current cell is a peak.

### State meaning
lowCol and highCol bound the columns where a peak may exist. midCol is the tested column. maxRow is the row of the largest value in midCol.

### Invariant
A peak exists within the current column range. Moving toward a larger horizontal neighbor preserves that promise because following increasing values cannot continue forever without ending at a peak.

### Edge cases
Treat missing neighbors outside the matrix as negative infinity. This lets border cells be peaks when they beat their existing neighbors.

### Complexity
Each column test scans all rows to find maxRow, and binary search tests O(log cols) columns. Time is O(rows log cols), space is O(1).

median-in-a-row-wise-sorted-matrix.name: Brute Force
median-in-a-row-wise-sorted-matrix.description: Build a correctness baseline for Median in Row-wise Sorted Matrix by directly enumerating the candidate states allowed by the problem. This version prioritizes transparent control flow and exhaustive coverage over asymptotic efficiency.
median-in-a-row-wise-sorted-matrix.timeComplexity: O(M*N*(log(M*N))) , sorting the linear array takes time complexity of O(M*N(logM*N)

median-in-a-row-wise-sorted-matrix.name: Optimal (Binary Search on Number Range)
median-in-a-row-wise-sorted-matrix.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Median in Row-wise Sorted Matrix asks for the middle value of all matrix elements when the rows are individually sorted. The matrix is not necessarily globally sorted, so we cannot flatten mentally by index like Search in 2D Matrix I.

### Core intuition
Binary search the value range. For a candidate value mid, count how many matrix elements are less than or equal to mid. Because each row is sorted, that count can be found with upper bound in each row. If too few values are <= mid, the median must be larger. If enough values are <= mid, mid might be high enough, so search smaller values.

### State meaning
low and high bound possible median values. required is half the total count. countLessEqual(mid) tells how many elements would sit on or before mid in sorted order.

### Invariant
The true median is always within the current value range. The algorithm searches for the smallest value whose count of <= values is greater than total / 2.

### Edge cases
Use the minimum first element and maximum last element as tight bounds when possible. This version assumes an odd total count, which is the common interview version.

### Complexity
Each value check runs upper bound on every row, giving O(rows log cols). The outer search over values adds O(log valueRange). Space is O(1).

maximum-depth-of-binary-tree.name: Optimal (Recursive DFS)
maximum-depth-of-binary-tree.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Maximum Depth of Binary Tree asks for the number of nodes on the longest path from the root down to any leaf. An empty tree has depth 0.

### Core intuition
A tree problem often answers itself recursively. The depth of a node is 1 plus the larger depth of its left and right subtrees. Leaves return 1 because both their child depths are 0.

### State meaning
The recursive function returns the depth of the subtree rooted at the current node. It does not need global state because each node's answer depends only on the answers from its children.

### Invariant
When maxDepth(node) returns, it returns the correct maximum depth for exactly that node's subtree. Parent calls can trust that value and build their own answer.

### Edge cases
If root is null, return 0. A skewed tree with only one branch has depth equal to its node count, and the recursion stack can also become that deep.

### Complexity
Every node is visited once, so time is O(N). Recursion uses O(H) stack space, where H is the height of the tree.

invert-binary-tree.name: Optimal (Recursive Structure Flipping)
invert-binary-tree.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Invert Binary Tree asks us to mirror the tree: every node's left child should become its right child, and every right child should become its left child.

### Core intuition
The whole tree becomes inverted if each individual node swaps its two child pointers and then the same rule is applied to the children. The operation is local, but applying it everywhere creates the global mirror.

### State meaning
The current node is the only state needed. After swapping current.left and current.right, recursion continues into both children so their subtrees are also mirrored.

### Invariant
After invertTree(node) returns, the subtree rooted at node is fully mirrored compared with its original structure.

### Edge cases
A null node returns null. A leaf node is unchanged because both child pointers are null. The algorithm mutates the tree in place and returns the original root pointer after its children have been swapped.

### Complexity
Every node is visited once, so time is O(N). Recursion uses O(H) stack space, where H is the tree height.

lowest-common-ancestor-of-a-binary-searc.name: Optimal (BST Math Pruning)
lowest-common-ancestor-of-a-binary-searc.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Lowest Common Ancestor of a BST asks for the lowest node that has both target nodes p and q in its subtree. Because the tree is a binary search tree, we can use values to move directly toward the answer.

### Core intuition
In a BST, values smaller than current go left and values larger than current go right. If both p and q are smaller than current, the LCA must be in the left subtree. If both are larger, it must be in the right subtree. The first node where they split, or where one target equals current, is the LCA.

### State meaning
current is the node being tested. pVal and qVal are the two target values. The algorithm walks downward until current is between them or equal to one of them.

### Invariant
At every step, the LCA is within the subtree rooted at current. Moving left or right is safe only when both targets are on that same side.

### Edge cases
If one target is an ancestor of the other, that ancestor is the LCA. The split condition handles this naturally because current equals one of the target values.

### Complexity
The walk follows one root-to-leaf path, so time is O(H). Iterative implementation uses O(1) space.

diameter-of-binary-tree.name: Brute Force
diameter-of-binary-tree.description: Build a correctness baseline for Diameter of Binary Tree by directly enumerating the candidate states allowed by the problem. This version prioritizes transparent control flow and exhaustive coverage over asymptotic efficiency.

diameter-of-binary-tree.name: Optimal (Global Maximum Tracker)
diameter-of-binary-tree.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Diameter of Binary Tree asks for the length of the longest path between any two nodes in the tree. The path may or may not pass through the root. In the common definition, length is counted in edges.

### Core intuition
At each node, the longest path passing through that node uses the deepest path in the left subtree plus the deepest path in the right subtree. So while a DFS computes subtree heights, it also updates a global best diameter with leftHeight + rightHeight.

### State meaning
The DFS returns height upward to the parent. The diameter variable records the best leftHeight + rightHeight seen at any node. These are different jobs: height helps parents, diameter is the final answer.

### Invariant
After DFS finishes a node, the global diameter has considered every path whose highest connecting point is inside that node's subtree.

### Edge cases
An empty tree has diameter 0. A single-node tree also has diameter 0 because there are no edges between two nodes.

### Complexity
Every node is visited once, so time is O(N). The recursion stack uses O(H) space.

balanced-binary-tree.name: Brute Force
balanced-binary-tree.description: Build a correctness baseline for Balanced Binary Tree by directly enumerating the candidate states allowed by the problem. This version prioritizes transparent control flow and exhaustive coverage over asymptotic efficiency.

balanced-binary-tree.name: Optimal (Bottom-Up Early Exit)
balanced-binary-tree.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Balanced Binary Tree asks whether every node has left and right subtree heights that differ by at most 1. This condition must hold at every node, not only at the root.

### Core intuition
A bottom-up DFS can compute height and check balance in the same pass. If a subtree is already unbalanced, return a special signal such as -1. That signal bubbles upward and stops unnecessary work.

### State meaning
The helper returns a real height when the subtree is balanced. It returns -1 when the subtree is unbalanced. The final answer is true if the root helper result is not -1.

### Invariant
When helper(node) returns a non-negative height, that subtree is balanced and the height is correct. When it returns -1, that subtree is unbalanced and no parent should trust it as a height.

### Edge cases
A null subtree has height 0 and is balanced. A single node is balanced. A long linked-list-shaped tree eventually becomes unbalanced when a node's child heights differ by more than 1.

### Complexity
Every node is visited at most once, so time is O(N). Recursion uses O(H) stack space.

level-order-traversal.name: Optimal (Queue-Based BFS)
level-order-traversal.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Level Order Traversal asks for the tree's node values grouped by depth: root level first, then its children, then grandchildren, and so on from left to right.

### Core intuition
Use BFS with a queue. The queue holds the next nodes to visit in arrival order. At the start of each level, record the current queue size. That size tells exactly how many nodes belong to this level before children for the next level are appended.

### State meaning
queue stores nodes waiting to be processed. levelSize freezes how many nodes are in the current depth. level stores values for that depth, and result stores all completed levels.

### Invariant
Before each outer loop, the queue contains exactly the nodes of the next level, followed by no deeper nodes yet. During the inner loop, children are appended for the following level.

### Edge cases
If root is null, return an empty list. Avoid using a shifting array in performance-sensitive JavaScript; a queue index or deque is better for large trees.

### Complexity
Every node is enqueued and dequeued once, so time is O(N). The queue can hold up to the width of the tree, which is O(N) in the worst case.

iterative-inorder.name: Optimal (Explicit Stack)
iterative-inorder.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Iterative Inorder asks for inorder traversal of a binary tree without recursion. Inorder means visit left subtree first, then the node itself, then the right subtree.

### Core intuition
Recursive inorder uses the call stack to remember the path back from the left side. The iterative version makes that stack explicit. Keep moving left while pushing nodes. When you cannot go left anymore, pop the last saved node, visit it, then move to its right child.

### State meaning
curr is the node we are currently trying to descend from. stack stores ancestors whose left side is being explored but whose own value has not been visited yet. result stores visited values in inorder order.

### Invariant
Before each pop, all nodes deeper on the current left path have already been pushed. The top of the stack is the next node whose left subtree is fully handled, so it is safe to visit.

### Edge cases
A null root returns an empty list. A tree with only right children works because the inner left-drill loop pushes one node at a time, visits it, then moves right.

### Complexity
Every node is pushed and popped once, so time is O(N). The stack stores at most the height of the tree, so space is O(H).

top-view-of-binary-tree.name: Optimal (Vertical Line Tracker)
top-view-of-binary-tree.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Top View asks which node values are visible if you stand above the binary tree and look straight down. If multiple nodes sit on the same vertical line, only the highest one should be visible.

### Core intuition
Give every node a horizontal distance from the root. The root is at distance 0. A left child moves to distance -1, and a right child moves to distance +1. Now each horizontal distance is like one vertical lane. The top view is the first node encountered in each lane when we scan the tree level by level.

### State meaning
The queue stores pairs of node and horizontal distance. The map stores the first value seen for each horizontal distance. min and max distances, or sorted map keys, let us print the answer from leftmost lane to rightmost lane.

### Invariant
Because BFS processes shallower nodes before deeper nodes, the first node written for a horizontal distance is always the topmost visible node for that lane. Once a lane has a value, later deeper nodes in the same lane must be ignored.

### Edge cases
If the root is null, return an empty list. If two nodes share the same horizontal distance at different depths, keep the shallower one. If the tree is skewed, the answer is simply the nodes along that skew in left-to-right lane order.

### Complexity
Every node enters and leaves the queue once, so the traversal is O(N). The map and queue can both grow to O(N) in the worst case.

right-left-view-of-binary-tree.name: Brute Force
right-left-view-of-binary-tree.description: Build a correctness baseline for Right View by directly enumerating the candidate states allowed by the problem. This version prioritizes transparent control flow and exhaustive coverage over asymptotic efficiency.

right-left-view-of-binary-tree.name: Optimal (DFS Level-First)
right-left-view-of-binary-tree.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Right View asks for the node visible at each depth when the tree is viewed from the right side. For every level, we only want the rightmost visible node.

### Core intuition
Use DFS, but visit the right child before the left child. The first time we reach a depth, that node must be the rightmost node at that depth, because we deliberately explored the right side first. After that, any node at the same depth is behind it from the right-side view.

### State meaning
result stores one chosen value per depth. depth tells which level the current node belongs to. The recursive call order is node, right subtree, then left subtree.

### Invariant
When depth equals result.length, this is the first node ever reached at that level. Because the traversal is right-first, first reached means rightmost visible. Once a value exists for a depth, later nodes at that depth are ignored.

### Edge cases
If the root is null, return an empty list. A tree with only left children still works: each left child is the first node at its depth because there is no right-side competitor.

### Complexity
Every node is visited once, so time is O(N). The recursion stack is O(H), where H is the height of the tree.

morris-preorder-traversal.name: Optimal (Threaded Binary Tree)
morris-preorder-traversal.description:
## Premium walkthrough
