### What this exact problem is asking
Morris Preorder Traversal asks us to produce preorder order, node then left then right, without recursion and without an explicit stack. The challenge is not the traversal order itself; the challenge is doing it with O(1) extra space.

### Core intuition
Normally, after exploring a left subtree, recursion or a stack tells us how to return to the current node. Morris traversal creates that return path temporarily. For a node with a left child, find the rightmost node in its left subtree. That node is the predecessor. If predecessor.right is empty, point it back to the current node, visit the current node for preorder, and move left. If predecessor.right already points to the current node, remove that temporary link and move right.

### State meaning
curr is the node currently being handled. predecessor is the rightmost node of curr.left. result stores preorder values. Temporary threads are short-lived right pointers used only to return from a left subtree.

### Invariant
Every temporary thread is removed the second time we encounter it. That means the tree is restored before the algorithm leaves that area. A node is added to result exactly when preorder would visit it: immediately if it has no left child, or just before creating the thread if it has a left child.

### Edge cases
If the root is null, return an empty list. A tree with no left children becomes a simple rightward walk. The main caution is to remove every created thread, otherwise the tree would be left modified.

### Complexity
Each edge is followed at most a constant number of times, so time is O(N). Apart from the output list, extra space is O(1).

climbing-stairs.name: Optimal (DP Tabulation)
climbing-stairs.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Climbing Stairs asks how many different ways there are to reach step n when each move can climb either 1 step or 2 steps.

### Core intuition
Focus on the final move. To land on step i, the previous position must have been step i - 1 with a 1-step move, or step i - 2 with a 2-step move. These two groups do not overlap, so the number of ways to reach i is ways(i - 1) plus ways(i - 2).

### State meaning
prev2 stores the number of ways to reach i - 2. prev1 stores the number of ways to reach i - 1. curr becomes the number of ways to reach the current step i.

### Invariant
After finishing step i, prev1 equals the number of ways to reach i, and prev2 equals the number of ways to reach i - 1. That keeps exactly the history needed for the next stair.

### Edge cases
For n = 1, there is 1 way. For n = 2, there are 2 ways: 1+1 or 2. These are the base cases that start the Fibonacci-like growth.

### Complexity
The loop runs once per step, so time is O(N). Only two previous counts are stored, so space is O(1).

coin-change.name: Bottom-Up Tabulation (1D Array)
coin-change.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Coin Change asks for the minimum number of coins needed to form a target amount. You may use each coin denomination as many times as needed. If the amount cannot be formed, return -1.

### Core intuition
Solve every smaller amount first. If we are trying to make amount a and choose coin c as the last coin, then the remaining amount is a - c. If dp[a - c] is already known, one candidate answer is dp[a - c] + 1. Try every coin and keep the smallest candidate.

### State meaning
dp[x] means the fewest coins needed to make amount x. dp[0] is 0 because zero coins make amount 0. Infinity means this amount is currently unreachable.

### Invariant
After finishing amount x, dp[x] is the best answer using all available coins, because every possible last coin has been considered.

### Edge cases
If amount is 0, return 0. If dp[amount] stays Infinity, return -1. Coin order does not affect correctness because the algorithm checks every denomination for every amount.

### Complexity
There are amount + 1 states, and each state checks every coin. Time is O(amount * numberOfCoins), and space is O(amount).

longest-common-subsequence.name: Better
longest-common-subsequence.description: Reduce repeated work in Longest Common Subsequence with the intermediate structure or cached state used by the standard better approach. It preserves the brute-force reasoning while improving one major time or space bottleneck.
longest-common-subsequence.spaceComplexity: O(N*M) + O(N+M) , We are using an auxiliary recursion stack space(O(N+M))

longest-common-subsequence.name: 2D Tabulation
longest-common-subsequence.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Longest Common Subsequence asks for the length of the longest sequence of characters that appears in both strings in the same relative order. The characters do not need to be contiguous.

### Core intuition
Compare prefixes of the two strings. If the last characters of the two prefixes match, they can extend a smaller common subsequence by 1. If they do not match, the best answer must come from ignoring one of those last characters: either ignore the last character of text1 or ignore the last character of text2.

### State meaning
dp[i][j] means the LCS length between the first i characters of text1 and the first j characters of text2. Row 0 or column 0 is 0 because an empty string has no common subsequence with anything.

### Invariant
When filling dp[i][j], the cells above, left, and diagonal are already solved. That gives all smaller prefix answers needed to decide the current prefix pair.

### Edge cases
If either string is empty, the answer is 0. Repeated characters are handled naturally because the DP works by prefix length, not by trying to greedily grab the first matching character.

### Complexity
The table has text1.length times text2.length meaningful cells. Time is O(N * M), and space is O(N * M), with a possible rolling-row optimization.

partition-equal-subset-sum.name: Optimal (DP on Subset Sum)
partition-equal-subset-sum.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Partition Equal Subset Sum asks whether the array can be split into two groups with the same total sum.

### Core intuition
If the total sum is odd, equal partition is impossible. If the total sum is even, each group must sum to total / 2. So the problem becomes: can we choose some numbers that add exactly to target?

### State meaning
dp[s] is true if we can form sum s using the numbers processed so far. dp[0] starts true because choosing nothing forms sum 0.

### Invariant
After processing a number num, dp[s] tells whether sum s is possible using each processed number at most once. The loop goes backward from target to num so the same number cannot be reused in the same round.

### Edge cases
An odd total returns false immediately. If any number equals target, the answer is true. Positive integers make the one-dimensional subset DP straightforward.

### Complexity
For each number, we scan sums up to target. Time is O(N * target), and space is O(target).

edit-distance.name: Brute Force
edit-distance.description: Build a correctness baseline for Edit Distance (Levenshtein) by directly enumerating the candidate states allowed by the problem. This version prioritizes transparent control flow and exhaustive coverage over asymptotic efficiency.
edit-distance.spaceComplexity: O(N × M) + O(N + M), as O(N × M)

edit-distance.name: Better
edit-distance.description: Reduce repeated work in Edit Distance (Levenshtein) with the intermediate structure or cached state used by the standard better approach. It preserves the brute-force reasoning while improving one major time or space bottleneck.

edit-distance.name: Optimal (2D Tabulation: The Edit Grid)
edit-distance.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Edit Distance asks for the fewest operations needed to turn word1 into word2. The allowed operations are insert one character, delete one character, or replace one character.

### Core intuition
Think in prefixes. dp[i][j] answers: how many edits are needed to convert the first i characters of word1 into the first j characters of word2? If the last characters already match, no new edit is needed. If they differ, we try the three legal moves and pick the cheapest.

### State meaning
dp[i][j] is the minimum edit count for word1 prefix length i and word2 prefix length j. dp[i][0] is i because converting i characters to empty needs i deletions. dp[0][j] is j because converting empty to j characters needs j insertions.

### Invariant
When dp[i][j] is computed, the three smaller worlds it depends on are already solved: replace from dp[i - 1][j - 1], delete from dp[i - 1][j], and insert from dp[i][j - 1].

### Edge cases
If both words are empty, return 0. If one word is empty, the answer is the length of the other word. Matching characters should copy the diagonal cell without adding an operation.

### Complexity
The DP table has (N + 1) times (M + 1) cells. Time is O(N * M), and space is O(N * M), with rolling-row optimization possible.

longest-increasing-subsequence.name: Optimal (Binary Search + Greedy)
longest-increasing-subsequence.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Longest Increasing Subsequence asks for the length of the longest strictly increasing sequence that can be formed by deleting some elements without changing the order of the remaining elements.

### Core intuition
Maintain a tails list. tails[k] is the smallest possible ending value of any increasing subsequence of length k + 1 found so far. A smaller ending value is better because it leaves more room for future numbers to extend the sequence.

### State meaning
tails is not necessarily the actual subsequence from the array. It is a compact scoreboard of best endings by length. For each number x, binary search finds the first tails position whose value is greater than or equal to x. Replace that value with x, or append x if it is larger than every tail.

### Invariant
After processing each number, tails remains sorted, and for every length represented, tails keeps the smallest ending value we have found for that length. The length of tails equals the LIS length so far.

### Edge cases
An empty array has LIS length 0. Because the subsequence must be strictly increasing, equal numbers replace the first equal tail instead of extending the list.

### Complexity
Each number performs one binary search over tails. Time is O(N log N), and space is O(N) for the tails list.

best-time-to-buy-and-sell-stock-ii.name: Optimal (Greedy/Peak-Valley)
best-time-to-buy-and-sell-stock-ii.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Buy and Sell Stock II asks for the maximum profit when you may make as many transactions as you want, as long as you do not hold more than one stock at a time.

### Core intuition
Every upward price movement can be captured as profit. If the price rises from yesterday to today, pretend we bought yesterday and sold today. Joining several small profitable steps gives the same profit as one long buy-low-sell-high transaction over that rising stretch.

### State meaning
profit stores the total captured gain so far. For each adjacent pair of days, dailyGain is prices[i] - prices[i - 1]. If dailyGain is positive, add it. If it is zero or negative, skip it.

### Invariant
After processing day i, profit equals the best profit obtainable from all completed upward moves ending on or before day i. No future choice can make a past downward move profitable, so skipping drops is safe.

### Edge cases
If prices is empty or has one day, no sale is possible, so profit is 0. Flat prices add nothing. Multiple independent rises are all counted because unlimited transactions are allowed.

### Complexity
We scan the prices once, so time is O(N). Only the running profit is stored, so space is O(1).

best-time-to-buy-and-sell-stock-iii.name: Optimal (DP with State Machine)
best-time-to-buy-and-sell-stock-iii.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Buy and Sell Stock III asks for the maximum profit with at most two complete transactions. A transaction means one buy followed by one sell.

### Core intuition
Track the best possible balance after each milestone: first buy, first sell, second buy, second sell. Buying reduces cash, selling increases cash. As each price arrives, update those four states as if today could be used for any milestone.

### State meaning
firstBuy is the best balance after buying the first stock. firstSell is the best profit after selling once. secondBuy is the best balance after buying again using the profit from the first sale. secondSell is the best final profit after two sales.

### Invariant
After each day, every state is the best value possible up to that day for its exact milestone. That means secondSell always represents the best answer seen so far with at most two transactions.

### Edge cases
If there are fewer than two prices, profit is 0. The algorithm naturally handles one transaction being better than two, because secondSell can carry forward the previous best value.

### Complexity
Each price updates four variables, so time is O(N). The four-state machine uses O(1) space.

best-time-to-buy-and-sell-stock-iv.name: Optimal (K-Transaction DP)
best-time-to-buy-and-sell-stock-iv.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Buy and Sell Stock IV asks for the maximum profit with at most k transactions. The number k is part of the input, so the solution must handle any transaction limit.

### Core intuition
For each transaction count t, ask: what is the best profit by each day if I can complete at most t transactions? Selling today means we must have bought on some earlier day, so we keep a running best buy balance called maxDiff instead of trying every buy day again and again.

### State meaning
dp[t][d] is the best profit up to day d with at most t transactions. maxDiff stores the best value of dp[t - 1][previousDay] - prices[previousDay], meaning the strongest position we could have after buying the t-th stock before today.

### Invariant
When scanning days for transaction count t, maxDiff is always the best possible buy setup from earlier days. Therefore dp[t][d] can choose between not selling today and selling today using that best setup.

### Edge cases
If k is 0 or prices is empty, profit is 0. If k is at least half the number of days, the limit is effectively unlimited, so the Stock II positive-slope shortcut can be used.

### Complexity
The DP version runs in O(k * N) time. A full table uses O(k * N) space, and it can be optimized to fewer rows if needed.

best-time-to-buy-and-sell-stock-with-coo.name: Optimal (State Machine DP)
best-time-to-buy-and-sell-stock-with-coo.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Stock with Cooldown asks for maximum profit with unlimited transactions, but after selling a stock, you must wait one full day before buying again.

### Core intuition
A cooldown rule means we cannot describe the day with just buy or sell. We need three end-of-day states: holding a stock, just sold today, or resting with no stock. Each day updates those states from yesterday's states.

### State meaning
hold is the best profit while ending today with a stock in hand. sold is the best profit when we sold today. rest is the best profit when we end today without stock and without selling today, so we are free to buy tomorrow.

### Invariant
After each day, hold, sold, and rest are the best possible profits for those exact end-of-day conditions. The cooldown is enforced because a new buy can only come from yesterday's rest, not yesterday's sold.

### Edge cases
If prices is empty, profit is 0. The final answer cannot be hold because unsold stock is not realized profit, so return the better of sold and rest.

### Complexity
Each day updates three numbers, so time is O(N), and space is O(1).

print-longest-common-subsequence.name: Optimal (Backtrack thru DP Table)
print-longest-common-subsequence.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Print LCS asks for the actual longest common subsequence string, not just its length. The characters must appear in both strings in the same order, but they do not need to be adjacent.

### Core intuition
First build the normal LCS length table. Then start at the bottom-right corner and walk backward. A matching character belongs to the answer, so take it and move diagonally. A mismatch means the answer came from either the cell above or the cell to the left, so move toward the larger value.

### State meaning
i and j point to prefix lengths in the two strings while backtracking. result stores matched characters collected from end to start. The DP table stores LCS lengths for every prefix pair.

### Invariant
At every backtracking step, dp[i][j] tells the length of the best LCS still available for the current prefixes. Moving to a neighbor with the same best value keeps us on a valid path to one optimal answer.

### Edge cases
If either string is empty, the answer is an empty string. If the table has ties during mismatch, moving either direction with the same value can produce a valid LCS, though not always the same one.

### Complexity
Building the table costs O(N * M). The backward walk costs O(N + M). Space is O(N * M) because the full table is needed to reconstruct the string.

longest-common-substring.name: Better
longest-common-substring.description: Reduce repeated work in Longest Common Substring with the intermediate structure or cached state used by the standard better approach. It preserves the brute-force reasoning while improving one major time or space bottleneck.

longest-common-substring.name: Optimal (2D Tabulation)
longest-common-substring.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Longest Common Substring asks for the maximum length of a contiguous block of characters that appears in both strings. Contiguous is the key difference from subsequence.

### Core intuition
Let dp[i][j] mean the length of the common suffix ending at s1[i - 1] and s2[j - 1]. If the characters match, the matching suffix extends by one from the diagonal cell. If they differ, the suffix is broken and the cell becomes 0.

### State meaning
dp[i][j] stores only a suffix length, not a general best length. best stores the largest value seen anywhere in the table. Optionally, bestEnd can remember where the winning substring ends in s1.

### Invariant
Every nonzero cell represents a contiguous match ending exactly at those two positions. Resetting mismatch cells to 0 is what prevents non-adjacent characters from being counted.

### Edge cases
If either string is empty, the answer is 0. Repeated characters are handled because each cell is tied to a precise ending position, not just a character value.

### Complexity
The table has N * M meaningful comparisons, so time is O(N * M). Space is O(N * M), or O(M) with a rolling row.

grid-unique-paths.name: Better
grid-unique-paths.description: Reduce repeated work in Unique Paths with the intermediate structure or cached state used by the standard better approach. It preserves the brute-force reasoning while improving one major time or space bottleneck.
grid-unique-paths.spaceComplexity: O((N-1)+(M-1)) + O(M*N) We are using a recursion stack space: O((N-1)+(M-1))

grid-unique-paths.name: Optimal (DP Tabulation)
grid-unique-paths.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Unique Paths asks how many ways a robot can move from the top-left cell to the bottom-right cell of an m by n grid when it may only move right or down.

### Core intuition
To arrive at any inner cell, the robot must come from exactly one of two places: the cell above or the cell to the left. So the number of ways into a cell is waysFromAbove plus waysFromLeft.

### State meaning
dp[r][c] is the number of ways to reach row r, column c. The first row is all 1 because only right moves can reach those cells. The first column is all 1 because only down moves can reach those cells.

### Invariant
When computing dp[r][c], the top and left cells are already solved. Therefore the current cell receives the complete count of all paths that can enter it.

### Edge cases
A 1 by n grid has exactly 1 path, all right moves. An m by 1 grid also has exactly 1 path, all down moves.

### Complexity
Filling the grid takes O(m * n) time. The table uses O(m * n) space, with a one-row O(n) optimization available.

minimum-path-sum-in-grid.name: Better
minimum-path-sum-in-grid.description: Reduce repeated work in Min Path Sum with the intermediate structure or cached state used by the standard better approach. It preserves the brute-force reasoning while improving one major time or space bottleneck.

minimum-path-sum-in-grid.name: Optimal (Tabulation)
minimum-path-sum-in-grid.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Minimum Path Sum asks for the cheapest path from the top-left cell to the bottom-right cell when you may only move right or down. The cost of a path is the sum of all visited cell values.

### Core intuition
To reach cell r,c, the previous step must be from above or from the left. Since we want the cheapest path, take the cheaper of those two already-computed costs and add the current cell's value.

### State meaning
dp[r][c], or grid[r][c] when updating in place, stores the minimum cost to reach that cell from the start. The first row and first column have only one possible direction, so their costs are simple running sums.

### Invariant
After a cell is processed, it no longer stores just its original grid value; it stores the best total cost to reach that cell. Future cells can safely use it as a solved predecessor.

### Edge cases
A single-cell grid returns that cell's value. The first row can only come from the left, and the first column can only come from above.

### Complexity
Every cell is processed once, so time is O(m * n). If the grid is updated in place, extra space is O(1).

subset-sum-equal-to-k.name: Better
subset-sum-equal-to-k.description: Reduce repeated work in Subset Sum = K with the intermediate structure or cached state used by the standard better approach. It preserves the brute-force reasoning while improving one major time or space bottleneck.

subset-sum-equal-to-k.name: Optimal (1D DP: The 'Can I Make This?' Table)
subset-sum-equal-to-k.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Subset Sum = K asks whether we can choose some elements from the array so their sum is exactly k. Each element can be used at most once.

### Core intuition
Keep a boolean row of reachable sums. Sum 0 starts reachable because choosing nothing makes 0. When a new number num arrives, any old reachable sum s can unlock s + num.

### State meaning
dp[s] is true if sum s can be formed using the numbers processed so far. The update dp[s] = dp[s] or dp[s - num] asks: was there a previous subset that could reach the leftover amount?

### Invariant
After processing each number, dp records exactly the sums reachable using each processed element at most once. Scanning backward is the key that prevents the current number from being reused immediately.

### Edge cases
If k is 0, return true. If all numbers are greater than k, only sum 0 may remain reachable. Positive numbers keep the DP range bounded from 0 to k.

### Complexity
For each number, we scan up to k sums. Time is O(N * K), and space is O(K).

0-1-knapsack.name: Better
0-1-knapsack.description: Reduce repeated work in 0/1 Knapsack with the intermediate structure or cached state used by the standard better approach. It preserves the brute-force reasoning while improving one major time or space bottleneck.
0-1-knapsack.spaceComplexity: O(N*W) + O(N) , We are using a recursion stack space (O(N)) and a 2D array (O(N*W))

0-1-knapsack.name: Optimal (1D Space-Optimized DP)
0-1-knapsack.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
0/1 Knapsack asks for the maximum value we can carry with a weight capacity limit. Each item can be either taken once or left behind, which is why it is called 0/1.

### Core intuition
For each item, every capacity has a choice: skip the item and keep the old value, or take the item if it fits and add its value to the best value for the remaining capacity. The best of those two choices becomes the new answer for that capacity.

### State meaning
dp[c] is the maximum value achievable with capacity c using the items processed so far. When processing an item with weight w and value v, dp[c - w] represents the best value before adding this item.

### Invariant
After each item, dp[c] is the best value possible for capacity c using only processed items. Scanning capacities backward ensures this item is used at most once, because dp[c - w] still belongs to the previous item layer.

### Edge cases
If capacity is 0 or there are no items, the answer is 0. Items heavier than the capacity are automatically skipped because they never fit into any scanned capacity.

### Complexity
For N items and capacity W, time is O(N * W). The one-dimensional DP array uses O(W) space.

triangle.name: Optimal (Bottom-Up Tabulation)
triangle.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Triangle asks for the minimum path sum from the top of a triangle-shaped array to the bottom. From each position, you may move to the cell directly below or the cell below-right.

### Core intuition
Start from the bottom instead of the top. If you already know the cheapest cost from every cell in the row below to the bottom, then each cell in the current row simply adds its own value to the cheaper of its two children.

### State meaning
dp[j] stores the minimum path cost from the position j in the row currently being considered down to the bottom. At the start, dp is just the last row because the cheapest path from a bottom cell is the cell itself.

### Invariant
After processing row r, dp[j] is the cheapest path sum from triangle[r][j] to the bottom. That makes the next row upward safe to compute using only dp[j] and dp[j + 1].

### Edge cases
A triangle with one row returns its only value. Negative numbers are fine because we compare complete child path sums, not greedy local cell values.

### Complexity
Every triangle cell is processed once, so time is O(total cells), often written as O(N^2) for N rows. The one-row dp array uses O(N) space.

rod-cutting-problem.name: Better
rod-cutting-problem.description: Reduce repeated work in Rod Cutting with the intermediate structure or cached state used by the standard better approach. It preserves the brute-force reasoning while improving one major time or space bottleneck.

rod-cutting-problem.name: Optimal (Unbounded Knapsack variant)
rod-cutting-problem.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Rod Cutting asks for the maximum money we can earn by cutting a rod of length n into pieces. Each possible piece length has a price, and we may use the same length multiple times.

### Core intuition
This is unbounded knapsack in disguise. For each rod length len, try making the first cut of every possible piece size. If the first piece has length cut, the remaining rod has length len - cut. The best value is price[cut - 1] plus the best value already known for that remaining length.

### State meaning
dp[len] stores the maximum obtainable price for a rod of length len. dp[0] is 0 because a rod of length 0 has no value.

### Invariant
After computing dp[len], it is the best revenue among all possible first cuts for that length. Because smaller rod lengths are already solved, every candidate first cut can trust dp[len - cut].

### Edge cases
If n is 0, return 0. Prices do not need to be sorted by value. Sometimes the best answer is no cut at all; that is naturally covered by trying cut = len.

### Complexity
For each length, we try all smaller piece lengths, so time is O(N^2). The dp array uses O(N) space.

matrix-chain-multiplication.name: Optimal (MCM Partitioning)
matrix-chain-multiplication.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Matrix Chain Multiplication asks for the cheapest way to parenthesize a sequence of matrix multiplications. The final matrix result is the same, but the number of scalar multiplications can change dramatically.

### Core intuition
A chain from matrix i to matrix j must be split somewhere: (i..k) multiplied with (k+1..j). If the cheapest cost of the left part and right part is already known, we only need to add the cost of multiplying those two resulting matrices together.

### State meaning
dp[i][j] is the minimum multiplication cost for matrices i through j. A single matrix costs 0 because no multiplication is needed. The dimensions array tells the merge cost: dims[i - 1] * dims[k] * dims[j].

### Invariant
When solving a chain of a certain length, every smaller subchain inside it has already been solved. So every possible split k can be evaluated exactly and compared fairly.

### Edge cases
A single matrix has cost 0. The order of matrices cannot be rearranged; only parentheses can change. This DP minimizes operation count, not memory layout.

### Complexity
There are O(N^2) subchains, and each tries O(N) split points, so time is O(N^3). The table uses O(N^2) space.

reverse-linked-list.name: Brute Force
reverse-linked-list.description: Build a correctness baseline for Reverse a Linked List by directly enumerating the candidate states allowed by the problem. This version prioritizes transparent control flow and exhaustive coverage over asymptotic efficiency.
reverse-linked-list.spaceComplexity: O(N) , We use an extra stack to store all the node values, which requires O(N)

reverse-linked-list.name: Optimal (Iterative 3-Pointers)
reverse-linked-list.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Reverse Linked List asks us to flip a singly linked list so every next pointer points backward and the old tail becomes the new head.

### Core intuition
At each node, save the next node before changing anything. Then point the current node back to the already-reversed part. After that, move the reversed boundary forward by one node.

### State meaning
prev is the head of the reversed portion built so far. curr is the node currently being flipped. nextNode temporarily saves the untouched remainder so the list is not lost when curr.next is overwritten.

### Invariant
Before each loop, prev points to a fully reversed prefix, and curr points to the first node of the still-original suffix. After one loop, the reversed prefix grows by exactly one node.

### Edge cases
An empty list returns null. A one-node list returns itself. The most common bug is forgetting to save curr.next before rewriting it.

### Complexity
Every node is visited once, so time is O(N). Only three pointers are used, so space is O(1).

linked-list-cycle.name: Brute Force
linked-list-cycle.description: Build a correctness baseline for Linked List Cycle Detection by directly enumerating the candidate states allowed by the problem. This version prioritizes transparent control flow and exhaustive coverage over asymptotic efficiency.

linked-list-cycle.name: Optimal (Floyd's Tortoise & Hare)
linked-list-cycle.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Linked List Cycle asks whether a linked list eventually loops back to an earlier node instead of ending at null.

### Core intuition
Use two pointers moving at different speeds. The slow pointer moves one step at a time. The fast pointer moves two steps at a time. If the list has a loop, the fast pointer keeps circling and must eventually catch the slow pointer. If there is no loop, fast reaches null.

### State meaning
slow marks the steady one-step position. fast marks the two-step position. A collision means both pointers refer to the exact same node object, not merely the same value.

### Invariant
If fast reaches the end, no cycle exists. If a cycle exists, once both pointers enter the cycle, the distance between them shrinks modulo the cycle length until they meet.

### Edge cases
An empty list and a single node pointing to null have no cycle. A single node pointing to itself is a cycle. Node values cannot be used to detect cycles because different nodes may hold equal values.

### Complexity
The chase takes O(N) time. Only two pointers are stored, so space is O(1).

merge-two-sorted-lists.name: Brute Force
merge-two-sorted-lists.description: Build a correctness baseline for Merge Two Sorted Lists by directly enumerating the candidate states allowed by the problem. This version prioritizes transparent control flow and exhaustive coverage over asymptotic efficiency.

merge-two-sorted-lists.name: Optimal (Iterative with Dummy Node)
merge-two-sorted-lists.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Merge Two Sorted Lists asks us to combine two already sorted linked lists into one sorted list by relinking nodes.

### Core intuition
Look at the current front node of each list. The smaller value must be the next node of the merged result because both lists are already sorted. Attach that node, advance that list, and repeat.

### State meaning
dummy is a fake node before the real answer, used to avoid special handling for the first node. curr is the tail of the merged list being built. l1 and l2 point to the remaining unmerged nodes in each list.

### Invariant
Before each comparison, dummy.next through curr is sorted and contains exactly the nodes already chosen. The unchosen suffixes l1 and l2 are still individually sorted.

### Edge cases
If one list is empty, return the other. If one list finishes during the merge, attach the remaining suffix of the other list directly because it is already sorted.

### Complexity
Each node is attached once, so time is O(N + M). The merge uses O(1) extra space because it reuses existing nodes.

middle-of-a-linkedlist.name: Optimal (Fast & Slow Pointers)
middle-of-a-linkedlist.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Middle of a LinkedList asks for the middle node of a singly linked list. If there are two middle nodes, this version returns the second middle.

### Core intuition
Move one pointer twice as fast as the other. When the fast pointer reaches the end, the slow pointer has taken half as many steps, so it is at the middle.

### State meaning
slow moves one node per loop. fast moves two nodes per loop. The loop continues while fast and fast.next exist, meaning fast can safely make a two-step move.

### Invariant
After k loop iterations, slow has moved k steps and fast has moved 2k steps. That fixed speed ratio is what places slow halfway when fast reaches the end.

### Edge cases
An empty list would return null if handled by the caller. A one-node list returns that node. For an even-length list, the standard loop condition returns the second middle.

### Complexity
The list is scanned once, so time is O(N). Only two pointers are used, so space is O(1).

check-if-ll-is-palindrome-or-not.name: Brute Force
check-if-ll-is-palindrome-or-not.description: Build a correctness baseline for Palindrome LinkedList by directly enumerating the candidate states allowed by the problem. This version prioritizes transparent control flow and exhaustive coverage over asymptotic efficiency.

check-if-ll-is-palindrome-or-not.name: Optimal (Reverse Second Half)
check-if-ll-is-palindrome-or-not.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Palindrome LinkedList asks whether the node values read the same forward and backward.

### Core intuition
A singly linked list cannot move backward, so create a backward-readable second half. Find the middle, reverse the second half, then compare the first half and reversed second half node by node.

### State meaning
slow and fast locate the middle. prev becomes the head of the reversed second half. left scans from the original head, and right scans from the reversed half.

### Invariant
During comparison, every pair checked so far matches symmetrically from the outside toward the center. If any pair differs, the list cannot be a palindrome.

### Edge cases
An empty or one-node list is a palindrome. Odd-length lists can include the middle node in the reversed half; comparing while right exists still works. Some production implementations restore the second half afterward.

### Complexity
Finding the middle, reversing half, and comparing are all linear passes, so time is O(N). Extra space is O(1).

remove-nth-node-from-the-back-of-the-ll.name: Brute Force
remove-nth-node-from-the-back-of-the-ll.description: Build a correctness baseline for Remove Nth Node From Back by directly enumerating the candidate states allowed by the problem. This version prioritizes transparent control flow and exhaustive coverage over asymptotic efficiency.

remove-nth-node-from-the-back-of-the-ll.name: Optimal (Maintain a Gap of N)
remove-nth-node-from-the-back-of-the-ll.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Remove Nth Node From Back asks us to delete the node that is n positions from the end of a singly linked list and return the updated head.

### Core intuition
Create a gap of n nodes between fast and slow. Then move both pointers together. When fast reaches the last node, slow is sitting just before the node that must be removed.

### State meaning
dummy is a fake node before head, which makes deleting the original head easy. fast is advanced n steps ahead. slow trails behind and eventually lands on the predecessor of the target node.

### Invariant
After the initial advance, fast is n nodes ahead of slow. Moving them together preserves that gap until fast reaches the end, placing slow exactly before the nth-from-end node.

### Edge cases
Deleting the head is handled by dummy. If n equals the list length, slow stays at dummy and dummy.next is skipped. The input usually guarantees n is valid.

### Complexity
The list is traversed in one pass after the initial gap setup, so time is O(N). Only two pointers and a dummy node are used, so space is O(1).

sort-a-ll-of-0-s-1-s-and-2-s.name: Optimal (Three Dummy Chains)
sort-a-ll-of-0-s-1-s-and-2-s.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Sort LL of 0s, 1s, 2s asks us to reorder a linked list whose values are only 0, 1, and 2 so that all 0s come first, then 1s, then 2s.

### Core intuition
Because there are only three possible values, build three chains while scanning the list: one chain for 0s, one for 1s, and one for 2s. Then connect the chains in order.

### State meaning
zero, one, and two are tail pointers for the three chains. zeroD, oneD, and twoD are dummy heads that remember where each chain starts. curr scans the original list.

### Invariant
After processing each node, that node has been appended to exactly one of the three chains. The relative order inside each value group is preserved, and no processed node should remain accidentally connected to an old next pointer at the end.

### Edge cases
The list may contain only one or two of the values. The final connection must skip empty chains correctly. Setting the 2-chain tail's next to null avoids accidental cycles or leftover links.

### Complexity
The list is scanned once and then stitched once, so time is O(N). The algorithm uses only a constant number of pointers, so space is O(1).

add-1-to-a-number-represented-by-ll.name: Optimal (Recursive Backtracking)
add-1-to-a-number-represented-by-ll.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Add 1 to LL asks us to treat a linked list as a number, where each node is one digit, and return the list after adding one.

### Core intuition
Addition starts from the least significant digit, which is the tail of this forward-order linked list. Recursion is useful because it walks to the tail first, then carries information back toward the head.

### State meaning
The helper returns carry, either 0 or 1. When it returns to a node, that node adds carry to its digit, keeps sum % 10 as the new digit, and sends Math.floor(sum / 10) back to the previous node.

### Invariant
After helper finishes a node's next side, that entire suffix has already been updated correctly, and carry tells whether the current node must absorb one extra unit.

### Edge cases
If the list is 999, every digit becomes 0 and a final carry creates a new head 1. If the last digit is not 9, the carry dies immediately after updating the tail.

### Complexity
Each node is visited once on the way down and once while returning, so time is O(N). Recursive stack space is O(N).

find-intersection-point-of-y-ll.name: Brute Force
find-intersection-point-of-y-ll.description: Build a correctness baseline for Intersection of Y LL by directly enumerating the candidate states allowed by the problem. This version prioritizes transparent control flow and exhaustive coverage over asymptotic efficiency.

find-intersection-point-of-y-ll.name: Better
find-intersection-point-of-y-ll.description: Reduce repeated work in Intersection of Y LL with the intermediate structure or cached state used by the standard better approach. It preserves the brute-force reasoning while improving one major time or space bottleneck.
find-intersection-point-of-y-ll.timeComplexity: O(n + m) , Iterating through list 1 first takes O(n)

find-intersection-point-of-y-ll.name: Optimal (The 'Infinite Loop' Sync Technique)
find-intersection-point-of-y-ll.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Intersection of Y LL asks for the exact node where two singly linked lists begin sharing the same tail. The answer is a node reference, not just a matching value.

### Core intuition
If two lists have different lengths, their heads are out of sync. Let pointer a walk list A, then switch to list B. Let pointer b walk list B, then switch to list A. After both have walked A length plus B length, the length difference is canceled out, so they meet at the intersection or both become null.

### State meaning
a and b are walking pointers. When a reaches null, it jumps to headB. When b reaches null, it jumps to headA. The loop stops when a and b point to the same node reference.

### Invariant
After each pointer switches lists once, both pointers have the same total remaining distance to the shared tail. That synchronization makes the meeting point exact.

### Edge cases
If either head is null, there is no intersection. If the lists do not intersect, both pointers eventually become null at the same time. Equal values do not matter unless they are the same node object.

### Complexity
Each pointer walks at most both lists once, so time is O(N + M). Only two pointers are used, so space is O(1).

reverse-nodes-in-k-group.name: Optimal (Iterative Fragment Reversal)
reverse-nodes-in-k-group.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Reverse in K-Group asks us to reverse nodes in groups of size k. If the final group has fewer than k nodes, it must stay in its original order.

### Core intuition
Treat each k-sized group like a small linked list reversal, then connect the reversed group back to the previous and next parts. A dummy node before the head makes it easier to reconnect the first group.

### State meaning
pre points to the node before the group being reversed. curr is the first node of the group and becomes the tail after reversal. nex is the node being moved to the front of the group during the internal reversal.

### Invariant
Before each group reversal, everything before pre is already finalized. During the group reversal, nodes are moved one by one to the front of that group without losing the rest of the list.

### Edge cases
If k is 1, the list is unchanged. If there are fewer than k nodes left, leave them untouched. A dummy node avoids special cases when the first group changes the head.

### Complexity
Every node is moved a constant number of times, so time is O(N). The iterative version uses O(1) extra space.

flattening-of-ll.name: Optimal (Recursion + Merge Pattern)
flattening-of-ll.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Flattening of LL asks us to turn a multi-level linked list into one sorted bottom-chain. Each top-level node may have a sorted bottom list, and top-level nodes are connected by next pointers.

### Core intuition
This is repeated sorted-list merging. Flatten the right side first, then merge the current bottom list with that already-flattened right side using bottom pointers. Doing this from right to left eventually produces one sorted vertical list.

### State meaning
root is the current top-level list head. root.next represents the columns to the right. merge(a, b) combines two sorted bottom chains and returns the smaller head.

### Invariant
After flatten(root.next), the entire right side is already one sorted bottom chain. Merging root's bottom chain into it preserves sorted order and produces a larger flattened chain.

### Edge cases
If root is null or root.next is null, it is already flattened. While merging, next pointers should not be used for the final chain; bottom pointers carry the flattened result.

### Complexity
If there are T total nodes, each merge visits its participating nodes. The common recursive merge approach can be O(T * numberOfColumns) in the worst shape. Recursion stack is O(numberOfColumns) plus merge recursion depth.

clone-a-linked-list-with-random-and-next.name: Brute Force
clone-a-linked-list-with-random-and-next.description: Build a correctness baseline for Clone LL with Random Pointers by directly enumerating the candidate states allowed by the problem. This version prioritizes transparent control flow and exhaustive coverage over asymptotic efficiency.

clone-a-linked-list-with-random-and-next.name: Optimal (The 'DNA Splice' Strategy)
clone-a-linked-list-with-random-and-next.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Clone LL with Random Pointers asks for a deep copy of a linked list where every node has both next and random pointers. The copied list must use brand-new nodes, but preserve the same pointer pattern.

### Core intuition
Instead of using a hash map from original to clone, temporarily weave each clone directly after its original node. Then the clone of any original node is original.next, so random pointers can be assigned by looking at original.random.next.

### State meaning
During the first pass, every original node X is followed by its copy X'. During the second pass, X'.random is assigned. During the third pass, the woven list is separated back into the original list and the copied list.

### Invariant
After weaving, every clone is adjacent to its original. That adjacency is a temporary lookup table, making random pointer assignment O(1) without extra map storage.

### Edge cases
If head is null, return null. If a node's random pointer is null, the copy's random pointer should also be null. The unweaving pass must restore the original list's next pointers.

### Complexity
There are three linear passes, so time is O(N). The weaving strategy uses O(1) extra space apart from the new cloned nodes required for the answer.

number-of-islands.name: Optimal (DFS Search)
number-of-islands.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Number of Islands asks how many disconnected groups of land cells exist in a grid. Land cells are "1", water cells are "0", and land connects only up, down, left, or right.

### Core intuition
Scan every cell. When you find an unvisited land cell, that is the start of one new island. Immediately run DFS or BFS from it to mark every connected land cell in that island, so none of them can be counted again.

### State meaning
count stores how many island starts have been found. DFS receives a row and column and marks connected land as visited, often by changing "1" to "0" or using a visited set.

### Invariant
After DFS finishes from a discovered land cell, the entire island containing that cell has been marked visited. Therefore future scan positions will only count truly new islands.

### Edge cases
An empty grid has 0 islands. Diagonal land does not connect. If the whole grid is land, the answer is 1 after one DFS marks everything.

### Complexity
Every cell is scanned, and each land cell is visited by DFS once. Time is O(rows * cols). Recursion or queue space can be O(rows * cols) in the worst case.

clone-graph.name: Optimal (DFS + Hash Map)
clone-graph.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Clone Graph asks for a deep copy of a connected graph. Every original node must get a new node with the same value, and every neighbor relationship must be recreated between cloned nodes.

### Core intuition
Graphs can contain cycles, so cloning by pure recursion without memory would loop forever. Use a map from original node to cloned node. If a node is seen again, return its existing clone instead of creating another copy.

### State meaning
visited maps original nodes to their clones. DFS returns the clone corresponding to the original node it receives. Each clone's neighbors are filled by recursively cloning the original node's neighbors.

### Invariant
Once an original node is stored in visited, there is exactly one clone for it. Any later edge pointing to that original node reuses the same clone, preserving graph structure and cycles.

### Edge cases
If the input node is null, return null. Self-loops and cycles are safe because the map is written before cloning neighbors.

### Complexity
Each node is cloned once and each edge is examined through adjacency lists, so time is O(V + E). The map and recursion/queue storage use O(V) space.

course-schedule.name: Optimal (Kahn's Algorithm - BFS)
course-schedule.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Course Schedule asks whether all courses can be completed given prerequisite pairs. If dependencies contain a cycle, some course waits on itself indirectly, making completion impossible.

### Core intuition
Use topological sorting with in-degree. Courses with no prerequisites can be taken immediately. Taking one course reduces the prerequisite count of courses it unlocks. If we can take every course this way, there is no cycle.

### State meaning
adj[pre] stores courses unlocked by pre. inDegree[course] stores how many prerequisites remain. The queue stores currently available courses with inDegree 0. takenCount counts how many courses have been removed from the dependency graph.

### Invariant
Every course in the queue currently has no unmet prerequisites. When it is processed, reducing its neighbors' in-degree accurately removes that prerequisite from the graph.

### Edge cases
If there are no prerequisites, all courses can be taken. A pair cycle like 0 needs 1 and 1 needs 0 leaves both with positive in-degree, so the queue cannot process them.

### Complexity
Building and processing the graph visits every course and prerequisite once, so time is O(V + E). Adjacency list, in-degree array, and queue use O(V + E) space.

dijkstra-s-algorithm.name: Optimal (Priority Queue / Min-Heap)
dijkstra-s-algorithm.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Dijkstra's Algorithm finds the shortest distance from one source node to every other node in a graph with non-negative edge weights.

### Core intuition
Always expand the currently known closest node. Because all edge weights are non-negative, once a node is popped from the min-heap with its best distance, no later path can sneak in and make it cheaper through a longer already-pending route.

### State meaning
dist[v] is the best distance to v found so far. The min-heap stores candidate pairs of distance and node. When a better route to a neighbor is found, update dist and push the new candidate into the heap.

### Invariant
Whenever the heap pops a node with distance equal to dist[node], that distance is finalized. Stale heap entries with larger distances are skipped because a better route was discovered after they were pushed.

### Edge cases
The algorithm assumes no negative edge weights. Unreachable nodes remain Infinity. Multiple heap entries for the same node are normal; the stale-check handles them.

### Complexity
Using a min-heap, each edge relaxation may push into the heap, so time is O(E log V). The graph, distance array, and heap use O(V + E) space.

bellman-ford-algorithm.name: Optimal (Global Edge Relaxation)
bellman-ford-algorithm.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Bellman-Ford finds shortest distances from one source node even when some edges have negative weights. It can also detect whether a reachable negative cycle exists.

### Core intuition
The shortest simple path in a graph with V vertices uses at most V - 1 edges. Bellman-Ford repeatedly relaxes every edge V - 1 times, allowing shortest paths with 1 edge, then 2 edges, and so on to fully form.

### State meaning
dist[v] is the best known distance from the source to v. Relaxing edge u -> v with weight w means checking whether dist[u] + w improves dist[v].

### Invariant
After the k-th full pass over all edges, all shortest paths that use at most k edges have been discovered. After V - 1 passes, every shortest simple path should be known.

### Edge cases
Unreachable nodes remain Infinity. Negative edges are allowed. If any edge can still improve after V - 1 passes, a reachable negative cycle exists and shortest distances are not well-defined.

### Complexity
Bellman-Ford scans every edge V - 1 times, so time is O(V * E). The distance array uses O(V) space.

floyd-warshall-algorithm.name: Optimal (Triple-Nested Dynamic Programming)
floyd-warshall-algorithm.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Floyd-Warshall asks for the shortest distance between every pair of vertices in a weighted graph. Instead of one source, it solves all source-destination pairs together.

### Core intuition
Let each vertex take a turn as an allowed middle stop. When vertex k is allowed as a middle stop, every pair i to j asks: is the old direct or previously known route better, or is going i -> k -> j better?

### State meaning
dist[i][j] stores the best known distance from i to j using only the intermediate vertices that have already been allowed. The triple loop expands the set of allowed intermediate vertices one by one.

### Invariant
After finishing intermediate k, dist[i][j] is the shortest path from i to j using only vertices 0 through k as possible middle stops. That promise makes the next k safe to use.

### Edge cases
dist[i][i] should start at 0. Missing edges should start as Infinity. Negative edges can work, but negative cycles make shortest paths undefined and are usually detected if dist[i][i] becomes negative.

### Complexity
There are three nested loops over the vertices, so time is O(V^3). The distance matrix uses O(V^2) space.

kruskal-s-algorithm.name: Optimal (Greedy Strategy with Disjoint Set Union)
kruskal-s-algorithm.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Kruskal's Algorithm finds a minimum spanning tree of an undirected weighted graph: connect all vertices with the smallest possible total edge weight and no cycles.

### Core intuition
Sort every edge by weight and try them from cheapest to most expensive. Take an edge only if it connects two components that are not already connected. Disjoint Set Union answers that question quickly.

### State meaning
The DSU parent structure tracks which component each vertex belongs to. mstWeight stores the total weight of accepted edges. edgesCount tells when the spanning tree has V - 1 edges.

### Invariant
After each accepted edge, the chosen edges form a forest with no cycles. Because edges are considered in increasing cost, every accepted edge is the cheapest safe connection between two currently separate components.

### Edge cases
If the graph is disconnected, a full spanning tree does not exist. Equal-weight edges can be chosen in different orders and still produce a valid minimum total. Self-loops are skipped because both endpoints are already in the same component.

### Complexity
Sorting dominates with O(E log E). DSU operations are almost constant with path compression, so the total is O(E log E). Space is O(V + E) if the edge list is stored.

kosaraju-s-algorithm-strongly-connected-.name: Optimal (Two-Pass DFS)
kosaraju-s-algorithm-strongly-connected-.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Kosaraju's Algorithm finds strongly connected components in a directed graph. Inside one strongly connected component, every node can reach every other node.

### Core intuition
First DFS records nodes by finish time, which places source-like component exits late on a stack. Then reverse every edge. Finally, process nodes in reverse finish order on the reversed graph. Each DFS in this second pass reveals exactly one strongly connected component.

### State meaning
visited marks nodes explored in each pass. stack stores nodes after their first-pass DFS finishes. reversedAdj stores every directed edge flipped. components collects the groups found in the second pass.

### Invariant
When a second-pass DFS starts from the highest remaining finish-time node in the reversed graph, it cannot leak into a different not-yet-assigned component. It collects exactly one SCC.

### Edge cases
Disconnected directed graphs need the first DFS to start from every unvisited node. A single node with no outgoing edges is its own SCC. Self-loops do not break the algorithm.

### Complexity
Both DFS passes and the graph reversal touch each vertex and edge a constant number of times, so time is O(V + E). Space is O(V + E).

bridges-in-graph-tarjan-s-algorithm.name: Optimal (One-pass DFS / Tarjan's)
bridges-in-graph-tarjan-s-algorithm.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Bridges in Graph asks for edges whose removal increases the number of connected components. In a network, a bridge is a connection with no backup route.

### Core intuition
During DFS, give each node an entry time tin and compute low, the earliest entry time reachable from that node's subtree using tree edges plus at most one back edge. For tree edge u-v, if low[v] is greater than tin[u], v's subtree cannot reach u or any ancestor without that edge, so u-v is a bridge.

### State meaning
tin[u] is when DFS first entered u. low[u] is the earliest ancestor reachable from u's DFS area. parent prevents treating the immediate edge back to the parent as a useful back edge.

### Invariant
After DFS finishes a child v, low[v] summarizes the best escape route from v's entire subtree. Comparing low[v] with tin[u] tells whether that subtree has another connection to the earlier graph.

### Edge cases
Disconnected graphs require DFS from every unvisited node. Parallel edges need careful parent-edge handling. A simple cycle has no bridges because every edge has an alternate route.

### Complexity
DFS visits every vertex and edge once, so time is O(V + E). The adjacency list plus tin, low, and recursion stack use O(V + E) space.

articulation-point.name: Optimal (Tarjan's DFS Variant)
articulation-point.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Articulation Points asks for vertices whose removal increases the number of connected components in an undirected graph.

### Core intuition
Use the same tin and low idea as bridge detection, but judge vertices instead of edges. For a non-root node u, if some child v has low[v] >= tin[u], then v's subtree cannot reach an ancestor of u without passing through u, so u is an articulation point.

### State meaning
tin[u] is DFS entry time. low[u] is the earliest reachable entry time from u's subtree. children counts how many independent DFS children a root has. result stores vertices proven critical.

### Invariant
After a child subtree returns, low[child] tells whether that child can escape upward without using the parent. If it cannot, the parent is a separation point for that subtree.

### Edge cases
The DFS root is special: it is an articulation point only if it has more than one DFS child. A leaf is usually not an articulation point. Disconnected graphs require DFS from every unvisited node.

### Complexity
Tarjan DFS visits every vertex and edge once, so time is O(V + E). Arrays and recursion stack use O(V), plus the graph storage.

palindrome-number.name: Optimal (Reverse Half)
palindrome-number.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Palindrome Number asks whether an integer reads the same from left to right and right to left, without needing to convert it to a string.

### Core intuition
Reverse only the last half of the number. If the number is a palindrome, the front half and reversed back half will match. Reversing only half avoids unnecessary work and reduces overflow risk compared with reversing the entire integer.

### State meaning
x keeps the remaining front part of the number. rev builds the reversed back part by taking x % 10 each loop. The loop stops when rev has at least as many digits as x.

### Invariant
After each loop, rev contains the digits peeled from the right side in reverse order, and x contains the still-unpeeled left side.

### Edge cases
Negative numbers are not palindromes because of the minus sign. Numbers ending in 0 are not palindromes unless the number is exactly 0. Odd-length numbers ignore the middle digit by comparing x with Math.floor(rev / 10).

### Complexity
Each loop removes one digit from x, and only half the digits are processed. Time is O(log10 N), and space is O(1).

sieve-of-eratosthenes.name: Brute Force
sieve-of-eratosthenes.description: Build a correctness baseline for Sieve of Eratosthenes by directly enumerating the candidate states allowed by the problem. This version prioritizes transparent control flow and exhaustive coverage over asymptotic efficiency.

sieve-of-eratosthenes.name: Optimal (Sieve Marking)
sieve-of-eratosthenes.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Sieve of Eratosthenes finds all prime numbers below or up to a limit, depending on the exact problem version. The local implementation counts primes less than n.

### Core intuition
Instead of testing every number by division, assume numbers are prime at first, then cross out multiples of each prime. A composite number must have some smaller prime factor, so it will be crossed out when that factor gets its turn.

### State meaning
isPrime[i] tells whether i is still believed to be prime. p is the current base prime. Multiples starting from p * p are marked false because smaller multiples were already handled by smaller factors.

### Invariant
Before moving past p, every composite number with a prime factor up to p has been marked non-prime. Numbers that remain true have not been disproven yet.

### Edge cases
0 and 1 are not prime. If n <= 2, there are no primes less than n. Start marking at p * p to avoid duplicate work and preserve efficiency.

### Complexity
The sieve runs in O(N log log N) time and uses O(N) space for the boolean array.

pow-x-n.name: Optimal (Binary Exponentiation)
pow-x-n.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Implement Pow(x, n) asks us to compute x raised to integer exponent n efficiently, including negative exponents.

### Core intuition
Use binary exponentiation. Instead of multiplying x n times, repeatedly square the base and halve the exponent. If the current exponent is odd, keep one extra copy of the base in the answer.

### State meaning
base is the value currently representing a power of x. exp is the remaining exponent work. result stores the product of bases chosen for the 1 bits of the exponent. For recursive versions, half stores pow(x, floor(n / 2)).

### Invariant
At every step, result * base^exp equals the original target power. Squaring base and halving exp keeps that value unchanged; multiplying result by base handles odd exponents.

### Edge cases
n = 0 returns 1. Negative n means compute the positive power of x and take reciprocal. Many languages need care when negating the minimum integer, so converting n to a wider integer is safer.

### Complexity
The exponent is halved each step, so time is O(log |n|). Recursive space is O(log |n|), while an iterative version can use O(1) space.

count-digits.name: Brute Force
count-digits.description: Build a correctness baseline for Count Digits by directly enumerating the candidate states allowed by the problem. This version prioritizes transparent control flow and exhaustive coverage over asymptotic efficiency.

count-digits.name: Optimal (Extraction)
count-digits.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Count Digits asks how many digits of a number divide the original number evenly. A digit divides evenly if original % digit equals 0.

### Core intuition
Keep the original number safe, then peel digits from a temporary copy using modulo 10. Each peeled digit is tested against the original number.

### State meaning
original stores the unchanged input for divisibility checks. temp is reduced digit by digit. digit is temp % 10. count is the number of valid nonzero digits found so far.

### Invariant
After each loop, every digit already peeled from the right side has been tested exactly once, and temp contains the remaining untested prefix.

### Edge cases
Digit 0 must be skipped because division by zero is invalid. If the number itself is 0, problem definitions vary, so handle according to platform expectations. Repeated digits are counted separately because each occurrence is a digit of the number.

### Complexity
Each loop removes one decimal digit, so time is O(log10 N). Only a few integer variables are used, so space is O(1).

reverse-a-number.name: Optimal (Modulo extraction)
reverse-a-number.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Reverse a Number asks us to reverse the decimal digits of an integer. For example, 123 becomes 321, and a negative sign should stay negative.

### Core intuition
Repeatedly take the last digit with n % 10 and append it to the reversed number by doing rev = rev * 10 + digit. Dividing n by 10 removes the digit that was just used.

### State meaning
sign remembers whether the original number was negative. n is made positive for digit work. rev stores the reversed digits built so far. digit is the current last digit.

### Invariant
After each loop, rev is the reverse of the digits already removed from the original right side, and n contains the digits still waiting to be removed.

### Edge cases
If the number is 0, the reversed value is 0. Negative numbers keep their sign after reversing the absolute value. On platforms with 32-bit bounds, overflow must be checked before returning.

### Complexity
Each loop removes one digit, so time is O(log10 N). The algorithm uses O(1) extra space.

gcd-or-hcf.name: Brute Force
gcd-or-hcf.description: Build a correctness baseline for GCD Or HCF by directly enumerating the candidate states allowed by the problem. This version prioritizes transparent control flow and exhaustive coverage over asymptotic efficiency.

gcd-or-hcf.name: Better
gcd-or-hcf.description: Reduce repeated work in GCD Or HCF with the intermediate structure or cached state used by the standard better approach. It preserves the brute-force reasoning while improving one major time or space bottleneck.

gcd-or-hcf.name: Optimal (Euclidean Algorithm)
gcd-or-hcf.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
GCD or HCF asks for the greatest number that divides both given numbers exactly. It is the largest shared factor.

### Core intuition
Use the Euclidean Algorithm. The key fact is that gcd(a, b) is the same as gcd(b, a % b). The remainder carries the only part that still matters for common divisibility. Keep replacing the bigger problem with the smaller remainder problem until the remainder becomes 0.

### State meaning
a and b are the current pair whose gcd is still being searched. a % b is the leftover after removing as many full b groups from a as possible. When b becomes 0, a is the gcd.

### Invariant
At every step, gcd(a, b) is unchanged by replacing the pair with (b, a % b). We are shrinking the numbers without changing the answer.

### Edge cases
If one number is 0, the gcd is the absolute value of the other number. Use absolute values if negative inputs are possible. If both numbers are equal, the gcd is that number.

### Complexity
The values shrink quickly with each remainder operation, so time is O(log(min(a, b))). The iterative version uses O(1) space.

armstrong-numbers.name: Optimal (Digit Extraction + Power)
armstrong-numbers.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Armstrong Numbers asks whether a number equals the sum of its digits, where each digit is raised to the power of the number of digits.

### Core intuition
First count how many digits the number has. Then walk through the digits one by one, add digit^count to a running sum, and compare that sum with the original number.

### State meaning
original stores the unchanged number. k is the number of digits. temp is a copy used for digit extraction. sum stores the total of digit powers.

### Invariant
After each digit is extracted, sum equals the powered contribution of all digits processed so far, and temp contains the digits still waiting to be processed.

### Edge cases
Single-digit non-negative numbers are Armstrong numbers because d^1 equals d. The number 0 is usually treated as Armstrong. Keep original unchanged for the final comparison.

### Complexity
Counting and processing digits are both proportional to the number of decimal digits, so time is O(log10 N). Extra space is O(1).

check-for-prime.name: Brute Force
check-for-prime.description: Build a correctness baseline for Check for Prime by directly enumerating the candidate states allowed by the problem. This version prioritizes transparent control flow and exhaustive coverage over asymptotic efficiency.

check-for-prime.name: Optimal (Square Root Rule)
check-for-prime.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Check for Prime asks whether a number has exactly two positive divisors: 1 and itself.

### Core intuition
If n has a factor larger than sqrt(n), it must pair with a factor smaller than sqrt(n). So it is enough to test divisors from 2 through floor(sqrt(n)). If none divide n, the number is prime.

### State meaning
i is the current possible divisor. n % i checks whether i divides n exactly. The loop only needs to continue while i * i <= n.

### Invariant
After testing every i up to the current point, no tested number divides n. If the loop finishes, no possible small factor exists, so no large paired factor exists either.

### Edge cases
Numbers less than or equal to 1 are not prime. 2 is prime. Even numbers greater than 2 can be rejected quickly, although the simple sqrt loop still works.

### Complexity
The loop checks up to sqrt(n) candidates, so time is O(sqrt(N)). Space is O(1).

valid-anagram.name: Brute Force
valid-anagram.description: Build a correctness baseline for Valid Anagram by directly enumerating the candidate states allowed by the problem. This version prioritizes transparent control flow and exhaustive coverage over asymptotic efficiency.

valid-anagram.name: Optimal (Frequency Array Hash)
valid-anagram.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Valid Anagram asks whether two strings contain exactly the same characters with exactly the same frequencies, possibly in a different order.

### Core intuition
An anagram is a perfect character inventory match. Count characters from the first string upward, subtract characters from the second string downward, and check whether every counter returns to zero.

### State meaning
count[ch] stores the net difference between how many times ch appears in s and how many times it appears in t. A zero at the end means the character balanced perfectly.

### Invariant
After processing the same number of characters from both strings, the count array represents the exact inventory difference seen so far. If all entries end at zero, the inventories match.

### Edge cases
If the lengths differ, return false immediately. The fixed 26-counter version assumes lowercase English letters; for general Unicode, use a map instead.

### Complexity
Each string is scanned once, so time is O(N). For lowercase English letters, space is O(1) because the counter size is fixed.

longest-substring-without-repeating-char.name: Optimal (Sliding Window & Hash Map)
longest-substring-without-repeating-char.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Longest Substring Without Repeating Characters asks for the maximum length of a contiguous substring where no character appears twice.

### Core intuition
Use a sliding window. Expand the right side one character at a time. If the new character was already inside the current window, move the left boundary just past its previous occurrence. This keeps the window duplicate-free.

### State meaning
left is the start index of the current valid window. right scans the string. lastSeen[ch] stores the most recent index of each character. best stores the longest valid window length seen so far.

### Invariant
After each right index is processed, the substring from left to right has no repeated characters. The left pointer never moves backward.

### Edge cases
An empty string returns 0. When a repeated character was seen before left, ignore it because it is no longer inside the current window. This is why left uses max(left, lastSeen[ch] + 1).

### Complexity
Each character is processed once by right, and left only moves forward, so time is O(N). The map uses O(min(N, character set size)) space.

longest-palindromic-substring.name: Optimal (Expand Around Center)
longest-palindromic-substring.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Longest Palindromic Substring asks for the longest contiguous part of a string that reads the same forward and backward.

### Core intuition
Every palindrome has a center. Odd-length palindromes center on a character, and even-length palindromes center between two characters. Try expanding around every possible center and remember the longest expansion.

### State meaning
left and right are expansion pointers. start and maxLength store the best palindrome found so far. Each center produces a candidate length after expansion stops.

### Invariant
For a chosen center, while s[left] equals s[right], the substring between them is a palindrome. When expansion stops, the last valid bounds describe the largest palindrome for that center.

### Edge cases
An empty string returns an empty string. Single characters are palindromes. Even-length palindromes like abba require checking the gap between characters, not only character centers.

### Complexity
There are O(N) centers and each can expand O(N), so time is O(N^2). Only pointer variables are used, so extra space is O(1).

reverse-words-in-a-given-string.name: Brute Force
reverse-words-in-a-given-string.description: Build a correctness baseline for Reverse Words in a String by directly enumerating the candidate states allowed by the problem. This version prioritizes transparent control flow and exhaustive coverage over asymptotic efficiency.
reverse-words-in-a-given-string.timeComplexity: O(N),We traverse the string once to collect words (O(N))

reverse-words-in-a-given-string.name: Optimal (The 'Sentence Shuffling' Strategy)
reverse-words-in-a-given-string.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Reverse Words in a String asks us to reverse the order of words, remove extra spaces, and join the words with a single space. The letters inside each word stay in the same order.

### Core intuition
Treat the sentence as a list of words. First extract clean words by ignoring extra spaces. Then reverse that word list and join it with one space.

### State meaning
words stores the meaningful tokens from the string. Reversing words changes word order, not character order. The final join controls spacing.

### Invariant
After tokenization, words contains only real words with no empty strings from extra spaces. After reversing, every original word appears exactly once in opposite order.

### Edge cases
Leading spaces, trailing spaces, and multiple spaces between words should disappear. A one-word string returns that word. An all-space string returns an empty string if allowed by the platform.

### Complexity
Tokenizing, reversing, and joining are linear in the string length, so time is O(N). The word list uses O(N) space.

remove-outermost-parenthesis.name: Optimal (Balance Counter)
remove-outermost-parenthesis.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Remove Outermost Parenthesis asks us to split a valid parentheses string into primitive balanced pieces and remove the first opening and last closing parenthesis from each piece.

### Core intuition
Track balance, the current nesting depth. An opening parenthesis is outermost only when balance is 0 before reading it. A closing parenthesis is outermost only when balance becomes 0 after reading it. Keep every other parenthesis.

### State meaning
balance counts currently open parentheses. result stores parentheses that are not outermost. For an opening character, decide whether to append before increasing balance. For a closing character, decrease balance before deciding whether to append.

### Invariant
balance always equals the depth after processing the characters seen so far. Characters appended to result are exactly those with depth inside a primitive, not the primitive wrapper itself.

### Edge cases
The input is assumed valid. A primitive like () contributes an empty string after removing its outer layer. Multiple primitives are handled naturally because balance returns to 0 between them.

### Complexity
The string is scanned once, so time is O(N). The output string can be O(N), so space is O(N).

largest-odd-number-in-a-string.name: Optimal (Scan from Right)
largest-odd-number-in-a-string.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Largest Odd Number in String asks for the largest-valued odd integer that is a non-empty prefix substring of the given numeric string after possibly cutting off some suffix.

### Core intuition
A decimal number is odd if and only if its last digit is odd. To get the largest possible value, keep the string as long as possible. So scan from the right until the first odd digit appears, then return everything from the start through that digit.

### State meaning
i scans from the last index toward the front. num[i] is the candidate final digit. If it is odd, num.slice(0, i + 1) is the largest odd prefix possible.

### Invariant
Every digit to the right of i has already been checked and is even, so no longer prefix ending there could be odd. The first odd digit from the right gives the longest valid prefix.

### Edge cases
