If no odd digit exists, return an empty string. Leading zeros should be preserved if they are part of the returned substring, according to string-output behavior.

### Complexity
The scan visits each digit at most once, so time is O(N). Apart from the returned substring, extra space is O(1).

isomorphic-strings.name: Optimal (Double Map)
isomorphic-strings.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Isomorphic Strings asks whether characters in one string can be consistently replaced to form the other string. Each character must map to one character, and two different source characters cannot map to the same target character.

### Core intuition
Use two maps. One map checks that every character from s always maps to the same character in t. The other map checks the reverse direction, preventing two source characters from sharing one target character.

### State meaning
mapST stores mappings from s characters to t characters. mapTS stores mappings from t characters back to s characters. At each index, both directions must agree with the current pair.

### Invariant
After processing each position, all mappings seen so far are one-to-one and consistent. Any contradiction means the strings are not isomorphic.

### Edge cases
Different lengths return false. Repeated characters are fine if they repeat with the same partner. The reverse map is essential for cases like s = ab and t = aa.

### Complexity
The strings are scanned once, so time is O(N). Space is O(character set size), which is O(1) for fixed ASCII-like alphabets.

rotate-string.name: Optimal (Concatenation Hint)
rotate-string.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Rotate String asks whether string s can become string t by repeatedly moving the first character of s to the end.

### Core intuition
All rotations of s appear inside s + s. If s = abcde, then s + s = abcdeabcde. Any rotation is just a length-s window inside that doubled string.

### State meaning
The length check ensures s and t could be rotations of the same characters. The doubled string stores every possible wrap-around view of s. The substring check asks whether t is one of those views.

### Invariant
If t is a rotation of s, it must appear as one continuous block inside s + s. If it does not appear there, no amount of shifting can create it.

### Edge cases
Different lengths return false immediately. Empty strings are rotations of each other. Repeated characters are handled naturally by the substring check.

### Complexity
Building s + s takes O(N) space and checking containment is O(N) on typical string-search implementations, though the exact engine can vary.

roman-number-to-integer.name: Optimal (The 'Look-Ahead' Deduction)
roman-number-to-integer.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Roman to Integer asks us to convert a Roman numeral string into its integer value.

### Core intuition
Roman symbols usually add from left to right, but a smaller value before a larger value means subtraction. So for each symbol, compare it with the next symbol. If the next value is larger, subtract the current value; otherwise add it.

### State meaning
map stores the seven Roman symbol values. result stores the running total. curr is the value of the current symbol, and next is the value of the symbol to its right.

### Invariant
After each character is processed, result equals the correct value of the prefix according to whether each symbol added or subtracted from the next larger symbol.

### Edge cases
The input is usually guaranteed to be a valid Roman numeral. The last character always adds because there is no larger symbol after it. Subtractive pairs like IV, IX, XL, XC, CD, and CM are handled by the same look-ahead rule.

### Complexity
The string is scanned once, so time is O(N). The value map is fixed size, so space is O(1).

implement-atoi.name: Optimal (State Machine Simulation)
implement-atoi.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
String to Integer (atoi) asks us to parse a string into a 32-bit signed integer using strict rules: skip leading spaces, read one optional sign, read digits, stop at the first non-digit, and clamp overflow.

### Core intuition
This is a small parser with phases. Do not try to understand the whole string at once. Walk left to right: ignore spaces, lock the sign, collect digits, and stop as soon as the numeric portion ends.

### State meaning
i is the current character index. sign is 1 or -1. result is the absolute numeric value being built. INT_MIN and INT_MAX are the clamp boundaries.

### Invariant
While reading digits, result contains exactly the number represented by all digit characters consumed so far, before applying sign. Once a non-digit appears, parsing is finished.

### Edge cases
A string with no digits returns 0. Only one sign is allowed and it must appear before digits. Overflow clamps to -2147483648 or 2147483647. Text after the number is ignored.

### Complexity
At most one pass is made over the useful prefix of the string, so time is O(N). Only a few variables are used, so space is O(1).

sort-characters-by-frequency.name: Optimal (Bucket Sort)
sort-characters-by-frequency.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Sort Characters by Frequency asks us to rearrange characters so higher-frequency characters appear before lower-frequency characters.

### Core intuition
First count how often each character appears. Then either sort characters by count or use buckets where bucket[f] stores all characters that appear f times. Reading buckets from high frequency to low frequency builds the answer.

### State meaning
freq maps each character to its count. buckets[index] stores characters with that exact frequency. result is built by repeating each character according to its frequency.

### Invariant
After counting, freq is the complete truth about character frequencies. After bucket placement, every unique character appears in exactly one frequency bucket.

### Edge cases
Multiple valid answers can exist when characters share the same frequency. An empty string returns an empty string. Case-sensitive characters are counted separately unless the problem says otherwise.

### Complexity
Counting is O(N). Bucket construction and output are O(N), so time is O(N). The frequency map and buckets use O(N) space in the worst case.

maximum-nesting-depth-of-parentheses.name: Optimal (Max Balance)
maximum-nesting-depth-of-parentheses.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Max Nesting Depth of Parentheses asks for the deepest level of parentheses nesting inside a valid parentheses string.

### Core intuition
Track the current balance. Every opening parenthesis increases depth by 1. Every closing parenthesis decreases depth by 1. The maximum balance ever reached is the answer.

### State meaning
current stores the current nesting depth. best stores the largest depth seen so far. Non-parenthesis characters do not affect either value.

### Invariant
After scanning each character, current equals the number of open parentheses not yet closed. best equals the maximum such value seen at any earlier point.

### Edge cases
A string with no parentheses has depth 0. The input is typically valid, so current should not go negative. Non-parenthesis characters are ignored.

### Complexity
The string is scanned once, so time is O(N). Only two counters are stored, so space is O(1).

longest-common-prefix.name: Optimal (Horizontal Scanning)
longest-common-prefix.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Longest Common Prefix asks for the longest starting substring shared by every string in an array.

### Core intuition
Start by assuming the first string is the prefix. Compare it with each next string. If that string does not start with the current prefix, shorten the prefix from the right until it does. Repeat until all strings agree or the prefix becomes empty.

### State meaning
prefix is the current shared-start candidate. The loop over strings tests whether each word begins with prefix. Shortening prefix removes characters that cannot be common to everyone.

### Invariant
After processing each string, prefix is the longest prefix shared by all strings seen so far. It never grows; it only stays the same or shrinks.

### Edge cases
An empty array returns an empty string. If any string is empty, the answer is empty. If the first characters differ, the prefix shrinks to empty quickly.

### Complexity
In the worst case, characters are compared across the strings, so time is O(S), where S is the total number of characters. Extra space is O(1) aside from the returned prefix.

string-pattern-matching-rabin-karp.name: Optimal (Rolling Hash)
string-pattern-matching-rabin-karp.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Rabin-Karp searches for a pattern inside a text. It uses rolling hash fingerprints to avoid comparing every pattern-sized window character by character.

### Core intuition
Compute a hash for the pattern and for the current text window of the same length. If hashes differ, the strings are definitely different. If hashes match, verify the actual characters to guard against collisions. Then roll the window hash forward in O(1).

### State meaning
patHash is the pattern fingerprint. windowHash is the current text-window fingerprint. h is the high-place multiplier used to remove the outgoing character from the hash. The modulus keeps numbers bounded.

### Invariant
At each starting index i, windowHash represents text[i..i+m-1]. After rolling, it represents the next window without recomputing from scratch.

### Edge cases
If the pattern is longer than the text, return -1. Hash collisions require a final substring comparison before accepting a match. Negative hash values after subtraction should be normalized under the modulus.

### Complexity
Average time is O(N + M). Worst case can degrade to O(N * M) if many collisions force verification. Extra space is O(1).

generate-parentheses.name: Optimal (Backtracking DSL)
generate-parentheses.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Generate Parentheses asks for every valid string made from n pairs of parentheses.

### Core intuition
Build the string one character at a time. You may add an opening parenthesis if you still have openings left. You may add a closing parenthesis only if it would not exceed the number of openings already used.

### State meaning
current is the string being built. openCount is how many opening parentheses have been used. closeCount is how many closing parentheses have been used. result stores complete valid strings.

### Invariant
At every recursive call, current is still a valid prefix: closeCount never exceeds openCount, and neither count exceeds n. That prevents invalid branches early.

### Edge cases
n = 0 returns a list containing the empty string or an empty list depending on platform convention. The usual LeetCode-style input starts at n >= 1.

### Complexity
The number of valid outputs is the nth Catalan number, so output size dominates. Recursion depth is 2n, and working stack space is O(n).

subsets.name: Optimal (Include/Exclude Backtracking)
subsets.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Subsets asks for the power set: every possible subset of the given unique elements.

### Core intuition
For each element, make a binary decision: include it or skip it. After making that decision for every element, the current path is one complete subset.

### State meaning
index tells which element is being decided. path stores the current subset under construction. result stores copies of completed paths.

### Invariant
At recursive depth index, decisions have already been made for elements before index, and path reflects exactly those decisions. Elements from index onward are still undecided.

### Edge cases
An empty input has one subset: the empty subset. Because elements are unique, no duplicate-handling is needed in this basic version.

### Complexity
There are 2^N subsets, and copying each subset can cost up to O(N), so time is O(N * 2^N). Recursion stack and path use O(N), excluding output.

n-queens.name: Brute Force
n-queens.description: Build a correctness baseline for N-Queens by directly enumerating the candidate states allowed by the problem. This version prioritizes transparent control flow and exhaustive coverage over asymptotic efficiency.

n-queens.name: Optimal (Backtracking with bit-sets or sets)
n-queens.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
N-Queens asks us to place n queens on an n by n board so that no two queens attack each other horizontally, vertically, or diagonally.

### Core intuition
Place exactly one queen per row. For each row, try every column that is not already attacked. Track used columns and diagonals so each safety check is fast. If a placement leads to a dead end, remove it and try the next column.

### State meaning
row is the row currently being filled. cols stores occupied columns. posDiag stores occupied r + c diagonals. negDiag stores occupied r - c diagonals. board stores the current queen layout.

### Invariant
Before entering a row, all earlier rows contain safe queen placements, and the three sets exactly describe the attacked columns and diagonals from those queens.

### Edge cases
n = 1 has one solution. n = 2 and n = 3 have no solutions. The board must be restored during backtracking or later branches inherit old queens incorrectly.

### Complexity
The search is factorial-like, commonly described as O(N!) because each row has fewer safe column choices as queens accumulate. Board/output storage can be O(N^2) per solution.

rat-in-a-maze.name: Optimal (Recursive DFS with Visited Matrix)
rat-in-a-maze.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Rat in a Maze asks for every valid path from the top-left cell to the bottom-right cell of an N by N maze. A 1 means the cell is open, and a 0 means the cell is blocked. The answer is not just yes or no; we must collect all valid move strings that reach the destination.

### Core intuition
Treat the maze like a set of possible stories. From the current open cell, try one move, extend the path string, and continue. Before leaving a cell, mark it as visited so this particular story cannot circle back into itself. When that story is finished, unmark the cell so a different story can use it later.

### State meaning
r and c are the current cell. path is the sequence of moves taken so far. visited, or temporarily setting the maze cell to 0, says which cells are already in the current path. res stores every completed path.

### Invariant
At the start of each recursive call, path exactly describes how we reached cell (r, c), and every visited cell belongs to that same path. No cell is used twice inside one candidate path.

### Edge cases
If the start cell or destination cell is blocked, there are no paths. Direction order affects the order of returned strings, not which paths are valid. Always unmark on the way back, or later paths will be unfairly blocked.

### Complexity
In the worst case, the search may branch in several directions across many cells, so the catalog uses O(4^(N^2)). The visited matrix and recursion stack can use O(N^2) space.

word-search.name: Optimal (Matrix Backtracking)
word-search.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Word Search asks whether a given word can be formed by walking through adjacent cells in a character grid. Each step may move up, down, left, or right, and the same cell cannot be reused in one word path.

### Core intuition
Every cell that matches the first character can be the beginning. From there, run DFS for the next character. Temporarily mark the current cell so the path cannot reuse it, ask the four neighbors to match the next index, and restore the cell before returning.

### State meaning
r and c are the current grid coordinates. i is the index of the next character we must match in word. The temporary marker, often '#', means this cell is already used in the current path.

### Invariant
When dfs(r, c, i) is called, the previous i characters of the word have already been matched by the path that led here. The current call is responsible for matching word[i] at this cell.

### Edge cases
If the board is empty, return false. If the word is empty, many implementations return true. A cell may appear multiple times in different attempted paths, but never twice in the same path. Restoring the cell after DFS is mandatory.

### Complexity
There are N * M possible starting cells, and each search can branch up to four ways for word length L, so time is O(N * M * 4^L). The recursion depth is O(L).

print-1-to-n-using-recursion.name: Optimal (Backtracking / Induction)
print-1-to-n-using-recursion.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Print 1 to N using recursion asks us to output the numbers in increasing order without using a loop.

### Core intuition
To print increasing order, delay printing the current number until after the smaller problem has finished. print1ToN(n) first asks print1ToN(n - 1) to print 1 through n - 1, then it prints n.

### State meaning
n is the largest number the current call is responsible for. The call stack stores all postponed numbers waiting to be printed after the base case returns.

### Invariant
After print1ToN(k) finishes, the output contains exactly 1 through k in increasing order. The recursive call establishes the smaller prefix before k is printed.

### Edge cases
For n less than 1, print nothing. The order depends on where the print statement is placed: after recursion gives 1 to N, before recursion gives N to 1.

### Complexity
There are N calls, so time is O(N). The recursion stack also grows to O(N).

print-n-to-1-using-recursion.name: Optimal (Direct Recursion)
print-n-to-1-using-recursion.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Print N to 1 using recursion asks us to output numbers in decreasing order without using loops.

### Core intuition
This version prints before it recurses. If the current number is n, print n immediately, then let the recursive call handle n - 1 down to 1.

### State meaning
n is the current number to print. After printing n, the next call receives n - 1. The base case stops once n becomes less than 1.

### Invariant
When printNTo1(k) starts, it will print k first and then print every positive number below k in descending order.

### Edge cases
If n is 0 or negative, print nothing. The position of the print statement is the entire trick: before recursion means descending order.

### Complexity
The function makes N recursive calls, so time is O(N). Stack depth is O(N).

sum-of-first-n-numbers.name: Brute Force
sum-of-first-n-numbers.description: Build a correctness baseline for Sum of first N numbers by directly enumerating the candidate states allowed by the problem. This version prioritizes transparent control flow and exhaustive coverage over asymptotic efficiency.

sum-of-first-n-numbers.name: Optimal (Parametric Recursion)
sum-of-first-n-numbers.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Sum of first N numbers asks for 1 + 2 + 3 + ... + N, expressed through recursion instead of a loop or formula.

### Core intuition
The sum up to n is n plus the sum up to n - 1. The base case is sum(0) = 0, because adding no natural numbers contributes nothing.

### State meaning
n is the largest number still waiting to be added. Each recursive return gives the completed sum of the smaller prefix.

### Invariant
sumOfN(k) always returns the exact value 1 + 2 + ... + k. It proves this by trusting sumOfN(k - 1) and adding k on top.

### Edge cases
For n = 0, return 0. For negative input, most learning versions either avoid it or add a guard. Large n can overflow fixed-size integer types or exceed recursion depth.

### Complexity
There are N calls and constant work per call, so time is O(N). The recursion stack is O(N).

factorial-of-n-numbers.name: Optimal (Functional Recursion)
factorial-of-n-numbers.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Factorial of N asks for N!, the product N * (N - 1) * ... * 1. It is a classic example of functional recursion.

### Core intuition
n! is n multiplied by (n - 1)!. The base case is 0! = 1, because 1 is the neutral value for multiplication and keeps the recurrence correct.

### State meaning
n is the current multiplier waiting to be applied. The returned value from factorial(n - 1) is the complete product below n.

### Invariant
factorial(k) returns the product of all positive integers from 1 through k. Each call preserves that promise by multiplying k with the correct product below it.

### Edge cases
0! and 1! both return 1. Negative factorial is not defined for this beginner algorithm. Factorials grow very quickly, so fixed-width integer languages can overflow even for moderate n.

### Complexity
The recursion makes N calls, so time is O(N). The call stack uses O(N) space.

reverse-an-array.name: Brute Force
reverse-an-array.description: Build a correctness baseline for Reverse an array by directly enumerating the candidate states allowed by the problem. This version prioritizes transparent control flow and exhaustive coverage over asymptotic efficiency.

reverse-an-array.name: Better
reverse-an-array.description: Reduce repeated work in Reverse an array with the intermediate structure or cached state used by the standard better approach. It preserves the brute-force reasoning while improving one major time or space bottleneck.

reverse-an-array.name: Optimal (Two Pointers Recursion)
reverse-an-array.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Reverse an array using recursion asks us to rearrange the array in-place so the first element becomes last, the second becomes second-last, and so on, without using a loop.

### Core intuition
Swap the two outside elements, then recursively reverse the smaller inside section. Once the left pointer meets or crosses the right pointer, every pair has been handled.

### State meaning
l points to the current left boundary and r points to the current right boundary. The section outside l and r is already reversed correctly. The section inside still needs work.

### Invariant
Before each recursive call, all elements outside the current [l, r] window are already in their final reversed positions. The call only needs to fix the remaining middle.

### Edge cases
An empty array or one-element array is already reversed. For even length, the pointers cross. For odd length, they meet at the middle element, which stays in place.

### Complexity
Each pair is swapped once, so time is O(N). The recursion stack can grow to O(N), though only O(N/2) meaningful swap calls occur.

check-if-a-string-is-palindrome-or-not.name: Brute Force
check-if-a-string-is-palindrome-or-not.description: Build a correctness baseline for Check if a string is palindrome or not by directly enumerating the candidate states allowed by the problem. This version prioritizes transparent control flow and exhaustive coverage over asymptotic efficiency.

check-if-a-string-is-palindrome-or-not.name: Optimal (Recursive Comparison)
check-if-a-string-is-palindrome-or-not.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Check if a string is palindrome or not asks whether the string reads the same from the front and the back, using recursion.

### Core intuition
Compare the outside characters first. If they differ, the answer is immediately false. If they match, the only remaining question is whether the inside substring is also a palindrome, so recurse with the next mirrored pair.

### State meaning
i is the offset from the left. The matching right index is n - i - 1. Every pair outside i has already been checked and confirmed equal.

### Invariant
Before checking index i, all mirrored pairs before it have matched. If s[i] equals s[n - i - 1], the palindrome question safely shrinks inward.

### Edge cases
Empty strings and one-character strings are palindromes. Case sensitivity and spaces depend on the problem statement; this catalog version compares characters exactly as given.

### Complexity
At most N/2 pairs are compared, so time is O(N). The recursion stack is O(N) in the simple recursive version.

fibonacci-number.name: Optimal (Multiple Recursion)
fibonacci-number.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Fibonacci Number asks for the N-th value in the sequence where F(0) = 0, F(1) = 1, and every later value is the sum of the two previous values.

### Core intuition
The direct recursive idea mirrors the definition: fib(n) = fib(n - 1) + fib(n - 2). It is excellent for learning recursion trees, but it repeats work heavily. For production, memoization or bottom-up DP is usually preferred.

### State meaning
n is the Fibonacci index requested by the current call. Calls with n <= 1 return immediately. Larger calls split into the two previous indices and add their answers.

### Invariant
fib(k) returns the correct k-th Fibonacci number. For k greater than 1, it relies on the correctness of fib(k - 1) and fib(k - 2).

### Edge cases
n = 0 returns 0 and n = 1 returns 1. Negative input is outside the usual definition for this beginner version. The unoptimized recursion becomes slow quickly because the same subproblems are recomputed many times.

### Complexity
The direct recursion branches into two calls repeatedly, so time is O(2^N). The deepest call chain is O(N), so stack space is O(N).

generate-all-binary-strings.name: Optimal (Recursive Generation)
generate-all-binary-strings.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Generate Binary Strings asks us to print or return every binary string of length N that does not contain consecutive 1s.

### Core intuition
At each position, placing 0 is always safe. Placing 1 is safe only when the previous character was not 1. This turns generation into a guarded recursion: try every allowed next character and stop when the string reaches length N.

### State meaning
remaining tells how many positions are still empty. s is the prefix built so far. last remembers the previous character so the algorithm can reject a second 1 in a row.

### Invariant
Every prefix passed into recursion already satisfies the no-consecutive-1s rule. The next choice must preserve that rule before the recursion continues.

### Edge cases
For N = 0, the only complete string is the empty string. For N = 1, both 0 and 1 are valid. Output order depends on whether the algorithm tries 0 before 1.

### Complexity
The output count follows a Fibonacci-like growth and is bounded by O(2^N). The recursion depth is O(N), excluding the space needed to store or print all generated strings.

combination-sum-i.name: Optimal (The 'Infinite Supply' Pick/Don't Pick)
combination-sum-i.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Combination Sum asks for every unique combination of candidate numbers that adds exactly to the target. Each candidate may be used unlimited times, so choosing a number does not consume it forever.

### Core intuition
At each candidate index, decide whether to take the current number or move past it. If you take it, stay at the same index because reuse is allowed. If you skip it, move to the next index so future combinations do not come back and reorder the same choices.

### State meaning
index chooses which candidate is currently available. path stores the numbers selected so far. total or remaining target tells how close the path is to the target. result stores copied paths once the target is reached.

### Invariant
Every recursive call represents combinations built from candidates at index or later. The path sum plus the remaining target always equals the original target.

### Edge cases
If remaining target becomes 0, save a copy of the path. If it becomes negative or the index runs out, stop that branch. Sorting is optional for the basic logic but helps prune branches early.

### Complexity
The search tree depends on the target and candidate values; the catalog describes it as O(2^T * K), where K is the average combination length copied into the answer. Stack depth can grow to target divided by the smallest candidate.

combination-sum-ii.name: Optimal (The 'Unique Loop' Strategy)
combination-sum-ii.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Combination Sum II asks for unique combinations that add to the target, but each input element may be used at most once. The input can contain duplicate values, and duplicate answer lists must not appear.

### Core intuition
Sort the array first. Then build combinations with a loop from the current start index. After choosing candidates[i], recurse from i + 1 because that exact element is used. If the same value appears again at the same recursion depth, skip it so it does not create the same combination in the same slot.

### State meaning
start is the first index allowed for the current slot. path stores chosen numbers. remaining is the target left to fill. The duplicate-skip condition i > start and candidates[i] == candidates[i - 1] means this value has already been tried for this slot.

### Invariant
At each recursion level, path is sorted by original index order, uses each element at most once, and contains no duplicate choice for the current slot.

### Edge cases
If remaining becomes 0, save the path. If the current sorted value is greater than remaining, break the loop because later values are even larger. Duplicates should be skipped only within the same level, not across different depths.

### Complexity
The worst-case number of subsets is O(2^N), and copying each answer costs up to K. Stack depth is O(N).

subset-sum-i.name: Optimal (Recursion)
subset-sum-i.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Subset Sum I asks for the sum of every possible subset of the input array. Unlike the yes-or-no subset-sum problem, here we collect all totals.

### Core intuition
Every element has two futures: include it in the current sum or leave it out. After making that decision for all elements, the current sum is one completed subset sum.

### State meaning
idx is the element currently being decided. currentSum is the sum of included elements so far. result stores one sum for every root-to-leaf decision path.

### Invariant
At recursion index idx, all elements before idx have already been either included or skipped, and currentSum exactly reflects those decisions.

### Edge cases
An empty array has one subset sum: 0. Negative numbers still work because the algorithm explores all include-skip choices. Sorting the final sums is a presentation choice, not part of generating them.

### Complexity
There are 2^N subsets, so time is O(2^N), plus sorting if the output is sorted. Recursion depth is O(N), excluding the output list.

palindrome-partitioning.name: Optimal (Recursive Pivot-and-Check)
palindrome-partitioning.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Palindrome Partitioning asks for every way to cut a string into pieces so that every piece is a palindrome.

### Core intuition
Stand at an index and try every possible next slice starting there. If the slice is a palindrome, accept it into the path and recurse from the character after the slice. If it is not a palindrome, that cut cannot start a valid partition, so skip it.

### State meaning
idx is the first uncut character. path stores palindrome slices already chosen. The palindrome check validates s[idx..end] before that slice is allowed into the path. result stores completed partitions.

### Invariant
At every recursive call, every string already in path is a palindrome, and their concatenation equals s[0..idx-1]. The remaining suffix s[idx..] still needs to be partitioned.

### Edge cases
A one-character slice is always a palindrome. If idx reaches the string length, copy the current path. Rechecking palindromes repeatedly is simple but can be optimized by precomputing palindrome ranges.

### Complexity
There can be up to 2^(N-1) cut patterns, and palindrome checking can add extra work, so the catalog uses O(N * 2^N). Stack depth is O(N), excluding output.

word-break.name: Optimal (Backtracking with Memo)
word-break.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Word Break asks whether a string can be segmented into dictionary words. We only need to know if at least one valid segmentation exists.

### Core intuition
At each start index, try every dictionary word that matches the string from that position. If a word fits, recursively ask whether the remaining suffix can be segmented. Memoize failed and successful start positions so the same suffix is not solved again and again.

### State meaning
start is the first character of the suffix still unsolved. memo[start] stores whether s[start..] can be broken into words. The dictionary supplies candidate jumps forward.

### Invariant
dp(start) returns true exactly when the suffix beginning at start can be segmented into dictionary words. Once memoized, that truth never changes.

### Edge cases
If start reaches the string length, the entire string has been segmented and the answer is true. Overlapping words are normal. Without memoization, repeated suffixes can make the recursion much slower.

### Complexity
With memoization, there are O(N) start positions. Trying dictionary words and comparing prefixes gives the catalog cost O(N * M * K), where M is dictionary size and K is average word length. Space is O(N).

sudoku-solver.name: Optimal (Brute-Force with Strategic Pruning)
sudoku-solver.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Sudoku Solver asks us to fill every empty cell in a 9 by 9 Sudoku board so that every row, every column, and every 3 by 3 box contains digits 1 through 9 without repetition.

### Core intuition
Find an empty cell, try every digit that is currently legal there, and recurse. If a later cell becomes impossible, undo the digit and try the next candidate. The board itself is the working path.

### State meaning
The board stores all committed choices so far. row and col identify the empty cell being tested. The safety check asks whether a digit already appears in the same row, same column, or same 3 by 3 box.

### Invariant
Before each recursive call, every filled cell on the board obeys Sudoku rules. The solver only places a digit when it keeps that invariant true.

### Edge cases
If there are no empty cells left, the board is solved. If an empty cell has no legal digit, return false so the previous call backtracks. The input is usually guaranteed to have one solution, but the backtracking idea can handle no-solution boards too.

### Complexity
In the theoretical worst case, each of many empty cells can try up to 9 digits, so the catalog uses O(9^(N*N)). The recursion stack is bounded by the number of cells.

validate-bst.name: Optimal (Recursive Range Boundaries)
validate-bst.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Validate Binary Search Tree is not asking whether each parent is bigger than its left child and smaller than its right child. That local check is too weak. A node deep inside the right subtree must still be greater than the original root, and a node deep inside the left subtree must still be smaller than the original root. The real question is: does every node obey the full range of promises inherited from all its ancestors?

### Core intuition
Every time we move down the tree, the allowed interval becomes tighter. At the root, any value is allowed. When we go left from value 10, every node in that left subtree must be less than 10. When we then go right from value 5, the allowed range becomes greater than 5 but still less than 10. That range memory is the part beginners often miss.

### Why boundary recursion works
The recursive function carries two guards: low and high. A node is valid only when low < node.val < high. If it passes, the left child inherits the same low but gets high = node.val. The right child inherits low = node.val but keeps the same high. Null children are valid because an empty subtree cannot break the BST property.

### Invariant
Before validating a node, low and high represent every ancestor decision that constrains it. If a node passes those boundaries, all ancestors remain satisfied. If it fails, the tree is invalid immediately.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not allow duplicates unless the problem statement explicitly defines a duplicate rule. The standard LeetCode version uses strict inequality. Also do not compare only with direct children; the classic failing tree is root 5 with right child 7 and 7's left child 4. Locally 4 is less than 7, but globally it belongs to the right side of 5 and is invalid.

### Complexity
Each node is visited once, so time is O(N). The recursion stack stores one path from root to leaf, so space is O(H).

kth-smallest-element-in-a-bst.name: Optimal (In-order Traversal Property)
kth-smallest-element-in-a-bst.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Kth Smallest Element in a BST asks for the value that would appear in position k if all BST values were sorted increasingly. The tree is not given as an array, but the BST property gives us a built-in sorted order: inorder traversal visits left subtree, then node, then right subtree.

### Core intuition
In a BST, every value in the left subtree is smaller than the current node, and every value in the right subtree is larger. So if we walk left-root-right, we meet values exactly the way a sorted list would present them. The kth node visited during this walk is the answer.

### Why the iterative stack version is useful
A recursive inorder traversal could collect all values, then return values[k - 1]. That is easy but wasteful. The optimal approach uses a stack to simulate recursion and stops as soon as k reaches zero. We first push all left ancestors, because the smallest unvisited value always waits at the top of that left chain. After popping a node, we count it, then move to its right subtree and again dive left.

### Invariant
The stack contains nodes whose left side has already been prepared but whose own value has not yet been counted. Whenever we pop, that popped node is the next smallest unvisited value in the entire tree.

### Mistakes to avoid
Remember that k is usually 1-based, so the first popped node is k = 1. Do not traverse the right subtree before counting the node. Also do not assume a balanced tree; stack space depends on height, not always log N.

### Complexity
The algorithm walks down height H and pops only K nodes before stopping, so time is O(H + K). The stack stores at most one root-to-leaf path, so space is O(H).

delete-node-in-a-bst.name: Optimal (Recursive Structural Realignment)
delete-node-in-a-bst.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Delete Node in a BST asks us to remove one key while keeping the remaining tree a valid BST. Searching for the key is straightforward: smaller keys live left, larger keys live right. The real work starts when the node is found, because deleting a tree node can disconnect one or two subtrees.

### Core intuition
There are three cases. If the node is a leaf, return null to its parent. If the node has one child, return that child so it moves up into the deleted node's place. If the node has two children, we need a replacement value that keeps order on both sides. The usual replacement is the inorder successor: the smallest value in the right subtree.

### Why the successor is safe
The successor is greater than everything in the left subtree because it lives in the right subtree. It is also the smallest value from that right side, so after copying it into the deleted node, the right subtree remains valid once we remove the successor from its old position. That successor has no left child, because if it did, it would not be the smallest.

### Invariant
Every recursive call returns the root of the corrected subtree after deletion. Parent links are repaired by assignments like root.left = deleteNode(root.left, key). This is why the recursive return value matters.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not simply drop a two-child node. Do not copy the successor value and forget to delete the successor duplicate from the right subtree. Also be careful to return the new subtree root in all cases, especially when deleting the original root.

### Complexity
Searching plus successor lookup follows tree height, so time is O(H). Recursive stack space is also O(H), where H can be N in a skewed tree.

ceil-in-a-bst.name: Optimal (Pruned Vertical Scan)
ceil-in-a-bst.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Ceil in BST asks for the smallest value in the tree that is greater than or equal to a target key. If key = 11 and the tree contains 12 and 14, the ceil is 12, not 14, because 12 is the tighter upper bound. If the exact key exists, the ceil is the key itself.

### Core intuition
At each BST node, ask whether the current value can serve as an answer. If node.val is smaller than key, it is too small to be the ceil, and every value in its left subtree is even smaller, so we must go right. If node.val is greater than key, it is a valid candidate, but there might be a smaller valid candidate in the left subtree, so we save it and go left.

### Why this is a pruned search
We never explore both sides. The BST property lets us discard one entire subtree at every move. Candidate saving is the important detail: when we move left from a valid larger node, we are trying to improve the answer, not forgetting that the current node already works.

### Invariant
ceil always stores the best valid upper bound seen so far: a value >= key, and the smallest among such values encountered on the search path. Any discarded subtree is proven unable to contain a better answer.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not update ceil when node.val < key; that value is below the target. Do not go right after finding a larger candidate, because right side only gets larger and cannot make the ceil tighter. Also decide what to return when no ceil exists; this catalog uses -1.

### Complexity
The search follows one vertical path, so time is O(H). The iterative version keeps only root and ceil, so extra space is O(1).

floor-in-a-bst.name: Optimal (Pruned Vertical Scan)
floor-in-a-bst.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Floor in BST asks for the largest value in the tree that is less than or equal to a target key. It is the mirror of ceil. If key = 11 and the tree contains 8, 10, and 12, the floor is 10 because it is the closest value that does not cross above 11.

### Core intuition
At each node, decide whether the current value is usable. If node.val is greater than key, it is too large, and every value in its right subtree is even larger, so go left. If node.val is less than key, it is a valid candidate, but there might be a larger valid candidate in the right subtree, so save it and go right. If node.val equals key, return immediately because no floor can be better than exact equality.

### Why this avoids full traversal
A sorted array floor search would use binary search. A BST gives the same idea through branches. Every move discards half-like structure: not necessarily half by size, but an entire impossible region by value.

### Invariant
floor stores the best valid lower bound seen so far: a value <= key, and the largest among such values encountered on the path. The algorithm moves only toward regions that could improve that stored value.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not save values greater than key. Do not move left after saving a smaller candidate, because left side gets even smaller and cannot improve the floor. Also remember that no valid floor may exist if all tree values are greater than key; this implementation returns -1.

### Complexity
The algorithm follows one path from root toward a leaf, so time is O(H). It uses O(1) auxiliary space in the iterative form.

inorder-successor-predecessor-in-bst.name: Better
inorder-successor-predecessor-in-bst.description: Reduce repeated work in BST Successor / Predecessor with the intermediate structure or cached state used by the standard better approach. It preserves the brute-force reasoning while improving one major time or space bottleneck.

inorder-successor-predecessor-in-bst.name: Optimal (Standard Search Logic)
inorder-successor-predecessor-in-bst.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
BST Successor / Predecessor asks for the value immediately after or immediately before a target in inorder sorted order. The successor is the next larger node. The predecessor is the next smaller node. We want this without flattening the whole tree into an array.

### Core intuition
For successor, any node larger than the target could be an answer, but we want the smallest such node. During normal BST search, when current.val > target, current is a possible successor, so save it and move left to find a tighter larger value. When current.val <= target, current and its left subtree are not large enough, so move right. Predecessor mirrors this: save current when current.val < target, then move right to get closer.

### Two ways to think about successor
If you already have the target node and it has a right subtree, the successor is the leftmost node of that right subtree. The iterative search method in this catalog works more generally by tracking the best ancestor candidate while walking from the root.

### Invariant
successor always stores the smallest saved value that is still greater than target among nodes already visited. predecessor stores the largest saved value that is still smaller than target. Each move tries to tighten that saved boundary.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not return the target itself as successor or predecessor. Equality should move right for successor and left for predecessor depending on your implementation. Also be clear whether the input is a node reference or just a value; the comparison idea is the same, but equality handling must match the signature.

### Complexity
The search touches one root-to-leaf path, so time is O(H). The iterative version uses O(1) extra space.

recover-bst.name: Optimal (One-Pass Inorder)
recover-bst.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Recover BST says two nodes in a BST were swapped by mistake, and we must restore the BST without changing its shape. That means we should swap the values back, not rebuild or rotate the tree. The structure stays exactly where it is; only the two wrong labels are corrected.

### Core intuition
An inorder traversal of a valid BST should produce a strictly increasing sequence. If two nodes are swapped, that sorted sequence will show one or two drops where prev.val > curr.val. Those drops identify the misplaced values. The first time a drop appears, prev is too large and should be remembered as first. Every time a drop appears, curr is too small and should be remembered as second. At the end, swap first.val and second.val.

### Adjacent versus non-adjacent swaps
If adjacent inorder values are swapped, like [1, 3, 2, 4], there is one drop: 3 > 2. first = 3 and second = 2. If non-adjacent values are swapped, like [1, 6, 3, 4, 5, 2, 7], there are two drops: 6 > 3 and 5 > 2. The first wrong high value is 6, and the final wrong low value is 2.

### Invariant
prev always holds the previously visited inorder node. first stores the first node that was too large. second stores the latest node that was too small. After the traversal, those two values are the only correction needed.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not stop after the first inversion unless you are certain the swapped nodes are adjacent. Do not alter links or tree shape. Also use node references, not just numeric values, because the final operation must swap the actual nodes' values.

### Complexity
The traversal visits all N nodes, so time is O(N). Recursive inorder uses O(H) stack space. Morris traversal can reduce space to O(1), but this catalog uses the clearer one-pass inorder idea.

largest-bst-in-binary-tree.name: Optimal (Post-order Info Bubbling)
largest-bst-in-binary-tree.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Largest BST in Binary Tree gives a normal binary tree, not necessarily a BST, and asks for the size of the biggest subtree that is a valid BST. A subtree must include a node and all descendants below it. We are not allowed to cherry-pick scattered nodes that happen to form a BST shape.

### Core intuition
To know whether a node's subtree is a BST, the parent needs four facts from each child: is the child subtree valid, how many nodes it has, what its minimum value is, and what its maximum value is. This is why the clean solution is postorder: solve left, solve right, then decide the current node.

### Why min and max matter
A node forms a BST root only when left is a BST, right is a BST, left.max < node.val, and node.val < right.min. The child roots alone are not enough. A bad value could be hidden deeper inside a child subtree. Passing min and max summarizes the entire subtree boundary.

### What to return upward
For a valid subtree, return valid = true, size = left.size + right.size + 1, min = minimum of left.min and node.val, max = maximum of right.max and node.val. For an invalid subtree, return valid = false so ancestors cannot accidentally include it. Keep a global best size along the way.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not check only direct children. Do not return a fake min/max from invalid subtrees that lets a parent pass. Also remember that an empty child is a valid BST of size 0 with min = infinity and max = -infinity, which makes leaf nodes work naturally.

### Complexity
Every node contributes one postorder calculation, so time is O(N). The recursion stack is O(H).

assign-cookies.name: Optimal (Sort & Two Pointers)
assign-cookies.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Assign Cookies gives greed factors for children and sizes for cookies. A child is content only if the assigned cookie size is at least that child's greed. Each cookie can be used once, and each child can receive at most one cookie. The goal is not to use every cookie; the goal is to maximize the number of content children.

### Core intuition
Sort both lists. Then try to satisfy the least greedy remaining child using the smallest remaining cookie that can work. This feels almost too simple, but it is powerful because a small child does not need a large cookie. Saving large cookies for harder-to-satisfy children preserves options.

### Why the greedy choice is safe
Suppose the current smallest cookie can satisfy the current least greedy child. Giving that cookie to a greedier child would not help more, because the least greedy child still needs some cookie later. Giving a larger cookie to the least greedy child would waste capacity. So the smallest working cookie is the best local match. If the current cookie is too small for the least greedy child, it cannot satisfy anyone else either, so discard it.

### Invariant
Before each step, all children before the child pointer are already content, and all cookies before the cookie pointer have either been used or proven too small. The remaining problem has the same shape as the original, only smaller.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not pair in input order; sorting is the reason the discard rule is safe. Do not increment the child pointer when a cookie is too small. Also remember the answer is the child pointer count, not the number of cookies examined.

### Complexity
Sorting dominates at O(G log G + S log S), where G is number of children and S is number of cookies. The matching scan is linear, and extra pointer space is O(1).

jump-game.name: Optimal (Greedy Goal Tracking)
jump-game.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Jump Game gives an array where nums[i] is the farthest jump length available from index i. Starting at index 0, we need to decide whether it is possible to reach the last index. The problem asks for reachability, not the minimum number of jumps.

### Core intuition
The catalog approach scans backward with a moving goal. At first, the goal is the final index. If some earlier index can jump to that goal or beyond it, then reaching that earlier index is just as good as reaching the old goal, because from there we can finish. So the goal moves left. If the goal eventually becomes 0, the start can reach the end.

### Why backward goal tracking is clean
Forward scanning often tracks farthestReach, which is also valid. Backward scanning flips the story: instead of asking how far we can go from the start, ask which earlier positions can guarantee arrival at the current safe zone. This avoids exploring choices. A position either reaches the goal or it does not.

### Invariant
goal is always an index from which the last index is known to be reachable. During the scan, if i + nums[i] >= goal, then index i also becomes a known-good position and goal updates to i. If not, i cannot currently join the safe chain.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not simulate every possible jump path; that becomes unnecessary branching. Do not require a jump to land exactly on goal; jumping beyond it is also fine. Also remember that zeros are only fatal if they block every path crossing them.

### Complexity
The backward scan checks each index once, so time is O(N). Only the goal variable is stored, so space is O(1).

gas-station.name: Optimal (One-Pass Greedy)
gas-station.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Gas Station gives two circular arrays. gas[i] is the fuel collected at station i, and cost[i] is the fuel needed to drive from station i to the next station. We need one starting index that lets the car complete the full circle, or -1 if no such start exists. The tank begins empty, but it can carry any amount of fuel.

### Core intuition
First check the whole trip. If total gas is less than total cost, no clever start can create fuel from nowhere, so the answer is -1. If total gas is enough, then the greedy scan can find the start. Keep a running tank from the current candidate start. If the tank ever goes negative at station i, then the candidate start and every station between that start and i are impossible starting points. The next possible start is i + 1.

### Why skipping a whole failed block is safe
Suppose we started at A and ran out at D. Every station between A and D had some nonnegative tank when reached from A; if any middle station B could complete the segment to D, then starting from A would also have survived because A reached B with extra or equal help. Since A failed before leaving the block, all those starts fail too. That is the greedy leap.

### Invariant
start is the first station after the most recent failed segment. tank is the net fuel from start through the current station. totalBalance records whether the full circle is possible at all.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not return a start just because the local tank never failed before checking total gas. Total gas must cover total cost. Do not reset start on tank equal to zero; zero still means the car exactly reaches the next station.

### Complexity
The algorithm scans the arrays once, so time is O(N). It stores only total balance, current tank, and start, so space is O(1).

n-meetings-in-one-room.name: Optimal (Sort by End Time)
n-meetings-in-one-room.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
N Meetings in one room asks for the maximum number of non-overlapping meetings that can be held in one room. Each meeting has a start time and an end time. The room can host only one meeting at a time, so accepting one meeting may block others.

### Core intuition
To maximize the number of meetings, choose the meeting that finishes earliest among the available options. The earlier a meeting ends, the more timeline remains for future meetings. A long meeting that starts early can look tempting, but if it ends late, it may swallow several shorter meetings.

### Why sorting by end time works
After sorting meetings by end time, the first compatible meeting is always the safest pick. If another meeting ends later, choosing it cannot create more room before that later end time. The earliest ending meeting leaves at least as much future space as any other currently selectable meeting.

### Invariant
lastEnd is the finish time of the most recently accepted meeting. Every accepted meeting is compatible with the previous accepted meeting. Because meetings are processed by increasing end time, whenever we accept one, it is the earliest finishing compatible choice remaining.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not sort by start time; that can choose a meeting that begins early but ends too late. Be clear about the overlap rule in the platform. This catalog uses start > lastEnd, so a meeting starting exactly when the previous one ends is not accepted under that strict rule.

### Complexity
Sorting costs O(N log N). The selection pass is O(N). Storing meeting tuples takes O(N) space.

job-sequencing-problem.name: Optimal (Sort by Profit + Deadline Slots)
job-sequencing-problem.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Job Sequencing gives jobs with deadlines and profits. Each job takes exactly one unit of time, and only one job can be done in each time slot. A job earns profit only if it finishes on or before its deadline. The goal is to schedule jobs to maximize total profit.

### Core intuition
Because every job takes the same amount of time, profit is the main priority. Sort jobs from highest profit to lowest. For each job, place it as late as possible before its deadline. Scheduling late preserves earlier slots for jobs with tighter deadlines.

### Why latest possible slot is important
If a job has deadline 4 and slot 2 is free, placing it at slot 2 may block another job that only has deadline 2. Placing the flexible job at slot 4 keeps early slots available. This is the small greedy detail that makes the profit-first order work better.

### Invariant
After each job is considered, every scheduled job occupies a unique slot no later than its deadline. Higher-profit jobs have been given priority, and each accepted job was placed in the latest free slot that still keeps it valid.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not schedule high-profit jobs at the earliest free slot. Do not treat deadlines as zero-based if your slot array is one-based. Also remember that some jobs will be skipped if all slots before their deadline are full.

### Complexity
Sorting jobs costs O(N log N). The simple slot search can take O(N * maxDeadline). The slot array uses O(maxDeadline) space.

lemonade-change.name: Optimal (Greedy Choice)
lemonade-change.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Lemonade Change gives customers paying with 5, 10, or 20 dollar bills for lemonade that costs 5 dollars. We start with no cash. For each customer in order, we must give correct change immediately. The question is whether every customer can be served.

### Core intuition
A 5 dollar bill is the most precious bill because it is needed for both 10 dollar customers and 20 dollar customers. A 10 dollar bill is useful only for giving change to a 20. So when a 20 arrives, the best change is usually one 10 plus one 5, because that saves two extra 5 bills for future customers.

### Why the greedy change choice works
For a 20 dollar bill, the customer needs 15 in change. If we have a 10 and a 5, using them consumes one flexible 5 and one less flexible 10. If instead we use three 5 bills, we destroy the bills needed for future 10 dollar customers. Saving 5s is the greedy priority.

### Invariant
After each customer, five and ten store the exact bills available in the drawer. If the algorithm returns false, it is because the current customer cannot receive valid change from the bills actually available at that moment.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not sort the bills; customer order is the entire problem. Do not give three 5s for a 20 when a 10 plus 5 is available. Also remember that 20 dollar bills never help make change in this problem, so we do not need to count them.

### Complexity
We process each bill once, so time is O(N). We track only two counters, so space is O(1).

valid-parenthesis-string.name: Optimal (Range of Possibility)
valid-parenthesis-string.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Valid Parenthesis String contains opening brackets, closing brackets, and stars. A star can act as an opening bracket, a closing bracket, or an empty string. We must decide whether there exists at least one interpretation that makes the whole string valid.

### Core intuition
Because each star has three possible roles, tracking one exact open count is too rigid. Instead, track a range. low is the minimum possible number of unmatched opening brackets after reading the prefix. high is the maximum possible number. If high ever drops below zero, even the most optimistic interpretation has too many closing brackets. At the end, low must be zero, meaning at least one interpretation can close everything.

### How characters affect the range
An opening bracket increases both low and high. A closing bracket decreases both. A star can be a closing bracket or empty or opening bracket, so it decreases low by one and increases high by one. Since open counts cannot be negative in a valid prefix, clamp low back to zero when it dips below zero.

### Invariant
After each character, every valid interpretation has an unmatched open count somewhere between low and high. The range represents all possibilities compactly without branching into every star choice.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not greedily decide each star permanently too early. A star that seems like empty now might need to become an opening bracket later. Also do not let low stay negative; negative minimum just means zero is possible.

### Complexity
The string is scanned once, so time is O(N). Only low and high are stored, so space is O(1).

jump-game-ii.name: Optimal (Greedy Range Expansion)
jump-game-ii.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Jump Game II asks for the minimum number of jumps needed to reach the last index. Unlike the earlier Jump Game, reaching the end is assumed possible in the common version. Now the challenge is not whether we can arrive, but how few jumps we need.

### Core intuition
Think of indices reachable with the same number of jumps as a layer. currentEnd marks the farthest index reachable using the current number of jumps. As we scan all indices inside that layer, farthest records the farthest place one more jump could reach. When the scan reaches currentEnd, the layer is finished, so we must spend one jump and move currentEnd to farthest.

### Why this is greedy BFS without a queue
A normal breadth-first search would explore all positions reachable in 1 jump, then all positions reachable in 2 jumps. The greedy range method compresses each BFS layer into two numbers: the current layer boundary and the next farthest boundary. We never need to store every edge.

### Invariant
Before crossing currentEnd, jumps is the number of jumps used to reach the current layer. farthest is the best boundary for the next layer based on all indices scanned so far in the current layer.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not increment jumps at every index. Increment only when i reaches currentEnd. Also stop the loop at length - 2, because once the last index is reachable, we do not need to jump from the last index.

### Complexity
One pass over the array gives O(N) time. The algorithm stores jumps, currentEnd, and farthest, so space is O(1).

candy.name: Optimal (Two-Pass Greedy Sweep)
candy.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Candy Distribution gives ratings for children in a line. Every child must receive at least one candy. If a child has a higher rating than an immediate neighbor, that child must receive more candy than that neighbor. We need the minimum total candies that satisfies both left and right neighbor rules.

### Core intuition
Each child is pulled by two local constraints. A child higher than the left neighbor needs more than the left neighbor. A child higher than the right neighbor needs more than the right neighbor. One left-to-right pass handles the left rule. One right-to-left pass handles the right rule. Taking the maximum preserves whichever side demands more.

### Why two passes are necessary
A single forward pass cannot see a descending slope properly. For ratings [5,4,3], the first child needs more than the second, and the second needs more than the third, but this is discovered from the right side. The backward pass repairs those descending requirements without breaking the forward ones.

### Invariant
After the forward pass, every increasing pair from left to right is valid. After the backward pass, every decreasing pair from left to right is valid too. Because we use max, no earlier valid candy count is reduced.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not give extra candy for equal ratings; the rule says higher rating, not greater than or equal. Do not overwrite a forward-pass value with a smaller backward value. Use max when fixing from the right.

### Complexity
Two linear passes give O(N) time. The candies array uses O(N) extra space.

fractional-knapsack.name: Optimal (Value/Weight Ratio)
fractional-knapsack.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Fractional Knapsack gives items with value and weight, plus a maximum capacity. Unlike 0-1 knapsack, we may take part of an item. The goal is to maximize total value inside the weight limit.

### Core intuition
If items can be split, the best item is not the one with the largest total value or the smallest weight. The best item is the one with the largest value per unit weight. Sort by value divided by weight, then take as much as possible from the densest remaining item.

### Why value density makes the greedy choice safe
When fractions are allowed, every unit of capacity can be filled independently. If one item gives 6 value per kg and another gives 4 value per kg, then spending a kg on the second while the first is still available loses value. So the densest available item should always be consumed first.

### Invariant
After processing the sorted prefix, the knapsack is filled with the best available value density choices so far. Remaining capacity should only be spent on items with equal or lower density.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not use this greedy method for 0-1 knapsack; it works because fractions are allowed. Do not sort by value alone. Also remember to add proportional value when the remaining capacity can hold only part of an item.

### Complexity
Sorting costs O(N log N). The fill pass is O(N). If sorting is in-place, auxiliary space is O(1) aside from the item list.

implement-trie.name: Optimal (Node Dictionary Architecture)
implement-trie.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Implement Trie asks us to build a prefix tree that supports insert, search, and startsWith. The data is a set of strings. A normal hash set can answer full-word search, but a trie also answers prefix questions naturally because words with the same prefix share the same path.

### Core intuition
Each node represents a point after reading some prefix. The root means we have read nothing. To insert cat, follow or create c, then a, then t. At the final node, mark isEndOfWord as true. That end marker matters because the path ca can exist as a prefix even if ca was never inserted as a complete word.

### How the operations differ
insert creates missing child nodes and marks the final node as a word. search follows children and returns true only if the final node is marked as a word. startsWith follows children and returns true as soon as the prefix path exists, even if the final node is not a word ending.

### Invariant
Every edge is labeled by one character, and every node corresponds to the prefix spelled from the root to that node. isEndOfWord tells whether that prefix was inserted as a complete word.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not make search return true just because the path exists. That would confuse app with apple after only apple was inserted. Do not recreate nodes when inserting a word that shares an existing prefix; reuse the path and branch only where needed.

### Complexity
Each operation touches one node per character, so time is O(L) for word or prefix length L. Space is O(T), where T is the number of unique trie nodes created across all inserted words.
implement-trie.timeComplexity: O(L) per word (L = length of word)

word-search-ii.name: Brute Force
word-search-ii.description: Build a correctness baseline for Word Search II by directly enumerating the candidate states allowed by the problem. This version prioritizes transparent control flow and exhaustive coverage over asymptotic efficiency.

word-search-ii.name: Optimal (Trie + DFS Backtracking)
word-search-ii.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Word Search II gives a character board and a list of target words. We must find every word that can be formed by walking adjacent cells horizontally or vertically, without reusing the same cell in one word. The challenge is that there may be many words, so checking each word with a separate DFS wastes a huge amount of repeated prefix work.

### Core intuition
Put all target words into a trie first. Then start DFS from each board cell while walking through the trie at the same time. If the board path spells o -> a, but the trie has no prefix oa, that path can stop immediately. The trie is a shared prefix filter for all words.

### Why trie plus backtracking is powerful
Backtracking explores board paths. The trie tells us whether the current path is still promising. A trie node may also store a complete word. When DFS lands on such a node, we add that word to the answer and clear it so the same word is not reported again from another path.

### Invariant
During a DFS call, the board path from the start cell to the current cell matches the trie node being held. Cells marked as visited cannot be reused in the same path. After recursion returns, the board cell is restored so other paths can use it.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not forget to unmark visited cells during backtracking. Do not continue DFS if the current character is missing from the trie children. Do not append the same word repeatedly; clear the stored word or use a set.

### Complexity
The trie stores O(T) characters across all words. DFS starts from each cell, but trie pruning cuts off paths that are not dictionary prefixes. A common upper bound is O(M * 4 * 3^(L - 1)) for board cells M and maximum word length L, plus trie space O(T).

implement-trie-ii.name: Optimal (Counter Nodes)
implement-trie-ii.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Implement Trie II asks us to support insert, countWordsEqualTo, countWordsStartingWith, and erase. Unlike a basic trie that only checks existence, this trie must track duplicate words and prefix frequencies.

### Core intuition
Each node represents a prefix. Two counters make the structure powerful: cntPrefix says how many inserted words pass through this node, and cntEnd says how many inserted words end exactly here. Insert increases prefix counts along the path and the end count at the final node. Erase reverses those counts for one existing word.

### Operation meaning
countWordsEqualTo walks the full word and returns cntEnd at the final node. countWordsStartingWith walks the prefix and returns cntPrefix at the prefix node. If a required child is missing, the count is 0.

### Invariant
After every insert or erase, each node's cntPrefix equals the number of stored words that have that node's prefix, and cntEnd equals the number of stored words exactly equal to that prefix.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not use a boolean end flag when duplicates are allowed. Do not decrement counts for erase unless the word exists, or the trie can go negative. Be consistent about whether the root stores counts; most implementations count after moving into each character node.

### Complexity
Each operation touches one node per character, so time is O(L) for word or prefix length L. Space is proportional to the number of created character nodes.

number-of-distinct-substrings-in-a-strin.name: Brute Force
number-of-distinct-substrings-in-a-strin.description: Build a correctness baseline for Distinct Substrings by directly enumerating the candidate states allowed by the problem. This version prioritizes transparent control flow and exhaustive coverage over asymptotic efficiency.

number-of-distinct-substrings-in-a-strin.name: Optimal (Trie Node Counting)
number-of-distinct-substrings-in-a-strin.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Distinct Substrings asks us to count how many different substrings a string has. In the Striver/Naukri version, the empty substring is usually included, so after counting all non-empty substrings we add 1.

### Core intuition
Every substring is a prefix of some suffix. So if we insert every suffix into a trie, each new trie node represents one new substring that has never appeared before. Counting new nodes while inserting suffixes gives the number of distinct non-empty substrings.

### Algorithm
Start every suffix at index i. Walk characters from i to the end. If the next character edge does not exist, create it and increment the count. If it already exists, that substring has been seen from an earlier suffix.

### Invariant
After processing suffixes starting before i, the trie contains every substring that begins at those earlier positions. New nodes created by suffix i are exactly the new substrings introduced by this suffix.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not insert only whole suffixes and count suffixes; substrings are represented by every prefix along each suffix path. Do not forget whether the platform wants the empty substring included. Also remember that this trie method is O(N squared), not a suffix automaton solution.

### Complexity
There are O(N squared) suffix-prefix steps, so time is O(N squared). In the worst case the trie can also contain O(N squared) nodes.

maximum-xor-of-two-numbers-in-an-array.name: Optimal (Bitwise Trie)
maximum-xor-of-two-numbers-in-an-array.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Max XOR of Two Numbers asks for the largest value of nums[i] XOR nums[j] using any two numbers from the array.

### Core intuition
XOR becomes large when high bits differ. A binary trie stores all numbers bit by bit from the most significant bit to the least significant bit. For a number x, we walk the trie and at every bit try to move to the opposite bit, because that makes the current XOR bit 1. If the opposite bit does not exist, we follow the same bit.

### Algorithm
Insert every number into a binary trie. Then query each number against the trie, greedily choosing opposite bits from high to low. The constructed XOR value is the best partner available for that number. The maximum over all queries is the answer.

### Invariant
At each bit position during a query, choosing an opposite high bit is always better than saving a possible improvement for lower bits, because higher bits carry larger value.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not traverse from low bit to high bit; that can waste the most important decisions. Use enough bits for the input constraints, often 30 or 31 for non-negative integers. Also do not confuse the partner number with the XOR value; the answer asks for the value.

### Complexity
Each number is inserted and queried across a fixed bit width, so time is O(N * B) and space is O(N * B), where B is usually 31. With fixed B this is effectively linear.

quick-sort.name: Optimal (Lomuto Partitioning)
quick-sort.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Quick Sort asks us to sort an array by repeatedly choosing a pivot, partitioning the current segment around it, and sorting the two remaining sides.

### Core intuition
The partition step is the heart. Pick a pivot, commonly the last element. Walk through the segment and move every smaller element into the left region. At the end, swap the pivot into the boundary position. The pivot is now final, so only the left and right subsegments still need sorting.

### State meaning
low and high mark the current segment. pivot is arr[high]. i marks the next position where a smaller-than-pivot value should go. j scans the segment looking for such values.

### Invariant
During partition, all elements before i are less than the pivot, and elements from i to j - 1 are greater than or equal to the pivot. After the final swap, the pivot sits in its final sorted position.

### Edge cases
Empty and one-element segments are already sorted. Already sorted data can cause O(N^2) behavior with a poor pivot choice. Randomized or median-like pivot choices reduce that risk in production.

### Complexity
Average time is O(N log N), because partition work is linear across each recursion level. Worst-case time is O(N^2). Average recursion stack space is O(log N).

merge-sort.name: Optimal (Divide & Merge)
merge-sort.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Merge Sort asks us to sort an array by splitting it into smaller arrays, sorting those pieces, and merging sorted pieces back together.

### Core intuition
A single-element array is already sorted. So keep splitting until every piece has one element, then merge two sorted pieces by repeatedly taking the smaller front element. Unlike quick sort, merge sort does most of the ordering work while merging on the way back up.

### State meaning
mid splits the array into left and right halves. The recursive calls return sorted halves. During merge, i and j point to the current front of the left and right sorted halves, and result receives the next smallest value.

### Invariant
During merge, result is always sorted and contains the smallest values already consumed from the two halves. The unconsumed suffixes of left and right remain sorted.

### Edge cases
Empty and one-element arrays return immediately. Merge sort is stable when equal values are taken from the left half first. Array merge sort usually needs O(N) extra buffer space.

### Complexity
There are O(log N) split levels, and each level merges O(N) total elements, so time is O(N log N). Extra space is O(N) for array merging.

selection-sort.name: Standard Optimal
selection-sort.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Selection Sort asks us to sort an array by repeatedly selecting the smallest element from the unsorted part and placing it at the front.

### Core intuition
