Keep a boundary between sorted and unsorted. For position i, scan everything from i to the end, find the minimum value, and swap it into position i. After that, position i is final forever.

### State meaning
i is the first unsorted position. mini stores the index of the smallest value found during the scan. j walks through the unsorted suffix looking for a smaller value.

### Invariant
Before each outer iteration, positions before i contain the smallest i elements in sorted order. The algorithm only needs to choose the next smallest element for position i.

### Edge cases
Empty and one-element arrays need no swaps. Selection sort performs the same comparison pattern even if the array is already sorted. It uses very little extra memory but is not usually stable when implemented with swaps.

### Complexity
The nested scans perform O(N^2) comparisons in best, average, and worst cases. Space is O(1) because sorting happens in-place.

bubble-sort.name: Optimal (With Early Exit)
bubble-sort.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Bubble Sort asks us to sort an array by repeatedly comparing adjacent elements and swapping them when they are in the wrong order.

### Core intuition
One full pass pushes the largest unsorted value to the end, because large values keep swapping right when they meet smaller neighbors. After each pass, the sorted suffix grows by one. If a pass makes no swaps, the array is already sorted and the algorithm can stop early.

### State meaning
i marks the last index of the unsorted section for the current pass. j scans adjacent pairs from the start up to i. didSwap records whether the pass changed anything.

### Invariant
After each outer pass, every position after i contains the largest values in final sorted order. The next pass only needs to bubble within the remaining prefix.

### Edge cases
Empty and one-element arrays are already sorted. Already sorted arrays finish in O(N) with the early-exit flag. Reverse-sorted arrays cause the maximum number of swaps.

### Complexity
Best case with early exit is O(N). Average and worst cases are O(N^2). Extra space is O(1).

insertion-sort.name: Standard Optimal
insertion-sort.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Insertion Sort asks us to sort an array by growing a sorted prefix one element at a time. Each new element is inserted into the correct place among the elements already seen.

### Core intuition
Think of sorting cards in your hand. The left side is already sorted. Pick the next card from the right, compare it with the cards before it, and keep moving it left until it reaches its correct position.

### State meaning
i is the index of the new element being inserted into the sorted prefix. j walks left while adjacent elements are out of order. The prefix before i is sorted before insertion begins, and it becomes sorted again after the inner shifting/swapping finishes.

### Invariant
Before each outer iteration, arr[0..i-1] is sorted. After inserting arr[i] into that prefix, arr[0..i] is sorted.

### Edge cases
Empty and one-element arrays are already sorted. Already sorted arrays run in O(N) because each new element checks only one neighbor. Reverse-sorted arrays do the maximum shifting.

### Complexity
Best case is O(N) for already sorted input. Average and worst cases are O(N^2). Space is O(1) because sorting happens in-place.

valid-parentheses.name: Optimal (Stack Matching)
valid-parentheses.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Valid Parentheses asks whether every opening bracket is closed by the correct type of bracket and in the correct nested order.

### Core intuition
The most recent opening bracket must be the first one closed. That is exactly stack behavior. Push opening brackets. When a closing bracket appears, pop the latest opener and check whether the pair matches.

### State meaning
stack stores opening brackets that are waiting to be closed. mapping tells which opener belongs to each closer. The current character decides whether we push a new expectation or verify the latest one.

### Invariant
After processing each character, the stack contains exactly the opening brackets from the scanned prefix that have not been closed yet, in the order they must be closed.

### Edge cases
A closing bracket when the stack is empty is invalid. A mismatched pair like (] is invalid. After the scan, the stack must be empty; leftover openers mean something was never closed.

### Complexity
Each character is processed once, so time is O(N). The stack can hold O(N) open brackets in the worst case.

min-stack.name: Optimal (Two Stacks Strategy)
min-stack.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Min Stack asks us to design a stack that supports push, pop, top, and getMin in constant time.

### Core intuition
A normal stack knows only its top. To know the minimum instantly, keep a second stack that records the minimum value active at each moment. When a new value is less than or equal to the current minimum, push it onto the min stack too. When that value leaves, remove it from the min stack.

### State meaning
stack stores all values normally. minStack stores the minimum values that are still alive. The top of minStack is always the current minimum.

### Invariant
After every push or pop, minStack.top is the minimum of all values currently in stack. Duplicate minimums are stored so popping one copy does not erase another active copy.

### Edge cases
Push duplicates using less-than-or-equal, not only less-than. Pop must synchronize minStack only when the popped value equals the current minimum. Operations on an empty stack are usually avoided by problem constraints.

### Complexity
Every operation is O(1). Space is O(N) because the auxiliary min stack can grow with the main stack.

implement-queue-using-stacks.name: Optimal (Amortized Cost Transfer)
implement-queue-using-stacks.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Implement Queue using Stacks asks us to build FIFO behavior using only LIFO stack operations.

### Core intuition
Use one stack for incoming values and one stack for outgoing values. New items always enter input stack. When the queue needs to pop or peek and output stack is empty, pour every item from input into output. The reversal makes the oldest item land on top.

### State meaning
inStack holds newly pushed elements in arrival order but with the newest on top. outStack holds elements ready to leave, with the oldest on top. A transfer happens only when outStack is empty.

### Invariant
If outStack has elements, its top is the queue front. If outStack is empty, transferring all of inStack makes its top the queue front. Together, outStack followed by reversed inStack represents the whole queue.

### Edge cases
Do not transfer on every push; that would waste work. Only pour when outStack is empty. Empty checks must consider both stacks.

### Complexity
Each individual transfer can cost O(N), but each element moves from inStack to outStack at most once, so push, pop, and peek are O(1) amortized. Space is O(N).

next-greater-element-i.name: Optimal (Monotonic Stack)
next-greater-element-i.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Next Greater Element asks, for each value, what is the first strictly greater value to its right. If no such value exists, the answer is -1.

### Core intuition
Scan from right to left with a decreasing stack of useful future values. Any value less than or equal to the current value cannot be the next greater for the current value, and it also becomes hidden for values further left, so pop it. The stack top after popping is the answer.

### State meaning
stack stores candidate greater values from the right side, arranged so the nearest useful greater value can sit on top. result[i] stores the next greater value for arr[i].

### Invariant
Before processing index i, the stack contains values to the right of i that are still useful as next-greater candidates. Values that are too small have been removed.

### Edge cases
Equal values are not greater, so pop while stack top is less than or equal to current. The rightmost element always gets -1. An empty stack means no greater value exists to the right.

### Complexity
Each element is pushed once and popped at most once, so time is O(N). The stack uses O(N) space.

trapping-rain-water.name: Brute Force
trapping-rain-water.description: Build a correctness baseline for Trapping Rain Water by directly enumerating the candidate states allowed by the problem. This version prioritizes transparent control flow and exhaustive coverage over asymptotic efficiency.

trapping-rain-water.name: Optimal (Two Pointers)
trapping-rain-water.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Trapping Rain Water asks how many units of water can sit between bars of different heights after rain.

### Core intuition
Water above a bar is limited by the shorter wall between the best wall on its left and the best wall on its right. The two-pointer method avoids precomputing arrays. Whichever side currently has the smaller maximum is the safe side to process, because the opposite side already has a wall at least that tall.

### State meaning
left and right are the current boundaries. leftMax is the tallest bar seen from the left. rightMax is the tallest bar seen from the right. water accumulates trapped units.

### Invariant
When processing the side with the smaller maximum, that side's maximum is the limiting wall for its current bar. Any missing height below that maximum is trapped water.

### Edge cases
Fewer than three bars trap no water. Monotonic increasing or decreasing heights trap no water. Equal boundary cases can process either side as long as max values are updated correctly.

### Complexity
The two pointers move inward once, so time is O(N). Only a few variables are stored, so space is O(1).

largest-rectangle-in-histogram.name: Optimal (Monotonic Stack)
largest-rectangle-in-histogram.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Largest Rectangle in Histogram asks for the maximum rectangular area that can be formed using contiguous bars, where the rectangle height is limited by the shortest bar inside it.

### Core intuition
For each bar, we need to know how far it can extend before a shorter bar stops it. A monotonic increasing stack stores bar indices. When a shorter bar appears, it becomes the right boundary for taller bars on the stack, so we pop those bars and calculate their best rectangle.

### State meaning
stack stores indices with increasing heights. When index top is popped, heights[top] is the rectangle height. The current index is the first shorter bar on the right. The new stack top after popping is the first shorter bar on the left.

### Invariant
The stack is increasing by height. Any bar inside the stack has not yet found its right boundary. When it is popped, both boundaries are known and its maximal rectangle can be computed.

### Edge cases
A sentinel 0 at the end forces all bars to be popped. Equal heights can be handled consistently by either keeping or popping them depending on implementation. Empty histograms return 0.

### Complexity
Each bar index is pushed once and popped once, so time is O(N). The stack uses O(N) space.

lru-cache.name: Optimal (Hash Map + Doubly Linked List)
lru-cache.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
LRU Cache asks us to design a cache with fixed capacity where get and put are O(1), and when full, the least recently used key is evicted.

### Core intuition
Use a hash map for instant key lookup and a doubly linked list for recency order. Move every accessed or updated node to the front. The tail always holds the least recently used node, so eviction is O(1).

### State meaning
map stores key to node. The linked list stores nodes from most recently used near the head to least recently used near the tail. Dummy head and tail nodes make insert and remove operations uniform.

### Invariant
The map and linked list contain the same cache entries. The list order always reflects most recent use at the front and least recent use at the back.

### Edge cases
Getting a missing key returns -1 and does not change order. Putting an existing key updates its value and moves it to the front. When capacity is exceeded, remove the real node before the dummy tail.

### Complexity
Hash lookup, node removal, node insertion, get, and put are all O(1). Space is O(capacity).

lfu-cache.name: Optimal (Frequency Map + Multiple DLLs)
lfu-cache.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
LFU Cache asks us to design a fixed-capacity cache that evicts the least frequently used key. If multiple keys have the same lowest frequency, evict the least recently used among them.

### Core intuition
LRU alone tracks recency, but LFU also tracks popularity. Keep a map from key to value and frequency, plus frequency buckets that preserve recency inside each frequency. On every get or update, promote the key from frequency f to f + 1.

### State meaning
values stores key to value and frequency. freqMap stores each frequency's keys in recency order. minFreq records the smallest frequency currently present, so eviction does not search all buckets.

### Invariant
Every live key appears in exactly one frequency bucket matching its stored frequency. minFreq always points to the lowest non-empty frequency bucket.

### Edge cases
Capacity 0 means put does nothing. Updating an existing key also increases its frequency. When a min frequency bucket becomes empty after promotion, minFreq must move forward. New keys enter with frequency 1.

### Complexity
With hash maps and ordered buckets or linked lists, get and put are O(1). Space is O(capacity).

sliding-window-maximum.name: Brute Force
sliding-window-maximum.description: Build a correctness baseline for Sliding Window Maximum by directly enumerating the candidate states allowed by the problem. This version prioritizes transparent control flow and exhaustive coverage over asymptotic efficiency.
sliding-window-maximum.timeComplexity: O(n * k) Each of the (n - k + 1)

sliding-window-maximum.name: Optimal (The 'Monotonic Deque' Strategy)
sliding-window-maximum.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Sliding Window Maximum asks for the maximum value in every contiguous window of size k as the window moves from left to right.

### Core intuition
Keep a deque of indices whose values are in decreasing order. The front is always the maximum for the current window. When a new value arrives, remove smaller or equal values from the back because they cannot become maximum while the new value is still in the window.

### State meaning
deq stores indices, not values. The front index is the current maximum candidate. The back holds smaller candidates that may matter later after larger values expire. i is the current right edge of the window.

### Invariant
The deque indices are inside the current window and their values are decreasing from front to back. Therefore nums[deq.front] is the window maximum.

### Edge cases
k = 1 returns the original array. If k equals the array length, there is one maximum. Remove expired indices from the front when they fall outside i - k + 1.

### Complexity
Each index enters and leaves the deque at most once, so time is O(N). The deque holds at most K indices, so space is O(K).

flood-fill.name: Optimal (Depth First Search)
flood-fill.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Flood Fill asks us to recolor the connected region that starts at one source pixel. Only pixels with the same original color as the source, connected through up, down, left, or right movement, should be changed.

### Core intuition
Remember the source pixel's original color. From the starting cell, recolor it, then spread to neighbors only if they still have that original color. Different colors act like walls. If the new color is already the original color, return immediately to avoid revisiting forever.

### State meaning
sr and sc are the starting row and column. oldColor is the color we are allowed to replace. newColor is the color we write. DFS or BFS explores the active frontier of same-colored pixels.

### Invariant
Every cell recolored so far belonged to the original connected component of the source. The search never crosses out of bounds or into a cell with a different original color.

### Edge cases
If oldColor equals newColor, do nothing. A one-cell image works normally. Very large connected regions can make recursive DFS deep, so iterative BFS/DFS may be safer in production.

### Complexity
In the worst case, every pixel is visited once, so time is O(N * M). The recursion stack or explicit queue can use O(N * M) space.

01-matrix-bipartite-graph.name: Optimal (Multi-Source BFS)
01-matrix-bipartite-graph.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
01 Matrix asks for each cell's distance to the nearest 0 in a binary matrix.

### Core intuition
Instead of starting a search from every 1, start from every 0 at the same time. This multi-source BFS spreads outward in distance layers. The first time a 1 is reached, it must be through the nearest 0 because BFS expands one step at a time.

### State meaning
The queue initially stores all zero cells. Unvisited 1 cells are marked as -1 or Infinity. When a neighbor is reached from cell (r, c), its distance becomes matrix[r][c] + 1.

### Invariant
When a cell leaves the BFS queue, its stored value is the shortest distance to any zero. Any later route to the same cell would be at least as long.

### Edge cases
If a cell is already 0, its answer is 0. Multiple zeros are all valid sources. Mark 1s as unvisited before BFS so they do not look like completed distance 1 values.

### Complexity
Every cell enters the queue at most once, so time is O(N * M). The queue can hold O(N * M) cells.

surrounded-regions.name: Optimal (Inverse Boundary Check)
surrounded-regions.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Surrounded Regions asks us to flip every O region that is fully surrounded by X. Any O connected to the boundary must stay O because it can escape the board.

### Core intuition
It is easier to find the safe O cells than to prove each region is trapped. Start DFS/BFS from boundary O cells and mark everything connected to them as safe. After that, any O not marked safe is surrounded and should be flipped.

### State meaning
The board stores X, O, and a temporary safe marker such as S. Boundary DFS marks O cells that should survive. The final sweep converts unsafe O to X and safe S back to O.

### Invariant
After boundary traversal, every O connected to the border has been marked safe. Any remaining O cannot reach the boundary through O cells.

### Edge cases
Empty boards should return immediately. O cells on the border are never captured. Temporary markers must be restored before returning the board.

### Complexity
The board is scanned and traversed in O(N * M) time. DFS recursion or BFS queue can use O(N * M) space in the worst case.

number-of-enclaves.name: Optimal (Boundary Neutralization)
number-of-enclaves.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Number of Enclaves asks how many land cells cannot reach the boundary of the grid by moving through land cells.

### Core intuition
Boundary-connected land is escapable, so remove it first. Run DFS/BFS from every land cell on the boundary and turn that connected land into water. Whatever land remains afterward is trapped inside the grid and should be counted.

### State meaning
A 1 is land and a 0 is water. Boundary traversal erases escapable land. The final count scans for land that was not erased.

### Invariant
After boundary cleanup, no remaining land cell can reach the boundary through land. Therefore every remaining 1 is an enclave cell.

### Edge cases
All-water grids return 0. Land touching the boundary is never counted, along with all land connected to it. Single-row or single-column grids cannot contain enclaves because every cell is on the boundary.

### Complexity
Each cell is visited a constant number of times, so time is O(N * M). Recursion or queue space can be O(N * M).

word-ladder-i.name: Optimal (Breadth First Search)
word-ladder-i.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Word Ladder I asks for the length of the shortest sequence from beginWord to endWord, changing one letter at a time, where every intermediate word must be in the dictionary.

### Core intuition
Treat each word as a node in an unweighted graph. Two words share an edge if they differ by one letter. BFS is the right tool because it explores all transformations at distance 1 before distance 2, and so on. The first time we reach endWord is the shortest path.

### State meaning
The queue stores words with their level or sequence length. wordSet stores unused dictionary words for O(1) checks. Removing a word when enqueued prevents cycles and duplicate work.

### Invariant
When a word is popped from the BFS queue, its level is the shortest transformation length from beginWord to that word.

### Edge cases
If endWord is not in the dictionary, return 0. beginWord itself may not be in the word list. Each transformation changes exactly one character, not zero or multiple.

### Complexity
For N words of length M, generating mutations costs about O(M * 26) per word, and string construction can add another O(M), matching the catalog's O(M^2 * N) style bound. Space is O(N) to O(M^2 * N) depending on representation.

word-ladder-ii.name: Optimal (BFS Layer Building + DFS Backtracking)
word-ladder-ii.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Word Ladder II asks for all shortest transformation sequences from beginWord to endWord, not just the length.

### Core intuition
Use BFS to discover only shortest-distance relationships, then backtrack through those relationships to build every shortest path. BFS gives the distance layers; DFS/backtracking turns the layer graph into actual sequences.

### State meaning
The BFS phase records distance or forward adjacency between words that belong to shortest paths. The DFS phase keeps a path list and only moves to neighbors one level farther from the start.

### Invariant
Every edge used during reconstruction advances exactly one BFS level. Therefore every completed path has shortest possible length, and no longer detours are included.

### Edge cases
If endWord is missing, return an empty list. Words discovered in the same BFS level should not block other parents from linking to them in that same level. The number of shortest paths can be large, so output size matters.

### Complexity
BFS costs roughly O(K * N * 26) using the catalog's notation, and reconstruction depends on the number of shortest paths returned. Space stores BFS maps plus output paths.

introduction-to-bit-manipulation.name: The 3 Primary Operations
introduction-to-bit-manipulation.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Intro to Bit Manipulation introduces how AND, OR, and XOR operate on individual binary positions.

### Core intuition
Think column by column in binary. AND keeps a 1 only when both inputs have 1. OR keeps a 1 when at least one input has 1. XOR keeps a 1 when the two bits are different. The operation happens independently at every bit position.

### State meaning
The two input numbers are viewed as aligned binary strings. Each bit column produces one output bit according to the chosen operator.

### Invariant
A bitwise operation never carries between columns. The result bit at position i depends only on the two input bits at position i.

### Edge cases
Leading zeros do not change the numeric value but help alignment. Negative numbers use language-specific signed binary representation, so beginner examples usually use non-negative integers.

### Complexity
At algorithm level these fixed-width bitwise operations are O(1) time and O(1) space.

check-if-a-number-is-odd-or-not.name: Optimal (The 'Binary Tail' Check)
check-if-a-number-is-odd-or-not.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Check Odd/Even asks whether an integer is odd or even using its least significant bit.

### Core intuition
In binary, the last bit represents 1. All other bit positions represent powers of two, which are even. So only the last bit decides parity: last bit 1 means odd, last bit 0 means even.

### State meaning
n is the number being tested. The mask 1 has binary form ...0001. The expression n & 1 keeps only the last bit and turns every other bit into 0.

### Invariant
n & 1 equals 1 exactly when the least significant bit of n is set. That is exactly the condition for oddness.

### Edge cases
0 is even because its last bit is 0. Negative number behavior can depend on representation, but in common two's-complement systems n & 1 still reflects parity.

### Complexity
The check is a single bitwise operation, so time is O(1) and space is O(1).

check-if-i-th-bit-is-set-or-not.name: Optimal (Bit Shifting & Masking)
check-if-i-th-bit-is-set-or-not.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Check i-th Bit asks whether the bit at position i is 1. The same mask idea also supports setting, clearing, and toggling that bit.

### Core intuition
Create a mask with a single 1 at position i by shifting 1 left i times. AND the number with this mask. If the result is nonzero, that bit was set; if the result is zero, that bit was off.

### State meaning
i is the target bit position, usually counted from 0 at the rightmost bit. mask = 1 << i isolates that column. n & mask is the filtered result.

### Invariant
The mask has exactly one active bit. Therefore n & mask can be nonzero only if n also has a 1 at that exact position.

### Edge cases
Be clear whether positions are 0-indexed or 1-indexed. Shifting beyond the integer width can overflow or behave differently by language. Use unsigned types when sign bits matter.

### Complexity
Mask creation and AND are constant-time fixed-width operations, so time and space are O(1).

remove-the-last-set-bit.name: Optimal (n & n-1)
remove-the-last-set-bit.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Remove Last Set Bit asks us to clear the rightmost 1 bit in a number while leaving all other higher bits unchanged.

### Core intuition
Subtracting 1 from a number flips the rightmost set bit to 0 and turns all lower bits into 1. When we AND n with n - 1, that rightmost set bit disappears, and the higher bits remain the same.

### State meaning
n is the original number. n - 1 is the number after the borrow operation. n & (n - 1) is the result with the lowest set bit removed.

### Invariant
For any positive n, n & (n - 1) has exactly one fewer set bit than n: specifically, the rightmost set bit is cleared.

### Edge cases
If n is 0, there is no set bit to remove, and the expression still gives 0 in typical integer arithmetic. This trick is the core of Brian Kernighan's set-bit counting algorithm.

### Complexity
The operation is O(1) time and O(1) space.

check-if-a-number-is-power-of-2-or-not.name: Optimal (Single Bit Property)
check-if-a-number-is-power-of-2-or-not.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Check Power of 2 asks whether a positive integer can be written as 2^k for some whole number k.

### Core intuition
A power of 2 has exactly one set bit in binary: 1 is 1, 2 is 10, 4 is 100, 8 is 1000. If a number has exactly one set bit, removing its last set bit with n & (n - 1) should turn it into 0.

### State meaning
n is the number being tested. n - 1 flips the only set bit and all lower bits if n is a power of two. n & (n - 1) checks whether any set bit remains.

### Invariant
For positive n, n is a power of two exactly when n has one set bit, which is exactly when n & (n - 1) equals 0.

### Edge cases
n must be greater than 0. Zero is not a power of two, and negative values are excluded in this integer-power definition. Large fixed-width integers should be handled with the language's usual integer rules.

### Complexity
The check uses a constant number of bitwise operations, so time is O(1) and space is O(1).

count-the-number-of-set-bits.name: Optimal (Brian Kernighan's Algorithm)
count-the-number-of-set-bits.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Count Set Bits asks for how many 1 bits appear in the binary representation of a number.

### Core intuition
Use Brian Kernighan's trick: n & (n - 1) removes the rightmost set bit. If each loop removes exactly one 1, then the number of loops before n becomes 0 is the number of set bits.

### State meaning
n is the remaining bits still to count. count stores how many set bits have already been removed. Each iteration replaces n with n & (n - 1).

### Invariant
After each loop, count equals the number of 1 bits removed from the original number, and n contains all the original set bits not yet counted.

### Edge cases
n = 0 has zero set bits. For signed integers, use the unsigned interpretation expected by the problem. The loop count depends on the number of set bits, not the total bit width.

### Complexity
Time is O(SetBits), because each iteration removes one 1. Space is O(1).

swap-two-numbers.name: Optimal (XOR Swap)
swap-two-numbers.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Swap Numbers with XOR asks us to exchange two integer values without using a temporary third variable.

### Core intuition
XOR can mix two values and later recover either one because x ^ x = 0 and x ^ 0 = x. The three assignments encode both values into one variable, extract the old first value into the second variable, then extract the old second value back into the first variable.

### State meaning
a and b are the two values. After a = a ^ b, a stores a combined mask. Then b = a ^ b recovers the original a. Finally a = a ^ b recovers the original b.

### Invariant
The pair's information is never lost because XOR is reversible. Each step keeps enough combined information to recover the value that was overwritten.

### Edge cases
This trick is mostly educational. If a and b refer to the same memory location in low-level languages, XOR swap can zero the value. In real code, a temporary variable or destructuring swap is usually clearer.

### Complexity
The swap uses constant operations and constant extra space: O(1) time and O(1) space.

divide-two-integers-without-using-multip.name: Optimal (Bitwise Exponential Search)
divide-two-integers-without-using-multip.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Divide Two Integers asks us to compute integer division without using multiplication, division, or modulo operators, while respecting 32-bit signed integer limits.

### Core intuition
Repeated subtraction is too slow. Instead, subtract the largest doubled divisor that still fits into the remaining dividend. Shifting left by x represents multiplying the divisor by 2^x, so each subtraction removes a large chunk and adds 2^x to the quotient.

### State meaning
sign records whether the result should be positive or negative. n is the absolute remaining dividend. d is the absolute divisor. quotient accumulates the powers of two that have been subtracted.

### Invariant
At every step, original absolute dividend equals quotient * d plus the current remainder n, even though the implementation builds quotient with shifts instead of multiplication.

### Edge cases
Division by 1 or -1 should still respect overflow, especially -2147483648 / -1, which clamps to 2147483647. Work with absolute values for the main loop and apply the sign at the end.

### Complexity
The catalog lists O(log^2 N): the outer loop removes large chunks, and the inner search finds the largest shift. Space is O(1).

find-the-number-that-appears-once-and-th.name: Optimal (XOR Property)
find-the-number-that-appears-once-and-th.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Single Number asks us to find the only value that appears once when every other value appears exactly twice.

### Core intuition
XOR cancels equal pairs: x ^ x = 0. XOR also leaves a value unchanged when combined with 0. So if we XOR every number together, every duplicate pair disappears and the single value remains.

### State meaning
result stores the running XOR of all values seen so far. Each new number either cancels a previous equal value or contributes bits that may remain until their pair appears.

### Invariant
After processing any prefix, result equals the XOR of values whose duplicate pairing has not fully canceled inside that prefix. After the full array, only the single number remains.

### Edge cases
The method relies on the exact condition that all non-answer values appear twice. It works with zero and negative integers under standard fixed-width XOR behavior.

### Complexity
The array is scanned once, so time is O(N). Only one running variable is stored, so space is O(1).

power-set.name: Optimal (Binary Counter)
power-set.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Power Set asks us to generate every possible subset of the input array using bitmasks.

### Core intuition
For N elements, each subset can be represented by N yes-or-no decisions. The integers from 0 to 2^N - 1 contain every possible pattern of N bits. If bit j is 1 in mask i, include nums[j] in that subset.

### State meaning
mask is the current subset pattern. j is the element index being tested. 1 << j creates a mask for that element. subset stores elements selected by the active bits.

### Invariant
For each mask, the generated subset contains exactly the elements whose bit positions are 1 in that mask. Every mask is unique, so every subset pattern appears exactly once.

### Edge cases
An empty input has one subset: the empty subset. For large N, the output size explodes to 2^N, so generation is naturally exponential.

### Complexity
There are 2^N masks and each checks N bit positions, so time is O(N * 2^N). Extra working space is O(1) excluding the returned subsets.

kth-largest-element-in-an-array.name: Optimal (Min-Heap)
kth-largest-element-in-an-array.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Kth Largest Element asks for the value that would appear in kth position if the array were sorted descending, without needing to fully sort the array.

### Core intuition
Keep a min-heap of size k containing the k largest values seen so far. The smallest value inside that heap is the current kth largest. When a new value is bigger than the heap root, it deserves a seat among the top k, so remove the root and insert the new value.

### State meaning
heap stores at most k values. heap[0] is the smallest among those selected top-k candidates. After all numbers are processed, heap[0] is the kth largest overall.

### Invariant
After processing each number, the heap contains the k largest values from the processed prefix, or all processed values if fewer than k have been seen.

### Edge cases
k = 1 returns the maximum. k equal to the array length returns the minimum. Duplicates count as separate positions, as they would in sorted order.

### Complexity
Each of N numbers may cause a heap operation of O(log K), so time is O(N * log K). Space is O(K).

find-median-from-data-stream.name: Optimal (Two Heaps Algorithm)
find-median-from-data-stream.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Median from Data Stream asks us to support adding numbers one by one and returning the current median at any time.

### Core intuition
Split the numbers into two halves. A max-heap stores the smaller half, so its top is the largest small value. A min-heap stores the larger half, so its top is the smallest large value. Keep the heaps balanced, and the median is either the top of the larger half or the average of both tops.

### State meaning
small is a max-heap for the lower half. large is a min-heap for the upper half. Their sizes differ by at most one, and every value in small is less than or equal to every value in large.

### Invariant
After every insertion, the two heaps are ordered across the split and balanced in size. Therefore the middle value or middle pair is available at the heap tops.

### Edge cases
With one value, the median is that value. With an even count, return the average of both heap tops. Negative values work normally; Python often stores max-heap values as negatives.

### Complexity
Adding a number costs O(log N) due to heap operations. Finding the median is O(1). Space is O(N).

top-k-frequent-elements.name: Optimal (Min-Heap + Hash Map)
top-k-frequent-elements.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Top K Frequent Elements asks for the k values that appear most often in an array.

### Core intuition
First count each value. Then keep a min-heap of size k ordered by frequency. If a new value has higher frequency than the smallest frequency currently in the heap, it enters and the weakest top-k candidate leaves.

### State meaning
freq maps each number to its count. heap stores at most k pairs of frequency and value. The heap root is the least frequent among the current top-k candidates.

### Invariant
After processing each unique value, the heap contains the k most frequent values seen so far, or all seen values if fewer than k exist.

### Edge cases
If k equals the number of unique values, return all unique values. Ties can be returned in any order unless the problem adds a tie rule. Counting must happen before heap selection.

### Complexity
Counting is O(N). Heap selection over unique values costs O(U log K), where U is the number of unique values. Space is O(U + K).

disjoint-set-union-by-rank-size.name: Optimal (Path Compression + Union by Rank)
disjoint-set-union-by-rank-size.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Disjoint Set Union tracks which elements belong to the same connected group while supporting fast find and union operations.

### Core intuition
Represent each group as a tree whose root is the group's leader. find(x) climbs to the leader. Path compression makes every visited node point directly to that leader. union(a, b) merges two groups by attaching the smaller or shallower tree under the larger one.

### State meaning
parent[x] stores x's immediate parent, or x itself if x is a root. rank or size estimates which root should become the parent during union. find returns the root representative of a set.

### Invariant
Two elements are in the same set exactly when find returns the same root for both. Parent pointers always lead upward to a valid root representative.

### Edge cases
Unioning elements already in the same set should do nothing and often returns false. Initialization must set every element as its own parent. Path compression changes parent pointers but not set membership.

### Complexity
With path compression and union by rank or size, operations are O(alpha(N)) amortized, which is effectively constant for real input sizes. Space is O(N).

segment-tree-point-update-range-query.name: Optimal (Recursive Range Storage)
segment-tree-point-update-range-query.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
This segment tree entry is about maintaining an array while two operations keep arriving: update one position, and answer a range query such as sum from L to R. The beginner trap is to think of the tree as a fancy array. It is better to think of it as a library shelf: every node stores the summary of one continuous shelf segment, and parent shelves combine the summaries of their two child shelves.

### Core intuition
A range query is fast because most shelves are either fully useful or completely irrelevant. If a node segment lies completely inside the query range, take its stored value and stop going deeper. If it lies outside, ignore it. Only when the query cuts through the middle do we split into children. An update is also local: change the leaf for the updated index, then rebuild exactly the ancestors on the path back to the root.

### Invariant
Every tree node must always equal the merged answer of the exact array segment it represents. For range sum, that merge is addition. After an update, the only stale nodes are the leaf and its ancestors; all other subtrees still summarize unchanged array ranges.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not recurse into both children blindly after discovering full overlap; that loses the whole advantage. Be precise with segment boundaries, especially inclusive L and R. Also keep the merge operation consistent: a sum tree, min tree, and max tree have the same shape, but their neutral values and merge rules are different.

### Complexity
Building the tree costs O(N). A point update touches one path of height O(log N). A range query visits only the boundary paths plus fully covered chunks, so it is also O(log N) for standard queries. The tree stores O(N) summarized nodes.

fenwick-tree-binary-indexed-tree.name: Optimal (Binary Skipping)
fenwick-tree-binary-indexed-tree.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
A Fenwick Tree, also called a Binary Indexed Tree, supports prefix queries and point updates on an array. The classic version stores sums: add a value at one index, and ask for the sum from the start up to some index. A range sum L to R then becomes prefix(R) minus prefix(L - 1). It solves a narrower job than a segment tree, but does that job with a very compact structure.

### Core intuition
Fenwick Tree works by letting each internal position be responsible for a block whose size is decided by the last set bit of its 1-based index. Index 8 can cover a block of 8 values, index 6 can cover a block of 2 values, and index 5 can cover a block of 1 value. The expression i & -i gives the size of the block that position owns.

### How updates and queries move
For an update, start at the changed 1-based index and move upward by adding the lowbit. Every visited tree cell is a block that includes this array position, so it must receive the delta. For a prefix query, start at the requested 1-based index and move downward by subtracting the lowbit. Every visited cell contributes one non-overlapping block of the prefix.

### Invariant
Each Fenwick cell stores the sum of exactly the block ending at that cell and sized by its lowbit. If that invariant holds, prefix queries decompose the prefix into clean blocks with no overlap and no missing index.

### Complexity
Both update and prefix query jump through O(log N) responsible blocks. Space is O(N), usually with a 1-based tree array of length N + 1.

pattern-1-square.name: Optimal (Nested Loops)
pattern-1-square.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Pattern 1 asks you to print an N by N square of stars. For n = 4, there are 4 rows, and every row has exactly 4 stars. This problem looks too simple, but it is the foundation for many pattern-printing questions because it teaches the first mental split: rows decide how many lines appear, columns decide what each line contains.

### Core intuition
Imagine the output as graph paper. The outer loop walks downward row by row. For each row, the inner loop walks left to right and places one star in each column. Since the square never changes width, the inner loop always runs N times. There is no dependence on the current row number yet; every row is a clone of the previous row.

### Invariant
Before printing row r, exactly r rows have already been printed, and each printed row contains N stars. During a row, after c inner-loop steps, the current line contains c stars. When c reaches N, the line is complete and can be printed.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not print each star with its own newline; that creates a vertical line instead of a square. Build one row as a string or print stars on the same line, then move to the next line only after the inner loop finishes. Also keep row count and column count separate in your head, even when both equal N here.

### Complexity
There are N rows and N stars per row, so the program performs O(N^2) star placements. Apart from the current row string or direct console output, extra space is O(1).

pattern-7-triangle-star.name: Optimal (Math-based Loops)
pattern-7-triangle-star.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Pattern 7 asks for a centered triangle of stars. For n = 4, the rows contain 1, 3, 5, and 7 stars, but the stars must be shifted right at the top so the triangle has a center line. This is the first pattern where printing stars alone is not enough; spaces are part of the shape.

### Core intuition
Each row has three zones: leading spaces, stars, and optional trailing spaces. The row number controls both the shrinking space count and the growing star count. If rows are zero-indexed, row i has n - i - 1 leading spaces and 2 * i + 1 stars. The spaces move the first star toward the left one step at a time. The odd star count expands the row equally around the center.

### Invariant
Before printing row i, rows above it already form a smaller centered triangle. For the current row, leading spaces plus stars must keep the same overall visual width. As i increases by 1, leading spaces decrease by 1 and stars increase by 2. That paired change is what keeps the triangle centered.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not use i stars; that makes a left-aligned triangle. Do not forget that the first row still has one star, not zero. And when testing in a console, use a fixed-width font or the alignment may appear misleading.

### Complexity
The printed area is proportional to N^2 because the bottom row has about 2N characters and there are N rows. The loop state itself is constant: row index, space count, star count, and the current line.

pattern-10-rotational-triangle.name: Optimal (Multi-phase Loops)
pattern-10-rotational-triangle.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Pattern 10, the half diamond, prints a triangle that grows from 1 star to N stars and then shrinks back to 1 star. For n = 3, the row widths are 1, 2, 3, 2, 1. It is not a full centered diamond because there are no leading spaces; all rows start from the left edge.

### Core intuition
Think of the output as two slopes joined at the peak. The first phase counts upward: 1 star, 2 stars, 3 stars. The second phase counts downward: 2 stars, 1 star. A neat single-loop formula is to let row i run from 1 to 2N - 1. If i is at most N, stars = i. If i is greater than N, stars = 2N - i. That second formula mirrors the first side without printing the peak twice.

### Invariant
At every row, the number of stars equals the distance from that row to the nearest end of the shape, capped at N. Another practical invariant: the peak row appears exactly once. Once the row counter crosses N, every next line must be one star shorter than the previous line.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not run the decreasing loop from N again, or the largest row will be printed twice. If you split the problem into two loops, the second loop should start at N - 1. If you use one loop, test n = 1 because the answer should be just one star.

### Complexity
The output contains roughly N^2 stars across 2N - 1 rows, so printing costs O(N^2). The loop uses constant extra state: the row counter and the computed star count.

user-input-output.name: Optimal (Topic-Specific)
user-input-output.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact topic is teaching
User input and output is the first conversation between a program and the outside world. Input is what the program receives: a name typed by a user, a number read from a test case, a line from a file, or a request from another service. Output is what the program sends back: text on the screen, an answer returned to an online judge, or a saved result.

### Core intuition
Treat every program like a tiny workshop. Input is the raw material entering the door. Processing is the workbench where the program checks, transforms, and decides. Output is the finished item leaving the workshop. The most common beginner mistake is mixing those phases together. A clean solution first reads, then understands, then prints.

### How to think in coding-platform problems
In algorithm problems, input is usually already provided to your function, so you do not always write scanner or readline code. But the idea remains the same: parameters are input, returned values are output. In beginner console programs, you read from standard input, convert text into the needed type, and then print the result.

### Invariant
Once input has been read and converted, the program should use the converted value consistently. If the input says 5, do not keep treating it as the text character five unless the problem is specifically about strings.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not assume input is already clean unless the problem guarantees it. Trim extra spaces when needed, parse numbers before arithmetic, and avoid printing prompts on online judges because they expect exact output only.

### Complexity
Input and output usually cost O(N) in the number of characters or values read and written. The memory cost depends on whether you store the whole input or process it piece by piece.

data-types.name: Optimal (Topic-Specific)
data-types.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact topic is teaching
Data types answer a simple but important question: what kind of thing is this value? The value 21 can be a number used for arithmetic. The text Asha is a string used for names and messages. true is a boolean used for decisions. A list like [90, 95, 88] is a collection used when one value is not enough.

### Core intuition
A variable is like a labeled box, but the type tells you what tools are safe to use on the thing inside the box. You can add two numbers. You can join two strings. You can ask a boolean to choose between two branches. You can loop through an array. Many bugs come from using the right value with the wrong tool.

### Why types matter in algorithms
Algorithm questions often hide type decisions inside constraints. If numbers can become very large, an int may overflow and you may need long long or long. If the answer is yes or no, a boolean communicates that better than storing 0 and 1 everywhere. If you need fast membership checks, the type might be a set rather than a list.

### Invariant
At each step, the program should know what operations are valid for each value. If a variable stores a count, it should behave like a number. If it stores user text, it should be treated like text until explicitly parsed.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not confuse a digit character with the number it represents. The character 5 and the number 5 may look similar when printed, but arithmetic treats them differently. Also be careful with decimal values, because floating-point numbers can store approximations.

### Complexity
Choosing a type is usually O(1), but the chosen structure affects later complexity. A number uses constant space, while an array of N values uses O(N) space.

if-else-statements.name: Optimal (Topic-Specific)
if-else-statements.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact topic is teaching
If-else statements teach a program to choose. Without conditions, instructions run like a straight railway track. With if-else, the program reaches a fork: if the condition is true, run one block; otherwise, run the other block. This is the beginning of real decision-making in code.

### Core intuition
Read an if-else like a question followed by two possible scenes. The question must produce a boolean answer: true or false. If the answer is true, the if block runs. If the answer is false, the else block runs. Exactly one of those two blocks runs in a basic if-else structure.

### Why it matters in algorithms
Algorithms constantly ask questions: is this number even, is the current sum larger than the best sum, did we reach the target, is this node already visited? If-else statements turn those questions into movement. They decide whether to update a variable, skip an element, return early, or continue searching.

### Invariant
The condition should describe the exact rule for entering the if block. After the branch finishes, the program should still be in a valid state no matter which path ran. For example, if both branches assign a result message, then after the if-else the message should always exist.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not write assignment when you mean comparison in languages where that is possible. Do not forget the boundary case: score >= 40 and score > 40 behave differently when score is exactly 40. Also avoid conditions so vague that the else branch accidentally catches important special cases.

### Complexity
A single condition check is usually O(1), though the work inside a branch can be larger. Space is typically O(1) unless a branch creates extra data structures.

switch-statement.name: Optimal (Topic-Specific)
switch-statement.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact topic is teaching
A switch statement chooses one action from many named cases based on a single value. It is useful when the question is not a broad true-or-false condition, but a menu: if the value is 1 do this, if it is 2 do that, if it is 3 do something else. The default case handles values that do not match any listed option.

### Core intuition
Think of switch as a labeled drawer cabinet. The expression gives you a label, and the program opens the matching drawer. If there is no matching drawer, it opens the default drawer. This is clearer than a long chain of if-else statements when every branch compares the same variable against fixed values.

### When to use it
Use switch when the value is discrete: day number, command name, menu choice, token type, or small enum-like category. Do not use it when each branch needs a different kind of condition, such as score >= 90, score >= 75, and score >= 40. That kind of range logic belongs in if-else.

### Invariant
Only the matching case should produce the selected action. In languages with fall-through behavior, each case usually needs a break or return so execution does not accidentally continue into the next case.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not forget default. It protects the program when input is outside the expected set. Also be careful about type matching: the number 3 and the string 3 may not match in strict languages or strict comparison modes.

### Complexity
A switch over a small fixed set is effectively O(1). Some languages compile dense cases into jump tables; others behave more like comparisons. Space is O(1) for normal control flow.

for-loops-while-loops.name: Optimal (Topic-Specific)
for-loops-while-loops.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact topic is teaching
For loops and while loops both repeat work, but they fit different stories. A for loop is best when you know the route: visit every index, run exactly N times, or scan each item in a collection. A while loop is best when you know the stopping condition but not the exact number of repetitions in advance.

### Core intuition
A loop is a controlled promise to repeat. It needs a starting state, a condition that says whether to continue, a body that does useful work, and progress that moves toward stopping. If any one of those is missing, the loop becomes confusing or infinite. Beginners should not think of loops as spinning; think of them as walking one deliberate step at a time.

### For versus while
Use a for loop when the counter or collection traversal is central to the task. Summing an array, printing rows, and checking every character are natural for-loop jobs. Use a while loop when the program is waiting for something to become false: keep dividing until the number becomes 0, keep moving pointers while left < right, or keep processing while a queue is not empty.

### Invariant
Before each loop iteration, the loop state should describe all work already completed. After the body runs, progress must update that state so the next iteration moves closer to the finish.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not forget to update the while-loop variable. Do not use <= when < is needed for zero-based arrays. And when summing or counting, initialize the accumulator before the loop starts.

### Complexity
If the loop runs N times and each body is O(1), the loop is O(N). Nested loops multiply when each level repeats independently. Space is O(1) unless the loop stores growing results.

pattern-printing-patterns-1-to-22.name: Optimal (Topic-Specific)
pattern-printing-patterns-1-to-22.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact topic is asking
Pattern Printing, covering patterns 1 to 22, is not one single algorithm. It is a training ground for loop control, row thinking, column thinking, spaces, stars, numbers, and symmetry. The real question behind every pattern is: for this row and this column, what character should appear here?

### Core intuition
Do not memorize twenty-two drawings separately. Treat every pattern as a grid story. First decide the height: how many rows will be printed. Then decide what each row contains: leading spaces, symbols, numbers, or blanks. Finally decide how the counts change as the row number changes. Squares keep the width constant. Triangles grow. Inverted triangles shrink. Diamonds grow and then shrink. Number patterns often reuse the row number, column number, or distance from an edge.

### The practical method
For a new pattern, write the output for a small n like 4. Label the rows from 0 or 1. For each row, count spaces and visible characters. Turn those counts into formulas. If row 0 has 3 spaces and row 1 has 2 spaces, that is probably n - row - 1. If stars go 1, 3, 5, 7, that is probably 2 * row + 1.

### Invariant
After finishing row r, every row above it already matches the requested drawing. The current row is built from left to right using formulas that depend only on n, row, and sometimes column.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not print a newline after every character. Do not ignore spaces; spaces are part of centered shapes. Also do not jump to code before counting the first few rows by hand.

### Complexity
The cost is proportional to the number of printed characters. Most n-sized patterns print O(N^2) characters, with O(1) extra state if each row is printed as it is built.

print-all-divisors.name: Brute Force
print-all-divisors.description: Build a correctness baseline for Print all Divisors by directly enumerating the candidate states allowed by the problem. This version prioritizes transparent control flow and exhaustive coverage over asymptotic efficiency.

print-all-divisors.name: Optimal (Topic-Specific)
print-all-divisors.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Print all divisors asks for every positive integer that divides n with remainder 0. For n = 36, the divisors are 1, 2, 3, 4, 6, 9, 12, 18, and 36. The naive way checks every number from 1 to n. The smarter way notices that divisors arrive in pairs.

### Core intuition
If i divides n, then n / i also divides n. For 36, when i = 3, the partner is 12. When i = 4, the partner is 9. That means we only need to search up to sqrt(n), because after the square root the partners have already been discovered from the smaller side.

### Step logic
Start with an empty answer list. For each i from 1 while i * i <= n, test n % i. If the remainder is 0, add i. Then add n / i too, unless both are the same number. The duplicate case happens for perfect squares, like 36 when i = 6.

### Invariant
After testing all numbers up to i, every divisor pair whose smaller member is at most i has been collected. Nothing beyond sqrt(n) needs to be tested directly because it would only be the larger partner of a smaller divisor.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not add the square root twice for perfect squares. If the problem expects sorted output, either collect the small and large divisors separately or sort at the end. Also define behavior for n <= 0 if the platform does not restrict input.

### Complexity
The scan runs O(sqrt N) iterations. If sorted output is required and you sort the collected divisors, there can be an extra sorting cost based on the number of divisors. Space is O(D), where D is the number of divisors returned.

understand-recursion-by-printing-somethi.name: Core Intuition
understand-recursion-by-printing-somethi.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
This recursion exercise asks you to print the same message exactly N times without using a normal loop. The goal is not that recursion is better for printing. The goal is to understand the two pieces that every recursion needs: a base case that stops, and a recursive call that moves toward that base case.

### Core intuition
Imagine a function holding a counter. If the counter is 0, it stops. Otherwise, it prints once and asks a smaller version of itself to handle the remaining prints. printNTimes(3) prints once, then trusts printNTimes(2). That call prints once, then trusts printNTimes(1). Finally printNTimes(0) says there is nothing left to do.

### Call stack picture
Each recursive call waits on the call stack until the smaller call finishes. For this problem, if you print before the recursive call, the visible output appears while the stack is going downward. If you print after the recursive call, the output appears while the stack is unwinding. That single placement changes the order in many recursion problems.

### Invariant
At the start of a call with value k, the function still needs to print the message k times. After printing once, the remaining job is exactly k - 1 prints, which is the same problem in a smaller form.

### Mistakes to avoid
Never forget the base case. Also make sure the recursive argument moves toward the base case; calling the same n again causes infinite recursion. For this exact task, time is O(N) and stack space is O(N), not exponential.

### Complexity
There are N printing calls plus one stopping call, so time is O(N). The deepest call stack has N active frames, so auxiliary space is O(N).

counting-frequencies-of-array-elements.name: Optimal (Topic-Specific)
counting-frequencies-of-array-elements.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Counting frequencies means reporting how many times each distinct array value appears. For [2, 3, 2, 5, 3, 2], the answer is 2 appears 3 times, 3 appears 2 times, and 5 appears 1 time. The important shift is from thinking about positions to thinking about counts per value.

### Core intuition
Use a hash map as a small notebook. Every time a number appears, open its page and increase the count by one. If the page does not exist yet, create it with count 1. After one pass, the notebook contains the complete frequency table.

### Why hashing fits
Without a map, you might compare each element with every other element, which can become O(N^2). A hash map gives average O(1) update time per element. That means the array can be processed in one clean scan.

### Invariant
After processing the first i elements, the map contains exact frequencies for only those i elements. It does not need to know the future. When the next element arrives, only that element's count changes.

### Mistakes to avoid
Initialize missing counts correctly. If you read freq[x] before x exists, some languages produce undefined, null, or an error. Also remember that object keys may become strings in JavaScript; use Map if key type precision matters.

### Complexity
The scan is O(N) average time. The map can store up to U distinct values, so space is O(U), which is O(N) in the worst case when every value is different.

find-the-highest-lowest-frequency-elemen.name: Optimal (Topic-Specific)
find-the-highest-lowest-frequency-elemen.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
This problem builds on frequency counting. Instead of returning every count, we must identify which element appears the most and which element appears the least. For example, in [4, 4, 5, 6, 6, 6, 7], the highest-frequency element is 6 with count 3, while the lowest-frequency elements are 5 and 7 with count 1.

### Core intuition
There are two phases. First, build the frequency map exactly like a normal counting problem. Second, walk through the map entries and compare counts. A value with a larger count becomes the current best for maximum frequency. A value with a smaller count becomes the current best for minimum frequency.

### Tie handling
Real platforms often specify a tie rule, such as choose the smaller value or choose the first value encountered. If the prompt does not specify it, define one before coding. In the dry run below, the lowest-frequency tie chooses the first value encountered in the original scan.

### Invariant
After scanning some map entries, maxElement stores the best highest-frequency candidate seen so far, and minElement stores the best lowest-frequency candidate seen so far. Every new map entry either improves one of those candidates or leaves them unchanged.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not try to decide highest and lowest before the counts are complete unless you are very careful. A value that starts frequent can be overtaken later. Also handle an empty array explicitly because there is no element to return.

### Complexity
Building the map is O(N) average time. Scanning U unique entries is O(U). Total time is O(N + U), which is O(N). Space is O(U).
find-the-highest-lowest-frequency-elemen.timeComplexity: O(1) for lookup, O(n) for insertion

largest-element-in-an-array.name: Optimal (Topic-Specific)
largest-element-in-an-array.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Largest Element asks for the maximum value present in an array. It is one of the cleanest examples of a running answer. You do not need sorting, extra arrays, or nested loops. You only need to remember the best value seen so far while scanning from left to right.

### Core intuition
Imagine reading exam scores one by one. The first score becomes the current champion because there is nothing else to compare against. Every next score challenges the champion. If the challenger is bigger, it becomes the new champion. If it is smaller or equal, the current champion stays.

### Why one pass is enough
The largest value must be one of the elements. Once an element has been compared with the current maximum, there is no future reason to revisit it. The running maximum carries forward all useful information from the prefix already scanned.

### Invariant
After processing index i, maxSoFar is the largest value among arr[0] through arr[i]. This invariant starts true at index 0 and remains true because every new element is compared directly against maxSoFar.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not initialize the answer to 0 unless the problem guarantees non-negative numbers. For arrays like [-8, -3, -10], initializing to 0 gives a value that never appeared. Start from arr[0] when the array is non-empty. Also define what to return for an empty array if the platform allows it.

### Complexity
The algorithm visits each element once, so time is O(N). It stores one running value, so extra space is O(1).

second-largest-element-in-an-array-witho.name: Optimal (Topic-Specific)
second-largest-element-in-an-array-witho.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Second Largest Element asks for the largest value that is strictly smaller than the maximum, without sorting the array. The word strictly matters. In [10, 10, 8], the second largest distinct value is 8, not 10 again. If the problem defines second largest by position after sorting with duplicates, the answer would be different, so read that detail carefully.

### Core intuition
Keep two champions while scanning: largest and secondLargest. largest is the best value seen so far. secondLargest is the best value seen so far that is still smaller than largest. When a new value beats largest, the old largest slides down into secondLargest. When a new value does not beat largest but beats secondLargest and is not equal to largest, it becomes the new secondLargest.

### Invariant
After processing each element, largest is the maximum distinct value in the scanned prefix, and secondLargest is the maximum distinct value smaller than largest. If no such value exists yet, secondLargest remains empty or negative infinity.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not sort if the task asks for O(N). Do not treat another copy of the maximum as second largest when the distinct version is required. Also do not initialize both answers to 0 if negative values are possible.

### Complexity
The array is scanned once, so time is O(N). Only two variables are maintained, so extra space is O(1).

check-if-the-array-is-sorted.name: Brute Force
check-if-the-array-is-sorted.description: Build a correctness baseline for Is Array Sorted? by directly enumerating the candidate states allowed by the problem. This version prioritizes transparent control flow and exhaustive coverage over asymptotic efficiency.

check-if-the-array-is-sorted.name: Optimal (Topic-Specific)
check-if-the-array-is-sorted.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Is Array Sorted usually asks whether the array is in non-decreasing order: every next element should be greater than or equal to the previous one. Duplicates are allowed because equal neighbors do not break sorted order. For example, [1, 2, 2, 4] is sorted, but [1, 2, 4, 3] is not.

### Core intuition
A sorted array is a local promise repeated everywhere. You do not need to compare every pair of elements. It is enough to check every neighboring pair. If each arr[i] <= arr[i + 1], then the whole array is non-decreasing. The first pair that violates this rule proves the answer is false immediately.

### Why adjacent comparison works
If every element is less than or equal to the next one, the order chains forward through the array. arr[0] <= arr[1], arr[1] <= arr[2], and so on. That chain guarantees earlier values never jump above later values.

### Invariant
Before checking pair i and i + 1, all previous adjacent pairs have already been verified. Therefore the prefix up to i is sorted. If the current pair is also valid, the sorted prefix extends by one.

### Mistakes to avoid
Use greater-than as the failure condition for non-decreasing order. If you use greater-than-or-equal as failure, duplicates are incorrectly rejected. Also empty and one-element arrays are sorted by definition because no bad adjacent pair exists.

### Complexity
The scan checks at most N - 1 adjacent pairs, so time is O(N). It uses no growing structure, so extra space is O(1).

remove-duplicates-from-sorted-array.name: Brute Force
remove-duplicates-from-sorted-array.description: Build a correctness baseline for Remove Duplicates by directly enumerating the candidate states allowed by the problem. This version prioritizes transparent control flow and exhaustive coverage over asymptotic efficiency.

remove-duplicates-from-sorted-array.name: Optimal (Topic-Specific)
remove-duplicates-from-sorted-array.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Remove Duplicates from Sorted Array asks us to keep only one copy of each value. Because the input is sorted, equal values sit next to each other. That property is the whole reason the optimal solution can use two pointers instead of a hash set.

### Core intuition
Use a write pointer to mark where the next unique value should be placed. Use a read pointer to scan the array. The first element is always kept. Whenever arr[read] differs from the previous value, it is a new unique value, so copy it into arr[write] and move write forward. Duplicates are skipped because they match the previous value.

### In-place meaning
Many versions of this problem do not require deleting elements physically. They ask you to return the number of unique values, k, and ensure the first k positions contain those unique values. Anything after k can be ignored.

### Invariant
Before each read step, arr[0..write-1] contains the unique values discovered so far, in sorted order. The read pointer explores the rest of the array looking for the next value that is different from the last accepted value.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not use a set if the problem expects O(1) extra space. Do not compare arr[read] with arr[write] after write has moved; compare against the previous original neighbor or the last accepted unique value. Handle an empty array by returning 0 or an empty result.

### Complexity
The read pointer visits every element once, so time is O(N). The in-place version uses O(1) extra space.

left-rotate-an-array-by-one-place.name: Optimal (Topic-Specific)
left-rotate-an-array-by-one-place.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Left Rotate by 1 asks us to move every element one position to the left, and move the first element to the end. For [10, 20, 30, 40], the result is [20, 30, 40, 10]. No value is removed; the order simply wraps around.

### Core intuition
The first element is the only value that would be overwritten if we shift directly. So save it first. Then move arr[1] into arr[0], arr[2] into arr[1], and so on. After the shift, the last position is empty from a logical point of view, so put the saved first element there.

### In-place story
If the problem allows returning a new array, slicing is simple. But the classic optimal version is in-place with O(1) extra space. The temporary variable holding the first element is enough because every other value moves into the spot just before it.

### Invariant
After shifting up to index i, positions before i contain their final rotated values. The saved first element is protected in temp until the very end, so it cannot be lost during overwrites.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not start shifting before saving arr[0]. Do not rotate an empty array without checking length. For a one-element array, the result is unchanged. Also remember that this is left rotation: the first value goes to the back, not the last value to the front.

### Complexity
Every element except the first is shifted once, so time is O(N). The in-place version uses one temporary variable, so extra space is O(1).

left-rotate-an-array-by-d-places.name: Optimal (Topic-Specific)
left-rotate-an-array-by-d-places.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Left Rotate by D asks us to move every element D positions to the left, with the elements that fall off the front wrapping around to the back. For [1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7] and d = 3, the answer is [4, 5, 6, 7, 1, 2, 3]. The array is not sorted or filtered; it is only rearranged circularly.

### Core intuition
First reduce d with d % n, because rotating by the array length changes nothing. Then the clean in-place trick is three reversals. Reverse the first d elements, reverse the remaining n - d elements, then reverse the whole array. The first two reversals prepare the two blocks, and the final reversal puts the blocks in the rotated order while restoring the internal order of each block.

### Why reversal works
A left rotation by d splits the array into A + B, where A is the first d elements and B is the rest. The desired result is B + A. Reversing A gives reverse(A), reversing B gives reverse(B), and reversing the whole reverse(A) + reverse(B) becomes B + A.

### Invariant
The array always contains the same values, just in different positions. After the first reversal, the left block is flipped. After the second, both blocks are flipped. After the final reversal, the blocks swap sides and each block's original order is restored.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not forget d %= n, especially when d is larger than n. Handle n = 0 before taking modulo. Also keep the direction clear: left rotation sends the first block to the end, while right rotation sends the last block to the front.

### Complexity
Each reversal touches part of the array, and together they touch O(N) elements. The in-place version uses O(1) extra space.

move-zeroes-to-end.name: Brute Force
move-zeroes-to-end.description: Build a correctness baseline for Move Zeroes to End by directly enumerating the candidate states allowed by the problem. This version prioritizes transparent control flow and exhaustive coverage over asymptotic efficiency.

move-zeroes-to-end.name: Optimal (Topic-Specific)
move-zeroes-to-end.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Move Zeroes to End asks us to move every 0 to the back while keeping the relative order of all non-zero elements. For [0, 1, 0, 3, 12], the answer is [1, 3, 12, 0, 0]. The non-zero values are not sorted; they stay in the same order they originally appeared.

### Core intuition
Use a write pointer that marks where the next non-zero value should go. Then scan with a read pointer. Whenever read sees a non-zero value, place it at write and move write forward. After the scan, every position from write to the end should be filled with zeroes.

### Stable movement
The reason this preserves order is simple: non-zero values are written in the same order the read pointer discovers them. The first non-zero goes to the first slot, the second non-zero goes to the second slot, and so on. Zeroes are not important individually, so they can all be written at the end.

### Invariant
Before each read step, positions before write contain exactly the non-zero values already seen, in their original order. Positions from write onward are still unfinished workspace.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not sort the array; sorting changes the order of non-zero values. Be careful with swap-based versions: swapping is fine when write and read are managed correctly, but direct write plus final zero-fill is often easier to reason about. Also handle arrays with all zeroes or no zeroes.

### Complexity
The scan is O(N), and the final zero-fill is also O(N) in total. The algorithm uses O(1) extra space.

linear-search.name: Linear Search
linear-search.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Linear Search asks whether a target value appears in an array, usually returning its index if found and -1 if not found. It makes no assumption that the array is sorted. Because there is no structure to exploit, the honest method is to inspect elements one by one.

### Core intuition
Think of checking seats in a row. Start at the first seat and ask, is this the target? If yes, stop immediately. If not, move to the next seat. The first match gives the earliest index. If the scan reaches the end without a match, the target is absent.

### Why stopping early is correct
Once arr[i] equals target, the search goal has been satisfied. If the task asks for first occurrence, returning immediately is exactly right because all earlier positions were already checked and failed.

### Invariant
Before checking index i, every index before i has already been proven not to contain the target. If index i also fails, the searched prefix grows by one.

### Mistakes to avoid
