Do not use binary search unless the array is sorted and the problem allows it. Do not return false too early after checking only the first element. Also be clear whether the expected answer is a boolean, an index, or all matching indices.

### Complexity
In the worst case, the target is absent or at the last position, so time is O(N). The search uses O(1) extra space.

find-the-union.name: Optimal (Topic-Specific)
find-the-union.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Union of Sorted Arrays asks for every distinct value that appears in either sorted array. For arr1 = [1, 1, 2, 3, 5] and arr2 = [2, 3, 4, 4, 6], the union is [1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6]. Because the arrays are already sorted, we can merge them like two sorted lists while skipping duplicates.

### Core intuition
Use two pointers, one in each array. Compare the current values. The smaller value is the next union candidate because it cannot appear later in the other array before a smaller value. If values are equal, add it once and move both pointers. After adding any value, skip repeated copies of that same value.

### Why sorted order helps
If arr1[i] is smaller than arr2[j], then arr1[i] should appear before arr2[j] in the union. No future value in arr2 can be smaller than arr2[j], so it is safe to add arr1[i] now. This is the same calm logic used in merge sort's merge step.

### Invariant
The result always contains the sorted distinct union of everything already passed by the two pointers. The next chosen value is the smallest unprocessed candidate among both arrays.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not add equal values twice. Do not forget to skip duplicates inside the same array. Also remember that this is union, not intersection: values unique to either array should still appear.

### Complexity
Each pointer only moves forward, so time is O(N + M). The result can contain up to N + M distinct values, so output space is O(N + M). Extra working state is O(1).

find-missing-number-in-an-array.name: Optimal (Topic-Specific)
find-missing-number-in-an-array.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Find Missing Number usually gives an array containing numbers from 1 to n with exactly one number missing. For [1, 2, 4, 5] with n = 5, the missing number is 3. A common LeetCode variant uses 0 to n instead; the same sum idea works after adjusting the expected range.

### Core intuition
If the full range were present, its sum would be known. For 1 to n, the expected sum is n * (n + 1) / 2. The actual sum is what the array gives you. The missing number is expectedSum - actualSum because every present number cancels itself out.

### Why one pass is enough
We do not need to search for each candidate number. The sum compresses the whole range into one total. Since exactly one number is missing and there are no duplicates, the gap between expected and actual total is exactly that number.

### Invariant
After scanning the first i elements, actualSum equals the sum of those i values. At the end, actualSum contains every present value exactly once, so the difference from the complete range reveals the missing value.

### Mistakes to avoid
Be clear about whether the range is 1 to n or 0 to n. Do not use nums[0] and nums[last] to infer the expected sum if the missing number might be at an edge. Use the known n from constraints or derive it from length carefully. For very large n, consider XOR or a wider integer type to avoid overflow.

### Complexity
The sum scan is O(N), and it uses O(1) extra space.

maximum-consecutive-ones.name: Optimal (Topic-Specific)
maximum-consecutive-ones.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Max Consecutive 1s asks for the length of the longest continuous streak of 1s in a binary array. It is about consecutive elements, not total number of ones. For [1, 1, 0, 1, 1, 1], the answer is 3 because the longest unbroken block is [1, 1, 1].

### Core intuition
Keep a current streak and a best streak. When you see a 1, extend the current streak by one and update best if needed. When you see a 0, the current streak is broken and must reset to 0. The best streak survives resets because it remembers the strongest run seen anywhere earlier.

### Why reset on zero
A zero cannot be part of a consecutive-ones segment. Any streak after that zero must begin fresh on the right side. That is why the current count resets, while the maximum count does not.

### Invariant
After processing index i, currentStreak is the length of the consecutive 1s ending exactly at i if arr[i] is 1, otherwise 0. bestStreak is the longest streak found anywhere in arr[0..i].

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not count all ones in the array. Total ones and consecutive ones are different. Also update the best answer while the streak grows, not only after seeing a zero, because the array may end with its longest streak.

### Complexity
The array is scanned once, so time is O(N). Only two counters are used, so extra space is O(1).

find-the-number-that-appears-once.name: Optimal (Topic-Specific)
find-the-number-that-appears-once.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Single Number asks for the one value that appears exactly once when every other value appears exactly twice. For [4, 1, 2, 1, 2], the answer is 4. The optimal solution uses XOR because duplicate numbers cancel each other out.

### Core intuition
XOR has two properties that make this problem almost poetic: x XOR x = 0, and x XOR 0 = x. It is also order-independent. So if we XOR every array value together, every paired value disappears, and the unpaired value remains.

### Why no extra map is needed
A frequency map would work, but it stores counts. XOR stores the same cancellation effect in one variable. Each duplicate pair erases itself as the scan proceeds, even if the two copies are far apart.

### Invariant
After processing some prefix, xorSoFar equals the XOR of exactly the values in that prefix. Any value that has appeared twice inside the prefix has already canceled to 0.

### Mistakes to avoid
This exact XOR trick assumes every non-answer value appears exactly twice. If values appear three times, use bit counting or another method. Also do not confuse XOR with addition; XOR cancellation is bitwise, not arithmetic subtraction.

### Complexity
The scan is O(N), and the algorithm stores only xorSoFar, so extra space is O(1).

longest-subarray-with-given-sum-k-positi.name: Optimal (Topic-Specific)
longest-subarray-with-given-sum-k-positi.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Longest Subarray with Sum K for positive numbers asks for the maximum length of a contiguous segment whose sum equals K. The positive-only condition is the reason a sliding window works. When all numbers are positive, extending the right side can only increase the sum, and moving the left side can only decrease it.

### Core intuition
Keep a window from left to right and track its sum. Add arr[right] as the window expands. If the sum becomes larger than K, shrink from the left until the sum is at most K. Whenever the sum equals K, update the best length.

### Why positivity matters
With positive numbers, the sum moves predictably. Bigger window means bigger sum, smaller window means smaller sum. That monotonic behavior lets us move each pointer forward only once. If negative numbers are allowed, this logic breaks because adding a number can decrease the sum.

### Invariant
After the shrink step for a given right pointer, the current window has sum <= K. Every removed left value was necessary to get back under control after the sum grew too large.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not use this sliding-window method for arrays with negatives. Do not reset the window when the sum is less than K; just keep expanding. Also remember subarray means contiguous, so you cannot skip elements inside the chosen segment.

### Complexity
Both left and right pointers move forward at most N times, so time is O(N). Extra space is O(1).

longest-subarray-with-given-sum-k-positi.name: Optimal (Topic-Specific)
longest-subarray-with-given-sum-k-positi.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Longest Subarray Sum K with positives and negatives asks for the longest contiguous segment whose sum equals K when numbers can move the sum up or down. The positive-only sliding window is no longer reliable, because adding a negative number can reduce the sum and removing a negative number can increase it.

### Core intuition
Use prefix sums. Let prefixSum at index i mean the sum from the start through i. If a subarray ending at i has sum K, then there must be an earlier prefix sum equal to prefixSum - K. The distance between that earlier index and i is the subarray length.

### Why earliest index matters
For the longest length, when a prefix sum first appears, keep its earliest index. If the same prefix sum appears again later, replacing it would only make future subarrays shorter. Earliest occurrence gives the widest possible window.

### Invariant
After processing index i, the map stores the earliest index for each prefix sum seen so far. bestLength is the longest valid subarray ending at or before i.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not use the positive-only sliding window when negatives are allowed. Do not overwrite an existing prefix sum index. Also seed prefix sum 0 at index -1, so a subarray starting at index 0 can be counted correctly.

### Complexity
The scan is O(N) average time with a hash map. Space is O(N) in the worst case when many prefix sums are distinct.

reverse-pairs.name: Brute Force
reverse-pairs.description: Build a correctness baseline for Reverse Pairs by directly enumerating the candidate states allowed by the problem. This version prioritizes transparent control flow and exhaustive coverage over asymptotic efficiency.

reverse-pairs.name: Optimal (Topic-Specific)
reverse-pairs.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Reverse Pairs asks us to count pairs of indices i < j such that nums[i] > 2 * nums[j]. This is stricter than a normal inversion, where nums[i] > nums[j]. For [1, 3, 2, 3, 1], the reverse pairs are the two 3-and-1 pairs, so the answer is 2.

### Core intuition
The brute force check of every pair is O(N^2). The optimized idea uses merge sort. While the left half and right half are sorted, we can count how many right-side values are less than half of each left-side value using a pointer that only moves forward. Then we merge the halves normally so upper recursion levels also receive sorted ranges.

### Counting before merging
For each value in the sorted left half, move pointer j through the sorted right half while leftValue > 2 * right[j]. Every right value before j forms a reverse pair with this left value. Because the left half is sorted, j never needs to move backward for the next left value.

### Invariant
Before merging a range, both halves are already sorted and all reverse pairs completely inside each half have already been counted. The current step counts only cross pairs, where i is in the left half and j is in the right half.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not confuse reverse pairs with two-sum pairs. The condition is nums[i] > 2 * nums[j], not nums[i] + nums[j] equals a target. Use a wider integer type when doubling nums[j], because 2 * nums[j] can overflow normal int for large values.

### Complexity
Merge sort creates O(log N) levels, and each level does O(N) counting plus merging work. Total time is O(N log N), and the merge buffer uses O(N) extra space.

count-occurrences-of-a-number-in-a-sorte.name: Brute Force
count-occurrences-of-a-number-in-a-sorte.description: Build a correctness baseline for Count Occurrences by directly enumerating the candidate states allowed by the problem. This version prioritizes transparent control flow and exhaustive coverage over asymptotic efficiency.

count-occurrences-of-a-number-in-a-sorte.name: Optimal (Topic-Specific)
count-occurrences-of-a-number-in-a-sorte.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Count Occurrences asks for how many times a target appears in a sorted array. For [1, 2, 2, 2, 3, 4] and target 2, the answer is 3. Since the array is sorted, all copies of the target sit together as one block. The job becomes finding the left edge and right edge of that block.

### Core intuition
Use binary search twice. The first search finds the first index where the target appears. When mid is target, do not stop; record mid and keep searching left. The second search finds the last index. When mid is target, record mid and keep searching right. The count is last - first + 1.

### Why this beats scanning
A normal binary search can find one copy, but one copy does not tell the count. Scanning outward from that copy can degrade to O(N) if the whole array is the target. Two boundary searches stay O(log N) even when there are many duplicates.

### Invariant
During the first search, ans stores the best leftmost target found so far, and the unexplored left side may contain an earlier copy. During the last search, ans stores the best rightmost target found so far, and the unexplored right side may contain a later copy.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not assume the array contains unique values; duplicates are the point of the problem. Do not return 1 after finding a single target. If first occurrence is -1, return 0 immediately because there is no block to measure.

### Complexity
Each boundary search takes O(log N), so total time is O(log N). Extra space is O(1).

search-in-rotated-sorted-array-i.name: Brute Force
search-in-rotated-sorted-array-i.description: Build a correctness baseline for Search in Rotated Array (Unique) by directly enumerating the candidate states allowed by the problem. This version prioritizes transparent control flow and exhaustive coverage over asymptotic efficiency.

search-in-rotated-sorted-array-i.name: Optimal (One-Pass Binary Search)
search-in-rotated-sorted-array-i.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Search in Rotated Array asks us to find a target in a sorted array that has been rotated, with unique values. For [4, 5, 6, 7, 0, 1, 2] and target 0, the answer is index 4. The array is not globally sorted, but every binary-search window still has at least one sorted half.

### Core intuition
Pick mid. If arr[low] <= arr[mid], the left half is sorted. Otherwise, the right half is sorted. Once you know which half is sorted, check whether the target lies inside that half's value range. If it does, keep that half. If it does not, discard that half and search the other side.

### Why one sorted half always exists
A rotated sorted array is two sorted pieces stitched together. Any mid either sits in a way that makes low..mid sorted or mid..high sorted. That sorted half gives a trustworthy range test.

### Invariant
At every step, if the target exists, it is inside the current low..high window. Each decision discards only a half whose sorted range proves the target cannot be there, or whose opposite half is the only possible place left.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not run ordinary binary search by comparing only target with arr[mid]; rotation breaks that. First identify the sorted half, then use range checks. This unique-values version does not need duplicate edge shrinking; that belongs to the duplicate variant.

### Complexity
The search space is halved each time, so time is O(log N). Extra space is O(1).

find-minimum-in-rotated-sorted-array.name: Brute Force
find-minimum-in-rotated-sorted-array.description: Build a correctness baseline for Find Min in Rotated Array by directly enumerating the candidate states allowed by the problem. This version prioritizes transparent control flow and exhaustive coverage over asymptotic efficiency.

find-minimum-in-rotated-sorted-array.name: Optimal (Binary Search on Sorted Half)
find-minimum-in-rotated-sorted-array.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Find Min in Rotated Array asks for the smallest value in a sorted array that was rotated. For [4, 5, 6, 7, 0, 1, 2], the answer is 0. The minimum is the pivot point where the sorted order wraps from large values back to small values.

### Core intuition
Compare the middle with the right edge. If arr[mid] > arr[high], the minimum must be to the right of mid, because mid is still in the larger left segment. If arr[mid] <= arr[high], the right half is sorted and the minimum is at mid or to the left. This keeps the true minimum inside the search window.

### Why the right edge comparison works
The right edge belongs to the lower sorted segment when the pivot is inside the window. If mid is bigger than that right edge, mid is on the high segment before the pivot. If mid is not bigger, mid is already on the low segment or the window is sorted.

### Invariant
The minimum element is always inside low..high. When arr[mid] > arr[high], low becomes mid + 1. Otherwise, high becomes mid because mid itself might be the minimum.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not discard mid when arr[mid] <= arr[high]; mid can be the answer. This classic version assumes distinct elements. With duplicates, equal comparisons require more careful shrinking.

### Complexity
Each step cuts the window roughly in half, so time is O(log N). Extra space is O(1).

find-out-how-many-times-array-has-been-r.name: Optimal (Index of Minimum via Binary Search)
find-out-how-many-times-array-has-been-r.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Rotation Count asks how many times a sorted array was left or right shifted into its current rotated form. For [4, 5, 6, 7, 0, 1, 2], the answer is 4 because the smallest element, 0, sits at index 4. In a distinct sorted array, the index of the minimum equals the rotation count.

### Core intuition
Find the minimum element with binary search, but return its index instead of its value. The minimum marks the boundary where the original sorted order restarted. Everything before it was shifted from the front to the back, or vice versa depending on how the rotation is described.

### Why minimum index equals rotation count
If [0, 1, 2, 4, 5, 6, 7] is rotated left by 4, the value 0 moves to index 4. More generally, after k rotations, the original first element appears at index n - k for right rotations or k for left rotations depending on convention. In this catalog's standard rotated-array framing, the pivot index is treated as the count.

### Invariant
The minimum index is always inside low..high. When mid belongs to the larger left segment, the pivot is to the right. When mid belongs to the smaller right segment, the pivot is at mid or to the left.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not count manually by rotating the array; that is O(N) or worse. Do not return the minimum value when the problem asks for how many rotations. Also clarify convention if an interviewer distinguishes left rotations from right rotations.

### Complexity
The minimum-index search is O(log N), and it uses O(1) extra space.

single-element-in-a-sorted-array.name: Brute Force
single-element-in-a-sorted-array.description: Build a correctness baseline for Single Element in Sorted Array by directly enumerating the candidate states allowed by the problem. This version prioritizes transparent control flow and exhaustive coverage over asymptotic efficiency.

single-element-in-a-sorted-array.name: Optimal (Index Parity via Binary Search)
single-element-in-a-sorted-array.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Single Element in Sorted Array gives a sorted array where every value appears exactly twice except one value that appears once. The target is the single value in O(log N). For [1, 1, 2, 3, 3, 4, 4], the answer is 2.

### Core intuition
Before the single element, pairs start at even indices and end at odd indices: 0-1, 2-3, 4-5. After the single element shifts the alignment, pairs start at odd indices and end at even indices. Binary search looks for the point where this pair parity breaks.

### Practical mid rule
Force mid to be even. If nums[mid] == nums[mid + 1], the pair at mid is healthy, so the single element is to the right. If they differ, the single element is at mid or to the left. This rule is compact and avoids many neighbor cases.

### Invariant
The search window always contains the single element. Healthy even-odd pairs before the answer can be discarded. A broken even-odd pair means the answer has already appeared at or before mid.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not use XOR if the problem specifically asks for O(log N) on a sorted array; XOR is O(N). Be careful not to read mid + 1 out of bounds. The even-mid method usually searches while low < high and keeps high inside the answer side.

### Complexity
Each step halves the search space, so time is O(log N). Extra space is O(1).

find-peak-element.name: Brute Force
find-peak-element.description: Build a correctness baseline for Find Peak Element by directly enumerating the candidate states allowed by the problem. This version prioritizes transparent control flow and exhaustive coverage over asymptotic efficiency.

find-peak-element.name: Optimal (Logarithmic Slope-Following)
find-peak-element.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Find Peak Element asks for any index whose value is greater than its neighbors. The array does not need to be sorted. A peak is local, not necessarily global. In [1, 2, 1, 3, 5, 6, 4], both index 1 with value 2 and index 5 with value 6 are valid peaks.

### Core intuition
Binary search still works because of slopes. Compare nums[mid] with nums[mid + 1]. If nums[mid] < nums[mid + 1], you are climbing upward, so some peak must exist to the right. If nums[mid] > nums[mid + 1], you are going downward from mid toward mid + 1, so a peak exists at mid or to the left.

### Why a peak is guaranteed
Treat values outside the array as negative infinity. If you keep walking toward a higher neighbor, you cannot walk forever without either reaching the edge or eventually falling. The place where the climb stops is a peak.

### Invariant
The current low..high window always contains at least one peak. Each comparison chooses a side that is guaranteed to contain a peak, even though the array is not globally sorted.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not search for the maximum unless the problem asks for the global maximum. Any local peak is acceptable. Also compare with mid + 1 carefully so the loop condition should keep mid + 1 inside bounds, commonly while low < high.

### Complexity
Each step discards half of a peak-containing window, so time is O(log N). Extra space is O(1).

find-square-root-of-a-number-in-log-n.name: Optimal (Binary Search on Answer Range)
find-square-root-of-a-number-in-log-n.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Square Root (Integer) asks for floor(sqrt(n)): the greatest integer x such that x * x <= n. For n = 27, the real square root is a little more than 5, so the integer answer is 5. The exact square root is not required unless n is a perfect square.

### Core intuition
The answer lies between 0 and n, and the function x * x grows monotonically as x grows. That monotonicity makes binary search possible. If mid * mid is at most n, mid is a valid candidate and we try to find a bigger one. If mid * mid is too large, we must search smaller values.

### Why store an answer
For non-perfect squares, the loop may never hit equality. We store the best valid mid seen so far. When the search ends, that stored candidate is the floor square root.

### Invariant
ans is always a number whose square is <= n. The true floor square root is either ans or still inside the current search range. Invalid large mids are removed by moving high left.

### Mistakes to avoid
Avoid mid * mid overflow in fixed-width languages by using long long or by comparing mid <= n / mid. Also handle n = 0 and n = 1 directly.

### Complexity
The numeric search range halves each step, so time is O(log N). Extra space is O(1).

find-the-nth-root-of-a-number.name: Brute Force
find-the-nth-root-of-a-number.description: Build a correctness baseline for Nth Root of an Integer by directly enumerating the candidate states allowed by the problem. This version prioritizes transparent control flow and exhaustive coverage over asymptotic efficiency.

find-the-nth-root-of-a-number.name: Optimal (Binary Search with Overflow Check)
find-the-nth-root-of-a-number.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Nth Root of an Integer asks for an integer x such that x raised to n equals m. If no exact integer root exists, return -1. For n = 3 and m = 27, the answer is 3. For n = 3 and m = 30, the answer is -1.

### Core intuition
The function x^n increases as x increases for positive integers. That means we can binary search x from 1 to m. For each mid, compute mid^n only until it either equals m, stays below m, or crosses above m.

### Why early stopping matters
mid^n can become huge quickly. In languages with fixed-size integers, multiplying blindly can overflow. A safe helper stops as soon as the product exceeds m and reports too large.

### Invariant
If mid^n is smaller than m, the root must be larger than mid. If mid^n is larger than m, the root must be smaller than mid. If it equals m, mid is the exact integer root.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not return floor nth root when the problem asks for exact integer root. For m = 30 and n = 3, floor cube root is 3, but 3^3 is 27, not 30, so the exact-root answer is -1. Also handle m = 1 cleanly.

### Complexity
Binary search takes O(log M) iterations, and each check can multiply up to n times, so time is O(n log M). Extra space is O(1).

kth-missing-positive-number.name: Optimal (Binary Search on Gap Count)
kth-missing-positive-number.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Kth Missing Positive gives a sorted positive array and asks for the kth positive integer that does not appear in it. For arr = [2, 3, 4, 7, 11] and k = 5, the missing positives are 1, 5, 6, 8, 9, 10, so the answer is 9.

### Core intuition
At index i, if no numbers were missing, arr[i] would equal i + 1. Therefore the count of missing numbers before or at arr[i] is arr[i] - (i + 1). This missing-count formula is monotonic, so binary search can find the first index where missingCount >= k.

### How the final formula appears
When the search ends, high points to the last index where missingCount < k. The answer is k + high + 1. Intuitively, we start from k and shift forward by the number of present array values that appear before the answer.

### Invariant
For every index left of low, the missing count is still less than k. For every index right of high after narrowing, the missing count is at least k. The boundary between them tells where the kth missing number falls.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not generate missing numbers one by one for large inputs. Be careful with zero-based indexing in missingCount = arr[i] - (i + 1). Also remember the answer may be beyond the end of the array.

### Complexity
Binary search over the array takes O(log N). Extra space is O(1).

split-array-largest-sum.name: Optimal (Binary Search on Sum)
split-array-largest-sum.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Split Array Largest Sum asks us to split an array into k non-empty contiguous parts while making the largest part sum as small as possible. For nums = [7, 2, 5, 10, 8] and k = 2, the best split is [7, 2, 5] and [10, 8], giving largest sum 18.

### Core intuition
Binary search the answer. The smallest possible largest sum is max(nums), because no subarray can avoid containing the largest single element. The largest possible largest sum is sum(nums), meaning no split at all. For a guessed limit, greedily scan left to right and start a new part whenever adding the next number would exceed the limit.

### Why feasibility is monotonic
If we can split the array into at most k parts with largest sum <= limit, then any larger limit is also feasible. If a limit is too small, every smaller limit is also impossible. That yes-no pattern is exactly what binary search needs.

### Invariant
ans stores the best feasible limit found so far. The true minimum feasible limit remains in the numeric search range until the window closes. The greedy feasibility check always uses the fewest parts possible for that limit.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not reorder the array; parts must be contiguous. Do not set the low bound to 0 because a part must contain the largest element. Also remember that feasible means using at most k parts; if fewer are used, you can always split further without increasing the largest sum when elements are non-negative.

### Complexity
Each feasibility check scans N numbers, and binary search tries O(log(sum - max)) limits. Total time is O(N log S), where S is the answer range. Extra space is O(1).

painter-s-partition-problem.name: Optimal (Binary Search on Time)
painter-s-partition-problem.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Painter's Partition gives board lengths and K painters. Each painter must paint a contiguous sequence of boards, and all painters work in parallel. The goal is to minimize the maximum workload assigned to any painter. For boards [10, 20, 30, 40] and k = 2, the best maximum time is 60.

### Core intuition
This is binary search on the answer. The minimum possible time is the largest single board, because some painter must paint it. The maximum possible time is the sum of all boards, where one painter paints everything. For a guessed time limit, greedily assign boards left to right until adding the next board would exceed the limit, then start a new painter.

### Why the greedy check works
For a fixed limit, the greedy scan uses the fewest painters possible because each painter takes as many contiguous boards as they can without crossing the limit. If even this best packing needs more than K painters, the limit is too small.

### Invariant
ans stores the smallest feasible time seen so far. If a time limit works with at most K painters, all larger limits also work, so search left. If it needs more than K painters, all smaller limits fail, so search right.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not reorder boards; contiguity is mandatory. Do not set low below max(board), because one board cannot be split between painters. Also remember that using fewer than K painters is still feasible because work can be further split without increasing the maximum load.

### Complexity
Each feasibility check is O(N), and binary search runs over the numeric range from max board to total sum. Time is O(N log S), where S is the sum range. Extra space is O(1).

minimize-max-distance-to-gas-station.name: Optimal (Binary Search on Answer Range - Double)
minimize-max-distance-to-gas-station.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Gas Station Distance asks us to add K new stations so the largest distance between adjacent stations becomes as small as possible. Unlike many binary search problems, the answer can be a decimal. If stations are at 0 and 10 and we can add one station, the best maximum distance is 5 by placing it at the middle.

### Core intuition
Binary search over the maximum allowed gap. If we guess a distance D, every original gap G tells us how many stations are needed to break that gap into pieces of length at most D. The needed count is ceil(G / D) - 1. If total needed stations is at most K, D is feasible and we try smaller. If it needs more than K, D is too ambitious and we try larger.

### Why fixed iterations are used
The answer is continuous, so low and high may never meet exactly. Instead of waiting for exact equality, run a fixed number of iterations or stop when high - low is tiny. After enough iterations, the interval is narrower than the required precision.

### Invariant
The true minimum maximum distance stays inside the floating-point range low..high. high is a feasible distance, and low is a distance that is too small or not yet proven feasible.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not use integer binary search here. Do not overcount when a gap divides exactly; for gap 10 and D 5, only one station is needed, not two. The formula ceil(G / D) - 1 handles this correctly.

### Complexity
Each feasibility check scans all gaps, so it is O(N). Running a fixed number of iterations such as 60 or 100 gives O(N * iterations) time and O(1) extra space.

median-of-two-sorted-arrays.name: Brute Force
median-of-two-sorted-arrays.description: Build a correctness baseline for Median of two sorted arrays by directly enumerating the candidate states allowed by the problem. This version prioritizes transparent control flow and exhaustive coverage over asymptotic efficiency.

median-of-two-sorted-arrays.name: Better
median-of-two-sorted-arrays.description: Reduce repeated work in Median of two sorted arrays with the intermediate structure or cached state used by the standard better approach. It preserves the brute-force reasoning while improving one major time or space bottleneck.

median-of-two-sorted-arrays.name: Optimal (Binary Search on Partitions)
median-of-two-sorted-arrays.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Median of two sorted arrays asks for the median of the combined sorted order without fully merging both arrays. For A = [1, 3] and B = [2], the combined order is [1, 2, 3], so the median is 2. The optimal method searches for a valid partition between left-half elements and right-half elements.

### Core intuition
Imagine cutting both arrays so the total number of elements on the left equals half of the combined size. A correct cut has every left-side value less than or equal to every right-side value. Then the median is either the largest left value for odd total length, or the average of largest-left and smallest-right for even total length.

### Why search the smaller array
If we choose how many elements to take from the smaller array, the number to take from the other array is forced. Searching the smaller array keeps the binary search range short and avoids invalid partitions.

### Invariant
The desired partition has leftSize = (n + m + 1) / 2. If left value from A is greater than right value from B, we took too many from A and must move left. If left value from B is greater than right value from A, we took too few from A and must move right.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not merge arrays if the problem expects logarithmic time. Handle empty partition sides with negative infinity and positive infinity sentinels. Always binary search the smaller array.

### Complexity
Binary search runs on the smaller array, so time is O(log min(N, M)). Extra space is O(1).

k-th-element-of-two-sorted-arrays.name: Optimal (Binary Search on Partitions)
k-th-element-of-two-sorted-arrays.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
K-th Element of Sorted Arrays asks for the kth smallest value in the merged order of two sorted arrays, without building that merged array. For A = [2, 3, 6, 7, 9], B = [1, 4, 8, 10], and k = 5, the merged order starts [1, 2, 3, 4, 6], so the answer is 6.

### Core intuition
This is the median partition idea with a custom left size. We need exactly k elements on the combined left side. If we take cutA elements from A, then we must take k - cutA elements from B. The correct partition is where every left-side element is <= every right-side element.

### Bounds matter
cutA cannot be negative, cannot exceed A's length, and cannot force taking more than B has. So low = max(0, k - len(B)) and high = min(k, len(A)). These bounds prevent invalid cuts before the search begins.

### Invariant
The correct cut keeps exactly k elements on the left. If A's left boundary is too large for B's right boundary, take fewer from A. If B's left boundary is too large for A's right boundary, take more from A.

### Mistakes to avoid
Be clear whether k is 1-based or 0-based; this problem usually uses 1-based k. Do not merge the arrays for the optimal solution. Use sentinels for empty sides at the edges.

### Complexity
Binary search runs on the smaller array, so time is O(log min(N, M)). Extra space is O(1).

count-number-of-substrings.name: Optimal (AtMost(K) - AtMost(K-1))
count-number-of-substrings.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Count Number of Substrings with K distinct characters asks for how many substrings contain exactly K different characters. For s = pqpqs and k = 2, the valid substrings include pq, pqp, pqpq, qp, qpq, pq, and qs, so the answer is 7.

### Core intuition
Counting exactly K directly is awkward because shrinking a window can jump between too many and too few distinct characters. The cleaner trick is: exactlyK = atMostK - atMost(K - 1). A substring with exactly K distinct characters is counted by atMostK, but not counted by atMost(K - 1).

### How atMost works
Use a sliding window with a frequency map. Expand right one character at a time. If distinct count becomes greater than K, shrink left until the window is valid again. Once valid, every substring ending at right and starting anywhere from left to right is valid, so add right - left + 1.

### Invariant
Inside countAtMost(K), after shrinking, the window left..right has at most K distinct characters. The total has already counted every valid substring ending at previous right positions.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not confuse subsequences with substrings; substrings are contiguous. Also handle k = 0 by returning 0 for atMost. If the alphabet is not limited to lowercase English letters, use a map instead of a fixed 26-size array.

### Complexity
countAtMost runs in O(N). Running it twice still gives O(N) time. Space is O(A), where A is alphabet size, often O(1) for fixed lowercase letters.

sum-of-beauty-of-all-substrings.name: Optimal (Nested Frequency Tracking)
sum-of-beauty-of-all-substrings.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Sum of Beauty of Substrings asks us to examine every substring and compute its beauty. Beauty means maximum character frequency minus minimum non-zero character frequency inside that substring. Then we add all those beauty values together. For s = aabcb, the total beauty is 5.

### Core intuition
Fix a starting index and extend the ending index one character at a time. As the substring grows, update a frequency table for its characters. At each extension, scan the frequency table to find the largest count and the smallest positive count, then add their difference to the answer.

### Why incremental counting helps
Without incremental frequency updates, every substring would need to be rebuilt and recounted from scratch. Instead, when end moves one step, only one character frequency changes. That turns a cubic brute force habit into a clean O(N^2 * alphabet) approach.

### Invariant
For a fixed start index, after processing end index j, the frequency table exactly describes s[start..j]. The running total already includes beauty for every substring ending before j for this same start.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not use zero frequencies when computing the minimum; characters absent from the substring should be ignored. Also remember that single-character substrings have beauty 0 because max and min positive frequency are both 1.

### Complexity
There are O(N^2) substrings. For lowercase English letters, scanning 26 frequencies is constant, so time is O(N^2) with O(1) extra space.

reverse-every-word-in-a-string.name: Optimal (Tokenize & Reverse)
reverse-every-word-in-a-string.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Reverse Every Word asks us to reverse the characters inside each word while keeping the word order the same. For hello world, the answer is olleh dlrow. This is different from reversing the whole sentence, which would also change word order.

### Core intuition
A sentence is a chain of word segments separated by spaces. Each segment can be reversed independently. Once a word boundary is found, reverse the characters from that word's start to its end, then continue with the next word.

### Two implementation styles
One style splits the string by spaces, reverses each token, and joins the tokens again. Another style scans the string and reverses each word range in place when using a mutable character array. The idea is the same: local reversal only.

### Invariant
After finishing a word, that word is reversed in the output, and all earlier words remain in their original order. Spaces continue to mark the same boundaries unless the problem says to normalize whitespace.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not reverse the order of words. Do not accidentally collapse multiple spaces if the problem says whitespace positions must be preserved. If using split and join, confirm whether the language preserves empty tokens for repeated spaces.

### Complexity
Every character is visited a constant number of times, so time is O(N). The extra space is O(N) for immutable-string approaches, or O(1) auxiliary space if reversing a mutable character array in place.

introduction-to-linkedlist-insert-delete.name: Optimal (Node Construction & Traversal)
introduction-to-linkedlist-insert-delete.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact topic is teaching
Introduction to Linked List teaches the basic shape of a singly linked list. A node stores data and a next pointer. The head pointer gives entry into the chain. Unlike an array, nodes do not need to sit next to each other in memory; each node knows where the next node lives.

### Core intuition
Think of a linked list as a treasure trail. You cannot jump to the third clue directly unless you already have its address. You start at head, read the current node, then follow next. The final node points to null, which tells traversal to stop.

### Building from an array
To build 10 -> 20 -> 30, create the head node with 10. Keep a current pointer at the tail of the constructed list. For every next value, create a node and set current.next to that node, then move current forward.

### Invariant
At any moment while building, head points to the first node and current points to the last node in the chain built so far. Every node before current is connected in the correct order.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not lose the head pointer while traversing; use a temporary pointer. Do not forget to set next to null for the last node. Also remember that linked lists have O(N) access by position because reaching a position requires walking through pointers.

### Complexity
Constructing or traversing N nodes takes O(N) time. The list stores N nodes, so space is O(N).

insert-a-node-in-linkedlist.name: Optimal (Case-based Insertion)
insert-a-node-in-linkedlist.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Insert Node in LinkedList asks us to add a new node without breaking the existing chain. The insertion might be at the head, at the tail, or at a specific position. The main skill is pointer order: the new node must point to the next part of the list before the previous node starts pointing to the new node.

### Core intuition
To insert between prev and curr, create newNode. Set newNode.next = curr. Then set prev.next = newNode. That two-step order preserves the rest of the list. Inserting at the head is a special case because there is no previous node; the new node simply points to the old head and becomes the new head.

### Position thinking
For a 1-based position k, insertion at k means the new node becomes the kth node. You usually traverse to node k - 1, then splice after it. For tail insertion, traverse until current.next is null and attach there.

### Invariant
Before rewiring, the original list is intact. After setting newNode.next, the new node already knows the continuation. After setting prev.next, the chain includes the new node and still reaches every old node.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not set prev.next = newNode before assigning newNode.next, or you may lose access to the rest of the list. Handle empty lists and head insertion separately. Also be clear whether positions are 0-based or 1-based.

### Complexity
Head insertion is O(1). Tail or kth-position insertion is O(N) without a stored tail pointer. Extra space is O(1) besides the new node itself.

delete-a-node-in-linkedlist.name: Optimal (Pointer Manipulation)
delete-a-node-in-linkedlist.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Delete Node in LinkedList asks us to remove a node while keeping the remaining nodes connected. For deleting position 2 from 10 -> 20 -> 30 -> 40, the result is 10 -> 30 -> 40. The value 20 disappears from the chain because the previous node skips over it.

### Core intuition
To delete a middle node, find its previous node. If prev points to target and target points to next, then set prev.next = next. The target node is no longer reachable from head. In languages with manual memory management, free or delete the removed node.

### Head and tail cases
Deleting the head is special because head itself must move to head.next. Deleting the tail means the second-to-last node's next becomes null. Middle deletion links around the target.

### Invariant
Before deletion, the list is a connected chain. After deletion, every node except the target remains reachable from head in the same relative order.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not traverse past null when k is out of range. Do not forget to update head when deleting the first node. In manual-memory languages, do not use the deleted node after freeing it.

### Complexity
Deleting the head is O(1). Deleting by position or tail is O(N) because we may need to traverse to the predecessor. Extra space is O(1).

find-the-length-of-the-linkedlist.name: Optimal (Linear Traversal)
find-the-length-of-the-linkedlist.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Length of LinkedList asks for the number of nodes in a singly linked list. A linked list does not usually store its length as a field, and nodes are not placed at array indexes. The only reliable path is to start at head and follow next pointers until null.

### Core intuition
Think of curr as a finger on the current node. Every time curr points to a real node, count it, then move curr to curr.next. When curr becomes null, we have stepped past the tail and counted every node exactly once.

### Why this cannot be faster in a plain list
Without a stored size field, there is no shortcut. The fifth node only knows the sixth, not how many nodes come after it. To know the total, we must visit each node in the chain.

### Invariant
Before each loop iteration, count equals the number of nodes already passed. curr points to the next uncounted node. When curr is null, there are no uncounted nodes left.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not forget to move curr forward, or the loop will never end. Do not count null as a node. Also handle an empty list: if head is null, the loop never runs and the answer is 0.

### Complexity
The traversal visits N nodes, so time is O(N). It uses only curr and count, so space is O(1).

search-an-element-in-the-ll.name: Optimal (Linear Search)
search-an-element-in-the-ll.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Search in LinkedList asks whether a target value exists somewhere in a singly linked list. Since a linked list has no random access, we cannot jump to the middle or use binary search. We must inspect nodes one by one from head to tail.

### Core intuition
At each node, compare node.data with the target. If it matches, we can stop immediately and return true. If it does not match, move to the next node. If the pointer reaches null, every node has been checked and the target is absent.

### Why early return is safe
The moment a match is found, the answer cannot become more true later. There is no need to count all occurrences unless the problem asks for that. This problem is existence search, so one match is enough.

### Invariant
All nodes before curr have already been checked and did not match the target. curr is the next node that might contain the target. If curr becomes null, no unchecked nodes remain.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not compare the node object itself with the target unless the target is a node reference. Most versions compare curr.data. Do not skip the head node. Also remember to return false after the loop finishes.

### Complexity
Worst-case time is O(N), when the target is at the tail or missing. Extra space is O(1).

introduction-to-doubly-linkedlist.name: Optimal (Node Construction & Mapping)
introduction-to-doubly-linkedlist.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact topic is teaching
Introduction to Doubly Linked List teaches a node shape with three fields: data, next, and prev. The next pointer moves forward. The prev pointer moves backward. This extra backward link is what makes a doubly linked list different from a singly linked list.

### Core intuition
In a singly linked list, each node only knows who comes next. In a doubly linked list, each node also knows who came before it. That means every neighboring pair must agree in both directions: if A.next is B, then B.prev should be A.

### Building from an array
To build a DLL from [10, 20, 30], create 10 as head with prev = null. Then create 20, set 10.next = 20 and 20.prev = 10. Then create 30, set 20.next = 30 and 30.prev = 20. The tail's next remains null.

### Invariant
For every connected pair, current.next.prev should point back to current. head.prev is null, and tail.next is null. These two boundary nulls tell traversal where the list begins and ends.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not set only next pointers; then the list is not truly doubly linked. Do not make head.prev point to itself. Also update both directions whenever inserting or deleting, or backward traversal will break even if forward traversal looks fine.

### Complexity
Constructing N nodes takes O(N) time and O(N) node storage. Traversing forward or backward is O(N).

insert-a-node-in-dll.name: Optimal (Bi-directional Linking)
insert-a-node-in-dll.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Insert Node in DLL asks us to place a new node into a doubly linked list while preserving both forward and backward links. Depending on the position, the new node may become the head, become the tail, or sit between two existing nodes.

### Core intuition
In a singly linked list, insertion mainly worries about next. In a doubly linked list, every affected relationship must be repaired in both directions. If the new node is inserted between prevNode and nextNode, set newNode.prev = prevNode, newNode.next = nextNode, prevNode.next = newNode, and if nextNode exists, nextNode.prev = newNode.

### Pointer order that stays safe
Before overwriting prevNode.next, store the old successor in a variable. Then connect the new node to both neighbors. Finally connect the neighbors back to the new node. This keeps access to the rest of the list throughout the operation.

### Invariant
After insertion, every modified pair must satisfy forward and backward symmetry. If A.next is B, then B.prev should be A. The head still has prev = null, and the tail still has next = null.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not update only one side of the connection. Do not forget the head case, where there is no previous node. Do not set nextNode.prev when nextNode is null at tail insertion.

### Complexity
Head insertion is O(1). Tail or kth-position insertion can be O(N) because we may need to traverse to the insertion point. Extra space is O(1) besides the new node.

delete-a-node-in-dll.name: Optimal (Bi-directional Bypass)
delete-a-node-in-dll.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Delete Node in DLL asks us to remove a node from a doubly linked list while keeping the remaining nodes connected in both directions. The deleted node may be the head, the tail, or a middle node.

### Core intuition
For a middle node curr, let p = curr.prev and s = curr.next. To remove curr, bridge around it: set p.next = s and s.prev = p. After that, curr is no longer reachable from the list. Head and tail cases are the same idea with one missing neighbor.

### Why DLL deletion is simpler with a node pointer
In a singly linked list, deleting a known node usually requires finding its previous node. In a doubly linked list, curr.prev already gives the previous node, so if curr is directly provided, deletion can be O(1). If only position k is provided, traversal to the kth node still costs O(N).

### Invariant
After deletion, all remaining adjacent nodes still satisfy next-prev symmetry. The new head, if any, has prev = null. The new tail, if any, has next = null.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not access curr.prev when curr is the head without checking for null. Do not forget to update s.prev, or backward traversal will still point to the deleted node. In manual-memory languages, do not use curr after freeing it.

### Complexity
Deleting a directly given node is O(1). Deleting by index is O(N) due to traversal. Extra space is O(1).

reverse-a-doubly-linkedlist.name: Brute Force
reverse-a-doubly-linkedlist.description: Build a correctness baseline for Reverse a Doubly LinkedList by directly enumerating the candidate states allowed by the problem. This version prioritizes transparent control flow and exhaustive coverage over asymptotic efficiency.

reverse-a-doubly-linkedlist.name: Optimal (Pointer Swapping)
reverse-a-doubly-linkedlist.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Reverse a Doubly LinkedList asks us to flip the order of a DLL in place. Because every node has next and prev, reversing means swapping those two pointers for every node. The old tail becomes the new head.

### Core intuition
At a node, its next pointer originally points forward and prev points backward. In the reversed list, those roles swap. So for each node, exchange next and prev. After swapping, the original next node is now reachable through curr.prev, so the traversal moves using curr = curr.prev.

### Why moving through curr.prev after swapping works
Before the swap, curr.next is the next original node. After the swap, that same original next node has moved into curr.prev. So curr.prev is the correct way to keep walking through the original list while reversing it.

### Invariant
All nodes already processed have their next and prev pointers reversed correctly. curr points to the next original node that still needs swapping. The last processed node will become the new head when traversal passes the old tail.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not move with curr.next after swapping; curr.next now points backward to the previous original node. Do not forget the empty and single-node cases. Also make sure the returned head is the old tail after all swaps.

### Complexity
Each node is visited once, so time is O(N). The reversal uses only a few pointer variables, so extra space is O(1).

find-the-starting-point-in-ll.name: Brute Force
find-the-starting-point-in-ll.description: Build a correctness baseline for Starting Point of Cycle by directly enumerating the candidate states allowed by the problem. This version prioritizes transparent control flow and exhaustive coverage over asymptotic efficiency.

find-the-starting-point-in-ll.name: Optimal (First Meeting + Head Meeting)
find-the-starting-point-in-ll.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Starting Point of Cycle asks for the exact node where a linked-list loop begins. Detecting that a loop exists is not enough. We need the first node that gets repeated when following next pointers from head.

### Core intuition
First use slow and fast pointers to detect a meeting inside the cycle. slow moves one step, fast moves two. If they meet, a cycle exists. Then reset one pointer to head and keep the other at the meeting point. Move both one step at a time. They meet again exactly at the cycle entry.

### Why the reset works
Let the distance from head to cycle entry be L, and let the meeting point be some distance inside the loop. The faster pointer's extra travel is a whole number of cycle lengths. That relationship makes the distance from meeting point to entry equal to the distance from head to entry, modulo the cycle length. Moving both one step aligns them at the entry.

### Invariant
Phase one either proves there is no loop when fast reaches null, or finds a meeting node inside the loop. In phase two, both pointers move at equal speed, and their remaining distance to the cycle entry shrinks together until they meet there.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not return the first meeting point as the entry; it is usually just somewhere inside the loop. Do not reset both pointers to head. Also handle the no-cycle case by returning null if fast reaches the end.

### Complexity
Both phases together are O(N). The algorithm uses only two pointers, so space is O(1).

length-of-loop-in-ll.name: Optimal (Meeting Point Traversal)
length-of-loop-in-ll.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Length of Cycle Loop asks us to measure how many nodes are inside a linked-list cycle. This is one step beyond detecting a cycle. Once slow and fast meet, we know they are somewhere inside the loop, but we still need to walk around the loop once to count its size.

### Core intuition
Floyd's slow-fast phase gives a meeting node inside the cycle. From that meeting node, keep one pointer fixed in your mind and move another pointer one step at a time. Count every node visited until the moving pointer returns to the meeting node. That count is exactly the loop length.

### Why this works
Inside a cycle, following next pointers will eventually return to the same node. There are no nulls once we are inside the loop. Starting from any cycle node and walking until we return to it visits every cycle node exactly once.

### Invariant
After a meeting is found, count represents the number of distinct cycle nodes walked from the meeting point so far. curr keeps moving through the loop. When curr returns to the meeting point, the full loop has been measured.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not start counting before a cycle is confirmed. Do not return the distance from head to the meeting point; that is not the cycle length. Initialize count carefully: if curr starts at meeting.next, count should start at 1 for the meeting node.

### Complexity
Cycle detection plus loop measurement is O(N). Only slow, fast, curr, and count are stored, so space is O(1).

segregate-even-and-odd-nodes-in-ll.name: Optimal (Node Re-linking)
segregate-even-and-odd-nodes-in-ll.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Segregate Even and Odd Nodes in this catalog means odd-positioned nodes first, then even-positioned nodes. It does not mean odd values and even values. For 1 -> 2 -> 3 -> 4 -> 5, the result is 1 -> 3 -> 5 -> 2 -> 4 because positions 1, 3, 5 come before positions 2, 4.

### Core intuition
Maintain two chains inside the same list. The odd pointer walks through odd-positioned nodes, and the even pointer walks through even-positioned nodes. evenHead remembers the first even node so we can attach the even chain after the odd chain at the end.

### Why the relinking pattern is safe
odd.next = odd.next.next skips the current even node and links odd to the next odd-positioned node. even.next = even.next.next skips the current odd node and links even to the next even-positioned node. The two chains are being woven out of the original alternating chain.

### Invariant
At every step, the odd chain contains odd-positioned nodes encountered so far in original order, and the even chain contains even-positioned nodes encountered so far in original order. evenHead always points to the first even node.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not group by node values. Do not lose evenHead, or you cannot attach the even chain at the end. Also handle lists with zero, one, or two nodes without assuming even.next exists.

### Complexity
The algorithm touches each node once, so time is O(N). It only rewires existing pointers, so extra space is O(1).

delete-the-middle-node-of-ll.name: Optimal (One Pass - Tortoise / Hare)
delete-the-middle-node-of-ll.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Delete Middle Node asks us to remove the middle node of a singly linked list. In the usual version, if the length is even, the second middle is deleted. We must return the head of the modified list, and if the list has only one node, deleting the middle leaves an empty list.

### Core intuition
Use slow and fast pointers to locate the middle. slow moves one step, fast moves two. When fast reaches the end, slow is at the middle. Because deletion in a singly linked list needs the previous node, we also keep prev, the node right before slow.

### Why prev is necessary
If slow is the node to delete, we cannot move backward from slow. The only way to remove slow from the chain is to set prev.next = slow.next. Without prev, we know the target but cannot bridge around it.

### Invariant
During traversal, prev is always the node immediately before slow. When the loop ends, slow is the node to delete and prev is ready to reconnect the list.

### Mistakes to avoid
Handle the single-node list separately. Do not delete the first middle in an even-length list if the platform expects the second middle. Do not forget to return the original head after relinking, unless the deleted node was the only node.

### Complexity
The two-pointer scan is O(N), and the deletion is O(1). Extra space is O(1).

sort-ll.name: Optimal (Merge Sort)
sort-ll.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Sort LinkedList asks us to reorder a linked list in nondecreasing order. The catalog uses merge sort because linked lists do not support efficient random access for quicksort-style partitioning or binary indexed operations. Merge sort fits lists naturally: split by pointers, sort halves, then merge by relinking nodes.

### Core intuition
A linked list can be divided by slow and fast pointers. Once split into two halves, each half is sorted recursively. Then the merge step compares the heads of two sorted lists and appends the smaller node to the result. Since nodes already exist, the merge can reuse pointers instead of copying arrays.

### Why merge sort is a good list sort
Arrays make merging expensive unless extra space is used, but linked lists can stitch nodes together by changing next pointers. Also, finding the middle with slow and fast gives balanced halves, creating O(log N) levels of recursion.

### Invariant
At each merge step, the output chain is sorted, and every node appended to it is the smallest remaining head among the two sorted input chains. At each recursive return, the returned sublist is fully sorted.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not forget to cut the list into two halves; if the first half still points to the second, recursion may never shrink. Do not allocate brand-new nodes unnecessarily. Also handle empty and single-node lists as base cases.

### Complexity
Splitting and merging across each level costs O(N), and there are O(log N) levels, so time is O(N log N). Recursive stack space is O(log N).

find-the-intersection-point-of-y-ll.name: Brute Force
find-the-intersection-point-of-y-ll.description: Build a correctness baseline for Intersection of Two LLs (Y-Shape) by directly enumerating the candidate states allowed by the problem. This version prioritizes transparent control flow and exhaustive coverage over asymptotic efficiency.

find-the-intersection-point-of-y-ll.name: Better
find-the-intersection-point-of-y-ll.description: Reduce repeated work in Intersection of Two LLs (Y-Shape) with the intermediate structure or cached state used by the standard better approach. It preserves the brute-force reasoning while improving one major time or space bottleneck.
find-the-intersection-point-of-y-ll.timeComplexity: O(n + m) , Iterating through list 1 first takes O(n)

find-the-intersection-point-of-y-ll.name: Optimal (Equidistant Pointer Sync)
find-the-intersection-point-of-y-ll.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Intersection of Two LLs asks for the first shared node by reference, not by value. Two lists may look like a Y shape: they start separately, then both point into the same tail chain. The answer is the node object where the two paths become identical.

### Core intuition
If one list is longer, its pointer reaches the shared tail later. The elegant trick is to let pointer A walk list A, then list B, and pointer B walk list B, then list A. After both have walked the same total distance, they line up at the intersection. If there is no intersection, they both become null at the same time.

### Why switching heads works
Pointer A travels lengthA + lengthB. Pointer B travels lengthB + lengthA. That makes their total journey equal. The extra prefix of the longer list is canceled out by the switch, so both pointers enter the shared tail together.

### Invariant
After each pointer switches lists at most once, both pointers have the same remaining distance to the intersection or to null. When p1 == p2, that reference is either the intersection node or null.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not compare node values; two different nodes can store the same value. Do not reset a pointer to its own head after reaching null; it must switch to the other list's head. Also make sure the loop stops when the references match.

### Complexity
Each pointer traverses at most N + M nodes, so time is O(N + M). Only two pointers are stored, so space is O(1).

add-two-numbers-represented-by-ll.name: Optimal (Iterative Carry Tracking)
add-two-numbers-represented-by-ll.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Add Two Numbers represents two non-negative integers as linked lists, with the ones digit at the head. We must return a new linked list representing their sum in the same reversed digit order. This mirrors grade-school addition from right to left.

### Core intuition
Because the least significant digit comes first, we can add from head to tail directly. At each step, take digit from list one, digit from list two, and the carry from the previous column. The output digit is sum % 10, and the new carry is floor(sum / 10).

### Why a dummy head helps
The result list is built node by node. A dummy head avoids special logic for the first result node. curr always points to the tail of the result built so far, and curr.next receives the next digit.

### Invariant
Before each loop iteration, the result list contains the correct sum digits for all processed positions. carry contains the overflow that must be added to the next position.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not stop when one list ends; the other list may still have digits. Do not forget a final carry after both lists are empty. Also remember that the digits are reversed in this version, so no stack or recursion is needed.

### Complexity
The loop runs for the length of the longer list plus a possible final carry, so time is O(max(N, M)). The result list uses O(max(N, M)) space.

reverse-ll-in-groups-of-size-k.name: Optimal (Recursive Batching)
reverse-ll-in-groups-of-size-k.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Reverse Nodes in K-Group asks us to reverse the linked list in chunks of size k. If the last chunk has fewer than k nodes, it must remain unchanged. This means we cannot just reverse whenever we see some nodes; we must first prove a full group exists.

### Core intuition
For each group, find the kth node from the current head. If it does not exist, return the current head unchanged. If it exists, detach that k-node segment, reverse it, then connect the old head of the segment to the recursively processed remainder.

### Why the old head becomes the connector
After reversing a group, the old head becomes the tail of that reversed group. That tail's next pointer should point to the head of the next processed group. This is why the recursive call is assigned to head.next after reversal.

### Invariant
At each recursive layer, either fewer than k nodes remain and they are returned unchanged, or exactly k nodes are reversed and correctly connected to the solution for the remaining list.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not reverse a partial final group. Do not forget to save nextNode before cutting kth.next. Do not lose track of the old head; it becomes the tail that connects to the rest.

### Complexity
Every node participates in constant pointer work, so time is O(N). Recursive batching uses O(N / K) stack frames. The pointer reversal itself uses O(1) extra space.

rotate-a-ll.name: Brute Force
rotate-a-ll.description: Build a correctness baseline for Rotate LinkedList by directly enumerating the candidate states allowed by the problem. This version prioritizes transparent control flow and exhaustive coverage over asymptotic efficiency.

rotate-a-ll.name: Optimal (Loop Completion + Selective Severing)
rotate-a-ll.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Rotate LinkedList asks us to shift the list to the right by k places. The relative order of nodes does not change; only the head position changes. For 1 -> 2 -> 3 -> 4 -> 5 rotated by 2, the last two nodes move to the front: 4 -> 5 -> 1 -> 2 -> 3.

### Core intuition
A rotation is easiest if we temporarily make the list circular. First find the length and tail. Then reduce k with k % length because rotating by the full length changes nothing. Connect tail.next to head to form a ring. The new tail is length - k - 1 steps from the old head, and newHead is newTail.next. Break the ring there.

### Why modulo matters
If length is 5 and k is 7, rotating 7 times is the same as rotating 2 times. Without k % length, we would walk extra laps and risk choosing the wrong break point.

### Invariant
After forming the ring, every node remains in the same circular order. Breaking the ring after the new tail produces the rotated list with no node lost and no cycle left behind.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not leave tail.next pointing to head at the end, or the result will still be cyclic. Handle empty, single-node, and k = 0 cases early. Do not forget to return the original head when k % length is 0.

### Complexity
Finding length and locating the break point takes O(N). The algorithm stores only a few pointers and counters, so space is O(1).

print-all-subsequences.name: Optimal (Pick / Unpick Recursion)
print-all-subsequences.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Print All Subsequences asks us to generate every possible subsequence of an array or string. A subsequence keeps original order but may skip elements. For [1, 2], the subsequences are [1, 2], [1], [2], and [].

### Core intuition
For each element, there are exactly two choices: pick it or do not pick it. Recursion turns these choices into a binary decision tree. At index i, the path already contains decisions for elements before i. Once i reaches the end, the current path is one complete subsequence.

### Why backtracking is needed
When we pick an element, we push it into the path and recurse. After returning, we pop it before exploring the unpick branch. That pop restores the path to the state it had before the pick decision, so sibling branches do not accidentally share choices.

### Invariant
At recursive call solve(index, path), path contains exactly the elements picked from positions 0 through index - 1. No decision has been made yet for index and beyond.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not forget to pop after the pick branch. Do not print only when path is non-empty unless the problem excludes the empty subsequence. Also remember the output count is exponential: 2^N subsequences.

### Complexity
There are 2^N subsequences, and copying or printing each may cost up to O(N), so time is O(2^N * N). Recursion depth is O(N).

learn-all-patterns-of-subsequences.name: Conceptual Model (Decisional Tree)
learn-all-patterns-of-subsequences.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact topic is teaching
Patterns of Subsequences is a foundation topic for recursion and backtracking. It teaches that many problems are the same decision tree with different base-case behavior: print all answers, count answers, return one answer, or prune impossible branches early.

### Core intuition
At each index, we choose take or not take. That single pattern can generate all subsequences, count subsequences with a target sum, stop after finding one valid subsequence, or prune a branch if a constraint is already broken. The skeleton stays the same; the return value and base case change.

### The three common patterns
For printing, the base case records the current path. For counting, the base case returns 1 or 0 and parent calls add the two branch counts. For existence, a branch can return true and stop the rest of the search. For pruning, a branch can return early before reaching the end if it can no longer become valid.

### Invariant
At index i, all decisions before i are fixed in the current path or current sum. The recursive call is responsible only for deciding elements from i onward.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not mix printing logic with counting logic without changing return values. Do not forget to undo a take decision before exploring not-take. Also do not prune unless the condition is mathematically safe, such as positive numbers with currentSum > target.

### Complexity
The full decision tree has O(2^N) states. Recursion depth is O(N). Some variants add O(N) per leaf for copying a path, and pruning can reduce practical work when the constraint allows it.

count-all-subsequences-with-sum-k.name: Optimal (Recursive Pick/Unpick Summation)
count-all-subsequences-with-sum-k.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Count Subsequences with Sum K asks for the number of subsequences whose chosen values add exactly to K. A subsequence is not required to be contiguous. For every element, we either take it or leave it, but the original order is still respected. The answer is a count, not a list, so the recursive function should return numbers that can be added together.

### Core intuition
Imagine each index as a doorway with two corridors. One corridor says include this value in the running sum. The other says walk past it. At the end of the array, the story is complete: if the sum is K, that exact path contributes 1 valid subsequence; otherwise it contributes 0. Parent calls add the two answers because the take and skip corridors represent disjoint choices.

### Invariant
At solve(index, currentSum), all decisions before index are already fixed. The function's job is only to count how many ways the suffix from index onward can finish the target.

### Why this is not a sliding window
A beginner might try to move left and right pointers, but subsequences can skip elements anywhere. Since each element has an independent take or skip decision, the decision tree is the natural first solution.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not stop at currentSum == K unless the problem guarantees all remaining numbers are positive and you are counting non-empty behavior carefully. Skipping future zeros may create additional valid subsequences. Also do not mutate one shared path unless you backtrack correctly; for counting, you usually do not need a path at all.

### Complexity
There are 2 choices for each of N elements, so time is O(2^N). The recursion stack is O(N). A dynamic programming version can optimize repeated states, but this entry is about understanding the pure subsequence recursion first.

check-if-there-exists-a-subsequence-with.name: Optimal (Backtracking with Early Exit)
check-if-there-exists-a-subsequence-with.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Existence of Subsequence with Sum K asks only whether at least one subsequence adds to K. It does not ask how many there are, and it does not ask us to print the chosen elements. That one word, existence, changes the personality of the recursion: once one branch succeeds, the rest of the tree can be ignored.

### Core intuition
Use the same take or skip decision tree as normal subsequences, but make each call return a boolean. If the take branch returns true, immediately send true back upward. If the take branch fails, try the skip branch. This makes the recursion feel like a search party: the first valid path is enough.

### Invariant
At solve(index, currentSum), the question is: can any subsequence formed from index to the end complete a total of K, given the decisions already made before index? The answer is true or false.

### Safe pruning
If all values are positive and currentSum is already greater than K, no future take decision can reduce the sum, so the branch can return false early. If the array may contain zero or negative numbers, that pruning is no longer automatically safe. A later negative value could bring the sum back down.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not keep counting after finding one answer. That wastes time and changes the meaning of the return value. Also be careful with the base case: when index reaches N, return whether currentSum equals K. Returning true just because currentSum once equaled K can be wrong in variants where empty future choices or zeros matter.

### Complexity
Worst-case time is O(2^N), because the valid subsequence might not exist or might be found late. The recursion stack uses O(N). In practice, early true returns and safe pruning can cut off large parts of the tree.

subset-sum-ii.name: Optimal (Sorted Loop-based Backtracking)
subset-sum-ii.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Subset Sum II, commonly known as Subsets II, asks for every unique subset when the input may contain duplicate values. The tricky part is not generating subsets; the tricky part is avoiding duplicate subsets such as two identical [2] answers coming from two different copies of the value 2.

### Core intuition
Sort the array first. Sorting brings equal values together, which lets us make a very precise rule: at the same recursion depth, do not start two branches with the same value. The first 2 at that depth is allowed. The second adjacent 2 is skipped because it would create the same family of subsets that the first 2 already created.

### Loop-based backtracking
Instead of a strict take or skip call for each index, use a loop from start to the end. At each level, record the current path as one valid subset, then choose the next element to append. After recursion returns, pop the element and continue the loop.

### Invariant
At recursion level start, path already contains a unique prefix. The loop may append any value from start onward, but duplicate values are allowed only when they are reached through deeper levels, not as duplicate starts at the same level.

### Mistakes to avoid
The skip condition must be depth-aware: skip when i > start and nums[i] equals nums[i - 1]. Do not skip every duplicate globally, because [2,2] is a valid subset when two copies exist. Also remember to add a copy of path, not the mutable path object itself.

### Complexity
