There can be up to 2^N unique subset choices, and copying each subset can cost O(N), so the usual bound is O(2^N * N). The recursion stack is O(N), not counting the output.

combination-sum-iii.name: Optimal (Backtracking with Depth and Sum Limits)
combination-sum-iii.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Combination Sum III asks for combinations of exactly K distinct numbers from 1 through 9 that add to N. Each number can be used at most once, and order does not matter. That means [1,2,4] and [2,1,4] are the same combination, so the recursion must build numbers in increasing order.

### Core intuition
Treat the digits 1 to 9 as a small shelf. Starting from a number, either choose it and move to the next number, or skip it and move to the next number. The path must satisfy two locks at the same time: path length must become K, and remaining target must become 0.

### Invariant
At solve(nextNumber, remaining, path), every value in path is strictly increasing and already chosen from numbers smaller than nextNumber. Therefore no number can repeat, and no rearranged duplicate combination can appear.

### Pruning that makes the story shorter
If path length is already K, only remaining == 0 can succeed. If remaining drops below 0, the branch is too heavy. If nextNumber passes 9, the shelf is empty. More advanced versions can also prune when there are not enough numbers left to fill K slots, but the core idea works without that optimization.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not restart from 1 after choosing a number, because that allows reuse and reordered duplicates. Do not accept a sum of N unless exactly K numbers were chosen. Also keep backtracking clean: push a number, recurse, then pop it before exploring the skip branch.

### Complexity
The search space is bounded by subsets of 9 numbers, so time is O(2^9), effectively constant for this fixed problem. Stack depth is at most 9, and the active path length is at most K.

letter-combinations-of-a-phone-number.name: Optimal (Backtracking with Mapping)
letter-combinations-of-a-phone-number.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Letter Combinations of a Phone Number gives a string of digits from 2 to 9 and asks for all strings formed by replacing each digit with one of its phone keypad letters. Digit 2 maps to a, b, c; digit 3 maps to d, e, f; and so on. The answer is the Cartesian product of those letter groups.

### Core intuition
Think of the recursion as filling one character slot per digit. At depth index, the previous slots already contain letters for digits before index. Look up the letters for digits[index], try each letter in that slot, and recurse to the next digit. When index reaches the length of digits, the path is a complete word and gets recorded.

### Invariant
At recursion depth i, path has exactly i characters, and each character came from the matching digit position. This keeps the generated strings the same length as the input and prevents mixing letters into the wrong slot.

### Empty input behavior
If digits is empty, return an empty list, not a list containing the empty string. There is no phone number to translate, so the user expects no combinations.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not permute all letters freely. The first output character must come from the first digit, the second output character from the second digit, and so on. Also do not forget that 7 and 9 have four letters while most other mapped digits have three.

### Complexity
If N is the number of digits, each digit branches by at most 4 letters. Time is O(4^N * N) when accounting for copying or building each answer string. The recursion stack uses O(N), excluding the output list.

m-coloring-problem.name: Backtracking
m-coloring-problem.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
The M-Coloring Problem asks whether every vertex of a graph can receive one of M colors so that no edge connects two vertices with the same color. The answer is usually true or false, not necessarily the final coloring, although the recursive search internally builds a coloring candidate.

### Core intuition
Color the graph one vertex at a time. For the current vertex, try color 1, then color 2, and so on up to M. A color is safe only if every already-colored neighbor has a different color. If a color is safe, place it and move to the next vertex. If the future fails, erase the color and try the next option.

### Invariant
Before coloring vertex v, all vertices before v have valid colors among themselves. That means the partial coloring never knowingly contains a conflict. The safety check only has to protect the current vertex against its neighbors.

### Why backtracking fits
This is a constraint satisfaction problem. A choice can look locally fine but later block another vertex. Backtracking gives each earlier vertex a chance to change color when the future proves impossible.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not mark a color safe just because one neighbor is fine; all adjacent vertices must be checked. Do not forget to reset color[v] to 0 when a branch fails. Also remember that disconnected graphs are still handled naturally because isolated components simply have fewer neighbor conflicts.

### Complexity
In the worst case, each of N vertices tries M colors, so time is O(M^N). The color array uses O(N), and recursion depth is O(N). Dense graphs make each safety check more expensive because more neighbors must be inspected.

expression-add-operators.name: Optimal (Backtracking with Running Value Maintenance)
expression-add-operators.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Expression Add Operators gives a digit string and a target, then asks us to insert +, -, and * between some digits so that the expression evaluates to the target. We may also choose not to cut between two digits, which forms a multi-digit number. For example, 123 can become 1+2+3, 1*2*3, 12+3, and many more.

### Core intuition
At every position, choose the next numeric chunk, then attach an operator before it. The first chunk has no operator. Addition and subtraction are easy because they directly change the running value. Multiplication is the special case because it has higher precedence than addition and subtraction.

### The previous operand trick
Keep two numbers: currentValue, the value of the expression so far, and previousOperand, the last signed operand that was added into currentValue. For multiplication by num, undo the previous operand, replace it with previousOperand * num, and continue. The formula is currentValue - previousOperand + previousOperand * num.

### Leading zero rule
A chunk like 05 is not a valid number in normal expression format. If the chunk starts with 0, only the single digit 0 may be used from that position.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not evaluate the expression string from scratch at every leaf if you can maintain value while recursing. Do not treat multiplication like currentValue * num; that breaks expressions such as 1+2*3, which should be 7, not 9. Use a wide integer type in languages where multiplication can overflow.

### Complexity
There are exponentially many ways to split and place operators, usually bounded as O(4^N). Recursion depth is O(N), and each saved expression can take O(N) space.

set-the-i-th-bit.name: Bitwise OR Masking
set-the-i-th-bit.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Set the i-th Bit asks us to force one specific bit of N to become 1. Positions are 0-indexed from the right, so bit 0 is the ones place, bit 1 is the twos place, bit 2 is the fours place, and so on. Every other bit must stay exactly as it was.

### Core intuition
Create a mask that has a 1 only at position i. The expression 1 shifted left by i creates that mask. Then use bitwise OR with N. OR has the exact behavior we need: a bit OR 1 becomes 1, and a bit OR 0 stays unchanged.

### Tiny truth table
For the target bit, 0 OR 1 becomes 1 and 1 OR 1 stays 1. For every non-target bit, the mask has 0, so 0 OR 0 stays 0 and 1 OR 0 stays 1. That is why one mask can touch only the chosen position.

### Invariant
The mask is the only new information introduced. Since all non-target mask bits are 0, no non-target bit can change during the OR operation.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not add 2^i arithmetically. If the bit is already set, addition would carry and change other bits. OR is idempotent: setting an already-set bit leaves the number the same.

### Complexity
The operation is O(1) time and O(1) space. It is a single constant-sized bit operation in normal integer representations.

clear-the-i-th-bit.name: Bitwise AND with Complement Mask
clear-the-i-th-bit.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Clear the i-th Bit asks us to force one specific bit of N to become 0. Bit positions are 0-indexed from the right. The target bit should become 0 whether it was 0 or 1 before, while all other bits must remain unchanged.

### Core intuition
To clear one bit, first create the usual one-bit mask with 1 shifted left by i. That mask has 1 at the target position. Then invert it, producing a mask with 0 at the target position and 1 everywhere else. Finally, AND this inverted mask with N.

### Why AND is the right operation
AND with 1 preserves a bit: 0 AND 1 is 0, and 1 AND 1 is 1. AND with 0 clears a bit: both 0 AND 0 and 1 AND 0 become 0. So a mask full of 1s except one 0 is exactly the tool for clearing one position.

### Invariant
Every non-target bit meets 1 in the mask, so every non-target bit survives. The target bit meets 0, so it is erased. Nothing else is allowed to change.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not XOR when the task says clear. XOR would flip the bit, turning 0 into 1 if it was already clear. Also be aware that the visual form of the inverted mask depends on integer width, but the logical idea is the same: all bits except i are 1.

### Complexity
Time is O(1) and space is O(1). The work is mask construction plus one AND operation.

toggle-the-i-th-bit.name: Bitwise XOR Masking
toggle-the-i-th-bit.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Toggle the i-th Bit asks us to flip one bit of N. If the target bit is 0, it must become 1. If it is 1, it must become 0. Every other bit should remain unchanged. This is different from set, which always makes the bit 1, and clear, which always makes it 0.

### Core intuition
Use XOR with a one-bit mask. XOR with 1 flips a bit, while XOR with 0 preserves a bit. So the same mask 1 shifted left by i works again, but the operator changes to XOR.

### Tiny truth table
For the target bit, 0 XOR 1 becomes 1 and 1 XOR 1 becomes 0. For every other bit, the mask has 0, so 0 XOR 0 stays 0 and 1 XOR 0 stays 1. That is the entire toggle behavior in one line.

### Invariant
Only the target mask bit is 1. Therefore only the target position can flip. All other positions meet 0 and stay unchanged.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not use OR for toggle, because OR cannot turn 1 into 0. Do not use AND with an inverted mask, because that only clears. Also keep the 0-indexed position straight: i = 1 means the twos place, not the first digit from the left.

### Complexity
The operation is O(1) time and O(1) space. It builds one mask and applies one XOR operation.

power-of-2.name: Bitwise Validation
power-of-2.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Power of 2 Check asks whether an integer N is one of 1, 2, 4, 8, 16, and so on. In binary, these numbers have a very special shape: exactly one bit is 1, and every other bit is 0. For example, 8 is 1000 and 16 is 10000. The number 12 is 1100, so it is not a power of two.

### Core intuition
The trick uses the relationship between N and N - 1. When N is a power of two, subtracting 1 turns its only set bit into 0 and turns all lower bits into 1. So 8 is 1000, and 7 is 0111. If we AND them together, every position becomes 0. That is why N & (N - 1) equals 0 for powers of two.

### Why N must be positive
Zero is not a power of two, even though 0 & -1 can behave like 0 in many integer systems. Negative numbers are also not part of the usual positive power-of-two sequence for this problem. So the full condition is N > 0 and N & (N - 1) equals 0.

### Invariant
A positive number is a power of two if and only if it has exactly one set bit. The bit trick does not guess the value; it tests that one-set-bit property directly.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not use repeated division without handling N = 0. Do not forget parentheses around N & (N - 1) in languages where comparison precedence can surprise you. Also do not confuse this with checking evenness: many even numbers, like 6 and 10, are not powers of two.

### Complexity
The bitwise check is O(1) time and O(1) space. It uses a subtraction, one AND, and one comparison.

count-total-set-bits.name: Brute Force
count-total-set-bits.description: Build a correctness baseline for Count Set Bits (1 to N) by directly enumerating the candidate states allowed by the problem. This version prioritizes transparent control flow and exhaustive coverage over asymptotic efficiency.

count-total-set-bits.name: Better
count-total-set-bits.description: Reduce repeated work in Count Set Bits (1 to N) with the intermediate structure or cached state used by the standard better approach. It preserves the brute-force reasoning while improving one major time or space bottleneck.

count-total-set-bits.name: Optimal (Recursive Power-of-2 Pattern)
count-total-set-bits.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Count Set Bits from 1 to N asks for the total number of 1 bits appearing in every binary number from 1 through N. For N = 5, we look at 1, 2, 3, 4, and 5: 1, 10, 11, 100, 101. Their set-bit counts are 1, 1, 2, 1, and 2, so the answer is 7.

### Core intuition
Do not count bits number by number. Instead, use the repeating structure created by powers of two. In the complete block from 0 to 2^x - 1, every bit position appears as 1 exactly half the time. With x bit positions, that contributes x * 2^(x - 1) set bits.

### Breaking N into a clean block and a tail
Find the largest power of two less than or equal to N, say 2^x. Everything before it, from 0 to 2^x - 1, is a perfect block. Then all numbers from 2^x to N have their most significant bit set, contributing N - 2^x + 1 more bits. What remains is the lower-bit pattern of numbers from 0 to N - 2^x, so recurse on that remainder.

### Invariant
Each recursive step removes the highest power-of-two block from the range. The answer is built from three non-overlapping parts: complete block bits, leading bits in the tail, and recursive lower bits.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not include N = 0 in the formula using x * 2^(x - 1), because x - 1 becomes awkward. Return 0 immediately for N <= 0. Also be careful with the C++ expression: x << (x - 1) only equals x * 2^(x - 1), but the intention should be understood as multiplication by a power of two.

### Complexity
Each recursion removes the highest set bit, so there are O(log N) steps. The recursion stack is O(log N), or O(1) if converted to an iterative loop.

minimum-bit-flips-to-convert-number.name: XOR + Popcount
minimum-bit-flips-to-convert-number.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Minimum Bit Flips to Convert Number asks how many individual bit positions must change to turn start into goal. A flip changes a 0 to 1 or a 1 to 0. We want the minimum count, and because each bit position is independent, the answer is simply the number of positions where start and goal differ.

### Core intuition
XOR is the difference detector. At any bit position, equal bits produce 0 and different bits produce 1. So start XOR goal creates a mask whose 1 bits mark exactly the positions that need flipping. After that, count the set bits in the mask.

### Counting the set bits efficiently
Use the remove-last-set-bit trick: x = x & (x - 1). Each time we apply it, exactly one 1 bit disappears from x. Count how many removals happen until x becomes 0. That count is the number of different bit positions.

### Invariant
The XOR mask contains one 1 for each required flip. Removing one set bit from the mask corresponds to accounting for one required flip. No bit outside the mask needs to change.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not subtract the numbers and count something from the difference; arithmetic carries do not represent per-bit mismatches. Do not compare decimal digits. Also remember that leading zeroes are harmless: if goal needs a higher bit, XOR will show that position as 1.

### Complexity
With Brian Kernighan counting, time is O(K), where K is the number of differing bits. In a fixed-width integer this is also O(1) bounded by word size. Space is O(1).

single-number-i.name: XOR Accumulation
single-number-i.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Single Number I gives an array where every value appears exactly twice except one value that appears once. We need return that lonely value using linear time and constant extra space. A hash map works, but the interview-grade solution uses XOR.

### Core intuition
XOR has a cancellation personality. A number XOR itself becomes 0, and a number XOR 0 stays the number. XOR also does not care about order, because it is associative and commutative. So if we XOR every element in the array, all pairs cancel out, leaving only the value that appeared once.

### Why the accumulator starts at 0
Zero is the identity for XOR. Starting with result = 0 means the first number enters unchanged. Every repeated number later cancels its earlier copy. The accumulator is not storing a count; it is storing the leftover bit pattern after pair cancellations.

### Invariant
After processing any prefix of the array, the accumulator equals the XOR of all numbers seen so far. Any value seen twice in that prefix has canceled out. Values seen once are still represented in the accumulator.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not use addition and subtraction for this unless you are willing to handle overflow and assumptions about exact duplicates. XOR is safer for bit patterns. Also do not sort if the problem asks for O(1) extra space and a linear solution; sorting changes the intended complexity.

### Complexity
One pass over N numbers gives O(N) time. The accumulator uses O(1) space.

single-number-ii.name: Optimal (Bucketing via Ones and Twos)
single-number-ii.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Single Number II gives an array where every value appears three times except one value that appears once. We need find the single value in O(N) time and O(1) extra space. Ordinary XOR is not enough now, because x XOR x XOR x equals x, not 0.

### Core intuition
For each bit position, counts are meaningful modulo 3. Bits belonging to numbers that appear three times should cycle back to 0. Bits belonging to the single number remain with count 1 modulo 3. The ones and twos variables implement this modulo-3 counting for all bit positions at once.

### How ones and twos behave
Ones stores bits seen once modulo 3. Twos stores bits seen twice modulo 3. When a bit appears a third time, it must be cleared from both. The update formulas move bits through this cycle: absent to ones, ones to twos, twos back to absent. At the end, ones contains the bits of the single number.

### Invariant
After processing each number, every bit position is in exactly one logical state: seen 0 times modulo 3, seen 1 time modulo 3, or seen 2 times modulo 3. Ones and twos encode those states without an array of 32 counters.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not use plain XOR and expect triples to disappear. Do not update twos using the old ones if your chosen formula expects the newly updated ones. Order matters for this state machine. Also be careful with negative numbers in languages with fixed-width signed integers; the bit logic still works, but representation details matter.

### Complexity
The algorithm scans the array once, so time is O(N). It uses two integer variables, so space is O(1).

single-number-iii.name: XOR + Partitioning
single-number-iii.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Single Number III gives an array where every number appears twice except two numbers that appear once. We must return those two unique numbers. A simple XOR of the whole array will not directly give both numbers; it gives their XOR combined together.

### Core intuition
First XOR every number. Duplicate pairs cancel, so the result becomes A XOR B, where A and B are the two single numbers. Since A and B are different, A XOR B has at least one set bit. That set bit marks a position where A and B differ: one has 1 there, the other has 0.

### Partitioning by a differing bit
Pick one set bit from A XOR B, usually the rightmost set bit. Now split every number into two groups based on whether that bit is set. Duplicates always go into the same group as their matching copy, so they still cancel. The two single numbers go into different groups because they differ on that selected bit. XOR each group, and the two answers appear separately.

### Invariant
The chosen mask separates A and B but never separates equal duplicate pairs. That is why cancellation still works after partitioning.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not try to recover A and B from A XOR B without partitioning; many pairs can have the same XOR. Do not choose a mask bit that is 0 in A XOR B, because then A and B would not be separated. In languages with signed integers, using xor & -xor is common, but use a wide type if overflow rules are strict.

### Complexity
The algorithm uses two linear passes: one to compute total XOR and one to partition and cancel. Time is O(N), and extra space is O(1).

xor-of-numbers-in-a-given-range.name: Pattern-based O(1) Method
xor-of-numbers-in-a-given-range.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
XOR In Range 0 to N asks for 0 XOR 1 XOR 2 XOR ... XOR N, but it wants the answer in constant time. The direct loop is easy to understand but unnecessary because cumulative XOR follows a repeating pattern.

### Core intuition
Write the answers for small N. XOR from 0 to 0 is 0. XOR from 0 to 1 is 1. XOR from 0 to 2 is 3. XOR from 0 to 3 is 0. XOR from 0 to 4 is 4. The result pattern repeats every four numbers because pairs of bits cancel in a stable cycle.

### The four-case rule
If N modulo 4 is 0, the answer is N. If N modulo 4 is 1, the answer is 1. If N modulo 4 is 2, the answer is N + 1. If N modulo 4 is 3, the answer is 0. This gives the cumulative XOR instantly.

### Invariant
Every block of four consecutive numbers aligned as 4k, 4k+1, 4k+2, 4k+3 has XOR 0 when considered as a complete block. The remainder after division by 4 tells us how much of the next block remains.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not confuse XOR with sum. The answer is not related to arithmetic series formulas. Also be clear about whether the range starts at 0 or 1. For XOR, including 0 changes nothing, but the naming should still be understood.

### Complexity
The solution uses one modulo operation and a small conditional, so time is O(1). Space is O(1).

find-xor-of-numbers-from-l-to-r.name: Prefix XOR Logic
find-xor-of-numbers-from-l-to-r.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
XOR In Range L to R asks for L XOR (L + 1) XOR ... XOR R in O(1) time. This is slightly different from the previous 0 to N version because the range begins in the middle. The clean way to handle the middle is to use prefix XOR cancellation.

### Core intuition
Let F(N) be XOR from 0 to N. If we compute F(R), it includes everything from 0 to R. If we also compute F(L - 1), it includes exactly the part before L. XORing those two prefixes cancels the shared prefix 0 to L - 1, leaving only L to R.

### Why cancellation works
Every number before L appears once in F(R) and once in F(L - 1). A value XOR itself becomes 0, so all those prefix numbers disappear. The numbers from L to R appear only in F(R), so they remain.

### Reusing the four-case helper
The helper F(N) uses the modulo-4 pattern: mod 0 gives N, mod 1 gives 1, mod 2 gives N + 1, and mod 3 gives 0. Range XOR is then F(R) XOR F(L - 1).

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not compute F(R) XOR F(L). That would cancel L as well and give the range L + 1 to R. Also be careful when L is 0; then F(L - 1) needs a helper behavior that treats negative input as 0 or a separate case.

### Complexity
Two prefix lookups and one XOR give O(1) time and O(1) space.

implement-stack-using-arrays.name: Pointer-based Array
implement-stack-using-arrays.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Implement Stack using Arrays asks us to build a Last-In-First-Out structure with an array. Last-In-First-Out means the most recently pushed item is the first one popped. The array stores the values, and a top pointer tells us which index currently holds the stack's top element.

### Core intuition
Initialize top to -1, meaning the stack is empty. When pushing x, move top one step forward and place x at arr[top]. When popping, first check whether top is -1. If it is, the stack is empty. Otherwise return arr[top] and move top one step backward.

### Why top is enough
A stack only exposes one end. We never remove from the bottom or middle. Because every operation touches the same end, one pointer is enough to represent the active part of the array.

### Invariant
If top is -1, the stack is empty. If top is k, then arr[0] through arr[k] are the active stack contents, and arr[k] is the next value that pop should return. Anything after top is ignored.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not increment top after writing during push, or you may leave the first slot unused depending on your convention. Choose one convention and stay consistent. Do not pop before checking underflow. If the array has fixed capacity, push should also check overflow before writing.

### Complexity
Push, pop, peek, and empty checks are O(1). The array stores up to N elements, so space is O(N).

implement-queue-using-arrays.name: Two-Pointer Array
implement-queue-using-arrays.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Implement Queue using Arrays asks us to build a First-In-First-Out structure with an array. First-In-First-Out means the earliest pushed item is the first one popped. Unlike a stack, a queue uses two ends: one end for insertion and one end for removal.

### Core intuition
Maintain two pointers: front and rear. Rear marks where the next pushed value will be written. Front marks the next value that pop should return. On push, write arr[rear] = x and move rear forward. On pop, check whether front equals rear. If they are equal, the queue is empty. Otherwise return arr[front] and move front forward.

### Linear versus circular array
The simplest teaching version lets front and rear only move forward. That works for a limited sequence but can waste space at the beginning after many pops. A production fixed-size queue usually makes the array circular with modulo capacity, so freed slots can be reused.

### Invariant
The active queue lives in the half-open range from front up to but not including rear. If front equals rear, there are no active elements. The pop operation always reads from front because that is the oldest remaining value.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not pop from rear; that would turn the structure into a stack-like behavior. Do not treat rear as the last valid element if your convention makes it the next empty slot. Also handle underflow before reading arr[front].

### Complexity
Push and pop are O(1). Storage is O(N). With circular logic, the queue can reuse all N slots efficiently.

implement-stack-using-queue.name: Single Queue (Re-enqueue)
implement-stack-using-queue.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Implement Stack via Queues asks us to create Last-In-First-Out behavior even though the only container we are allowed to use is a queue, which naturally gives First-In-First-Out behavior. A normal queue removes the oldest value first. A stack must remove the newest value first. The whole problem is about bending queue order until the newest value sits at the front.

### Core intuition
Use one queue. When pushing a new value x, first enqueue x at the back. Then rotate all older elements behind it by repeatedly removing the front element and adding it back to the rear. After size - 1 rotations, x becomes the front of the queue. Now pop can simply remove from the front in O(1), and that front is the stack top.

### Invariant
After every push, the queue is arranged in stack order from front to rear: newest element first, oldest element last. Because that invariant is maintained during push, pop and top stay simple.

### Why the cost sits in push
The queue is not naturally designed for LIFO, so we pay the reordering price when a value enters. That makes push O(N), but pop O(1). This is a deliberate tradeoff.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not rotate the newly inserted element too. Only the previous size elements must move behind it. Also be careful with queue size during the loop; store the old count or loop exactly current size - 1 times after enqueue.

### Complexity
Push takes O(N) because it rotates older elements. Pop and top are O(1). The queue stores N elements, so space is O(N).

implement-queue-using-stack.name: Two Stacks (Amortized O(1))
implement-queue-using-stack.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Implement Queue via Stacks asks us to create First-In-First-Out behavior using stacks, which naturally give Last-In-First-Out behavior. A stack removes the newest value first. A queue must remove the oldest value first. The standard solution uses two stacks so that one reversal fixes the order.

### Core intuition
Keep an input stack for new pushes and an output stack for pops and peeks. Push is easy: put the new value on input. When the queue needs to pop or peek, check output. If output has values, its top is already the oldest queued value. If output is empty, move every element from input into output. This transfer reverses the order, turning the oldest input value into the top of output.

### Invariant
All values in output are older than all values still in input. If output is non-empty, its top is the queue front. If output is empty, transferring input to output restores the correct FIFO order.

### Why the time is amortized O(1)
A single pop may move many elements, but each element moves from input to output at most once before it is popped. Across many operations, the transfer cost spreads out.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not transfer input into output on every pop if output already has values. That breaks the amortized benefit and can disturb order. Also remember that pop should call peek or perform the transfer first, then remove from output.

### Complexity
Push is O(1). Pop and peek are amortized O(1), with occasional O(N) transfer. Space is O(N) across the two stacks.

implement-stack-using-linked-list.name: Head-Insertion Method
implement-stack-using-linked-list.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Implement Stack using Linked List asks us to build a Last-In-First-Out stack without a fixed-size array. Each pushed value becomes a node. The top of the stack is represented by the head pointer of the linked list.

### Core intuition
Use head as top. To push, create a new node, point its next to the current head, then move head to the new node. To pop, return head.data and move head to head.next. Both operations touch only the first node, so they are constant time.

### Why the head is the right end
A singly linked list can insert and delete at the head cheaply. Removing from the tail would require walking through the list to find the previous node, which would be O(N). Since stack behavior only needs one active end, the head is perfect.

### Invariant
If head is null, the stack is empty. If head is not null, head.data is the next value pop should return. The rest of the linked list, following next pointers, stores older stack values in order.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not append pushed values at the tail unless you also maintain efficient tail deletion, which a singly linked list does not naturally provide. Do not forget to save the value before moving head during pop. In manual-memory languages, free or delete the removed node.

### Complexity
Push, pop, and top are O(1). Space is O(N), one node per stored element. The structure grows dynamically as values are pushed.

implement-queue-using-linked-list.name: Two-Pointer LinkedList
implement-queue-using-linked-list.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Implement Queue using Linked List asks us to build a First-In-First-Out queue with dynamically allocated nodes. A queue needs two active ends: the front, where values are removed, and the rear, where new values are added.

### Core intuition
Maintain two pointers. Front points to the oldest node, the one pop should remove. Rear points to the newest node, the one whose next should receive the next pushed node. To push into an empty queue, set both front and rear to the new node. To push into a non-empty queue, connect rear.next to the new node and move rear forward. To pop, remove front and advance front to front.next.

### Empty queue reset
After popping the last node, front becomes null. Rear must also become null, or it will point to a node that is no longer active. This detail prevents later pushes from linking through stale state.

### Invariant
All active nodes are reachable from front by following next pointers. Rear is the last active node and has no active next node. If front is null, rear must also be null.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not push at the front if you want queue behavior; that would make new values leave too early. Do not forget the empty-case branch for the first push. Also do not leave rear unchanged after removing the last element.

### Complexity
Push and pop are O(1) because each uses direct pointer updates. Space is O(N), one node per queue element.

check-for-balanced-parentheses.name: Stack-based Matching
check-for-balanced-parentheses.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Balanced Parentheses asks whether every opening bracket is closed by the correct type of closing bracket in the correct nested order. The valid bracket types are usually parentheses, square brackets, and braces. A string like ({[]}) is valid, while ([)] is not, because the closing order crosses the nesting.

### Core intuition
Nested structures need memory of the most recent unfinished opening bracket. A stack gives exactly that. When an opening bracket appears, push it. When a closing bracket appears, it must match the stack top, because the most recently opened bracket must close first. If the stack is empty or the type does not match, the string is invalid immediately.

### Invariant
After reading any prefix of the string, the stack contains exactly the opening brackets that have not been closed yet, ordered from oldest at the bottom to newest at the top. The next closing bracket must match the top.

### Final condition
Finishing the scan is not enough. The stack must also be empty. If openings remain, they were never closed, so the string is unbalanced.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not only count opening and closing brackets. Counts can match while order is wrong, as in ([)]. Do not accept a closing bracket when the stack is empty. Also make sure each closing type maps to the correct opening type.

### Complexity
The string is scanned once, so time is O(N). In the worst case all characters are opening brackets, so stack space is O(N).

infix-to-postfix-conversion.name: Operator Stack Algorithm
infix-to-postfix-conversion.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Infix to Postfix Conversion asks us to transform a normal expression such as A+B*C into postfix form, such as ABC*+. In infix notation, operators sit between operands, so precedence and parentheses matter. In postfix notation, operators come after their operands, which makes evaluation easy with a stack.

### Core intuition
Operands are safe to output immediately because their relative order does not need negotiation. Operators are not always safe immediately, because a higher-precedence operator may need to appear before a lower-precedence operator in postfix. So we keep operators on a stack until it is correct to release them.

### The operator stack rules
If the token is an operand, append it to the answer. If it is an opening parenthesis, push it. If it is a closing parenthesis, pop operators until the matching opening parenthesis appears. If it is an operator, pop and output stack operators with greater or equal precedence before pushing the current operator. At the end, pop all remaining operators.

### Invariant
At every point, the output string contains operands and operators whose order is already decided. The stack holds operators still waiting for their right-side operands or for a lower-precedence boundary.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not output an operator just because you see it. Its precedence relative to the stack top decides whether older operators must leave first. Do not leave parentheses in the final answer. Also be careful with exponent associativity if the version treats ^ as right-associative.

### Complexity
The expression is scanned once and every operator enters and leaves the stack once, so time is O(N). Stack space is O(N).

prefix-to-infix-conversion.name: Reverse Stack Algorithm
prefix-to-infix-conversion.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Prefix to Infix Conversion asks us to transform an expression where each operator comes before its operands into the familiar form where operators sit between operands. For example, prefix *+ABC represents (A+B)*C. The answer is usually fully parenthesized so the original evaluation order is preserved.

### Core intuition
Prefix is easiest from right to left. When scanning backward, operands appear before the operator that will combine them. Push operands onto a stack. When an operator appears, pop the first operand expression, pop the second operand expression, and build a new expression with the operator in the middle: open parenthesis, first operand, operator, second operand, close parenthesis.

### Operand order matters
When scanning prefix from right to left, the first pop is the left operand and the second pop is the right operand. Reversing them changes the expression, especially for subtraction and division.

### Invariant
The stack always contains valid infix expressions for chunks already processed from the right side. When an operator is processed, the top two expressions are exactly its two operands.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not scan prefix left to right with this stack rule; the operands will not be ready. Do not skip parentheses unless you also handle precedence carefully. Parentheses are the safest beginner-friendly way to preserve structure.

### Complexity
The scan is O(N). String concatenation can make some language implementations closer to O(N^2), but conceptually each token is processed once. Stack space is O(N).

prefix-to-postfix-conversion.name: Reverse Stack Accumulation
prefix-to-postfix-conversion.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Prefix to Postfix Conversion asks us to transform an expression from operator-before-operands form to operands-before-operator form. For example, prefix *+ABC becomes postfix AB+C*. Both forms encode the same expression tree, but they place operators on opposite sides of their operands.

### Core intuition
Just like prefix to infix, scan from right to left. Push operands onto a stack. When an operator appears, pop the first operand expression and the second operand expression. For postfix, combine them as first operand, second operand, operator. Then push that combined postfix expression back onto the stack.

### Why right-to-left works
In prefix, the operator appears before its operands. Scanning from the right means the operands are seen and stored before their operator arrives. That makes the operator's job simple: take two ready-made expressions and glue them in postfix order.

### Invariant
The stack contains valid postfix expressions for subtrees already processed. When an operator is read, the top two stack items are the exact left and right subexpressions for that operator.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not append the operator between the operands; that would create infix, not postfix. Do not reverse the two popped operands. For non-commutative operators like minus and divide, wrong pop order changes the meaning.

### Complexity
Each token is processed once, so the algorithmic scan is O(N). The stack stores partial expressions, so space is O(N), excluding the final answer string.

postfix-to-prefix-conversion.name: Stack Accumulation (Left-to-Right)
postfix-to-prefix-conversion.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Postfix to Prefix Conversion asks us to transform an expression where each operator comes after its operands into one where each operator comes before its operands. For example, postfix AB+C* becomes prefix *+ABC. Both expressions describe (A+B)*C.

### Core intuition
Postfix is naturally scanned left to right. When operands appear, push them. When an operator appears, the two most recent expressions on the stack are its operands. Pop the right operand first, then the left operand. To form prefix, place the operator before them: operator, left operand, right operand.

### Operand order matters
Because a stack pops the most recent item first, the first pop is the right operand and the second pop is the left operand. If you combine them in the wrong order, subtraction and division expressions become incorrect.

### Invariant
After processing any prefix of the postfix string, the stack contains valid prefix expressions for the completed subexpressions seen so far. Operators reduce the top two expressions into one larger expression.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not scan postfix from right to left using this rule. Do not write operator + right + left. The correct prefix result is operator + left + right. Also make sure the final stack has exactly one expression for a valid input.

### Complexity
The scan is O(N), with O(N) stack space for partial expression strings. String concatenation costs depend on the language, but the algorithmic structure is linear.

postfix-to-infix-conversion.name: In-Place Parenthesizing
postfix-to-infix-conversion.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Postfix to Infix Conversion asks us to convert an expression like AB+C* back into a normal parenthesized expression such as ((A+B)*C). Postfix has no parentheses because operator order is encoded by position. When converting back to infix, parentheses make that hidden structure explicit.

### Core intuition
Scan postfix from left to right. Push operands as small expression strings. When an operator appears, pop the right operand first and the left operand second. Then wrap them as open parenthesis, left expression, operator, right expression, close parenthesis. Push that combined infix expression back.

### Why parentheses are important
Without parentheses, converting AB+C* to A+B*C would change the meaning because multiplication would be read before addition. The correct expression is (A+B)*C, so the conversion safely writes ((A+B)*C).

### Invariant
The stack always contains valid infix expressions for postfix chunks that have already been completed. Each operator consumes the top two completed expressions and creates a larger completed expression.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not pop operands in the wrong order. The first pop is the right operand, the second is the left operand. Do not remove parentheses unless you implement precedence-aware simplification. For learning and correctness, full parentheses are best.

### Complexity
The scan is O(N), and stack space is O(N). As with other string-building conversions, repeated concatenation may add overhead in some languages, but each token's logical role is processed once.

next-smaller-element.name: Monotonic Increasing Stack
next-smaller-element.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Next Smaller Element asks for every array position to find the first value on its right that is strictly smaller. It is not asking for any smaller value, or the smallest value to the right. It asks for the nearest smaller value encountered when walking right from that index. If no such value exists, the answer for that index is -1.

### Core intuition
Scan from right to left because the answer for an index always lives on its right. Keep a stack of possible next-smaller values. Before answering for current, remove every stack value that is greater than or equal to current, because none of those can be the next smaller for current or for any earlier value blocked by current. After the popping stops, the stack top is the closest smaller value to the right.

### Why the stack is monotonic
The stack is kept in increasing order from top toward deeper useful candidates. Values that are too large are discarded when a smaller current value arrives. This makes each value enter and leave the stack at most once.

### Invariant
Before processing index i, the stack contains values from the right side that can still become next-smaller answers for positions to the left. After popping values greater than or equal to arr[i], the top is the nearest valid smaller value.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not scan left to right unless you change the logic. Do not keep equal values on the stack when the problem says strictly smaller. Equal is not smaller, so it must be popped. Also do not confuse this with previous smaller element; the direction decides the problem.

### Complexity
Every element is pushed once and popped at most once, so time is O(N). The stack can hold up to N values, so space is O(N).

number-of-nges-to-the-right.name: Standard Suffix Count
number-of-nges-to-the-right.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Count Greater on Right asks, for each queried index, how many elements strictly greater than arr[index] appear to its right. This is different from Next Greater Element. Next Greater Element asks for the first greater value. This problem asks for the total count of all greater values after the index.

### Core intuition
For the straightforward catalog approach, handle each query independently. Take the value at the query index, scan from query + 1 to the end, and count how many values are greater. This is easy to reason about and works well when the input size or query count is moderate.

### Why a monotonic stack is not enough by itself
A monotonic stack is excellent for finding the first greater element, but it does not automatically count every greater element to the right. Counting all greater values is a frequency/rank question. A more advanced solution could process offline queries with a Fenwick tree or segment tree, but the listed approach is the direct suffix scan.

### Invariant
During one query scan, count always equals the number of already-seen elements in the suffix that are strictly greater than arr[query]. Every suffix element is checked once.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not stop after seeing the first greater value. The problem wants all greater values to the right. Do not count equal values, because the comparison is strictly greater. Also do not include the queried index itself.

### Complexity
For Q queries and an array of size N, the simple approach takes O(Q * N) time in the worst case. It uses O(1) extra working space apart from the output list.

trapping-rainwater.name: Brute Force
trapping-rainwater.description: Build a correctness baseline for Trapping Rainwater by directly enumerating the candidate states allowed by the problem. This version prioritizes transparent control flow and exhaustive coverage over asymptotic efficiency.

trapping-rainwater.name: Two Pointers (Optimal)
trapping-rainwater.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Trapping Rainwater gives bar heights and asks how many water units can sit between them after rain. Water above an index is limited by the shorter of the tallest bar on its left and the tallest bar on its right. If one side has no tall boundary, water spills away.

### Core intuition
For index i, water is min(leftMax, rightMax) - height[i], if that value is positive. The two-pointer solution avoids building leftMax and rightMax arrays. Put one pointer at each end, keep leftMax and rightMax, and always process the side with the smaller current height. That side's water is safe to decide because the opposite side already has a boundary at least as tall as the smaller side.

### Why the smaller side can move
If height[left] is less than height[right], then a right boundary exists that is taller than height[left]. The only uncertainty for left is the best left boundary seen so far, which leftMax tracks. The same logic mirrors on the right side.

### Invariant
At every step, leftMax is the highest bar seen from the left up to left, and rightMax is the highest bar seen from the right down to right. The pointer that moves has enough opposite-side support to finalize its trapped water.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not add negative water. Update the max first, then add max minus current height. Do not move both pointers at once. Also do not assume local valleys alone are enough; water depends on boundaries on both sides.

### Complexity
The two pointers meet after one pass, so time is O(N). The algorithm uses O(1) extra space.

sum-of-subarray-minimums.name: Monotonic Stack (Contribution Technique)
sum-of-subarray-minimums.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Sum of Subarray Minimums asks for every contiguous subarray, find its minimum value, then add all those minimums together. Listing every subarray would be too slow for large arrays, so the better view is contribution: for each element, count how many subarrays use that element as the minimum.

### Core intuition
For arr[i], find how far it can stretch left and right while still remaining the chosen minimum. If it can choose L starting positions on the left and R ending positions on the right, it contributes arr[i] * L * R to the answer. Monotonic stacks compute those distances for every index in linear time.

### Duplicate handling
Duplicates need a tie-breaking rule so the same subarray is not counted twice. The listed logic pops strictly greater values on the left pass, so an equal value to the left can stop the span. On the right pass, it pops greater than or equal values, so equal values are assigned consistently to one side.

### Invariant
Left[i] is the number of valid starts for which arr[i] can be the minimum under the tie rule. Right[i] is the number of valid ends. Their product is the number of subarrays where arr[i] contributes as minimum.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not use previous smaller and next smaller with inconsistent equality rules. Duplicate values will double-count or under-count. Do not forget the modulo 1e9 + 7 in platforms that require it.

### Complexity
Two monotonic stack passes plus one summation pass give O(N) time and O(N) space.

asteroid-collision.name: Stack Simulation
asteroid-collision.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Asteroid Collision simulates asteroids moving in one dimension. A positive number moves right, and a negative number moves left. The absolute value is the asteroid size. Only a right-moving asteroid already on the stack can collide with a new left-moving asteroid. Same direction asteroids never meet, and a left-moving asteroid before a right-moving one is moving away from it.

### Core intuition
Use a stack of surviving asteroids seen so far. When the current asteroid moves right, push it. When it moves left, compare it with the stack top while the top moves right. Smaller asteroids explode. Equal sizes both explode. If the current left-moving asteroid survives all right-moving blockers, push it as a survivor.

### Invariant
The stack always contains asteroids that have survived every collision possible among the processed prefix. A collision remains possible only when stack top is positive and current asteroid is negative.

### Collision outcomes
If stack top size is smaller than current size, pop the top and keep checking. If equal, pop the top and destroy current too. If stack top is larger, current is destroyed and nothing is pushed.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not collide asteroids just because their signs differ. The order matters: negative followed by positive means they move apart. Also do not stop after one pop if the current asteroid is large enough to hit earlier survivors.

### Complexity
Each asteroid is pushed once and popped at most once, so time is O(N). The survivor stack uses O(N) space.

sum-of-subarray-ranges.name: Contribution Technique (SumMax - SumMin)
sum-of-subarray-ranges.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Sum of Subarray Ranges asks us to consider every contiguous subarray, compute maximum minus minimum for that subarray, and add all those range values together. A direct solution lists all subarrays and scans for min and max, but the intended hard solution uses contribution counting.

### Core intuition
The range of a subarray is max minus min. So the total answer is sum of all subarray maximums minus sum of all subarray minimums. Instead of asking each subarray for its max and min, ask each element how many subarrays treat it as the maximum and how many treat it as the minimum.

### Monotonic stack contribution
For each index, compute how far it can stretch left and right as a maximum. That gives its maximum contribution. Then compute how far it can stretch as a minimum. The difference between those two total contributions across all elements is the answer. Equality rules must be chosen consistently to avoid duplicate ownership when equal values appear.

### Invariant
Every subarray contributes exactly one maximum owner and one minimum owner under the tie-breaking rules. The final total is the sum of all maximum-owner payments minus all minimum-owner payments.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not try to use only one monotonic stack pass. You need maximum spans and minimum spans. Do not ignore duplicates; equality handling decides which equal index owns a subarray. Also use a wide integer type because the total can grow large.

### Complexity
Four linear stack passes are still O(N). The auxiliary arrays or stack state use O(N) space, depending on implementation.

remove-k-digits.name: Monotonic Increasing Stack
remove-k-digits.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Remove K Digits gives a non-negative integer as a string and asks us to delete exactly K digits so that the remaining number is as small as possible. The original order of the remaining digits must stay the same. If all digits disappear or only leading zeroes remain, the answer should be 0.

### Core intuition
Digits on the left matter more because they are more significant. If a larger digit appears before a smaller digit, removing that larger earlier digit usually makes the number smaller. Use a stack of kept digits. For each new digit, while K is still positive and the stack top is greater than the new digit, pop the stack. Then push the new digit.

### Why the stack is increasing
The algorithm tries to make the kept prefix as small as possible. A decreasing pair like 4 then 3 is a warning: keeping 4 before 3 makes the number larger than necessary, so 4 should be removed if we still can.

### Leftover removals
If the scan ends and K is still positive, the digits are already increasing. In that case, remove from the end, because the rightmost digits are least significant among the remaining choices.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not sort the digits; order must be preserved. Do not forget to strip leading zeroes after deletion. Also do not stop after one pop if the current digit can remove several larger previous digits.

### Complexity
Each digit is pushed once and popped at most once, so time is O(N). The stack or result string uses O(N) space.

maximal-rectangle.name: Histogram-per-Row Logic
maximal-rectangle.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Maximal Rectangle gives a binary matrix and asks for the largest area rectangle made entirely of 1 values. The rectangle must be axis-aligned. A brute force approach tries many corners, but the stronger idea converts each row into a histogram and reuses the largest-rectangle-in-histogram pattern.

### Core intuition
Treat each row as the bottom of a potential rectangle. For every column, maintain height[col], the number of consecutive 1 values ending at the current row. If the current cell is 1, height increases by 1. If the current cell is 0, height resets to 0. After updating a row, the problem becomes: what is the largest rectangle in this histogram?

### Why histograms work
Any all-1 rectangle has some bottom row. When we process that bottom row, the columns inside the rectangle have heights at least as tall as the rectangle. The histogram solver can discover it at that exact row.

### Invariant
After processing row r, heights[c] is the vertical streak of 1 values ending at row r in column c. Running histogram max on heights gives the best rectangle whose bottom edge is row r.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not carry height through a zero; zero breaks vertical continuity. Do not only check each row as a flat array; rectangles can span multiple rows. Also remember to append or simulate a sentinel zero in the histogram solver so remaining bars get processed.

### Complexity
For R rows and C columns, each row update is O(C) and each histogram solve is O(C), so total time is O(R * C). The heights array and stack use O(C) space.

stock-span-problem.name: Monotonic Stack (PLE)
stock-span-problem.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
The Stock Span Problem asks, for each day's price, how many consecutive days ending today had price less than or equal to today's price. The count always includes today. If today's price is higher than several previous prices, it absorbs their spans.

### Core intuition
Keep a monotonic decreasing stack of pairs: price and span. When a new price arrives, start span at 1 for today. While the stack top price is less than or equal to the current price, pop it and add its stored span. Those popped days, and the days they already covered, are all consecutive days that current price dominates. Push current price with the merged span.

### Why span merging works
A popped pair represents a whole block of consecutive earlier days whose prices were less than or equal to that pair's price. If current price is at least that pair's price, current price also dominates the entire block, so the stored span can be added at once.

### Invariant
The stack prices are strictly greater from top-block boundaries toward older blockers. Each pair stores the span block that belongs to that price and has not been absorbed by a later higher price.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not pop only one smaller price. A high current price may absorb many earlier blocks. Do not use strictly less if the problem says less than or equal; equal prices should be included in the span.

### Complexity
Each price is pushed once and popped at most once, so total time is O(N) across all calls. Space is O(N) in the worst case.

the-celebrity-problem.name: Two-Pointer Elimination
the-celebrity-problem.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
The Celebrity Problem gives a knows matrix. A celebrity is known by everyone else but knows no one else. We must return the celebrity index if one exists, otherwise -1. The hard part is reducing candidates without checking every pair blindly.

### Core intuition
Use elimination. Compare two people A and B. If A knows B, then A cannot be the celebrity, because a celebrity knows nobody. B might still be possible. If A does not know B, then B cannot be the celebrity, because at least one person, A, does not know B. This single comparison always eliminates one candidate.

### Candidate selection
The catalog includes both two-pointer elimination and a candidate scan. In the candidate scan, start candidate at 0. For each person i, if candidate knows i, candidate is disqualified and i becomes the new candidate. After one pass, only one possible candidate remains.

### Verification is mandatory
The elimination pass only finds a possible celebrity. We must verify the candidate's row and column: candidate should know nobody else, and everyone else should know candidate. If either condition fails, return -1.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not skip verification. Elimination can leave a candidate even when no celebrity exists. Do not require candidate to know themselves; usually diagonal entries are ignored. Also keep the two conditions separate: row all zero, column all one except self.

### Complexity
Candidate elimination is O(N), and verification is O(N), assuming each knows lookup is O(1). Space is O(1).

max-consecutive-ones-iii.name: Two-Pointer Sliding Window
max-consecutive-ones-iii.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Max Consecutive Ones III gives a binary array and lets us flip at most K zeroes into ones. We need the longest contiguous stretch that can become all ones after those flips. The important phrase is at most K: a window is valid as long as it contains no more than K zeroes.

### Core intuition
Use a sliding window. Expand the right pointer to include more elements. Count how many zeroes are inside the window. If the zero count becomes greater than K, the window is no longer affordable, so move the left pointer forward until the zero count is back within the budget. Every valid window length is a candidate answer.

### Why this works
The window represents the segment we plan to turn into all ones. Ones are free. Zeroes cost one flip each. Therefore the only constraint is the number of zeroes in the window. Once a window is invalid, extending it further cannot make it valid; the left side must move.

### Invariant
After the shrink step, the current window always contains at most K zeroes. The answer records the largest valid window seen so far.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not count groups of ones separately. The flipped zeroes join neighboring ones into one continuous segment. Do not shrink when zero count equals K; equality is still valid. Also remember that K may be 0, in which case the problem becomes longest run of original ones.

### Complexity
Each pointer moves from left to right at most once, so time is O(N). The algorithm uses O(1) extra space.

fruit-into-baskets.name: Two-Pointer Sliding Window
fruit-into-baskets.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Fruit Into Baskets describes walking along a row of fruit trees while carrying two baskets. Each basket can hold only one fruit type, but there is no limit on quantity. Because you must walk in order and collect from a contiguous stretch, the problem is exactly: find the longest subarray with at most two distinct values.

### Core intuition
Use a sliding window with a frequency map. Expand the right pointer and add the new fruit type. If the map has at most two fruit types, the window is valid. If a third fruit type appears, move the left pointer forward, decreasing counts, until only two types remain. Track the largest valid window length.

### Why counts are needed
When left moves past one fruit, that fruit type might still exist elsewhere inside the window. We remove a type from the map only when its count falls to zero. This keeps the map size equal to the actual number of fruit types in the current window.

### Invariant
After shrinking, the window always contains at most two distinct fruit types, and the map accurately stores their counts.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not restart the window from the current tree when a third type appears; only shrink as much as necessary. Do not track only the last two fruit values without counts unless you use a carefully specialized method. The map version is clearer and safer.

### Complexity
The right and left pointers each move at most N times, so time is O(N). The map holds at most three types briefly and at most two after shrinking, so space is O(1).

longest-repeating-character-replacement.name: Optimized Sliding Window
longest-repeating-character-replacement.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Longest Repeating Character Replacement gives a string and lets us replace at most K characters. We need the longest substring that can be turned into one repeated character. The target character does not need to be chosen in advance; the best choice is simply the most frequent character inside the current window.

### Core intuition
For a window of length W, if the most frequent character appears maxFreq times, then W - maxFreq characters must be replaced to make the entire window equal to that character. If W - maxFreq is at most K, the window is valid. If it exceeds K, move left to reduce the window.

### Why maxFreq is enough
We do not care which less frequent characters exist, only how many characters are not the dominant one. Keeping the largest count in the window tells us the minimum replacement cost for that window.

### About stale maxFreq
Many optimal implementations do not decrease maxFreq when left moves. That is okay for finding length because maxFreq is used as a permissive historical bound, and the window length is corrected by shrinking over time. A fully recomputed maxFreq also works, just with a slightly higher constant.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not require the window to already contain equal letters. Replacement is allowed. Do not shrink while W - maxFreq equals K; that is still affordable. Also remember duplicates in T-like problems are different; this problem only needs one dominant character.

### Complexity
The string is scanned once, so time is O(N). The frequency table is O(1) for uppercase English letters.

minimum-window-subsequence.name: Two-Pointer (Forward-Reverse Search)
minimum-window-subsequence.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Minimum Window Subsequence gives strings S and T and asks for the shortest substring of S in which T appears as a subsequence. This means the characters of T must appear in order, but they do not need to be adjacent. This is not the same as Minimum Window Substring, where character order does not matter but all required counts must be inside the window.

### Core intuition
Use a forward scan to find an ending position where all of T has appeared in order. Once a full subsequence match is found, walk backward from that end to find the latest possible starting position that still preserves T as a subsequence. That backward walk minimizes the current window. Then continue searching after that start.

### Why the reverse step matters
The forward scan proves a valid window exists, but its start may be too early. Walking backward trims unnecessary prefix characters while keeping T matched in reverse order.

### Invariant
Forward search identifies a window containing T as a subsequence. Reverse search tightens that same window to the smallest window ending at the found right boundary.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not use a frequency map; order matters here. A window containing b, d, and e is not enough unless they appear as b then d then e. Do not confuse subsequence with substring; T's characters may be separated inside the window.

### Complexity
The forward and backward scans can repeat for multiple matches, giving O(S * T) in the catalog approach. Extra space is O(1).

count-number-of-nice-subarrays.name: Two-Pointer (atMost Transformation)
count-number-of-nice-subarrays.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Count Number of Nice Subarrays asks for the number of contiguous subarrays containing exactly K odd numbers. Even numbers do not change the odd count, but they can extend valid subarrays on either side, so they still affect the total.

### Core intuition
Turn the problem into a binary-sum problem: odd numbers behave like 1, even numbers behave like 0. We need subarrays with sum exactly K. Exactly K is easier to count as atMost(K) minus atMost(K - 1). atMost(K) counts subarrays with no more than K odds. Removing atMost(K - 1) leaves only those with exactly K odds.

### Why atMost works
For each right pointer, after shrinking until the window has at most goal odd numbers, every start from left through right creates a valid subarray ending at right. That adds right - left + 1 subarrays in one step.

### Invariant
Inside atMost(goal), the active window always has no more than goal odd numbers after shrinking, and every subarray ending at right with start at or after left is valid.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not ignore even numbers; they multiply the number of valid choices. Do not try to count only windows beginning and ending at odd numbers unless you carefully include even extensions. Also handle K = 0 if a variant allows it.

### Complexity
Two atMost passes give O(N) time. Space is O(1).

number-of-substrings-containing-all-thre.name: Sliding Window (Last Seen)
number-of-substrings-containing-all-thre.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Number of Substrings Containing All Three Characters gives a string made only of a, b, and c. We need count how many substrings contain at least one of each character. The substring must be contiguous, and we count all possible start and end positions that satisfy the condition.

### Core intuition
Track the last seen index of a, b, and c while scanning left to right. At position i, if all three characters have been seen, then the earliest last-seen index among the three tells us how many valid starting positions exist for substrings ending at i. Any start from 0 through that minimum index will include all three characters.

### Why the minimum last seen index matters
Suppose the last seen positions are a at 3, b at 5, and c at 6. Any substring ending now must start at or before 3 to include the most distant required character, a. That gives 3 + 1 valid starts.

### Invariant
After processing index i, lastSeen stores the most recent position of each required character. If the minimum is m, then exactly m + 1 substrings ending at i contain a, b, and c.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not count only the shortest valid substring. Every earlier start is also valid once all three characters are included. Do not use this exact trick for larger alphabets without adapting the minimum tracking.

### Complexity
The scan is O(N), and storage is O(1) for the three last-seen indices.

maximum-points-you-can-obtain-from-cards.name: Sliding Window (Minimum Subarray Sum)
maximum-points-you-can-obtain-from-cards.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Maximum Points from Cards gives an array of card points and says we may take exactly K cards from either end of the row. We need the maximum possible score. The chosen cards always come from a prefix plus a suffix, because middle cards can only be reached after removing cards before them.

### Core intuition
There are two equivalent views. One view says try taking x cards from the left and K - x cards from the right for every x. The other says taking K cards means leaving exactly N - K contiguous cards in the middle; maximizing taken points is total sum minus the minimum sum left behind. The catalog implementation uses the first view with a rolling left-sum and right-sum.

### Rolling end-window method
Start by taking all K cards from the left. Then one by one, give back one card from the left end of that chosen prefix and take one more card from the right end. Each step represents a different split between left and right picks.

### Invariant
At iteration i, leftSum plus rightSum represents one legal way to choose exactly K cards from the two ends. The answer stores the maximum split score seen so far.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not choose arbitrary K cards; only end cards are available. Do not forget the split where all K cards come from the left or all K come from the right. Also handle K equal to N, where the answer is the total sum.

### Complexity
The rolling method checks K + 1 splits in O(K) time and uses O(1) extra space.

longest-substring-with-at-most-k-distinc.name: Two-Pointer Sliding Window
longest-substring-with-at-most-k-distinc.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Longest Substring with At Most K Distinct Characters asks for the longest contiguous substring whose number of unique characters is no more than K. Repetition is allowed. The constraint is only on how many different character types appear inside the window.

### Core intuition
Use a frequency map and a sliding window. Expand right and add the current character. If the map has at most K keys, the window is valid. If the map grows beyond K keys, move left forward and decrement counts until one character count becomes zero and the map returns to K keys.

### Why frequency counts matter
A character may occur multiple times in the window. Moving left past one copy does not necessarily remove that character type. The type disappears from the distinct count only when its frequency reaches zero.

### Invariant
After shrinking, the current window has at most K distinct characters, and the map accurately describes the window. The best answer is the largest valid window length seen.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not shrink just because a repeated character appears; repeats are allowed. Do not delete a character key too early. Also handle K = 0, where no non-empty substring can be valid.

### Complexity
Both pointers move at most N steps, so time is O(N). The map stores at most K + 1 character types briefly, so space is O(K) for general alphabets.

subarrays-with-k-different-integers.name: Two-Pointer (atMost Transformation)
subarrays-with-k-different-integers.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Subarrays with K Different Integers asks for the number of contiguous subarrays containing exactly K distinct integer values. Exactly K is usually harder than at most K, because adding or removing one value can change the distinct count sharply.

### Core intuition
Use the standard transformation: exactly(K) equals atMost(K) minus atMost(K - 1). Counting at most K distinct values is sliding-window friendly. For each right pointer, shrink left until the window has no more than K distinct numbers. Then every start position from left to right forms a valid at-most-K subarray ending at right.

### Why subtraction gives exactness
Every subarray with fewer than K distinct values appears in both atMost(K) and atMost(K - 1), so it cancels. Every subarray with exactly K distinct values appears only in atMost(K), so it remains.

### Invariant
Inside atMost(goal), the active window contains no more than goal distinct integers after shrinking. The count added at each right step is the number of valid starts for subarrays ending at right.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not try to count exact windows directly by adding one whenever map size equals K; multiple starts may be valid for the same right endpoint. Do not delete a number from the map until its frequency becomes zero.

### Complexity
Two atMost passes give O(N) time. The frequency map uses O(K) space during each pass.

minimum-window-substring.name: Brute Force
minimum-window-substring.description: Build a correctness baseline for Minimum Window Substring by directly enumerating the candidate states allowed by the problem. This version prioritizes transparent control flow and exhaustive coverage over asymptotic efficiency.

minimum-window-substring.name: Sliding Window (Frequency Map)
minimum-window-substring.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Minimum Window Substring gives strings S and T and asks for the shortest substring of S that contains every character required by T, including duplicate counts. Order does not matter here. If T is AABC, the window must contain two As, one B, and one C somewhere inside it.

### Core intuition
Build a need map from T. Expand the right pointer through S and count characters in the current window. When a character's count reaches exactly what need requires, one required character type is satisfied. Once all required types are satisfied, the window is valid. Then shrink from the left while it remains valid, recording the smallest valid window seen.

### Why matched counts types, not characters
If T has duplicate letters, satisfying one copy is not enough. A type becomes satisfied only when the window count reaches the needed count for that type. If removing from the left makes the count fall below need, the window stops being valid.

### Invariant
When matched equals the number of required character types, the current window contains all required counts. The inner loop shrinks left to remove unnecessary prefix characters while preserving validity.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not use a set for T, because duplicates matter. Do not confuse this with minimum window subsequence; here order inside the window is irrelevant. Also update matched only when crossing the needed count boundary.

### Complexity
Each pointer moves through S at most once, and building the need map costs O(M), so time is O(N + M). The maps use O(K) space for the character set.

introduction-to-priority-queues-using-bi.name: Conceptual Model
introduction-to-priority-queues-using-bi.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact topic is teaching
Priority Queues and Binary Heaps introduce a structure where the next item is chosen by priority, not by arrival order. A normal queue removes the oldest item. A priority queue removes the item with the highest or lowest priority, depending on the design.

### Core intuition
A binary heap is the usual array-backed implementation. It acts like a complete binary tree, filled left to right, but it lives inside an array. For index i, parent is (i - 1) / 2, left child is 2i + 1, and right child is 2i + 2. These formulas replace explicit node pointers.

### Heap property
In a min-heap, every parent is less than or equal to its children, so the smallest value is always at index 0. In a max-heap, every parent is greater than or equal to its children, so the largest value is at index 0. The full array is not sorted; only parent-child priority is guaranteed.

### Why operations are fast
Insertion places the new item at the end and bubbles it upward. Extracting the top moves the last item to the root and bubbles it downward. A complete binary tree has height O(log N), so these repairs are logarithmic.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not expect the heap array to be sorted. Do not confuse peek with extract: peek reads the root in O(1), while extract removes it and repairs the heap.

### Complexity
