Peek is O(1). Insert and extract are O(log N). Space is O(N).

min-heap-and-max-heap-implementation.name: Standard Heapify
min-heap-and-max-heap-implementation.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Min Heap Implementation asks us to support push and pop while keeping the smallest value at the root. A max-heap uses the same structure with reversed comparisons, but this catalog entry focuses on min-heap behavior.

### Core intuition
The heap is stored in an array. Push appends the new value at the end so the complete-tree shape stays valid. The only possible violation is upward, between the new value and its ancestors, so we bubble the value up while it is smaller than its parent.

Pop removes the root, which is the minimum. To preserve the complete-tree shape, move the last value to the root, remove the last slot, and bubble the new root downward. At each step, compare it with the smaller child and swap if the child is smaller.

### Invariant
After each operation, every parent is less than or equal to its children. The tree is also complete because insertion and deletion happen at the array end.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not pop the root and leave a hole. Do not bubble down toward the larger child in a min-heap. Handle empty heaps and single-element heaps directly, because pop has no child repair in those cases.

### Complexity
Push and pop repair one root-to-leaf path, so they are O(log N). Peek is O(1). Storage is O(N).

check-if-an-array-is-a-min-heap.name: Iterative Parent-Child Check
check-if-an-array-is-a-min-heap.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Check if Array is Min-Heap asks whether an array represents a valid min-heap using binary-heap indexing. For index i, the left child is 2i + 1 and the right child is 2i + 2. The array is valid only if every parent is less than or equal to each existing child.

### Core intuition
The heap property is local. We do not need to compare every element with every later value. If every parent satisfies the rule with its direct children, then the complete tree satisfies the min-heap property.

### Which indices need checking
All indices from floor(N / 2) onward are leaves. Leaves have no children, so they cannot violate the parent-child rule. Only parent indices from 0 through N / 2 - 1 need checking.

### Invariant
For each checked index, arr[i] must be less than or equal to arr[2i + 1] and arr[2i + 2], when those children exist.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not require the array to be sorted. A valid heap can look unsorted. Do not read the right child unless it is inside bounds. Equality is allowed unless the problem says strictly smaller.

### Complexity
Each parent is checked once, so time is O(N). Extra space is O(1).

kth-smallest-element-in-an-array.name: Max-Heap of size K
kth-smallest-element-in-an-array.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Kth Smallest Element asks for the value that would sit at position K in sorted ascending order. Duplicates count as separate positions unless the problem explicitly asks for distinct values.

### Core intuition
Keep only the K smallest values seen so far. To know which kept value is the easiest to discard, use a max-heap of size K. Its root is the largest among the current K smallest values. When a new value is smaller than that root, it belongs in the K-smallest group, so replace the root. When a new value is larger, ignore it.

### Why a max-heap
For Kth smallest, the boundary value is the largest value among the K smallest. A max-heap gives that boundary at the root.

### Invariant
After processing each prefix, the heap contains the K smallest values from that prefix, and the root is the Kth smallest for that prefix.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not remove duplicates unless required. Do not use a min-heap of size K for this exact strategy; min-heaps are natural for Kth largest. Validate that K is between 1 and N.

### Complexity
The heap size is K, so each replacement costs O(log K). Total time is O(N log K), and space is O(K).

sort-k-sorted-array.name: Min-Heap (Gold Standard)
sort-k-sorted-array.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Sort a K-Sorted Array gives an array where every element is at most K positions away from its correct sorted position. This promise means the next sorted output value must be nearby, not somewhere far across the array.

### Core intuition
Use a min-heap of size K + 1. The first output value must be among the first K + 1 elements, because any element that belongs at index 0 can be displaced at most K positions. Pop the smallest as the next sorted value, then push the next unseen array element. Repeat until the input is consumed, then drain the heap.

### Invariant
Before writing each output position, the heap contains all candidates that could legally belong there. Therefore popping the heap gives the correct next sorted value.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not use heap size K; the candidate window has K + 1 elements. Do not forget to drain remaining heap values. Also do not apply this optimization unless the K-sorted guarantee exists.

### Complexity
Each of N values is pushed and popped once from a heap of size K + 1, so time is O(N log K). Space is O(K).

replace-elements-by-its-rank-in-the-arra.name: Brute Force
replace-elements-by-its-rank-in-the-arra.description: Build a correctness baseline for Replace Elements by its Rank by directly enumerating the candidate states allowed by the problem. This version prioritizes transparent control flow and exhaustive coverage over asymptotic efficiency.
replace-elements-by-its-rank-in-the-arra.timeComplexity: O(N²) ,Because for each element (N times), we iterate the full array again (N)

replace-elements-by-its-rank-in-the-arra.name: Sorting + Map
replace-elements-by-its-rank-in-the-arra.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Replace Elements by Rank asks us to replace each value by its rank among sorted unique values. The smallest unique value gets rank 1, the next unique value gets rank 2, and equal values receive the same rank.

### Core intuition
Ranks are based on sorted value order, not original position. First collect unique values, sort them, and assign ranks from 1 upward. Then walk the original array and translate each value using the rank map.

### Why duplicates share rank
If 20 appears three times, all copies still represent the same value on the sorted scale. Ranking distinct values prevents duplicate copies from shifting later ranks.

### Invariant
After building the rank map, every unique input value has exactly one 1-based rank. The final pass preserves the original array order while replacing values.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not return the sorted ranks; output must match original positions. Do not give different ranks to equal values. Negative numbers and zero rank normally by numeric order.

### Complexity
Sorting unique values dominates. Time is O(N log N) in the worst case, and space is O(N) for the unique list and map.

task-scheduler.name: Max-Heap (Gold Standard)
task-scheduler.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Task Scheduler gives tasks and a cooldown N. The same task type must be separated by at least N time units. We need the minimum total time to finish all tasks, counting idle slots if no legal task can run.

### Core intuition
The most frequent task types create the most scheduling pressure. Use a max-heap to run the task type with the largest remaining count whenever it is available. After running a task, if copies remain, place it into a cooldown queue with the time when it can return to the heap.

### Heap and cooldown roles
The heap contains task types available now. The queue contains task types that still have work left but are cooling down. Time advances one unit at a time until both are empty.

### Invariant
At every time unit, a task can run only if it is in the heap. A task in cooldown cannot be selected until its cooldown expiry time arrives.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not immediately reinsert a task after running it. Do not ignore idle time when the heap is empty but cooldown tasks remain. Be consistent about whether a task returns at time + N or time + N + 1 based on your clock update style.

### Complexity
For T tasks and A distinct task types, heap work is O(T log A). Space is O(A).

hand-of-straights.name: Min-Heap (Gold Standard)
hand-of-straights.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Hand of Straights asks whether all cards can be rearranged into groups of size groupSize where each group is consecutive. Every card must be used exactly once.

### Core intuition
Always start from the smallest remaining card. It cannot appear in the middle of a group that starts earlier, because no smaller remaining card exists. So if the smallest card is x, we must be able to consume x, x + 1, up to x + groupSize - 1. A frequency map tracks card counts, and a min-heap finds the smallest remaining card.

### Why smallest-first is safe
Any valid arrangement must place the smallest remaining card at the beginning of some group. If the required following cards are missing, no later decision can fix that.

### Invariant
The heap root is the smallest card value with remaining count. Each successful group consumes one copy of each required consecutive value.

### Mistakes to avoid
Reject immediately if hand length is not divisible by groupSize. Do not leave zero-count values acting as active heap roots. Do not sort into chunks without respecting duplicate counts.

### Complexity
Using counts and heap gives O(N log U) style behavior, where U is the number of distinct values. Space is O(U).

design-twitter.name: K-Way Merge (Gold Standard)
design-twitter.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Design Twitter asks us to support posting tweets, following and unfollowing users, and returning the 10 most recent tweet IDs in a user's news feed. The feed includes the user's own tweets and the tweets of followed users.

### Core intuition
Store each user's tweets in time order with a global timestamp. To build a feed, merge the latest tweets from several users. This is a K-way merge: each user contributes one sorted tweet list. Seed a heap with the newest tweet from each relevant user, pop the newest, and then insert the previous tweet from that same user.

### Data structures
Use a map from user ID to tweet list, and a map from user ID to a followee set. During getNewsFeed, include the user themself, then heap-merge at most 10 tweets.

### Invariant
The heap contains the current newest not-yet-output tweet from each active source list. Popping one tweet advances only that source list.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not scan every tweet from every followed user when only 10 are needed. Do not forget self tweets. Be careful with unfollow so a user does not lose access to their own posts.

### Complexity
Posting is O(1). Follow and unfollow are O(1) average. Feed generation is O(F + 10 log F), where F is the number of followed users with tweets. Space is O(U + T).

merge-k-sorted-lists.name: Min-Heap of Nodes
merge-k-sorted-lists.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Merge K Sorted Lists gives several already-sorted linked lists and asks for one sorted linked list containing all nodes. Since every input list is sorted, we should not flatten everything and sort again if we can avoid it.

### Core intuition
This is a K-way merge. Put the head node of each non-empty list into a min-heap ordered by node value. The heap root is the smallest current node across all lists. Pop it, append it to the result, and if that node has a next pointer, push the next node from the same list into the heap.

### Why the heap stays small
At any moment, the heap needs only one active candidate from each list. Once a node is chosen from a list, its successor becomes that list's new candidate.

### Invariant
The heap contains the smallest not-yet-used node from each list that still has remaining nodes. Popping the heap always gives the next node in global sorted order.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not compare linked-list nodes directly in languages where equal values make objects unorderable; include a tie breaker such as list index or counter. Do not forget to push node.next after popping a node.

### Complexity
Every one of the N total nodes is pushed and popped once from a heap of size at most K. Time is O(N log K), and heap space is O(K).

find-minimum-number-of-coins.name: Greedy Denomination Selection
find-minimum-number-of-coins.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Find Minimum Number of Coins asks for the fewest coins or notes needed to make a target amount using a standard denomination set, such as 1, 2, 5, 10, 20, 50, 100, 500, and 2000. The catalog approach uses greedy selection.

### Core intuition
Always take the largest denomination that does not exceed the remaining amount. Real-world denomination systems are usually designed so this greedy choice is optimal. Taking a large note reduces the remaining amount as much as possible without overshooting.

### Important limitation
Greedy is not correct for every imaginary coin system. For coins [1, 3, 4] and amount 6, greedy takes 4 + 1 + 1, but 3 + 3 is better. The method is correct for the standard denominations used in this problem.

### Invariant
After each selection, the remaining amount is still non-negative, and the chosen notes sum to the original amount minus the remainder.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not use greedy blindly for arbitrary coin sets. Do not skip smaller denominations after taking large ones; the remainder may need them. Also return the count if the problem asks count, or the actual list if it asks denominations.

### Complexity
For a fixed currency set, time is effectively O(1). More generally it is O(D + number of coins chosen), where D is the number of denominations. Space is O(1) if only count is returned.

minimum-number-of-platforms-required-for.name: Brute Force
minimum-number-of-platforms-required-for.description: Build a correctness baseline for Minimum Platforms by directly enumerating the candidate states allowed by the problem. This version prioritizes transparent control flow and exhaustive coverage over asymptotic efficiency.

minimum-number-of-platforms-required-for.name: Two-Pointer (Sorting)
minimum-number-of-platforms-required-for.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Minimum Platforms asks how many railway platforms are needed so that no arriving train has to wait. Each train occupies a platform from its arrival time until its departure time. The answer is the maximum number of trains present at the station at the same time.

### Core intuition
Sort all arrival times and departure times separately. Walk through time using two pointers. If the next arrival is before or equal to the earliest pending departure, a new train needs a platform. If the next departure happens before the next arrival, one platform is freed.

### Why separate sorting works
We do not need to preserve which departure belongs to which train. For platform count, we only care about the event timeline: arrivals increase active trains, departures decrease active trains.

### Invariant
platformsNeeded is the number of trains currently occupying platforms after processing events up to the current point. The result is the maximum value it ever reaches.

### Mistakes to avoid
If arrival time equals departure time, many versions count that as needing another platform, so use arrival <= departure for allocation. Do not compare unsorted schedules. Do not reset the count per train; this is about concurrent occupancy.

### Complexity
Sorting dominates with O(N log N). The two-pointer scan is O(N). Extra space is O(1) beyond sorted arrays.

insert-interval.name: Linear Scan (Three-Phase)
insert-interval.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Insert Interval gives sorted, non-overlapping intervals and one new interval. We must insert the new interval in the correct place and merge any intervals that overlap with it. The existing sorted order is a gift: we can solve it in one scan.

### Core intuition
There are three phases. First, copy every interval that ends before the new interval starts; these cannot overlap. Second, while intervals overlap the new interval, merge them by expanding the new interval's start and end. Third, append the merged new interval and then copy the remaining intervals.

### Overlap rule
An interval [a, b] overlaps the current new interval [x, y] if a <= y and b >= x. In the scan, after the before-phase, overlap checking is usually intervals[i][0] <= newEnd.

### Invariant
Before the merge phase, result contains all intervals definitely before the new interval. During merge, newInterval always represents the union of all overlapping intervals seen so far.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not append the new interval before merging all overlaps. Do not forget intervals after the merge phase. Also be careful with touching endpoints if the problem treats [1,2] and [2,3] as overlapping; the usual closed interval rule says they overlap.

### Complexity
The scan is O(N), and the result array uses O(N) space.

non-overlapping-intervals.name: Greedy by Finish Time
non-overlapping-intervals.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Non-overlapping Intervals asks for the minimum number of intervals to remove so that the remaining intervals do not overlap. Instead of directly choosing removals, it is easier to choose the maximum number of intervals we can keep. Removals equal total intervals minus kept intervals.

### Core intuition
Sort intervals by end time and greedily keep the interval that finishes earliest. An interval that ends earlier leaves more room for future intervals, so it is the safest one to keep. Then scan forward and keep an interval only if its start is at least the end of the last kept interval.

### Invariant
lastEnd is the end time of the most recently kept interval. The kept set is non-overlapping, and among choices so far it leaves as much future room as possible.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not sort by start time for this greedy proof; earliest finish is the key. Do not count touching intervals as overlapping if the problem allows start >= lastEnd. Also remember the answer is removals, not kept count.

### Complexity
Sorting costs O(N log N). The scan is O(N), and extra space is O(1) aside from sorting overhead.

introduction-to-trees.name: Structure & Concepts
introduction-to-trees.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact topic is teaching
Introduction to Trees explains hierarchical data. A tree is made of nodes connected by edges. One node is the root, and every other node descends from it. In a binary tree, each node has at most two children, usually called left and right.

### Core intuition
Unlike arrays and linked lists, trees branch. A node can lead to multiple smaller substructures called subtrees. That is why tree algorithms often feel recursive: solve the current node, then solve the left subtree, then solve the right subtree, or some variation of that order.

### Key vocabulary
The root is the top node. A leaf has no children. A parent points to children. Ancestors are nodes above a node on the path to root. Descendants are nodes below. Depth counts edges from root to a node. Height counts the longest downward path from a node to a leaf.

### Invariant
Every subtree is itself a tree. This self-similar structure is the reason recursion maps so naturally onto trees.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not assume a binary tree is sorted; that is only true for binary search trees. Do not assume every node has two children. Also distinguish depth from height: one looks upward to the root, the other downward to leaves.

### Complexity
This is a conceptual entry. Traversing a tree with N nodes usually takes O(N), and recursion stack depends on height H.

binary-tree-representation-in-c.name: Pointer-Based Representation
binary-tree-representation-in-c.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Binary Tree Representation asks how to store a binary tree node in code. Each node needs a value and two links: one to the left child and one to the right child. Those links may be null when a child is missing.

### Core intuition
A pointer-based node is a tiny record: data, left pointer, right pointer. By connecting nodes through these pointers, the tree can grow dynamically and does not need to be stored contiguously in memory.

### Why pointers or references
A tree branches, so each node must know where its children live. In C or C++, those links are pointers. In languages like Python or JavaScript, they are object references. The idea is the same.

### Invariant
For any node, left points to the root of the left subtree, right points to the root of the right subtree, and null means that subtree is empty.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not forget to initialize left and right to null. Uninitialized pointers can point anywhere in low-level languages. Do not confuse this representation with heap-style array representation; pointer trees do not require complete shape.

### Complexity
Creating one node is O(1) time and O(1) space. A tree with N nodes uses O(N) total node storage.

preorder-traversal.name: Recursive / Iterative
preorder-traversal.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Preorder Traversal asks us to visit every node in Root, Left, Right order. That means we process the current node before exploring either child subtree.

### Core intuition
Preorder is a process-as-you-arrive traversal. At each node, record its value immediately. Then recursively traverse the left subtree, then recursively traverse the right subtree. The iterative version uses a stack; because stacks are last-in-first-out, push right child before left child so left is processed first.

### Invariant
When preorder visits a node, that node is recorded before any node in its left or right subtree. Left subtree nodes appear before right subtree nodes.

### When preorder is useful
Preorder is natural for copying or serializing a tree because it writes the root before the structure beneath it. With null markers, preorder can reconstruct tree shape.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not record the node between left and right; that is inorder. Do not record after both children; that is postorder. In iterative preorder, pushing left before right causes right to be processed first, which is wrong unless you reverse later.

### Complexity
Every node is visited once, so time is O(N). Recursion or stack space is O(H), where H is tree height, or O(N) in the worst skewed case.

postorder-traversal.name: Recursive / Iterative
postorder-traversal.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Postorder Traversal asks us to visit every node in Left, Right, Root order. The parent is processed only after both child subtrees are completely processed.

### Core intuition
Postorder is a finish-children-first traversal. Recursively traverse the left subtree, then the right subtree, then record the current node. This makes it ideal for tasks where a parent depends on information from its children, such as computing height, deleting a tree, or evaluating expression trees.

### Invariant
A node appears in the output only after every node in its left subtree and every node in its right subtree have already appeared.

### Iterative idea
One common iterative trick performs reverse preorder: Root, Right, Left. Reversing that result gives Left, Right, Root. More advanced one-stack methods simulate the recursion directly.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not record the root first; that is preorder. Do not record between left and right; that is inorder. Also remember that postorder of an empty tree is an empty list.

### Complexity
Every node is visited once, so time is O(N). Stack space is O(H) for recursion, or O(N) in the worst case.

iterative-preorder.name: Stack Approach
iterative-preorder.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Iterative Preorder asks for preorder traversal without using recursion. Preorder means visit the current node first, then its left subtree, then its right subtree: Root, Left, Right.

### Core intuition
Recursive preorder automatically remembers where to return using the call stack. The iterative version uses an explicit stack. Start by pushing the root. Each time, pop a node, record it, then push its right child before its left child. Because a stack is last-in-first-out, the left child comes out first.

### State meaning
The stack stores nodes whose turn is still pending. The result list stores nodes that have already been visited in preorder order.

### Invariant
Whenever a node is popped, every ancestor that should appear before it has already been recorded. By pushing right first and left second, the stack always gives priority to the left subtree before the right subtree.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not push left before right unless you intentionally reverse the final order; doing so makes the right child pop first. Also do not delay recording the node until after children, because that changes the traversal to another order.

### Complexity
Each node is pushed and popped once, so time is O(N). The stack can hold up to O(H) nodes for a balanced tree and O(N) in the worst skewed tree.

iterative-postorder-using-2-stacks.name: Two-Stack Reverse Strategy
iterative-postorder-using-2-stacks.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Iterative Postorder using 2 Stacks asks for Left, Right, Root order without recursion. Postorder is naturally bottom-up, so the parent should be recorded only after both children.

### Core intuition
Use the first stack to perform a modified preorder: Root, Left, Right or Root, Right, Left depending on push order. Store the popped nodes in a second stack. When the second stack is later reversed or popped, the order becomes Left, Right, Root.

### Why two stacks feel easier
The first stack discovers nodes. The second stack delays output until children have been placed before their parent. This avoids the tricky last-visited bookkeeping needed in the one-stack version.

### Invariant
A node enters the second stack before its children are output. Popping the second stack reverses that relationship, so children appear before the node.

### Mistakes to avoid
Be consistent with child push order and reversal. Also remember the second stack may store node references or values; if you store values, you cannot later use them to traverse children.

### Complexity
Every node is pushed and popped a constant number of times, so time is O(N). Space is O(N) because the two stacks can together hold many nodes.

iterative-postorder-using-1-stack.name: Last Visited Tracking
iterative-postorder-using-1-stack.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Iterative Postorder using 1 Stack asks for Left, Right, Root order while using only one explicit stack. This is harder because the algorithm must know whether a node's right subtree has already been processed.

### Core intuition
Move left as far as possible, pushing ancestors. When there is no left child to enter, peek at the stack top. If its right child exists and has not been visited yet, move to that right child. Otherwise, both children are done, so pop and record the node.

### State meaning
curr is the node currently being explored. stack stores ancestors waiting for their postorder moment. lastVisited remembers the most recent node that was fully processed, so we do not enter the same right subtree twice.

### Invariant
A node is recorded only when its left subtree is done and its right subtree is either absent or already equal to lastVisited.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not pop a node immediately after finishing its left side if it still has an unvisited right side. Also update lastVisited exactly when a node is output, not when it is merely seen.

### Complexity
Each node is pushed once and inspected a small constant number of times, so time is O(N). Stack space is O(H), or O(N) for a skewed tree.

preorder-inorder-postorder-in-a-single-t.name: Stack State Management
preorder-inorder-postorder-in-a-single-t.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
This problem asks us to produce preorder, inorder, and postorder traversals in one pass over the tree. Instead of running three separate traversals, each node carries a small state that says which visit moment it is currently at.

### Core intuition
Push pairs of (node, state). State 1 means the node has just been reached, so add it to preorder, change state to 2, and go left. State 2 means the left side is done, so add it to inorder, change state to 3, and go right. State 3 means both sides are done, so add it to postorder and pop it.

### State meaning
The state simulates the three natural moments in a recursive call: before left recursion, between left and right recursion, and after right recursion.

### Invariant
A node appears in preorder at state 1, inorder at state 2, and postorder at state 3. No node is removed from the stack until all three moments have been handled.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not push children before updating the parent's state, or the algorithm may revisit the same phase. Also keep separate output arrays; the same node value belongs in all three, but at different times.

### Complexity
Each node goes through three constant-time states, so time is O(N). The stack uses O(H) space on average and O(N) in the worst case.

check-if-a-tree-is-balanced-or-not.name: Brute Force
check-if-a-tree-is-balanced-or-not.description: Build a correctness baseline for Balanced Binary Tree by directly enumerating the candidate states allowed by the problem. This version prioritizes transparent control flow and exhaustive coverage over asymptotic efficiency.

check-if-a-tree-is-balanced-or-not.name: Optimized Recursion
check-if-a-tree-is-balanced-or-not.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Balanced Binary Tree asks whether every node's left and right subtree heights differ by at most 1. The important phrase is every node: a tree can look balanced at the root but still contain an unbalanced subtree lower down.

### Core intuition
Use a bottom-up DFS. For each subtree, return its height if it is balanced. If it is not balanced, return -1 as a warning signal. Once -1 appears, it bubbles upward, because any tree containing an unbalanced subtree is also unbalanced.

### Why this beats the naive method
A naive solution computes height again and again for many nodes, which can become O(N^2). The optimized method checks balance and height together in one postorder traversal.

### Invariant
The helper returns a non-negative height only when the subtree is truly balanced. It returns -1 when either child is already unbalanced or the current height difference is greater than 1.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not check only the root's left and right heights. Do not keep computing height separately inside every node's balance check. Also be consistent about null height being 0.

### Complexity
Every node is processed once, so time is O(N). The recursion stack uses O(H) space.

maximum-path-sum.name: Greedy Path Selection (Recursive)
maximum-path-sum.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Maximum Path Sum asks for the highest sum of values along any path in a binary tree. A path can start and end at any nodes, but it must follow parent-child connections and cannot branch more than once through a node.

### Core intuition
At each node, there are two different values to think about. The best path that uses this node as the highest turning point may take the left gain and the right gain together. But the value returned to the parent can use only one side, because a parent path cannot split into both children and still remain a path.

### State meaning
The recursive helper returns the best one-branch gain starting at the current node and going downward. A global answer stores the best complete path seen anywhere, possibly using both branches at a node.

### Invariant
After processing a node, the global answer has considered the best path whose peak is that node. The returned value remains a single chain that the parent can extend.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not return left plus right to the parent; that would create a fork. Also clamp negative child gains to 0, because a negative branch only makes the path worse. Initialize the answer to negative infinity so all-negative trees work.

### Complexity
Every node is visited once, so time is O(N). Recursion uses O(H) stack space.

check-if-two-trees-are-identical-or-not.name: Recursive Comparison
check-if-two-trees-are-identical-or-not.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Identical Trees asks whether two binary trees have exactly the same shape and the same value at every corresponding node. Both structure and data must match.

### Core intuition
Compare the two roots. If both are null, that part matches. If only one is null, the shape differs. If both exist, their values must match, and their left subtrees and right subtrees must also be identical.

### Why recursion fits
A tree is built from smaller trees. So the sentence two trees are same becomes: roots match, left subtrees are same, right subtrees are same. That is already the recursive function.

### Invariant
isSame(p, q) returns true only when the entire subtree rooted at p matches the entire subtree rooted at q in both structure and values.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not compare only traversal arrays unless null markers are included; different shapes can share the same traversal values. Also do not stop after root values match, because children may differ.

### Complexity
Each pair of corresponding nodes is visited once, so time is O(N), where N is the smaller comparable traversal size until a mismatch or the full tree. Recursion uses O(H) space.

zig-zag-traversal.name: Level Order with Toggle
zig-zag-traversal.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Zig-Zag Traversal asks for level order traversal, but the reading direction alternates at each depth: left to right, then right to left, then left to right again.

### Core intuition
Use normal BFS to process one level at a time. For each level, collect the node values. If the current direction is right-to-left, reverse that level before appending it to the result. Then toggle the direction for the next level.

### State meaning
The queue stores nodes for BFS. direction tells how the current level should be read. level stores values for one depth before they are appended.

### Invariant
Nodes are always discovered left child before right child, so the queue remains stable for future levels. The zig-zag effect is applied only to how the current level is written into the answer.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not reverse the queue itself casually; that can disturb child discovery for later levels. If performance matters, fill values into a fixed-size level array from either end instead of reversing each level.

### Complexity
Every node is processed once. Time is O(N), plus reversals whose total work is O(N). Space is O(N) for the queue/result.

boundary-traversal.name: Composite Traversal
boundary-traversal.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Boundary Traversal asks for the outside edge of a binary tree in anti-clockwise order: root, left boundary, all leaves, then right boundary in reverse. Leaves should appear only once.

### Core intuition
Break the boundary into three controlled parts. Add the left boundary excluding leaves. Add all leaves from left to right. Add the right boundary excluding leaves, but collect it temporarily and append it reversed.

### Why exclude leaves from boundaries
Leaves are handled by the leaf pass. If the left or right boundary also adds them, corner leaves get duplicated.

### Invariant
The result first follows the left wall downward, then sweeps every leaf left-to-right, then climbs the right wall upward by reversing the collected right boundary.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not add the root twice when the root is also a leaf. Do not include leaf nodes in left/right boundary helpers. On a missing left boundary, follow right children as the visible left edge, and vice versa for the right boundary.

### Complexity
Each node is visited at most a constant number of times, so time is O(N). Recursion stack for leaves is O(H), and the temporary right boundary list is O(H).

vertical-order-traversal.name: BFS with Coordinate Map
vertical-order-traversal.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Vertical Order Traversal asks us to view the tree in vertical columns. Give the root column 0. A left child moves to column -1, and a right child moves to column +1. Nodes in the same column are ordered by row, and ties are ordered by value for the LeetCode version.

### Core intuition
Traverse the tree while assigning each node coordinates: column x and row y. Store each node as (x, y, value). After traversal, sort by x first, then y, then value. Finally, group values with the same x into columns.

### Why coordinates remove confusion
Without coordinates, it is easy to argue about which node should appear first when two nodes share a column. The coordinate rule gives a precise ordering: upper nodes first, and equal-position nodes sorted by value.

### Invariant
Every stored tuple accurately represents where the node appears in the tree grid. Sorting those tuples creates the required top-to-bottom and left-to-right vertical view.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not group by column only and ignore row ordering. For LeetCode 987, if two nodes share both row and column, smaller value comes first. Also handle negative column keys cleanly when sorting.

### Complexity
Traversal is O(N). Sorting all node tuples costs O(N log N). The coordinate map or tuple list uses O(N) space.

bottom-view-of-binary-tree.name: BFS with Map Overwriting
bottom-view-of-binary-tree.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Bottom View asks which nodes are visible if we look at the binary tree from below. Assign each node a horizontal distance from the root: root is 0, left child is -1, right child is +1. For every horizontal distance, the visible node is the lowest one in that column.

### Core intuition
Use BFS with horizontal distances. BFS moves level by level from top to bottom. For each visited node, overwrite the value stored for its horizontal distance. Since lower nodes are processed later, the final value left in each column is the bottom-most visible node.

### State meaning
The queue stores pairs of node and horizontal distance. The map stores horizontal distance to the latest visible value for that column. At the end, sorting horizontal distances from left to right gives the answer.

### Invariant
After processing all nodes up to some level, the map contains the deepest processed node for each horizontal distance seen so far.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not store only the first node per column; that is top view. Do not forget to sort horizontal distances before output. If a platform has a special tie rule for nodes at the same depth and same column, follow that rule; the common BFS overwrite version naturally lets the later visited node win.

### Complexity
Each node is visited once, so traversal is O(N). Sorting column keys costs O(K log K), where K is the number of columns. Space is O(N) for queue and map in the worst case.

symmetric-binary-tree.name: Recursive Mirror Check
symmetric-binary-tree.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Symmetric Binary Tree asks whether a tree is a mirror of itself around the root. The left subtree and right subtree must have matching values in opposite positions.

### Core intuition
Compare two nodes at a time as mirrors. If both are null, that mirror position is fine. If only one is null, the shape breaks symmetry. If both exist, their values must match, and the outside pair must mirror each other while the inside pair must mirror each other: left.left with right.right, and left.right with right.left.

### State meaning
The helper receives two nodes that should be mirror partners. It returns true only if their entire subtrees reflect each other.

### Invariant
At every recursive step, the two input nodes occupy symmetric positions relative to the root. The tree remains symmetric only if those two positions match in value and their children cross-match.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not compare left.left with right.left; that checks same-side shape, not mirror shape. Do not rely only on inorder traversal values, because structure matters. Also remember an empty tree is symmetric.

### Complexity
Each node is compared once with its mirror partner, so time is O(N). The recursion stack uses O(H) space.

root-to-node-path.name: Backtracking / DFS
root-to-node-path.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Root to Node Path asks for the sequence of values from the root of a binary tree to a given target node. The path must follow actual parent-child edges.

### Core intuition
Use DFS with backtracking. When you enter a node, add it to the path. If it is the target, the path is complete. Otherwise search left and right. If neither side finds the target, remove the current node before returning false, because this node is not on the final path.

### State meaning
The path list is the current root-to-current-node trail. The boolean return value tells the caller whether the target was found somewhere below the current node.

### Invariant
At the moment DFS is inside a node, path contains exactly the nodes from root to that node. If the search leaves that node without success, path is restored to what it was before entering.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not forget to pop during backtracking, or failed branches will leak into the answer. Do not stop after checking only one subtree unless it returned true. If node values are not unique, search by node reference instead of value.

### Complexity
In the worst case, DFS visits every node, so time is O(N). The path and recursion stack use O(H) space, or O(N) for a skewed tree.

lca-in-binary-tree.name: Recursive Search
lca-in-binary-tree.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Lowest Common Ancestor in a Binary Tree asks for the deepest node that has both target nodes p and q in its subtree. Unlike a BST problem, there is no ordering rule, so we must search both sides.

### Core intuition
A recursive call returns null if it finds neither target. It returns a target node if it finds p or q. It may also return an already-discovered LCA from below. At a node, search left and right. If both sides return non-null, one target was found on each side, so the current node is the LCA. If only one side returns non-null, pass that result upward.

### State meaning
The return value is a signal: nothing found, one target found, or the final ancestor found. The same return channel carries all three meanings through node references.

### Invariant
After processing a node, the returned value correctly represents the LCA or found target within that node's subtree.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not use BST value comparisons in a normal binary tree. Do not stop at the first found target unless the current root itself is p or q; the other target may be elsewhere. Many versions assume both nodes exist; if not, add existence validation.

### Complexity
The search may visit every node, so time is O(N). The recursion stack uses O(H) space.

maximum-width-of-a-binary-tree.name: BFS with Indexing
maximum-width-of-a-binary-tree.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Maximum Width asks for the widest level of a binary tree, counting the null gaps between the leftmost and rightmost non-null nodes at that level. So this is not just the number of real nodes on a level.

### Core intuition
Pretend the tree is stored like a heap array. Give the root index 0. For a node with index i, left child gets 2i and right child gets 2i + 1. At each BFS level, width is lastIndex - firstIndex + 1. Normalize indices at each level by subtracting the first index to avoid huge numbers.

### State meaning
The queue stores pairs of node and position index. The answer stores the largest width seen so far.

### Invariant
Within one level, the assigned indices preserve each node's position as if missing null nodes still occupied slots. Therefore the difference between the last and first indices includes internal gaps.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not count only queue size; that misses gaps. Do not let indices grow without normalization in fixed-width integer languages. Also compute width before pushing the next level's children or carefully freeze the current level size.

### Complexity
Every real node is visited once, so time is O(N). The queue can hold O(N) nodes in the worst case.

check-for-children-sum-property.name: Top-Down / Bottom-Up Adjustment
check-for-children-sum-property.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Children Sum Property asks us to transform a binary tree so that every non-leaf node's value equals the sum of its children's values. The usual constraint is that values may be increased but not decreased.

### Core intuition
Use a top-down then bottom-up DFS. On the way down, if the current node is larger than the sum of its children, push that value into its children so they have enough value to support the parent later. If the children already sum to more, raise the current node. After processing children recursively, set the current node to the final sum of its children.

### Why two directions are needed
Top-down propagation prevents children from being too small. Bottom-up recomputation makes each parent exactly equal to the finished child sum.

### Invariant
After a subtree is processed, it satisfies the children sum property, and no node value was decreased.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not update a leaf to zero just because it has no children; leaves are already valid. Do not only fix the root. Also be careful when only one child exists: the missing child contributes 0.

### Complexity
Each node is adjusted a constant number of times, so time is O(N). Recursion uses O(H) stack space.

print-all-the-nodes-at-a-distance-of-k-i.name: BFS with Parent Map
print-all-the-nodes-at-a-distance-of-k-i.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Nodes at Distance K asks for all nodes exactly K edges away from a target node in a binary tree. From the target, distance can move downward to children or upward to the parent.

### Core intuition
A binary tree node usually knows only its children, so first build a parent map. Then treat the tree like an undirected graph. Start BFS from the target and explore three possible neighbors: left child, right child, and parent. When the BFS level reaches K, the nodes currently found are the answer.

### State meaning
The parent map gives upward edges. The visited set prevents walking back and forth forever. The BFS queue stores each node with its current distance from the target.

### Invariant
When a node is popped with distance d, BFS has reached it through the shortest path from the target. Therefore all nodes first seen at distance K are exactly K edges away.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not search only below the target; answers can be in an ancestor's other subtree. Do not skip the visited set after adding parent pointers, because the structure becomes an undirected graph. Also stop expanding nodes once distance equals K.

### Complexity
Building the parent map is O(N), and BFS is O(N) in the worst case. Space is O(N) for the map, queue, and visited set.

minimum-time-taken-to-burn-the-binary-tr.name: BFS (Propagated Expansion)
minimum-time-taken-to-burn-the-binary-tr.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Burn Time of Binary Tree asks how many time units it takes for fire starting at one target node to reach every node. Each minute, fire spreads from a burning node to its left child, right child, and parent.

### Core intuition
This is the same as finding the farthest distance from the starting node after turning the tree into an undirected graph. Build parent links, find the target node, then run BFS from the target. The answer is the largest BFS level reached.

### State meaning
The queue stores burning frontier nodes with their time. The visited set stores nodes already burned. The maximum time seen so far becomes the burn time.

### Invariant
When BFS processes time t, every node at distance t from the start is burning. Once BFS finishes, the largest t is the time when the final node caught fire.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not spread only downward; fire can travel up through parents and then into sibling subtrees. Do not revisit the previous node, or the BFS will loop. Also handle the case where the start value is not found if the platform does not guarantee it.

### Complexity
Parent-map construction and burn BFS each take O(N). Space is O(N) for parent links, visited set, and queue.

count-total-nodes-in-a-complete-binary-t.name: Logarithmic Search
count-total-nodes-in-a-complete-binary-t.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Count Nodes in a Complete Binary Tree asks for the number of nodes faster than a plain traversal. A complete tree has every level full except maybe the last, and the last level is filled from left to right.

### Core intuition
For any subtree, measure the height along its far-left path and far-right path. If those heights are equal, the subtree is perfect, meaning all levels are full, so its node count is 2^height - 1. If the heights differ, recurse into children.

### Why this is faster
A complete tree contains many perfect subtrees. Instead of visiting every node inside those perfect parts, we count them with a formula.

### Invariant
Whenever left height equals right height, the subtree is perfect under the complete-tree property. Whenever they differ, at least one side needs further inspection.

### Mistakes to avoid
This optimization depends on completeness; do not use it for arbitrary binary trees. Be consistent about height counting nodes versus edges. Also remember an empty subtree has count 0.

### Complexity
At each recursive level we compute heights in O(log N), and there are O(log N) levels, so time is O(log^2 N). Recursion space is O(log N).

construct-binary-tree-from-inorder-and-p.name: Hash-Mapping & Recursion
construct-binary-tree-from-inorder-and-p.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Construct Binary Tree from Inorder and Postorder asks us to rebuild the original binary tree from two traversal arrays. Inorder tells how each root splits the tree into left and right subtrees. Postorder tells roots late: the last unused value is always the current root.

### Core intuition
Take the last value from postorder as the root. Find that value in inorder. Everything left of it in inorder belongs to the left subtree; everything right belongs to the right subtree. Because postorder is Left, Right, Root, when consuming it from the end we must build the right subtree before the left subtree.

### State meaning
The inorder bounds describe the current subtree. The postorder pointer, moving from right to left, gives the next root to place. A hashmap from value to inorder index lets each split happen in O(1).

### Invariant
Every recursive call rebuilds exactly the subtree represented by its inorder slice, using the remaining postorder values from the back.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not build the left subtree first when popping postorder from the end; that consumes right-subtree roots in the wrong place. Do not repeatedly scan inorder if you want O(N). This reconstruction assumes unique values.

### Complexity
With an index map, every node is created once, so time is O(N). Space is O(N) for the map and recursion stack in the worst case.

serialize-and-deserialize-binary-tree.name: Level-Order (BFS)
serialize-and-deserialize-binary-tree.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Serialize and Deserialize Binary Tree asks us to convert a tree into a string and then rebuild the exact same tree from that string. Exact shape matters, not just values.

### Core intuition
Use level-order traversal with null markers. During serialization, push real node values into the output and use a marker such as # for missing children. During deserialization, read tokens from left to right and use a queue of parents waiting for children. Each parent consumes two tokens: one for left, one for right.

### Why null markers are necessary
Without null markers, different tree shapes can produce the same value order. The markers preserve where children are missing, so reconstruction knows which child slots stay empty.

### State meaning
The serialization queue visits nodes breadth-first. The deserialization queue stores real nodes whose children have not been assigned yet. The token index points to the next child slot in the serialized data.

### Invariant
After processing a parent during deserialization, its left and right child slots exactly match the next two serialized tokens.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not omit nulls unless your format has another way to encode structure. Do not enqueue null children during deserialization. Also be careful with empty input representing an empty tree.

### Complexity
Serialization and deserialization each visit every stored token once, so time is O(N). Space is O(N) for the string and queue.

flatten-binary-tree-to-linkedlist.name: Better
flatten-binary-tree-to-linkedlist.description: Reduce repeated work in Flatten to LinkedList with the intermediate structure or cached state used by the standard better approach. It preserves the brute-force reasoning while improving one major time or space bottleneck.

flatten-binary-tree-to-linkedlist.name: Morris-based Re-wiring
flatten-binary-tree-to-linkedlist.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Flatten Binary Tree to Linked List asks us to rewrite the tree in-place so it becomes a single right-pointer chain following preorder traversal. Every left pointer should become null.

### Core intuition
At a node with a left subtree, the preorder order is node, then left subtree, then original right subtree. So find the rightmost node of the left subtree, attach the original right subtree there, move the left subtree to the right, and set left to null. Then continue along the right pointer.

### State meaning
curr is the node currently being rewired. prev is the rightmost node inside curr's left subtree. The right chain behind curr gradually becomes the flattened preorder list.

### Invariant
After processing a node, its left pointer is null, and its right pointer starts the preorder-flattened version of what used to be under that node.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not lose the original right subtree before attaching it to the left subtree's tail. Do not create new nodes if the problem asks in-place. Also remember the final shape is not a sorted list; it is preorder order.

### Complexity
Each node is visited, and each rightmost search is bounded by tree rewiring work overall in the Morris-style approach. Time is O(N), and extra space is O(1).

search-in-a-bst.name: Recursive / Iterative Search
search-in-a-bst.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Search in BST asks us to find the node whose value equals a target value. Because the tree is a Binary Search Tree, every left subtree value is smaller than the node, and every right subtree value is larger.

### Core intuition
Start at the root. If the target equals the current value, return the current node. If the target is smaller, the answer can only be in the left subtree. If larger, it can only be in the right subtree. This prunes half the direction at every step in a balanced tree.

### State meaning
current is the root of the remaining search area. Each comparison either finishes the search or discards one entire subtree.

### Invariant
If the target exists, it is always inside the subtree rooted at current. Moving left or right is safe because the BST ordering rules exclude the other side.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not search both subtrees like a normal binary tree; that wastes the BST property. Do not return just true if the platform asks for the node/subtree. Also clarify how duplicates are handled if the BST allows them.

### Complexity
Time is O(H), where H is tree height: O(log N) for a balanced tree and O(N) for a skewed tree. Iterative space is O(1).

find-min-max-in-bst.name: Greedy Traversal
find-min-max-in-bst.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Find Min and Max in BST asks for the smallest and largest values stored in a Binary Search Tree.

### Core intuition
In a BST, smaller values live to the left and larger values live to the right. Therefore the minimum is found by walking left until no left child exists. The maximum is found by walking right until no right child exists.

### State meaning
For minimum, current moves down the left spine. For maximum, current moves down the right spine. The last reachable node in that direction is the answer.

### Invariant
While finding minimum, every step left moves to a strictly smaller candidate, and anything to the right of the current path cannot be smaller than the current node. The maximum follows the mirrored rule.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not traverse the whole tree. Do not return the root just because it is near the middle. Handle an empty tree according to the platform convention, such as returning -1 or null.

### Complexity
Each search follows one root-to-leaf path, so time is O(H). Iterative space is O(1).

insert-a-given-node-in-bst.name: Iterative Pointer Hunt
insert-a-given-node-in-bst.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Insert into BST asks us to add a new value while preserving the Binary Search Tree ordering rule. The existing tree structure should remain as unchanged as possible, with one new leaf attached in the correct position.

### Core intuition
Search for the value as if it were already in the tree. When the search reaches a missing child pointer, that is exactly where the new node belongs. If the value is smaller than current, move left; otherwise move right, according to the duplicate policy used by the implementation.

### State meaning
current walks down the tree. The parent is implicitly the node whose missing child will receive the new value.

### Invariant
At every step, the future insertion point is inside the current subtree. Moving left or right preserves all ancestor constraints.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not rebuild or sort the whole tree. Do not insert the node at the first null without respecting all comparisons along the path. If duplicates are possible, decide consistently whether they go left or right.

### Complexity
Insertion follows one root-to-leaf path, so time is O(H). Iterative insertion uses O(1) extra space.

delete-a-node-in-bst.name: Structural Re-linking
delete-a-node-in-bst.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Delete from BST asks us to remove a key while keeping the Binary Search Tree valid. The tricky part is reconnecting children correctly after the target node disappears.

### Core intuition
First search for the node. If it has no child, replace it with null. If it has one child, replace it with that child. If it has two children, use either the inorder predecessor or successor to bridge the gap. The catalog approach attaches the original right subtree to the rightmost node of the left subtree, then returns the left subtree as the replacement.

### State meaning
The search finds the parent link that points to the node being deleted. The helper returns the subtree root that should replace the deleted node at that link.

### Invariant
After helper returns, every value in the replacement subtree still satisfies the lower and upper bounds imposed by the deleted node's ancestors.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not simply drop a node with two children or an entire subtree will be lost. Do not attach the right subtree to an arbitrary node; it must go after the largest value in the left subtree if using predecessor-style relinking. Handle deletion of the root specially by returning the replacement.

### Complexity
Search and relinking follow tree height, so time is O(H). Recursive versions use O(H) stack; iterative relinking can use O(1) extra space.

construct-a-bst-from-a-preorder-traversa.name: Brute Force
construct-a-bst-from-a-preorder-traversa.description: Build a correctness baseline for Construct BST from Preorder by directly enumerating the candidate states allowed by the problem. This version prioritizes transparent control flow and exhaustive coverage over asymptotic efficiency.

construct-a-bst-from-a-preorder-traversa.name: Recursive Bound Constraint
construct-a-bst-from-a-preorder-traversa.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Construct BST from Preorder asks us to rebuild a Binary Search Tree from its preorder traversal. Preorder gives nodes in Root, Left, Right order, and the BST property tells which values are allowed in each subtree.

### Core intuition
Read preorder from left to right. Each recursive call has an upper bound. If the next value is greater than the bound, it does not belong in this subtree. Otherwise create a node, build its left subtree with bound equal to the node's value, and build its right subtree with the inherited bound.

### State meaning
The index points to the next preorder value not yet placed. The bound says the maximum value allowed in the current subtree.

### Invariant
Every recursive call consumes exactly the values that belong under its bound and leaves the first non-fitting value for an ancestor or right-side call.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not sort preorder; sorting would destroy the original shape. Do not repeatedly split arrays if you can avoid it. Be clear about duplicates; the strict version assumes unique values or a consistent duplicate side.

### Complexity
Each preorder value is consumed once, so time is O(N). Recursion stack is O(H), O(N) in the worst skewed case.

check-if-a-tree-is-a-bst-or-not.name: Range Constraint (Min/Max)
check-if-a-tree-is-a-bst-or-not.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Validate BST asks whether a binary tree satisfies the Binary Search Tree rule globally: every node in the left subtree must be smaller than the node, and every node in the right subtree must be larger.

### Core intuition
Local child checks are not enough. A node deep inside the left subtree must still be less than the original root. Carry a valid range down the recursion. The root starts with (-infinity, infinity). For a left child, the upper bound becomes the parent value. For a right child, the lower bound becomes the parent value.

### State meaning
low and high are the exclusive bounds that the current node's value must fit inside. Each recursive step tightens one side of the range.

### Invariant
If validate(node, low, high) returns true, every value in that subtree lies strictly inside the bounds and the subtree itself is a valid BST.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not only check node.left < node < node.right. That misses violations such as a value 6 inside the left subtree of root 5. Also handle duplicate rules carefully; LeetCode's usual BST definition is strict.

### Complexity
Every node is visited once, so time is O(N). Recursion stack is O(H).

lca-in-bst.name: BST Property Split
lca-in-bst.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
LCA in BST asks for the lowest node that has two given nodes p and q as descendants. Because the tree is a BST, values tell us which direction both targets lie.

### Core intuition
At the current node, compare p and q with current.val. If both are smaller, the LCA must be in the left subtree. If both are larger, it must be in the right subtree. If the targets split across current, or one target equals current, then current is the lowest common ancestor.

### State meaning
current is the root of the still-possible answer region. pVal and qVal are the target values. Each move keeps both targets inside the chosen subtree until a split is found.

### Invariant
The true LCA is always inside the subtree rooted at current. Moving left or right is valid only when both targets are on that same side.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not use the plain binary-tree LCA search when the BST property gives a faster path. Do not require both targets to be strictly on opposite sides; if current equals one target, current is the LCA. Ensure p and q values are ordered or compare both explicitly.

### Complexity
The algorithm follows one root-to-leaf path, so time is O(H). Iterative space is O(1).

inorder-predecessor-successor-in-bst.name: Better
inorder-predecessor-successor-in-bst.description: Reduce repeated work in Predecessor & Successor with the intermediate structure or cached state used by the standard better approach. It preserves the brute-force reasoning while improving one major time or space bottleneck.

inorder-predecessor-successor-in-bst.name: Successive Approximation
inorder-predecessor-successor-in-bst.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Predecessor and Successor in a BST asks for the closest values around a key in sorted order. The predecessor is the greatest value strictly smaller than the key. The successor is the smallest value strictly greater than the key.

### Core intuition
A BST already behaves like a sorted structure. For successor, whenever current.val is greater than the key, current is a possible answer, but there may be a smaller valid answer on the left, so move left. If current.val is less than or equal to the key, move right. Predecessor is the mirror: whenever current.val is less than the key, save it and move right to look for a larger smaller value.

### State meaning
successor and predecessor store the best candidates seen so far. current walks down one BST path, using comparisons to discard impossible subtrees.

### Invariant
The saved successor is always greater than the key and is the smallest such value seen so far. The saved predecessor is always smaller than the key and is the largest such value seen so far.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not do a full inorder traversal unless simplicity matters more than efficiency. Do not return the key itself as predecessor or successor under the strict definition. If duplicates are allowed, confirm the platform's duplicate rule.

### Complexity
Each search follows one root-to-leaf path, so time is O(H). Iterative space is O(1).

graph-and-types.name: Fundamental Taxonomy
graph-and-types.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact topic is teaching
Graph Anatomy and Types introduces graphs as a collection of vertices and edges. Vertices are entities. Edges are relationships. Unlike trees, graphs can have cycles, disconnected parts, and many different connection rules.

### Core intuition
A graph is the natural model whenever one thing can connect to many other things without a strict parent-child hierarchy. Friends in a social network, roads between cities, web links between pages, and prerequisites between courses are all graph-shaped.

### Important classifications
A graph is directed when edges have arrows and undirected when connections work both ways. It is weighted when edges carry costs. It is cyclic if a path can return to an earlier vertex. It is connected if every vertex can reach every other vertex in the relevant sense.

### State meaning
V is the set of vertices, and E is the set of edges. Algorithms usually care about how quickly they can visit vertices and inspect edges.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not assume every graph is connected. Do not assume an edge works both directions unless the graph is undirected. Do not assume all edges have equal cost when weights exist.

### Complexity
This entry is conceptual. Most graph traversals are measured in O(V + E), meaning each vertex and each stored edge is considered once.

graph-representation-in-c-java.name: Adjacency List (Standard)
graph-representation-in-c-java.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact topic is teaching
Graph Representation explains how to store graph connections in code. The two classic choices are adjacency matrix and adjacency list.

### Core intuition
An adjacency matrix is a V by V table where matrix[u][v] says whether an edge exists. It answers edge-existence questions quickly but uses a lot of space. An adjacency list stores only real neighbors: adj[u] contains the vertices connected to u. This is usually better for sparse graphs.

### When to choose what
Use a matrix when the graph is dense or when O(1) edge lookup is the main need. Use a list when you will traverse neighbors, run BFS/DFS, or store a graph with far fewer edges than V squared.

### State meaning
For an adjacency list, each vertex owns a list of outgoing neighbors. In an undirected graph, edge u-v is stored twice: v in adj[u] and u in adj[v].

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not forget the second insertion for undirected graphs. Do not use a matrix for very large sparse graphs unless memory is acceptable. Be consistent with 0-based or 1-based indexing.

### Complexity
Adjacency list space is O(V + E), or O(V + 2E) for undirected storage. Matrix space is O(V squared). Traversing a list graph is O(V + E).

connected-components.name: Visited Array Barrier
connected-components.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Connected Components asks how to handle a graph that may be split into disconnected groups. A single BFS or DFS from one node visits only the group reachable from that node, not necessarily the whole graph.

### Core intuition
Keep a visited array. Loop over every vertex. When you find an unvisited vertex, that vertex starts a new component. Launch BFS or DFS from it to mark its entire component, then continue the outer loop.

### State meaning
visited tells whether a vertex already belongs to a discovered component. components counts how many separate launches were needed.

### Invariant
After each traversal launch finishes, every vertex in that connected component is marked visited. Any future unvisited vertex must belong to a different component.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not start traversal only from node 1 and assume the whole graph is covered. Do not increment the component count for every node; increment only when starting from an unvisited node. Handle isolated vertices as one-node components.

### Complexity
Across all launches, every vertex and edge is processed once, so time is O(V + E). Space is O(V) for visited plus traversal queue or stack.

bfs.name: Queue-Based Layering
bfs.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Breadth-First Search asks us to visit graph nodes level by level from a starting node. It explores all neighbors at distance 1 before nodes at distance 2, and so on.

### Core intuition
Use a queue. Push the start node and mark it visited immediately. Then repeatedly pop the front node, record it, and push each unvisited neighbor. The queue's first-in-first-out behavior preserves layer order.

### State meaning
queue stores the frontier waiting to be processed. visited prevents the same node from entering the queue multiple times. result records traversal order.

### Invariant
When a node leaves the queue, BFS has reached it by the shortest number of edges from the start in an unweighted graph.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not mark nodes only when popping if duplicates can be enqueued first; mark when pushing. Do not use BFS alone from one start if the graph may be disconnected and you need all nodes. Neighbor order affects traversal output, so follow the adjacency list order.

### Complexity
BFS visits each vertex once and scans each edge from adjacency lists, so time is O(V + E), often written O(V + 2E) for undirected graphs. Space is O(V).

dfs.name: Recursive Depth Dive
dfs.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Depth-First Search asks us to explore a graph by going as deep as possible along one path before backing up and trying the next path.

### Core intuition
Use recursion or an explicit stack. Mark the current node visited, record it, then recursively visit each unvisited neighbor. When a node has no unvisited neighbors left, the call returns to its parent and exploration continues from there.

### State meaning
visited prevents cycles from causing infinite recursion. The recursion stack is the current path being explored. result records the discovery order.

### Invariant
Once DFS enters a node, it fully explores every reachable unvisited node through that node before returning to the caller.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not omit visited in graphs with cycles. Do not expect DFS order to be unique; it depends on adjacency order. For very deep graphs, recursive DFS can hit call-stack limits, so an iterative stack may be safer.

### Complexity
DFS visits each vertex once and scans all adjacency entries, so time is O(V + E). Space is O(V) for visited and recursion stack in the worst case.

number-of-provinces.name: Traversal Counting
number-of-provinces.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Number of Provinces gives an adjacency matrix of cities and asks how many disconnected groups of directly or indirectly connected cities exist. Each group is one province.

### Core intuition
This is connected components on a matrix. Iterate over each city. If it has not been visited, start DFS or BFS from it and mark every city connected to it through the matrix. Each fresh traversal means one new province.

### State meaning
visited marks cities already assigned to a province. provinces counts how many traversal launches were needed. The matrix row for city u tells which cities v are directly connected to u.

### Invariant
After DFS from city i finishes, every city in i's province is visited. Any later unvisited city must begin a different province.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not count direct edges only; connections can be indirect. Do not forget matrix diagonal values are usually 1 because every city is connected to itself. Since the input is a matrix, scanning neighbors costs O(V) per city.

### Complexity
The matrix version scans V rows of V entries, so time is O(V squared). Space is O(V) for visited and recursion or queue.

rotting-oranges.name: Multi-Source BFS
rotting-oranges.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Rotting Oranges asks for the minimum minutes needed until every fresh orange becomes rotten. Rotten oranges spread rot to their four-directional fresh neighbors each minute. If some fresh orange can never be reached, return -1.

### Core intuition
Use multi-source BFS. Put every initially rotten orange into the queue at time 0. The BFS expands all rotten fronts together, so the first time a fresh orange is reached is the earliest minute it can rot.

### State meaning
The queue stores orange coordinates and time. fresh counts how many fresh oranges remain. The grid itself can be updated from 1 to 2 to mark newly rotten oranges.

### Invariant
When a cell is popped with time t, it became rotten at the earliest possible minute t. Every fresh neighbor enqueued from it will rot at t + 1.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not start BFS from only one rotten orange. Do not count empty cells as oranges. Mark a fresh orange rotten as soon as it is enqueued, or it may be queued multiple times.

### Complexity
Every grid cell is inspected a constant number of times, so time is O(R times C). The queue can store O(R times C) cells.

01-matrix.name: Multi-Source BFS
01-matrix.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
This catalog entry asks for the distance from each cell in a binary matrix to the nearest cell containing 1. The local implementation treats every 1 as a distance-0 source and fills distances for all other cells.

### Core intuition
Start BFS from all 1-cells simultaneously. Set their distance to 0. Every time BFS moves one step to an unvisited neighbor, assign distance current + 1. Because BFS expands in layers, the first assigned distance is the nearest possible distance to any 1.

### State meaning
dist stores the answer for each cell, initialized to -1 for unknown cells. The queue contains cells whose neighbors still need to be relaxed.

### Invariant
When a cell receives a distance, it is the shortest distance to the nearest source cell containing 1. No later path can improve it because BFS reaches cells in increasing distance order.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not run a separate BFS from every 0-cell; that is much slower. Do not forget to enqueue all source 1-cells before the BFS starts. Also note the variant: many platforms ask distance to nearest 0, but this catalog entry asks nearest 1.

### Complexity
Every cell is enqueued at most once, so time is O(R times C). The distance matrix and queue use O(R times C) space.

bipartite-graph.name: 2-Coloring BFS
bipartite-graph.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Bipartite Graph asks whether vertices can be split into two groups so that every edge connects nodes from different groups. Equivalently, the graph must be two-colorable without any edge joining equal colors.

### Core intuition
Run BFS or DFS and assign colors 0 and 1. Start an uncolored component with color 0. Every uncolored neighbor gets the opposite color. If a neighbor is already colored with the same color as the current node, the graph is not bipartite.

### State meaning
color[v] is -1 if v is unvisited, otherwise 0 or 1. The queue or recursion explores each component while enforcing opposite colors across edges.

### Invariant
For every processed edge so far, its endpoints have different colors. A same-color edge is a direct contradiction.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not check only one connected component; disconnected graphs need an outer loop. Do not confuse bipartite with acyclic: even cycles are allowed, odd cycles are not. Mark colors when pushing neighbors so they are not colored inconsistently later.

### Complexity
Every vertex and edge is processed once, so time is O(V + E). The color array and queue use O(V) space.

topological-sort.name: DFS with Stack
topological-sort.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Topological Sort asks for a linear ordering of vertices in a directed acyclic graph so that for every directed edge u -> v, u appears before v. It only makes sense when there is no directed cycle.

### Core intuition
