With DFS, a node should be placed into the final ordering only after all nodes reachable from it have been fully handled. So we run DFS, and when a node's recursive calls finish, we push that node onto a stack. Reversing that finishing stack gives a valid topological order.

### State meaning
visited marks nodes already explored. The recursion stack represents the path currently being expanded. The topo stack stores nodes in finish-time order.

### Invariant
When a node is pushed to the stack, every dependency reachable from it has already been pushed. Therefore reversing the stack places the node before the nodes it points to.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not use topological sort on an undirected graph or on a directed graph with a cycle. Do not push a node before visiting its neighbors in the DFS version; that would not preserve dependency order.

### Complexity
DFS visits every vertex and edge once, so time is O(V + E). Space is O(V) for visited, recursion, and stack.

kahn-s-algorithm.name: In-degree Reduction
kahn-s-algorithm.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Kahn's Algorithm asks for a BFS-style topological sort of a directed acyclic graph using in-degrees. In-degree means how many incoming edges, or prerequisites, a node still has.

### Core intuition
Any node with in-degree 0 has no unfinished prerequisite, so it can be processed now. Put all such nodes in a queue. When a node is processed, pretend it is removed from the graph and decrement the in-degree of its neighbors. Any neighbor whose in-degree becomes 0 is now ready.

### State meaning
indegree[v] counts how many incoming edges to v remain unprocessed. The queue stores nodes currently free of prerequisites. The result records the topological order.

### Invariant
Every node in the queue has all prerequisites satisfied. Every edge from a popped node is removed exactly once by decrementing its neighbor's in-degree.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not enqueue a node before its in-degree becomes 0. Do not forget to initialize the queue with all zero in-degree nodes, not just one. If the final result has fewer than V nodes, the graph had a cycle.

### Complexity
Computing in-degrees plus processing all edges takes O(V + E). Space is O(V) for queue, in-degree, and result.

undirected-cycle-detection.name: DFS with Parent Tracking
undirected-cycle-detection.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Cycle Detection in an Undirected Graph asks whether there is any path that can leave a node and later return to it without simply walking back across the same edge.

### Core intuition
Use DFS or BFS with parent tracking. In an undirected graph, when you stand at node u, its parent p is expected to appear as a visited neighbor because the edge works both ways. But if you find another visited neighbor that is not the parent, you have found a back connection, which means a cycle.

### State meaning
visited marks nodes already reached. parent records the node from which the current node was first discovered.

### Invariant
During DFS, a visited neighbor different from parent means there are two different paths to that neighbor, which creates a cycle in an undirected graph.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not treat the parent edge as a cycle. Do not check only the component containing node 0; disconnected graphs need an outer loop. Mark nodes visited before exploring their neighbors.

### Complexity
Every vertex and undirected edge adjacency is inspected once, so time is O(V + E). Space is O(V) for visited and recursion or queue.

cycle-detection-in-directed-graph.name: DFS Path Tracking
cycle-detection-in-directed-graph.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Cycle Detection in a Directed Graph asks whether following arrow directions can lead from a node back to a node already on the same active path.

### Core intuition
Parent tracking is not enough for directed graphs. Instead, keep two arrays: visited for nodes seen at least once, and pathVisited for nodes currently in the recursion chain. If DFS reaches a neighbor that is already pathVisited, the graph has a directed cycle.

### State meaning
visited[u] means u has been explored or is being explored. pathVisited[u] means u is on the current DFS path and has not been fully exited yet.

### Invariant
A directed cycle exists exactly when an edge points to a node that is still active on the current recursion path.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not use the undirected parent rule here. Do not forget to clear pathVisited[u] when backtracking out of u. Also inspect every component, because a cycle may be unreachable from the first node.

### Complexity
DFS scans every vertex and directed edge once, so time is O(V + E). Space is O(V) for arrays and recursion stack.

cycle-detection-in-directed-graph.name: Kahn's (BFS) Approach
cycle-detection-in-directed-graph.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Cycle Detection in a Directed Graph asks whether following arrow directions can lead from a node back to a node already on the same active path.

### Core intuition
Parent tracking is not enough for directed graphs. Instead, keep two arrays: visited for nodes seen at least once, and pathVisited for nodes currently in the recursion chain. If DFS reaches a neighbor that is already pathVisited, the graph has a directed cycle.

### State meaning
visited[u] means u has been explored or is being explored. pathVisited[u] means u is on the current DFS path and has not been fully exited yet.

### Invariant
A directed cycle exists exactly when an edge points to a node that is still active on the current recursion path.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not use the undirected parent rule here. Do not forget to clear pathVisited[u] when backtracking out of u. Also inspect every component, because a cycle may be unreachable from the first node.

### Complexity
DFS scans every vertex and directed edge once, so time is O(V + E). Space is O(V) for arrays and recursion stack.

course-schedule-i.name: Cycle Detection (Kahn's BFS)
course-schedule-i.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Course Schedule I asks whether all courses can be completed given prerequisite pairs. This is a directed dependency graph problem: an edge prerequisite -> course means the prerequisite must come first.

### Core intuition
All courses can be finished if and only if the dependency graph has no cycle. Kahn's algorithm tests this by repeatedly taking courses with zero remaining prerequisites. If every course can be removed, scheduling is possible. If some courses remain stuck, they are part of a circular dependency.

### State meaning
indegree[course] is the number of prerequisites not yet satisfied. The queue stores courses currently available to take. count tracks how many courses have been processed.

### Invariant
Every course popped from the queue is takeable at that moment because all its prerequisites have already been processed.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not reverse the edge direction accidentally. For pair [course, prereq], the edge is prereq -> course. Do not return true just because one course is available; all courses must be processed.

### Complexity
Building the graph and running Kahn's algorithm takes O(V + E). Space is O(V + E).

course-schedule-ii.name: Topological Sort (Kahn's)
course-schedule-ii.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Course Schedule II asks for an actual valid order to take all courses. If no such order exists because of a cycle, return an empty array.

### Core intuition
Use Kahn's topological sort. Courses with in-degree 0 can be taken now, so append them to the answer. Each time a course is taken, reduce the in-degree of courses that depend on it. If all courses enter the answer, that answer is a valid schedule.

### State meaning
The queue stores currently takeable courses. topo stores the schedule being built. indegree stores remaining prerequisite counts.

### Invariant
Every course appended to topo has all prerequisites already placed earlier in topo.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not return a partial order if a cycle blocks some courses. Check topo.length == numCourses. Also remember multiple valid orders may exist, depending on queue order.

### Complexity
Graph construction and topological sorting take O(V + E). Space is O(V + E).

find-eventual-safe-states.name: Brute Force
find-eventual-safe-states.description: Build a correctness baseline for Find Eventual Safe States by directly enumerating the candidate states allowed by the problem. This version prioritizes transparent control flow and exhaustive coverage over asymptotic efficiency.

find-eventual-safe-states.name: Cycle Detection (Reverse Logic)
find-eventual-safe-states.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Find Eventual Safe States asks for nodes where every possible path eventually ends at a terminal node. A node is unsafe if it can reach a directed cycle, because a path could loop forever.

### Core intuition
One clean method reverses the graph. Original terminal nodes have out-degree 0, so they are definitely safe. In the reversed graph, remove safe nodes backward: whenever all outgoing edges of a predecessor lead to safe nodes, that predecessor also becomes safe.

### State meaning
The indegree variable in the catalog's reversed approach is really the original out-degree count: how many outgoing edges from a node are not yet proven safe. The queue starts with terminal nodes.

### Invariant
A node enters the safe queue only when all original paths leaving it go to already-safe nodes.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not mark a node safe just because one path reaches a terminal node; every path must be safe. Do not forget to return safe nodes sorted if the problem expects ascending order.

### Complexity
Reversing edges and processing the queue takes O(V + E). Space is O(V + E).

alien-dictionary.name: Graph Construction + Topo Sort
alien-dictionary.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Alien Dictionary gives words already sorted according to an unknown alphabet and asks us to infer a valid character order. The sorted word list hides ordering constraints between characters.

### Core intuition
Compare adjacent words. The first position where they differ is the only position that reveals a direct ordering rule. If word1 has character a and word2 has character b at that first difference, then a must come before b. Build a directed graph of such rules, then topologically sort the characters.

### State meaning
Each character is a graph node. An edge a -> b means a must appear before b in the alien alphabet. In-degree tracks unresolved earlier-character requirements.

### Invariant
Every edge added comes from the first meaningful difference between two adjacent sorted words. A topological order that respects all edges is a valid alien alphabet.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not compare later differing characters after the first difference; they do not imply order. Handle invalid prefix cases such as "abcd" before "ab". Include characters even if they have no edges.

### Complexity
Comparing adjacent words costs O(total characters compared). Topological sorting costs O(K + E), where K is alphabet size.

shortest-path-in-directed-acyclic-graph.name: Topological Sort + Edge Relaxation
shortest-path-in-directed-acyclic-graph.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Shortest Path in a DAG asks for shortest distances from a source in a directed acyclic graph with weighted edges. Because the graph has no cycles, topological order lets us relax edges once in a safe order.

### Core intuition
First compute a topological order. In that order, every predecessor of a node appears before the node. So when we process a node, its shortest distance is already final. Relax all outgoing edges exactly once: if dist[u] + weight is better than dist[v], update dist[v].

### State meaning
dist[v] is the best known distance from the source to v. The topological order controls when each node's outgoing edges are relaxed.

### Invariant
When a node is processed in topological order, all possible incoming paths from earlier nodes have already had their chance to update it.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not use plain BFS when weights are not all 1. Do not relax outgoing edges from unreachable nodes whose distance is infinity. This method relies on the graph being acyclic.

### Complexity
Topological sort plus one pass over edges takes O(V + E). Space is O(V + E) for adjacency, stack/order, and distances.

shortest-path-in-undirected-graph-with-u.name: Standard BFS
shortest-path-in-undirected-graph-with-u.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Shortest Path in an Undirected Graph with Unit Weights asks for the minimum number of edges from a source to every other node when every edge has equal cost 1.

### Core intuition
Use BFS. In an unweighted graph, BFS explores all nodes at distance 1, then distance 2, then distance 3. The first time a node is reached, that distance is guaranteed to be the shortest possible.

### State meaning
dist[v] stores the shortest number of edges from source to v. The queue stores nodes whose neighbors may receive a distance next.

### Invariant
When a node is popped from the queue, its dist value is final. Every unvisited neighbor receives dist[current] + 1.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not use DFS for shortest path in an unweighted graph; it may find a longer path first. Do not keep infinity for unreachable nodes if the platform expects -1. Mark/update distance when enqueuing so nodes are not pushed repeatedly.

### Complexity
BFS scans each vertex and undirected adjacency entry, so time is O(V + E). Space is O(V) for distance and queue, plus adjacency storage.

path-with-minimum-effort.name: Modified Dijkstra
path-with-minimum-effort.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Path With Minimum Effort asks for a path from the top-left cell to the bottom-right cell where the path cost is the largest height difference between any two adjacent cells on that path. We want to minimize that maximum jump.

### Core intuition
This is Dijkstra with a different relaxation rule. Instead of adding edge weights, the cost of entering a neighbor is max(currentEffort, absolute height difference). The best path to a cell is the one that keeps the worst jump so far as small as possible.

### State meaning
efforts[r][c] is the smallest possible maximum jump needed to reach that cell. The heap pops the cell with the currently lowest effort.

### Invariant
When a cell is popped from the heap with its best effort, no future path can reach it with a smaller maximum edge difference.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not sum all height differences; the problem cares only about the largest single step. Do not mark a cell final before it is popped with the best effort. Return immediately when the destination is popped.

### Complexity
There are R times C cells and each has up to 4 edges. Heap Dijkstra costs O(RC log(RC)) time and O(RC) space.

cheapest-flights-within-k-stops.name: Modified BFS / Dijkstra by Stops
cheapest-flights-within-k-stops.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Cheapest Flights Within K Stops asks for the lowest price from source to destination while using at most K intermediate stops. A route with too many stops is invalid even if it is cheap.

### Core intuition
The stop constraint changes normal shortest path logic. Use a queue or level-based relaxation where each state carries current city, total price, and stops or edges used. Only expand a state if it still respects the stop limit. Update a city price when this route is cheaper within the allowed number of stops.

### State meaning
The queue stores partial routes. price[city] stores the cheapest known cost discovered so far under the current exploration. The stop counter prevents paths from growing beyond the allowed layers.

### Invariant
A route is expanded only while it can still produce a valid answer under the K-stop limit.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not run plain Dijkstra by price alone without accounting for stops. A slightly more expensive route with fewer stops can still be necessary. Be precise: K stops means at most K intermediate cities, which is at most K + 1 flight edges.

### Complexity
A level-based relaxation processes edges across allowed stop layers. The common bound is O(K * E) time and O(V + E) space.

network-delay-time.name: Standard Dijkstra
network-delay-time.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Network Delay Time asks how long it takes for a signal sent from node k to reach every node in a directed weighted network. Each edge weight is travel time.

### Core intuition
Run Dijkstra from k. The shortest distance to each node is the earliest time that node can receive the signal. The whole network is done only when the slowest reachable node receives it, so the answer is the maximum shortest distance. If any node stays unreachable, return -1.

### State meaning
dist[v] is the earliest known signal arrival time at node v. The heap expands the next node with smallest arrival time.

### Invariant
When a node is popped with its best distance, its signal arrival time is finalized.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not return the distance to the last popped node blindly if stale heap entries are present. Do not treat edges as undirected; the signal follows the given direction. Check all nodes from 1 to n, not index 0 if the graph is 1-indexed.

### Complexity
Dijkstra with a heap costs O(E log V) time and O(V + E) space.

number-of-ways-to-arrive-at-destination.name: Dijkstra with Combinatorics
number-of-ways-to-arrive-at-destination.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Number of Ways to Arrive at Destination asks how many different shortest-time paths exist from node 0 to node n - 1 in a weighted undirected graph. The result is usually returned modulo 1e9 + 7.

### Core intuition
Run Dijkstra, but track two arrays. dist[v] is the shortest known distance to v. ways[v] is how many shortest paths achieve dist[v]. When a strictly shorter route to v is found, replace both dist[v] and ways[v]. When an equally short route is found, add ways[u] into ways[v].

### State meaning
dist controls shortest-path quality. ways counts how many routes achieve that quality. The heap still orders states by current distance.

### Invariant
Whenever dist[v] is finalized, ways[v] counts all shortest paths to v discovered through finalized predecessors.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not count longer paths. Do not reset ways on equal distance; add them. Do reset ways when a strictly shorter distance appears. Apply modulo during additions.

### Complexity
Dijkstra processes edges with heap operations, so time is O(E log V). Space is O(V + E).

prim-s-algorithm.name: Greedy with Priority Queue
prim-s-algorithm.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Prim's Algorithm asks for the total weight of a Minimum Spanning Tree in a connected undirected weighted graph. A spanning tree connects all vertices with no cycles, and minimum means the total edge weight is as small as possible.

### Core intuition
Grow the tree from any starting node. At every step, choose the cheapest edge that connects the already-built tree to a new outside vertex. A min-heap keeps those candidate edges available.

### State meaning
visited[v] tells whether v is already inside the MST. The heap stores boundary edges leading from the current MST to nearby vertices. mstSum accumulates the weights of chosen edges.

### Invariant
Each accepted heap edge is the cheapest safe edge crossing from the current tree to an unvisited vertex, so it can be part of some MST.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not add an edge if its node is already visited; that would create a cycle. Do not use Prim on a disconnected graph unless you intentionally want a minimum spanning forest. Store undirected edges both ways.

### Complexity
With adjacency lists and a heap, Prim runs in O(E log E), commonly O(E log V). Space is O(V + E).

number-of-operations-to-make-network-con.name: DSU - Component Counting
number-of-operations-to-make-network-con.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Network Connectivity asks for the minimum number of cable moves needed to connect all computers. A cable can be removed from a redundant connection and used to connect two separate components.

### Core intuition
First, a connected network of n computers needs at least n - 1 cables. If fewer exist, it is impossible. Otherwise, use DSU to count connected components. To connect C components together, we need C - 1 cable moves. The redundant cables inside components provide the spare cables.

### State meaning
DSU groups computers already connected by existing cables. components starts at n and decreases whenever a union joins two different groups.

### Invariant
After processing all cables, DSU's component count equals the number of disconnected computer groups. With enough total cables, components - 1 operations are sufficient and necessary.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not count only redundant edges without first checking total cables >= n - 1. Do not forget that an edge inside the same DSU component is spare, not useful for reducing components directly.

### Complexity
DSU operations are effectively near constant, so time is O(n + E). Space is O(n).

most-stones-removed-with-same-row-or-col.name: DSU - Coordinate Mapping
most-stones-removed-with-same-row-or-col.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Most Stones Removed asks for the maximum stones we can remove where a stone is removable if another remaining stone shares its row or column. Stones connected by row or column form components.

### Core intuition
Inside any connected component of k stones, we can remove k - 1 stones and leave one anchor stone. Therefore the answer is total stones minus number of connected components. DSU can model each stone as connecting its row node to its column node.

### State meaning
A row index and a column index become DSU nodes. A stone at (r, c) unions row r with column c plus an offset. Every connected row-column group corresponds to one stone component.

### Invariant
All stones linked through shared rows or columns end up in the same DSU component, and each component must leave at least one stone behind.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not union row 1 with column 1 directly without an offset or separate namespace. Do not count unused DSU coordinates as components. Count only roots among coordinates that appear in stones.

### Complexity
For N stones, DSU work is near O(N). Space is O(number of used rows and columns).

accounts-merge.name: DSU - Email Mapping
accounts-merge.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Accounts Merge asks us to combine account rows that belong to the same person. Two accounts are the same person if they share at least one email. The final merged account should contain the name and sorted unique emails.

### Core intuition
Treat each account index as a DSU node. As we scan emails, remember which account first owned each email. If the same email appears in another account, union those two account indices. After all unions, gather emails by their root account and sort each group.

### State meaning
emailToAccount maps an email to the first account index that saw it. DSU links account indices that share emails. merged[root] collects all emails belonging to that root group.

### Invariant
After processing all accounts, any accounts connected by a chain of shared emails have the same DSU root.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not merge by name alone; two different people can have the same name. Do not forget transitive merging: if A shares with B and B shares with C, all three merge. Sort emails in the final output.

### Complexity
If there are M total emails, DSU processing is near O(M). Sorting grouped emails costs O(M log M) overall. Space is O(M + number of accounts).

number-of-islands-ii-online-queries.name: Disjoint Set Union (DSU)
number-of-islands-ii-online-queries.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Number of Islands II asks us to process land-addition queries on a water grid and report the island count after each addition. Because land appears over time, recomputing islands from scratch after every query would be too slow.

### Core intuition
Use DSU for dynamic connectivity. Each cell can be converted to an id: row * cols + col. When a new land cell appears, it initially creates one new island. Then check its four neighbors. For each neighboring land cell in a different DSU component, union them and reduce the island count by one.

### State meaning
visited or land marks which cells have already been added. DSU stores connected land components. count is the current number of islands after each query.

### Invariant
After each query is processed, count equals the number of DSU components among currently active land cells.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not increase the island count for a duplicate land query. Do not union with water cells. Convert 2D coordinates to ids consistently. Reduce count only when union actually merges two different components.

### Complexity
For K queries, each checks four neighbors and performs near-constant DSU operations, so time is O(K alpha(RC)). Space is O(RC).

swim-in-rising-water.name: Modified Dijkstra
swim-in-rising-water.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Swim in Rising Water asks for the earliest time when we can travel from the top-left cell to the bottom-right cell. A cell with height h can be entered only when water time is at least h.

### Core intuition
The cost of a path is the maximum cell height encountered on that path. We want to minimize that maximum. This is a Dijkstra-style problem where moving into a neighbor has new time max(currentTime, neighborHeight). The first time the destination is popped from the min-heap, that is the minimum possible time.

### State meaning
The heap stores states by the smallest known required water time. visited prevents processing the same cell again after its best time is finalized.

### Invariant
When a cell is popped from the heap, we have found the smallest water level that can reach it.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not add heights like normal path costs. Do not use plain BFS, because a shorter route in steps may require a much higher water level. Mark cells when pushed or popped consistently to avoid duplicate work.

### Complexity
There are N squared cells and each has up to four moves. Heap-based Dijkstra costs O(N squared log N) time and O(N squared) space.

frog-jump.name: Better
frog-jump.description: Reduce repeated work in Frog Jump with the intermediate structure or cached state used by the standard better approach. It preserves the brute-force reasoning while improving one major time or space bottleneck.
frog-jump.spaceComplexity: O(n) for the DP array + O(n) recursion stack, leading to O(n)

frog-jump.name: DP with Space Optimization
frog-jump.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Frog Jump asks for the minimum energy needed for a frog to reach the last stair. From stair i, the frog can come from i - 1 or i - 2. Jump cost is the absolute height difference between the two stairs.

### Core intuition
Let dp[i] be the minimum energy needed to stand on stair i. To reach i, the previous jump came from i - 1 or i - 2. So dp[i] is the cheaper of those two choices, including the jump's height difference.

### State meaning
prev stores dp[i - 1], and prev2 stores dp[i - 2]. Because each state only needs the last two states, the DP array can be compressed to O(1) space.

### Invariant
After processing stair i, prev is the minimum possible energy to reach stair i.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not greedily pick the smaller immediate jump; a slightly larger jump now may reduce total future cost. Handle n = 1 by returning 0. Use absolute height differences.

### Complexity
The algorithm scans the heights once, so time is O(N). Space is O(1) with two variables.

frog-jump-with-k-distances.name: Better
frog-jump-with-k-distances.description: Reduce repeated work in Frog Jump with K Steps with the intermediate structure or cached state used by the standard better approach. It preserves the brute-force reasoning while improving one major time or space bottleneck.

frog-jump-with-k-distances.name: Iterative DP
frog-jump-with-k-distances.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Frog Jump with K Steps generalizes Frog Jump. The frog may jump from any of the previous K stairs to the current stair, paying the absolute height difference.

### Core intuition
Let dp[i] be the minimum energy to reach stair i. For every possible jump length j from 1 to K, if i - j is valid, try dp[i - j] + abs(height[i] - height[i - j]). The minimum over those choices becomes dp[i].

### State meaning
dp[i] stores the best cost to reach stair i. The inner loop tests every allowed previous landing point.

### Invariant
After computing dp[i], every legal last jump into stair i has been considered.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not check only i - 1 and i - 2 in this version. Do not let i - j go negative. If K is large, the inner loop still stops at the start of the array.

### Complexity
There are N stairs and up to K previous jumps per stair, so time is O(N * K). Space is O(N) for the DP array.

maximum-sum-of-non-adjacent-elements.name: DP with Space Optimization
maximum-sum-of-non-adjacent-elements.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Maximum Sum of Non-adjacent Elements asks for the largest sum we can collect from an array without choosing two neighboring elements.

### Core intuition
At each index, we have two choices. Pick the current value, which means the previous index cannot be picked, so we add dp[i - 2]. Or skip the current value, which keeps dp[i - 1]. The better of those two becomes dp[i].

### State meaning
prev stores the best answer up to the previous index. prev2 stores the best answer up to two indices back. curr becomes the best answer up to the current index.

### Invariant
After processing index i, prev is the maximum valid non-adjacent sum using elements from 0 through i.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not greedily pick every locally large number; adjacency constraints can make that invalid. Handle arrays with one element. If negative values are allowed, clarify whether choosing nothing is permitted.

### Complexity
One pass gives O(N) time. Space is O(1) after compression.

house-robber.name: DP with Sub-arrays
house-robber.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
House Robber II asks for the maximum money we can rob from houses arranged in a circle. Adjacent houses cannot both be robbed, and in a circle the first and last houses are also adjacent.

### Core intuition
Break the circle into two linear problems. Either we exclude the last house and consider houses 0 through n - 2, or we exclude the first house and consider houses 1 through n - 1. Each linear problem is the maximum sum of non-adjacent elements. Take the maximum of the two results.

### State meaning
The helper solveLinear tracks the best robbery total so far using the previous two DP states. The outer function handles the circular conflict.

### Invariant
In each linear run, no two selected houses are adjacent. Considering both excluded-end cases covers every valid circular robbery plan.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not run the plain House Robber I logic on the whole circular array; it may choose both first and last. Handle n = 1 separately. Do not physically need to copy arrays if index ranges are used.

### Complexity
Two linear passes take O(N) time. Space is O(1).

ninja-s-training.name: Better
ninja-s-training.description: Reduce repeated work in Ninja's Training with the intermediate structure or cached state used by the standard better approach. It preserves the brute-force reasoning while improving one major time or space bottleneck.

ninja-s-training.name: 2D DP with Space Optimization
ninja-s-training.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Ninja's Training asks for the maximum merit points over N days. Each day has three possible tasks, and the ninja cannot do the same task on two consecutive days.

### Core intuition
Use DP where the state remembers what task was done yesterday, or more generally which task is forbidden today. For each day and each forbidden last task, try all allowed tasks today and add today's points to the best score from the previous day.

### State meaning
prev[last] stores the best total up to the previous day when task last is not allowed today. last = 3 means no task is forbidden, used for the final answer.

### Invariant
After each day, prev[last] correctly represents the best score so far under the condition that today's chosen task is not last.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not greedily choose the highest points each day; it may block a better next day. Do not allow the same task on consecutive days. Keep the meaning of last consistent: it refers to the task forbidden on the current choice.

### Complexity
There are N days, 4 last states, and 3 task choices, so time is O(N). Space is O(1) with the four-state array.

unique-paths-ii.name: DP with Space Optimization
unique-paths-ii.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Unique Paths II asks how many ways a robot can move from top-left to bottom-right using only right and down moves, while avoiding obstacle cells marked as 1.

### Core intuition
The normal unique paths recurrence still works, except obstacle cells contribute 0 ways. For a free cell, ways = ways from above + ways from left. If the start or destination is blocked, the answer becomes 0 naturally.

### State meaning
prev[j] stores ways to reach column j from the previous row. curr[j] stores ways for the current row. Obstacles reset curr[j] to 0.

### Invariant
After processing cell (i, j), curr[j] is the exact number of valid obstacle-avoiding paths to that cell.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not let paths pass through obstacle cells. Do not initialize the first row with all 1s if an obstacle appears; everything after that obstacle may become unreachable. Handle the starting obstacle.

### Complexity
Every grid cell is processed once, so time is O(M * N). Space can be O(N) using one row.

minimum-falling-path-sum.name: DP with Space Optimization
minimum-falling-path-sum.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Minimum Falling Path Sum asks for the smallest sum of a path starting anywhere in the first row and ending anywhere in the last row. From a cell, the path may move down-left, down, or down-right.

### Core intuition
For each cell, the cheapest way to land there comes from one of three cells in the previous row: above-left, above, or above-right. Add the current cell value to the minimum of those valid previous values. The answer is the minimum value in the final row.

### State meaning
prev[j] stores the minimum falling-path sum ending at column j in the previous row. curr[j] computes the same for the current row.

### Invariant
After processing row i, prev[j] is the minimum sum of any valid falling path ending at cell (i, j).

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not force the path to start at column 0; it can start anywhere in the first row. Do not allow out-of-bounds diagonal moves. Return the minimum of the last row, not necessarily the bottom-right cell.

### Complexity
The DP visits every matrix cell once, so time is O(R * C). Space is O(C) with row compression.

cherry-pickup-ii-3d-dp.name: 3D DP (Tabulation)
cherry-pickup-ii-3d-dp.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Cherry Pickup II asks for the maximum cherries two robots can collect while moving from the top row to the bottom row. Robot 1 starts at column 0 and robot 2 starts at the last column. Each step, both move to the next row and may shift left, stay, or shift right.

### Core intuition
Both robots are always on the same row after the same number of moves, so the state is row, robot1 column, robot2 column. At each state, collect cherries from both cells, counting once if both robots meet. Then try all 9 combinations of next moves for the two robots and take the best future value.

### State meaning
dp[row][j1][j2] is the maximum cherries collectable from row to the bottom when robot 1 is at j1 and robot 2 is at j2. A space-optimized version keeps only the next row's C by C table.

### Invariant
For every pair of robot columns on a row, the DP value represents the best possible coordinated future from that exact pair of positions.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not double-count cherries when both robots land on the same cell. Do not move robots independently in separate DPs; their paths interact through shared cells. Check column bounds for all 9 next-move pairs.

### Complexity
There are R * C * C states and 9 transitions per state, so time is O(R * C squared * 9). Space can be optimized to O(C squared).

partition-a-set-into-two-subsets-with-mi.name: DP Tabulation Analysis
partition-a-set-into-two-subsets-with-mi.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Minimum Subset Sum Difference asks us to split the array into two subsets so that the absolute difference between their sums is as small as possible.

### Core intuition
If the total sum is total and one subset has sum s, the other subset has sum total - s. The difference is abs(total - 2 * s). So the real work is finding which subset sums are achievable. Once we know that, we only need to inspect achievable sums up to total / 2.

### State meaning
dp[s] means some subset of processed elements can form sum s. After all elements are processed, every true value in dp is a possible subset sum.

### Invariant
After each number is considered, dp represents exactly the subset sums possible using numbers seen so far.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not try every partition directly; there are exponentially many. Do not inspect sums above total / 2 for the final answer, because they mirror smaller subset sums. Iterate target sums backward for each number so each element is used at most once.

### Complexity
The subset-sum DP costs O(N * totalSum) time and O(totalSum) space.

count-subsets-with-sum-k.name: DP with Space Optimization
count-subsets-with-sum-k.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Count Subsets with Sum K asks how many subsequences or subsets of the array add exactly to K. This is not just yes or no; every valid subset must be counted.

### Core intuition
This is subset sum with counts instead of booleans. dp[t] means how many subsets seen so far can form sum t. For each number x, every old way to form t - x can become a new way to form t by taking x. Iterate backward so each array element is used once.

### State meaning
dp[0] starts as 1 because the empty subset forms sum 0. Each dp[t] accumulates the number of ways to form t using processed elements.

### Invariant
After processing element i, dp[t] equals the number of subsets from elements 0 through i that sum to t.

### Mistakes to avoid
Zeros need special care because taking or not taking zero creates two distinct subsets with the same sum. Do not iterate target forward for 0/1 subset counting, or the same item may be reused in one iteration. Apply modulo if the platform requires it.

### Complexity
The DP runs in O(N * K) time and uses O(K) space.

count-partitions-with-given-difference.name: Reduction to Subset Sum
count-partitions-with-given-difference.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Count Partitions with Given Difference asks how many ways to split the array into two subsets S1 and S2 so that S1 minus S2 equals D.

### Core intuition
We have two equations: S1 - S2 = D and S1 + S2 = total. Adding them gives 2 * S1 = total + D. So the problem becomes counting subsets with sum (total + D) / 2. If total + D is odd, or D is larger than total, no such partition exists.

### State meaning
target is the derived subset sum. dp[t] counts how many subsets can form t. Each counted subset represents one valid partition under the chosen equation.

### Invariant
Every subset counted at the derived target creates a complementary subset whose difference is exactly D.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not solve for the target if total + D is odd. Do not ignore zeros; each zero can double the number of ways. Be consistent about whether ordered partitions are considered distinct; this standard reduction counts by choosing S1.

### Complexity
Counting subsets to the derived target takes O(N * target) time and O(target) space.

minimum-coins.name: Better
minimum-coins.description: Reduce repeated work in Minimum Coins (Coin Change) with the intermediate structure or cached state used by the standard better approach. It preserves the brute-force reasoning while improving one major time or space bottleneck.

minimum-coins.name: DP with Space Optimization
minimum-coins.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Minimum Coins asks for the fewest coins needed to make a target amount when every coin denomination can be used unlimited times. If the amount cannot be formed, return -1.

### Core intuition
Use unbounded coin-change DP. dp[t] is the minimum number of coins needed to make amount t. For each coin, scan target amounts forward, because forward scanning allows the same coin to be reused multiple times. The transition is dp[t] = min(dp[t], 1 + dp[t - coin]).

### State meaning
dp[0] is 0 because no coins make amount 0. Infinity means the amount is not reachable yet.

### Invariant
After processing coins up to a point, dp[t] stores the fewest coins to make t using those coin types with unlimited supply.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not use greedy for arbitrary denominations; it fails on sets like [1, 3, 4] for amount 6. Do not scan target backward in the unbounded version, because that prevents reusing the same coin in the same stage.

### Complexity
For N coin types and amount T, time is O(N * T), and space is O(T).

target-sum.name: Better
target-sum.description: Reduce repeated work in Target Sum with the intermediate structure or cached state used by the standard better approach. It preserves the brute-force reasoning while improving one major time or space bottleneck.

target-sum.name: Reduction to Count Partitions
target-sum.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Target Sum asks how many ways we can place plus or minus signs before every number so the final expression equals target.

### Core intuition
Numbers assigned plus form subset S1. Numbers assigned minus form subset S2. We need S1 - S2 = target, and S1 + S2 = total. This reduces to counting subsets with sum (total + target) / 2, as long as that value is a valid integer.

### State meaning
dp[t] counts how many subsets can make sum t. Each subset chosen as S1 determines which numbers get plus signs; all remaining numbers get minus signs.

### Invariant
Each counted subset at the derived target corresponds to one sign assignment reaching the requested target.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not forget negative target values; use total plus target and check absolute feasibility. If total + target is odd, answer is 0. Zeros double the number of sign assignments because plus zero and minus zero are distinct choices.

### Complexity
The subset-count DP is O(N * derivedTarget) time and O(derivedTarget) space.

coin-change-2.name: Better
coin-change-2.description: Reduce repeated work in Coin Change 2 (Ways) with the intermediate structure or cached state used by the standard better approach. It preserves the brute-force reasoning while improving one major time or space bottleneck.
coin-change-2.timeComplexity: O(N × Target), since each state (ind, T)
coin-change-2.spaceComplexity: O(N × Target) for the DP table, plus O(Target)

coin-change-2.name: DP with Space Optimization
coin-change-2.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Coin Change 2 asks how many combinations can form an amount using unlimited copies of each coin. Order does not matter: 2 + 1 + 1 is the same combination as 1 + 2 + 1.

### Core intuition
Use unbounded counting DP. dp[t] means number of combinations to make amount t using coins processed so far. For each coin, scan t forward from coin to amount and add dp[t - coin]. Processing coins in outer loop prevents counting different orders as separate combinations.

### State meaning
dp[0] is 1 because there is one way to make amount 0: choose no coins. Each coin expands combinations that may include that coin any number of times.

### Invariant
After processing coin i, dp[t] counts combinations for amount t using only coins up to i.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not loop amount outside and coins inside if the problem wants combinations, because that counts permutations. Do not reset dp between coins. Forward iteration is required for unlimited reuse.

### Complexity
For N coins and amount T, time is O(N * T), and space is O(T).

unbounded-knapsack.name: Better
unbounded-knapsack.description: Reduce repeated work in Unbounded Knapsack with the intermediate structure or cached state used by the standard better approach. It preserves the brute-force reasoning while improving one major time or space bottleneck.

unbounded-knapsack.name: Iterative DP
unbounded-knapsack.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Unbounded Knapsack asks for the maximum value that fits within capacity W when each item can be chosen unlimited times.

### Core intuition
This is knapsack with reuse. dp[w] stores the best value for capacity w. For each item, scan capacities forward. If item weight fits, try value[i] + dp[w - weight[i]]. Forward scanning allows the same item to contribute repeatedly.

### State meaning
dp[w] is the best value achievable for capacity w using processed item types with unlimited copies.

### Invariant
After processing an item, dp includes the best values for using that item zero or more times along with earlier items.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not scan capacities backward; that turns it into 0/1 knapsack where each item is used once. Do not confuse item index with capacity. If weights can be zero with positive value, the problem becomes unbounded in a bad way; standard inputs avoid that.

### Complexity
The DP takes O(N * W) time and O(W) space.

longest-palindromic-subsequence.name: LCS Reduction
longest-palindromic-subsequence.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Longest Palindromic Subsequence asks for the longest sequence of characters that can be kept from the string, in order, so that it reads the same forward and backward. Characters do not need to be contiguous.

### Core intuition
A palindrome reads the same as its reverse. So the longest palindromic subsequence of s is the longest common subsequence between s and reverse(s). The LCS finds the longest ordered character sequence that survives in both directions.

### State meaning
The LCS DP compares prefixes of s and reversed s. A match extends a palindromic candidate; a mismatch chooses the better of skipping from one side.

### Invariant
Each DP cell stores the best palindromic subsequence length possible from the compared prefixes.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not solve longest palindromic substring; substring requires contiguity and is a different problem. Do not reverse the answer mentally and expect all matched LCS strings to be unique; only the length is needed here.

### Complexity
LCS of two length-N strings takes O(N squared) time and O(N) space with row optimization.

minimum-insertions-to-make-string-palind.name: Better
minimum-insertions-to-make-string-palind.description: Reduce repeated work in Min Insertions for Palindrome with the intermediate structure or cached state used by the standard better approach. It preserves the brute-force reasoning while improving one major time or space bottleneck.

minimum-insertions-to-make-string-palind.name: LPS Reduction
minimum-insertions-to-make-string-palind.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Minimum Insertions to Make a String Palindrome asks for the fewest characters we must insert anywhere so the string becomes a palindrome.

### Core intuition
Keep the longest palindromic subsequence already present. Those characters can stay in place as the backbone of the final palindrome. Every other character must be matched by inserting a counterpart. Therefore the answer is length of string minus LPS length.

### State meaning
LPS length tells how many characters are already symmetrically reusable. n - LPS counts the characters that need inserted partners.

### Invariant
No solution needs to insert matches for characters already inside the kept palindromic subsequence, and every character outside that subsequence needs one inserted match.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not confuse insertions with replacements. Do not compute longest palindromic substring. Also remember the palindrome can be built by inserting characters, not by reordering the original string.

### Complexity
Computing LPS through LCS with the reversed string takes O(N squared) time and O(N) space if optimized.

minimum-insertions-deletions-to-convert-.name: Better
minimum-insertions-deletions-to-convert-.description: Reduce repeated work in Min Operations to Convert A to B with the intermediate structure or cached state used by the standard better approach. It preserves the brute-force reasoning while improving one major time or space bottleneck.

minimum-insertions-deletions-to-convert-.name: LCS Reduction
minimum-insertions-deletions-to-convert-.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Minimum Insertions and Deletions to Convert String A to String B asks for the fewest delete and insert operations needed to transform one string into another. Replacement is not counted as a single operation here.

### Core intuition
Keep the longest common subsequence between A and B. Those characters are already in the right relative order and do not need to be deleted or inserted. Delete the characters in A that are not in the LCS, then insert the characters in B that are not in the LCS.

### State meaning
LCS length is the number of characters that can be preserved. Deletions equal len(A) - LCS. Insertions equal len(B) - LCS.

### Invariant
Any valid conversion can preserve at most the LCS characters in order, so all other characters must be removed from A or added to reach B.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not use edit distance with replacement unless the problem allows replacement. Do not look only at common character counts; order matters, so LCS is required.

### Complexity
Computing LCS for lengths N and M takes O(N * M) time and O(M) space with row optimization.

shortest-common-supersequence.name: LCS Backtracking
shortest-common-supersequence.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Shortest Common Supersequence asks for the shortest string that contains both input strings as subsequences. We are allowed to merge the strings, sharing common characters instead of writing them twice.

### Core intuition
The longest common subsequence is the part both strings can share. Build the LCS table, then walk backward from the bottom-right. If characters match, add that character once. If they differ, move toward the larger LCS value and add the character from the string you moved from. After one string is exhausted, append the leftover characters from the other string.

### State meaning
The DP table stores LCS lengths for prefixes. During backtracking, i and j point to the remaining suffix boundary of both strings, and the result is built backward.

### Invariant
Every backtracking step preserves both strings as future subsequences of the final reversed answer.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not simply concatenate both strings; that is always a supersequence but not shortest. Do not add matching characters twice. If there is a tie in the DP table, either direction can produce a valid shortest answer, though the exact string may differ.

### Complexity
Building the LCS table takes O(N * M) time and O(N * M) space. Backtracking takes O(N + M).

distinct-subsequences.name: DP with Space Optimization
distinct-subsequences.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Distinct Subsequences asks how many ways we can delete characters from source string S so that the remaining characters equal target string T. The order must stay the same.

### Core intuition
Scan S from left to right while maintaining counts for prefixes of T. dp[j] means how many ways we can form the first j characters of T using characters seen so far in S. When S character matches T[j - 1], it can be used to extend every way that formed T prefix j - 1. We update j backward so one source character is used only once.

### State meaning
dp[0] is 1 because the empty target can always be formed by deleting everything. dp[m] becomes the final answer for the full target.

### Invariant
After processing S up to index i, dp[j] equals the number of subsequences of S[0..i] that form T[0..j-1].

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not update j forward, or the same source character can help form multiple target positions in one step. Do not treat equal letters as interchangeable without order; positions matter.

### Complexity
The DP takes O(N * M) time for source length N and target length M, with O(M) space.

wildcard-matching.name: Better
wildcard-matching.description: Reduce repeated work in Wildcard Matching with the intermediate structure or cached state used by the standard better approach. It preserves the brute-force reasoning while improving one major time or space bottleneck.
wildcard-matching.spaceComplexity: O(N*M) + O(N+M) , We are using a recursion stack space(O(N+M))

wildcard-matching.name: DP with Space Optimization
wildcard-matching.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Wildcard Matching asks whether an entire string matches a pattern containing two special characters: question mark matches exactly one character, and star matches any sequence, including the empty sequence.

### Core intuition
Use DP over string and pattern prefixes. If pattern character equals the string character, or it is question mark, the match depends on the previous prefixes. If pattern character is star, it has two choices: match nothing and move past the star, or match one more string character and keep the star active.

### State meaning
dp[i][j] means the first i characters of the string match the first j characters of the pattern. In space-optimized form, prev is the previous string row and curr is the current row.

### Invariant
Every DP cell represents a full-prefix match, never a partial substring match.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not treat star as exactly one character; that is what question mark does. Do not forget that leading stars can match an empty string. The whole string must match the whole pattern.

### Complexity
The DP evaluates O(N * M) states and can use O(M) space with row optimization.

best-time-to-buy-and-sell-stock-with-tra.name: DP with 2 States
best-time-to-buy-and-sell-stock-with-tra.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Stock with Transaction Fee asks for the maximum profit from unlimited buy-sell transactions, but every completed sale pays a fixed fee. We can hold at most one stock at a time.

### Core intuition
Track two states. held is the best profit after buying or while holding a stock. cash is the best profit while not holding a stock. Each day, either keep the old state or switch: sell held stock into cash after paying the fee, or buy stock from cash.

### State meaning
held represents profit if one stock is currently in hand. cash represents profit if no stock is currently held and all previous transactions are settled.

### Invariant
After each price, held and cash are the best possible profits for their respective states using days seen so far.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not subtract the fee on both buy and sell; subtract it once per transaction. Do not allow buying again before selling. Be careful to update states from the previous day's values conceptually.

### Complexity
One pass over prices gives O(N) time and O(1) space.

print-longest-increasing-subsequence.name: DP with Parent Tracking
print-longest-increasing-subsequence.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Print Longest Increasing Subsequence asks for the actual increasing subsequence, not only its length. That means we must remember how the best sequence was formed.

### Core intuition
Use the O(N squared) LIS DP with parent tracking. dp[i] is the length of the LIS ending at i. parent[i] stores the previous index in that LIS. Whenever nums[j] is smaller than nums[i] and improves dp[i], set parent[i] = j. After the table is built, start from the index with maximum dp and follow parents backward.

### State meaning
dp stores lengths, parent stores reconstruction links, and maxIndex stores where the best LIS ends.

### Invariant
If parent[i] = j, then nums[j] is immediately before nums[i] in the best increasing subsequence ending at i.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not use the O(N log N) tails array alone if you need straightforward reconstruction; it needs extra bookkeeping. Do not forget to reverse the backtracked result. Strictly increasing means use greater than, not greater than or equal.

### Complexity
The DP uses O(N squared) time and O(N) space.

largest-divisible-subset.name: Sort + LIS Variant
largest-divisible-subset.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Largest Divisible Subset asks for the largest subset where for every pair, one number divides the other. We must return the subset itself, not just its size.

### Core intuition
Sort the numbers. After sorting, if nums[i] is divisible by nums[j] for j less than i, then nums[i] can extend the divisible chain ending at j. This becomes an LIS-style DP with parent tracking.

### State meaning
dp[i] is the length of the largest divisible subset ending at i. parent[i] points to the previous number in that subset.

### Invariant
The chain ending at i is valid because every later chosen number is divisible by the previous one, and sorting makes divisibility transitive across the chain.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not skip sorting; pairwise divisibility is much easier in increasing order. Do not require adjacent values in the original array. If nums[i] % nums[j] == 0 improves the chain, update both dp and parent.

### Complexity
Sorting costs O(N log N), and the DP costs O(N squared). Space is O(N).

longest-string-chain.name: Sort + Hash Map DP
longest-string-chain.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Longest String Chain asks for the longest sequence of words where each next word can be formed by adding exactly one character to the previous word.

### Core intuition
Sort words by length. For each word, try deleting one character at every position. If the shorter word exists, it can be a predecessor. Then dp[word] is one plus the best dp among all valid predecessors.

### State meaning
dp[word] stores the longest chain ending at that word. The dictionary is safe because shorter words are processed before longer words.

### Invariant
When processing a word, every possible predecessor of length one less has already been processed.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not compare every pair character by character if deletion generation is simpler. Do not sort lexicographically only; length order is the important one. A predecessor must be exactly one character shorter.

### Complexity
For N words of maximum length L, generating deletions costs about O(N * L squared) because slicing creates strings. Space is O(N * L) for stored words and DP keys.

longest-bitonic-subsequence.name: Bidirectional DP
longest-bitonic-subsequence.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Longest Bitonic Subsequence asks for the longest subsequence that first strictly increases and then strictly decreases. The turning point is the peak.

### Core intuition
For every index i, compute LIS ending at i from the left and LDS starting at i from the right. If i is the peak, the bitonic length is lisLeft[i] + ldsRight[i] - 1, subtracting one because the peak is counted twice.

### State meaning
lisLeft[i] is the best increasing length ending at i. ldsRight[i] is the best decreasing length starting at i. The answer is the best peak combination.

### Invariant
For each possible peak, the left DP gives the best climb into it and the right DP gives the best descent out of it.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not count the peak twice. Do not use non-decreasing comparisons if the problem says strictly increasing and decreasing. Some variants require both sides to have length greater than 1; follow the platform statement.

### Complexity
Two O(N squared) DP passes give O(N squared) time and O(N) space.

number-of-longest-increasing-subsequence.name: DP with Count Array
number-of-longest-increasing-subsequence.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Number of Longest Increasing Subsequences asks how many different increasing subsequences achieve the maximum possible LIS length.

### Core intuition
Track two things for each index. length[i] is the LIS length ending at i. count[i] is how many such sequences end at i. If nums[j] can precede nums[i] and creates a longer length, replace length[i] and reset count[i] to count[j]. If it creates another way with the same best length, add count[j].

### State meaning
length stores best lengths by ending position. count stores how many best sequences have that exact ending position. The final answer sums counts at indices whose length equals the global maximum.

### Invariant
After processing i, count[i] counts only subsequences that end at i and have length length[i].

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not count all increasing subsequences; count only those with maximum length. Do not add counts when a longer path is found; reset first, then add only ties. Strictly increasing means nums[i] must be greater than nums[j].

### Complexity
The nested DP costs O(N squared) time and O(N) space.

minimum-cost-to-cut-the-stick.name: MCM-style Partitioning
minimum-cost-to-cut-the-stick.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Minimum Cost to Cut the Stick gives a stick length and cut positions. Every time we cut a segment, the cost is the current segment length. We need the order of cuts with minimum total cost.

### Core intuition
This is interval DP. Sort the cuts and add boundaries 0 and n. For an interval between two boundaries, choose which cut inside it happens first. If cut k is first inside that interval, we pay the whole interval length now, then solve the left and right subintervals independently. Try every possible first cut and take the minimum.

### State meaning
dp[i][j] is the minimum cost to perform cuts from index i through j in the sorted cuts list. The surrounding boundaries are cuts[i - 1] and cuts[j + 1].

### Invariant
Every dp interval assumes the outside boundary cuts already exist, so its current segment length is fixed and known.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not cut in sorted order greedily; that can be expensive. Do not forget to add 0 and n as boundaries. Be careful with interval indices: empty intervals cost 0.

### Complexity
For M cuts, there are O(M squared) intervals and O(M) choices inside each, so time is O(M cubed). Space is O(M squared).

burst-balloons.name: Reverse DP (Final Burst)
burst-balloons.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Burst Balloons gives us an array of balloon values. When a balloon is burst, the coins earned are left neighbor value times current balloon value times right neighbor value, using the neighbors that are still alive at that moment. We need the maximum coins over all possible bursting orders.

### Core intuition
The first burst is hard to reason about because it changes the neighbors for everything nearby. The trick is to reverse the question: for a chosen interval, which balloon is burst last? If balloon k is last inside interval i to j, then all other balloons in that interval are already gone, so k's neighbors are fixed: i - 1 and j + 1. That gives one clean formula.

### State meaning
dp[i][j] is the maximum coins we can collect by bursting every balloon from i through j, after adding virtual balloons of value 1 at both ends.

### Transition
Try every k from i to j as the last balloon. The score is dp[i][k - 1] + nums[i - 1] * nums[k] * nums[j + 1] + dp[k + 1][j]. Keep the maximum.

### Invariant
When solving dp[i][j], the outside balloons i - 1 and j + 1 are still present, so they are safe fixed boundaries for the final burst inside this interval.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not simulate greedy first bursts; a locally high product can damage future choices. Do not forget the two boundary 1s. Also build smaller intervals before larger intervals, because a large interval depends on its left and right subinterval answers.

### Complexity
There are O(N squared) intervals and O(N) choices for the last balloon in each interval, so time is O(N cubed). The DP table uses O(N squared) space.

evaluate-boolean-expression-to-true.name: Brute Force
evaluate-boolean-expression-to-true.description: Build a correctness baseline for Boolean Evaluation to True by directly enumerating the candidate states allowed by the problem. This version prioritizes transparent control flow and exhaustive coverage over asymptotic efficiency.

evaluate-boolean-expression-to-true.name: Better
evaluate-boolean-expression-to-true.description: Reduce repeated work in Boolean Evaluation to True with the intermediate structure or cached state used by the standard better approach. It preserves the brute-force reasoning while improving one major time or space bottleneck.

evaluate-boolean-expression-to-true.name: 3D DP Partitioning
evaluate-boolean-expression-to-true.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Boolean Evaluation to True gives an expression made from T, F, and the operators &, |, and ^. We must count how many ways to parenthesize the expression so the final value becomes true.

### Core intuition
Every operator splits the expression into a left expression and a right expression. For each side, knowing only true ways is not enough; false ways also matter. For example, XOR becomes true from true-false or false-true, while AND becomes true only from true-true.

### State meaning
dp[i][j][true] stores how many ways substring i to j can evaluate to true. dp[i][j][false] stores how many ways the same substring can evaluate to false. Valid operands are at even indices and operators are between them.

### Transition
For every operator position k between i and j, combine left true/false counts with right true/false counts using that operator's truth table. Add the resulting true and false counts into dp[i][j].

### Invariant
Before computing a longer expression, every smaller left and right expression it can split into already has correct true and false counts.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not count only the true side; false counts are needed to form true results for | and ^. Do not split at operand indices. If the platform asks for modulo arithmetic, apply the modulo after additions to avoid overflow.

### Complexity
There are O(N squared) substrings and O(N) operator splits per substring, so time is O(N cubed). Storing true and false counts for intervals uses O(N squared) space.

palindrome-partitioning-ii.name: Linear DP with Palindrome Pre-check
palindrome-partitioning-ii.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Palindrome Partitioning II asks for the minimum number of cuts needed so every resulting substring is a palindrome. We do not need to list every partition; we only need the smallest cut count.

### Core intuition
If a substring s[j..i] is a palindrome, then the best answer for prefix s[0..i] can end with that block. If j is 0, the whole prefix is already a palindrome and needs 0 cuts. Otherwise, we take the best answer for s[0..j-1] and add one cut before j.

### State meaning
dp[i] is the minimum cuts needed for prefix s[0..i]. A palindrome table or on-the-fly palindrome expansion tells us quickly whether s[j..i] is valid as the last block.

### Invariant
When computing dp[i], every smaller prefix ending before i already has its minimum cut count, so any palindromic suffix can safely attach to the best earlier answer.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not confuse number of pieces with number of cuts; three pieces need two cuts. Do not add one cut when j is 0. Also avoid rechecking every substring naively with a nested scan, because that can push the solution toward O(N cubed).

### Complexity
With palindrome precomputation or integrated palindrome filling, time is O(N squared). The palindrome table uses O(N squared) space, while the cut DP itself is O(N).

partition-array-for-maximum-sum.name: Linear DP (Look-back)
partition-array-for-maximum-sum.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Partition Array for Maximum Sum lets us split the array into groups of length at most K. Inside each group, every element is replaced by that group's maximum value. We must maximize the final total sum.

### Core intuition
Build the answer from left to right. When deciding dp[i], meaning the best score for the first i elements, the last group could have length 1, 2, up to K. As we extend that last group backward, we keep its maximum value and compute what score it would contribute.

### State meaning
dp[i] is the maximum score obtainable from the first i elements of the array. For each possible last group length len, the candidate is dp[i - len] plus max(last group) times len.

### Invariant
Before dp[i] is finalized, all dp values for shorter prefixes are already optimal, so choosing a final group length only needs one look back.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not sort the group; partitions must preserve array order. Do not allow a group longer than K. Also remember that the group's maximum replaces every element in that group, so the contribution is max value times group length.

### Complexity
For each of N positions we inspect up to K possible final group lengths. Time is O(NK), and the dp array uses O(N) space.

longest-word-with-all-prefixes.name: Trie-based Pre-prefix Check
longest-word-with-all-prefixes.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Complete String asks us to find the longest word such that every prefix of that word is also present as a complete word in the array. If multiple valid words have the same length, we choose the lexicographically smallest one. If no word qualifies, return None.

### Core intuition
A trie makes prefix checking natural, but a path existing is not enough. Every node along the word's path must be marked as an end of a word. For example, having the path n-i-n-j-a does not help unless n, ni, nin, ninj, and ninja are all complete words.

### Algorithm
Insert all words into the trie and mark terminal nodes. Then test each word by walking its characters. If any prefix node is not terminal, reject the word. Otherwise, compare it with the current best by length and lexicographic order.

### Invariant
A word is accepted only if every prefix visited during its trie traversal has already been inserted as a full word.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not accept a word just because all characters can be traversed. Do not forget the tie rule; for equal lengths, the smaller dictionary word wins. Also return the platform's expected empty answer, often None or an empty string, when no complete word exists.

### Complexity
Inserting and checking all words costs O(total characters). The trie also uses O(total characters) space in the worst case.

bit-prerequisites-for-tries.name: Fundamental Ops
bit-prerequisites-for-tries.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact topic is asking
Bitwise Pre-Requisites is the small toolbox needed before binary trie problems. We need to read, set, clear, and reason about individual bits because XOR tries make decisions one bit at a time.

### Core intuition
A bit mask isolates one position. The expression 1 << i creates a number whose only set bit is position i, counting from the right starting at 0. Combining that mask with &, |, or negation lets us check, set, or clear that bit.

### Operations
To check bit i, use (n >> i) & 1. To set bit i, use n | (1 << i). To clear bit i, use n & ~(1 << i). XOR compares bits: different bits produce 1, equal bits produce 0.

### Invariant
Every operation changes or reads only the targeted bit position, while all other bits either stay untouched or are shifted only for inspection.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not count bit positions from the left unless the problem explicitly says so. Do not use logical operators where bitwise operators are needed. Also remember that to maximize XOR, at each high bit you prefer the opposite bit if it exists.

### Complexity
Single bit operations are O(1). In trie problems, scanning a fixed number of bits such as 31 or 32 is treated as constant per number.

maximum-xor-with-an-element-from-array.name: Offline Sorting + Trie
maximum-xor-with-an-element-from-array.description:
## Premium walkthrough

### What this exact problem is asking
Maximum XOR With an Element From Array gives nums and queries of the form x, m. For each query, choose a number from nums that is less than or equal to m and maximize x XOR that number. If no number is eligible, return -1.

### Core intuition
A normal XOR trie can answer max XOR quickly, but the limit m changes per query. Handle this offline: sort nums ascending, sort queries by m, and gradually insert into the trie only the numbers that have become eligible for the current m. Then each query sees exactly the allowed numbers.

### Algorithm
Attach original indices to queries, sort queries by m, and keep a pointer in sorted nums. For each query, insert all nums[p] <= m that were not inserted before. If the trie is empty, answer -1. Otherwise, run the usual max-XOR trie search for x and store the result at the query's original index.

### Invariant
When processing queries in increasing m, the trie contains all and only numbers from nums that are <= the current query limit.

### Mistakes to avoid
Do not answer queries in sorted order without restoring original order. Do not rebuild the trie from scratch for every query. Also check the empty-trie case before searching, because some small limits may allow no numbers.

### Complexity
Sorting costs O(N log N + Q log Q). Each number is inserted once and each query scans a fixed bit width, so trie work is O((N + Q) * B). Space is O(N * B).
